Adding Value
Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
title: Harbourline Outfitters: Store Expansion and Revenue Recognition
case_background:
It is October 2026. You, CPA, work at Beausoleil and Fry LLP and have been assigned to Harbourline Outfitters Inc. (HOI), a private company that sells outdoor apparel and camping gear through 14 stores across Atlantic Canada and a growing online channel. HOI is owned by siblings Denise and Marc Poirier, who each hold 50 percent of the common shares and sit as the only two members of the board. The company has a September 30 year end and your firm performs a review engagement each year because HOI's operating line of credit with Coastal Trust requires review-level financial statements as a covenant condition.

Denise, who serves as CEO, has asked you to meet with her before fieldwork begins because she wants your advice on two matters before the board meeting next week, plus your firm's usual review procedures. HOI's revenue was 18.4 million dollars in fiscal 2026, up from 15.9 million in fiscal 2025, and pre-tax income was 1.1 million dollars, down from 1.6 million the prior year despite the revenue growth. Denise is concerned about what she should tell the bank and her brother about the profit decline, and she wants to open two new stores in Nova Scotia next spring using a construction loan, which would push HOI's debt to equity ratio close to the 2.5 to 1 maximum allowed under the Coastal Trust covenant.

In April 2026, HOI launched a customer loyalty program called Harbour Rewards. Customers earn one point for every dollar spent, and 500 points can be redeemed for a 25 dollar merchandise credit good for 18 months. HOI's controller, a bookkeeper without an accounting designation, recorded the full sale as revenue at the point of sale and expensed the estimated cost of future redemptions as a marketing expense when points are issued, based on an estimate that 60 percent of points will eventually be redeemed. Denise mentioned that finance staff picked this approach because it was the simplest to program into the point of sale system, and she is not sure whether it is correct but does not want to disrupt the system again before the holiday season.

Separately, in June 2026 HOI entered into a new 10 year lease for its Halifax flagship store. The lease requires monthly payments of 22,000 dollars, includes an option to renew for a further 5 years at market rent, and requires HOI to pay all property taxes, insurance and maintenance. The landlord also contributed 180,000 dollars toward leasehold improvements, which HOI recorded as a reduction of rent expense as the contribution was received rather than as a liability. The controller expensed the full 22,000 dollars monthly payment as rent expense, consistent with how HOI has always accounted for its other store leases, all of which are shorter term.

Denise also privately tells you that Marc has been pushing to take an unusually large owner draw before year end to cover a personal real estate purchase, and she is worried this will make the debt to equity ratio worse right before the bank renews the credit facility in December. She asks whether there is anything she needs to disclose to the bank about this, and whether you can help her think through how to present the profit decline and the expansion plan to Marc and to the board, since the two siblings have historically disagreed on how much risk the company should take.

Your engagement partner has asked you to prepare a memo addressing the accounting issues, the covenant and expansion implications, and how Denise should approach the upcoming board discussion, being mindful that HOI is a private company where the two owners have competing personal interests.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Harbour Rewards Program Data (fiscal 2026)
exhibits.content: Points issued since program launch (April 1 to September 30, 2026): 2,400,000 points, corresponding to 2,400,000 dollars of qualifying sales included in the 18.4 million dollars of total revenue. Redemption rate estimate provided by the marketing department, based on industry benchmarks for similar loyalty programs: 60 percent of points issued are expected to be redeemed within the 18 month window. Point value: 500 points equal a 25 dollar merchandise credit, meaning each point issued represents a stand alone value of 0.05 dollars to the customer at the time of redemption. Marketing expense recorded for estimated future redemptions: 2,400,000 points times 0.05 dollars times 60 percent = 72,000 dollars, included in selling expenses. No liability was recorded on the balance sheet for unredeemed points outstanding at September 30, 2026.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Halifax Flagship Store Lease Summary
exhibits.content: Lease commencement: July 1, 2026. Term: 10 years with one 5 year renewal option at market rent. Monthly base rent: 22,000 dollars, with 2.5 percent annual escalation starting year 2. HOI is responsible for property tax, insurance and common area maintenance (a triple net lease). Landlord provided a tenant improvement allowance of 180,000 dollars, paid in July 2026, which HOI used toward fixtures and build out costs of 310,000 dollars capitalized as leasehold improvements. The store has no purchase option and title to the leased space never transfers to HOI. Estimated useful life of the leasehold improvements is 12 years. Fiscal 2026 rent expense recorded: 22,000 dollars times 3 months (July, August, September) equals 66,000 dollars, net of no adjustment for the tenant allowance recognized separately as other income of 180,000 dollars in the same period.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 3: Coastal Trust Credit Agreement Extract and Draft Balance Sheet Data
exhibits.content: Covenant clause 4.2: HOI shall maintain a ratio of total liabilities to shareholders equity of not more than 2.5 to 1, tested quarterly, reported within 30 days of quarter end. Draft balance sheet at September 30, 2026 (before considering the two issues above): total liabilities 9.8 million dollars, total shareholders equity 4.3 million dollars, giving a ratio of 2.28 to 1. Marc has requested a shareholder draw of 500,000 dollars to be paid in November 2026, which would be recorded against retained earnings. The proposed Nova Scotia expansion would require a new construction loan of approximately 1.6 million dollars, expected to draw in April 2027, which falls in fiscal 2027 but which the bank has asked HOI to discuss in principle at the December 2026 renewal meeting.

requirements: Advise Denise on the appropriate accounting treatment for the Harbour Rewards loyalty program and the Halifax store lease, including the impact on fiscal 2026 financial statements.
Assess the impact of the accounting issues, the proposed owner draw, and the planned expansion on the Coastal Trust debt covenant, and advise on how Denise should approach the bank.
Advise Denise on how to communicate the profit decline and the expansion decision to Marc and the board, given the ownership dynamics and the ethical considerations raised by Marc's draw request.

model_response.understand_issue: You are a CPA at HOI's review engagement firm, asked by the CEO, Denise, to advise both on year end accounting treatment and on how to navigate a sensitive ownership and financing situation before a board meeting. The audience is Denise directly, but the analysis must anticipate how the bank and Marc will read the statements. The real ask is threefold: get the loyalty program and lease accounting right under the framework HOI uses, translate those corrections into covenant and financing consequences, and give Denise practical, ethically sound advice for managing a co owner who wants a large personal draw right before a bank renewal.
model_response.analyze_case:
Loyalty program: Harbour Rewards points are a separate performance obligation under the private enterprise revenue framework HOI would apply, since customers are receiving a material right to future discounted merchandise they would not get without the purchase. The current policy of recognizing full revenue at sale and expensing an estimated redemption cost misstates the pattern of revenue recognition, because part of the 2,400,000 dollars of qualifying sales relates to consideration for the points, not the initial purchase. Using the relative stand alone selling price approach, a portion of transaction price should be allocated to a contract liability (deferred revenue) for unredeemed points and recognized as revenue only as points are redeemed or expire, not expensed as a marketing cost. The 60 percent redemption estimate is relevant to measuring the deferred revenue and the breakage expected to be recognized, not to a cash expense estimate. This changes both the balance sheet, by adding a liability rather than reducing equity through an expense, and the income statement, since revenue recognized in the period should be reduced rather than an expense added, with a partially offsetting effect on net income depending on final numbers, but a liability now appears in place of what had been treated as fully earned revenue.

Lease accounting: the Halifax lease is a 10 year triple net lease where HOI bears essentially all costs of using the space and has an economic incentive to exercise the renewal, since it invested 310,000 dollars in improvements with a 12 year useful life that outlasts the initial term. Treating this the same as HOI's short term store leases, straight line rent expense with no recognition of the escalation clause, is not appropriate given the newer leases standard most private companies are now applying or transitioning toward; even under the older model, the 2.5 percent annual escalations should be straight lined over the lease term rather than expensed as paid, and the tenant improvement allowance of 180,000 dollars should be recorded as a lease incentive that reduces the leasehold improvement asset or is amortized as a reduction of rent expense over the lease term, not booked as other income in the period received. Recording it as immediate income overstates fiscal 2026 net income and misstates the nature of the item.

Covenant and financing: correcting the loyalty program creates a new deferred revenue liability, correcting the lease incentive removes 180,000 dollars of one time other income and instead spreads a smaller benefit over 10 years, and both changes reduce fiscal 2026 net income and retained earnings relative to what was reported, while also adding liabilities. Since the draft ratio of 2.28 to 1 is already close to the 2.5 to 1 ceiling, an increase in liabilities and a decrease in equity both push the ratio in the wrong direction, and this needs to be quantified precisely before the December renewal conversation, not discovered by the bank later. Layering Marc's 500,000 dollar draw on top, which directly reduces equity, and then planning a further 1.6 million dollar construction loan for spring, means HOI could breach or come dangerously close to breaching the covenant, and Denise needs full visibility into the combined effect before committing to any of these steps.
model_response.recommendation: Recommend correcting the loyalty program accounting to recognize a contract liability for unredeemed points measured using the relative stand alone selling price method, updated at each reporting date for the 60 percent redemption estimate, with revenue recognized as points are redeemed or expire rather than at initial sale. Recommend restating the lease accounting to straight line the escalating rent payments over the 10 year term and to treat the 180,000 dollar tenant allowance as a lease incentive amortized over the term rather than one time income, and to reassess whether the lease meets criteria that would require recognition of a right of use asset and lease liability depending on the accounting framework HOI has adopted. Recommend Denise quantify the combined effect of both corrections on the debt to equity ratio before the board meeting and share this proactively with Coastal Trust rather than waiting for the reviewed statements to surface it, since covenant lenders generally respond better to early disclosure with a credible plan than to a surprise breach. On Marc's draw, recommend Denise raise directly with Marc, ideally with you present or through the board, that a 500,000 dollar draw taken while the company is planning to seek 1.6 million dollars of new construction financing and is already near the covenant ceiling creates real risk to the bank relationship and to both owners equally, and that the timing and size of any draw should be a board level decision made with full information about the corrected financial position, not a unilateral personal decision. Recommend the expansion decision itself be deferred until the corrected year end numbers and covenant headroom are clear, and documented through a board resolution reflecting both owners informed agreement.
model_response.conclusion: HOI's underlying business is growing and profitable, but two accounting policies applied for system convenience rather than technical correctness have overstated fiscal 2026 income and understated liabilities at exactly the moment the company is approaching a covenant limit and planning new debt. Denise's real task is not just fixing two entries, it is bringing accurate numbers to the table before decisions are made about Marc's draw and the Nova Scotia expansion, so that both owners and the bank are working from the same accurate picture rather than one that looks better than reality until the review is complete.

marking_guide.criterion: Identifies that the loyalty program creates a separate performance obligation requiring allocation of transaction price and a deferred revenue liability, not a marketing expense
Uses the case facts (2,400,000 points, 0.05 dollars per point, 60 percent redemption) to explain how the deferred revenue amount would be estimated
Identifies that the tenant improvement allowance should be treated as a lease incentive amortized over the lease term rather than immediate other income
Identifies that escalating rent payments should be straight lined over the lease term rather than expensed as paid
Quantifies or clearly explains the directional impact of both corrections plus the proposed draw on the 2.5 to 1 covenant ratio using the 2.28 to 1 draft figure
Recommends proactive disclosure to the lender rather than waiting for the reviewed statements to reveal the issue
Recommends deferring or reassessing the expansion timeline pending confirmed covenant headroom

marking_guide.competency: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
marking_guide.criterion: Recognizes the conflict of interest in Marc taking a personal draw that affects a shared covenant and both owners financial interests, and frames it as a governance issue rather than staying silent

marking_guide.competency: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
marking_guide.criterion: Does not simply tell Denise what she wants to hear about the profit decline but presents the corrected, less favourable picture candidly

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Structures the response by first understanding what HOI's accounting policy choices were driven by (system limitations) before concluding they are wrong, showing balanced judgment

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Connects the two seemingly separate accounting issues to a single downstream consequence, the covenant ratio, rather than analyzing them in isolation
Recommends the draw and expansion decisions be brought to the full board with both owners rather than resolved informally between Denise and the writer
Provides Denise with practical, specific language or approach for raising the draw issue with Marc without escalating the sibling conflict unnecessarily
Goes beyond identifying the accounting errors to advise on timing and sequencing of the bank conversation relative to the board meeting and the expansion financing
Identifies that HOI's underlying revenue and cash generation are still healthy despite the corrections, giving Denise a constructive framing for the bank and Marc rather than only bad news

rating_guidance: A nominal competence response identifies that something might be wrong with either the loyalty program or the lease treatment but does not explain why using the specific point values, redemption percentage, or lease terms given, and gives generic advice to talk to the bank without connecting it to the covenant math. A reaching competence response correctly identifies at least one of the two accounting issues with reference to case facts and recognizes the covenant is a concern, but does not fully link the corrections to the 2.28 to 1 ratio or does not address the Marc draw as a governance issue. A competent response correctly diagnoses both the loyalty program and lease issues using the specific numbers in the exhibits, quantifies or clearly reasons through the directional covenant impact including the draw and expansion, and gives Denise concrete, professionally grounded advice for the board and lender conversations that treats Marc's draw as a shared governance decision rather than staying silent on it. A response reaching competence with distinction does all of the above and also integrates the pieces into a coherent sequencing recommendation, for example correcting the statements first, quantifying headroom, then deciding the draw and expansion together as one board decision, while proactively identifying the underlying business strength as a counterbalance to the bad news, showing sophisticated judgment about how the pieces of this case interact rather than treating them as a checklist.

title: Prairie Steel Fabricators: Costing and a Capital Investment Decision
case_background:
It is January 2027. You, CPA, are the newly hired controller at Prairie Steel Fabricators Ltd. (PSF), a private manufacturer near Regina that custom fabricates steel components for agricultural equipment makers and grain handling companies. PSF has two production lines: the Standard line, which makes high volume, low complexity brackets and frames, and the Custom line, which makes low volume, highly engineered components to customer specifications. PSF has used a single plant wide overhead rate based on direct labour hours for over 15 years, applied at 140 dollars per direct labour hour for fiscal 2026.

The founder and majority owner, Wade Bilodeau, is planning to retire in three years and wants to sell PSF to his daughter Anya, who currently runs operations. Before finalizing the sale price and the transition plan, Wade has asked you, in your first month on the job, to review whether PSF's product costing is giving management an accurate picture of profitability, because he has noticed that the Custom line seems to be growing in volume but he cannot tell from the current numbers whether it is actually more profitable than the Standard line or just busier.

Separately, Anya wants to invest in a new robotic welding cell that would primarily benefit the Custom line, since it can handle small batch, high precision welds that currently require slow manual work. The equipment costs 640,000 dollars installed, has an estimated useful life of 8 years with no salvage value, and PSF's cost of capital is 9 percent. Anya projects the robotic cell will reduce direct labour hours on Custom line jobs by 35 percent and will allow PSF to take on an additional 400,000 dollars of Custom line revenue annually that it currently turns away due to capacity constraints, at an estimated contribution margin of 40 percent on that incremental revenue before considering the labour savings.

Wade is cautious about the investment because PSF's line of credit is already drawn to 70 percent of its 1.2 million dollar limit, and he does not want to jeopardize the business before the ownership transition to Anya is complete. He has also privately mentioned to you that he is worried Anya is overly optimistic about growth projections because she is eager to prove herself before taking over, and he wants your independent, unbiased view, not just an evaluation that confirms what Anya wants to hear.

You pull PSF's fiscal 2026 production data to begin the costing review, and you also need to build a capital budgeting analysis for the robotic welding cell that Wade can rely on as he finalizes the succession plan.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Fiscal 2026 Production and Cost Data by Line
exhibits.content: Standard line: direct labour hours 18,000, direct labour cost 22 dollars per hour, direct materials cost 1,850,000 dollars, revenue 4,900,000 dollars, number of production runs 40, number of quality inspections 120. Custom line: direct labour hours 9,000, direct labour cost 28 dollars per hour (more skilled welders), direct materials cost 1,050,000 dollars, revenue 3,600,000 dollars, number of production runs 210, number of quality inspections 480 (custom parts require inspection at multiple stages). Total plant wide overhead for fiscal 2026: 3,780,000 dollars, currently applied at 140 dollars per direct labour hour across both lines (27,000 total direct labour hours times 140 dollars equals 3,780,000 dollars). A review of the overhead pool shows it includes: machine setup and changeover costs of 1,260,000 dollars (driven by number of production runs), quality inspection costs of 900,000 dollars (driven by number of inspections), and remaining factory overhead (rent, utilities, supervision, depreciation) of 1,620,000 dollars, which is reasonably related to direct labour hours.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Robotic Welding Cell Proposal
exhibits.content: Installed cost: 640,000 dollars, useful life 8 years, straight line depreciation, no salvage value. Estimated annual direct labour hour reduction on Custom line: 35 percent of current 9,000 hours, at the Custom line's 28 dollars per hour rate, plus associated variable overhead savings estimated at 15 dollars per hour saved (relating to setup efficiency gains). Incremental Custom line revenue enabled by added capacity: 400,000 dollars annually, at a 40 percent contribution margin before labour savings are counted separately. Estimated annual maintenance and operating cost of the robotic cell: 58,000 dollars. PSF's after tax cost of capital: 9 percent. PSF's effective income tax rate: 27 percent. PSF's current line of credit: 1.2 million dollar limit, drawn to 840,000 dollars (70 percent utilized) as of December 31, 2026.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 3: Wade's Notes on the Succession Plan
exhibits.content: Wade's handwritten notes, shared with you: Sale price for PSF to be based on a multiple of normalized EBITDA, to be negotiated with Anya's lawyer over the next 18 months. Wade wants confidence that Custom line growth is real profit growth, not just revenue growth, before agreeing to a higher multiple based partly on Custom line momentum. Wade notes that the bank has indicated it would consider increasing the line of credit limit to 1.5 million dollars if PSF's debt service coverage ratio stays above 1.25, but has not yet been asked about financing the welding cell specifically. Wade also notes: Anya has told two long standing Custom line customers that PSF will be able to take on more of their work starting in the second quarter, before the equipment has been approved or ordered.

requirements: Recalculate the fiscal 2026 profitability of the Standard and Custom lines using a more appropriate costing approach, and explain what this reveals about whether Custom line growth is genuine profit growth.
Evaluate the financial merits of the robotic welding cell investment for the Custom line, including a quantitative assessment of whether it should proceed.
Advise Wade on how to approach Anya about the investment decision and her communication to customers, balancing Anya's role in the succession plan with the need for disciplined, independent analysis.

model_response.understand_issue: You are the new controller reporting to Wade, the founder and majority owner, who wants an independent, unbiased assessment separate from his daughter Anya's enthusiasm as she prepares to take over the business. The real questions are whether the current single overhead rate is distorting product line profitability, whether the robotic welding cell is a sound investment on its own financial merits, and how Wade should handle a succession situation where his successor may be getting ahead of approved decisions. The audience is Wade specifically, and the advice needs to support a sale price negotiation as well as an operating decision.
model_response.analyze_case:
Costing issue: PSF's plant wide rate of 140 dollars per direct labour hour assumes all overhead is driven by labour hours, but the overhead pool breaks into three components with different drivers. Setup and changeover costs of 1,260,000 dollars are driven by number of production runs (250 total runs: 40 Standard, 210 Custom), giving a rate of 5,040 dollars per run. Quality inspection costs of 900,000 dollars are driven by number of inspections (600 total: 120 Standard, 480 Custom), giving a rate of 1,500 dollars per inspection. The remaining 1,620,000 dollars relates reasonably to the 27,000 total direct labour hours, giving 60 dollars per hour. Applying activity based costing: Standard line overhead equals (40 runs times 5,040) plus (120 inspections times 1,500) plus (18,000 hours times 60) equals 201,600 plus 180,000 plus 1,080,000 equals 1,461,600 dollars. Custom line overhead equals (210 times 5,040) plus (480 times 1,500) plus (9,000 times 60) equals 1,058,400 plus 720,000 plus 540,000 equals 2,318,400 dollars. This contrasts sharply with the current plant wide allocation of 2,520,000 dollars to Standard (18,000 hours times 140) and 1,260,000 dollars to Custom (9,000 hours times 140), which understates Custom line overhead by roughly 1,058,000 dollars and overstates Standard line overhead by a similar amount. Recomputing margins: Standard line under ABC has revenue 4,900,000 dollars less direct materials 1,850,000, direct labour 396,000 (18,000 times 22), and overhead 1,461,600, giving operating income of roughly 1,192,400 dollars, a margin of about 24 percent, actually better than the plant wide method suggested. Custom line has revenue 3,600,000 less direct materials 1,050,000, direct labour 252,000 (9,000 times 28), and overhead 2,318,400, giving operating income of roughly negative 20,400 dollars, essentially break even to slightly negative, far worse than the plant wide method implied. This means Custom line growth, which Wade observed as increasing volume, has not been translating into real profit growth once the high number of setups and inspections that small batch custom work requires is properly costed, and Wade's instinct to question this before agreeing to a higher sale multiple is well founded.

Capital investment: the robotic welding cell's annual benefits are labour and overhead savings on existing Custom hours (9,000 hours times 35 percent equals 3,150 hours saved, at 28 dollars labour plus 15 dollars variable overhead equals 43 dollars per hour, giving 135,450 dollars) plus contribution margin on incremental revenue (400,000 dollars times 40 percent equals 160,000 dollars), for total annual benefit of 295,450 dollars, less annual operating cost of 58,000 dollars, giving net annual pre tax benefit of 237,450 dollars. After tax, using the 27 percent rate, this is roughly 173,338 dollars, plus a depreciation tax shield of 640,000 divided by 8 years times 27 percent equals 21,600 dollars, giving total annual after tax cash flow of approximately 194,938 dollars. Over 8 years discounted at 9 percent, the present value of an annuity factor is approximately 5.535, giving a present value of roughly 1,079,300 dollars against an initial investment of 640,000 dollars, a net present value of approximately 439,300 dollars, strongly positive, with payback in roughly 3.3 years. On pure financial merit the investment looks attractive, but this directly benefits the same Custom line that the ABC analysis shows is currently break even, and the projected 400,000 dollars of incremental revenue depends on capacity that does not yet exist and customer commitments Anya has already made informally.
model_response.recommendation: Recommend PSF adopt activity based costing, or at minimum a two driver approach separating setup and inspection costs from a labour hour based pool, to give management and the eventual sale negotiation an accurate picture, and recommend this analysis directly inform the EBITDA normalization Wade uses in valuing the business, since Custom line growth should not be credited at face value. Recommend the robotic welding cell proceed on financial grounds, since the NPV is strongly positive even under fairly conservative assumptions, but recommend it be evaluated together with the corrected Custom line costing, since the equipment's real value is in fixing the setup and changeover cost burden that is currently making Custom line unprofitable, not simply in adding capacity. Recommend Wade confirm financing terms with the bank before approval, given the line of credit is already 70 percent drawn and the 640,000 dollar cost would need to be funded through the increased limit the bank has conditionally offered, contingent on maintaining the debt service coverage ratio above 1.25. On Anya, recommend Wade address directly and promptly that she has committed to customers before the equipment is approved, since this creates real business risk if the investment is delayed or modified, and recommend this be framed as a governance and process issue about aligning commitments with approved capital decisions, not as a judgment on her capability, especially given her upcoming role as owner.
model_response.conclusion: The plant wide overhead rate has been masking a real problem, Custom line volume growth that Wade sensed but could not quantify has not been translating into profit, and getting this right matters both for day to day decisions and for the sale price Wade will negotiate with Anya. The robotic welding cell is a financially sound investment that also happens to be the right fix for the underlying costing problem, but Wade should tie its approval to disciplined process, proper bank financing conversations, and a clear conversation with Anya about not making customer commitments ahead of approved capital decisions, so that her transition to ownership starts on a foundation of accurate numbers and sound governance rather than optimism alone.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly identifies multiple cost drivers (setup/runs, inspections, labour hours) and recalculates overhead allocation to each line using an activity based approach with the case's numbers
Quantifies the resulting shift in operating income or margin for both lines and explicitly concludes Custom line profitability is materially worse than the plant wide rate suggested
Explains why the plant wide labour hour rate systematically understates costs for the low volume, high complexity Custom line

Builds a capital budgeting analysis incorporating labour savings, incremental contribution margin, operating costs, tax effects and depreciation tax shield, and computes NPV or an equivalent quantitative measure
Uses the 9 percent cost of capital and 8 year life correctly in a discounted cash flow calculation
Identifies the line of credit utilization and the conditional bank financing as a real constraint that must be addressed before proceeding, not just a footnote

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Connects the costing analysis and the capital investment analysis, recognizing the robotic cell addresses the specific driver (setups) causing Custom line's poor profitability

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Does not simply approve the investment because NPV is positive, but qualifies the recommendation based on the revenue growth assumption and financing constraint
Explicitly ties the corrected profitability picture to the succession sale price negotiation Wade is planning
Identifies that the growth Wade observed is not necessarily the growth he should reward in valuation, providing insight beyond the immediate question asked
Maintains independence and does not simply validate Anya's optimistic projections, explicitly noting where assumptions should be tested or are unproven
Recommends a specific, professionally framed approach for Wade to raise the customer commitment issue with Anya that protects the succession relationship
Presents the costing and investment conclusions in a way usable directly in a succession and valuation conversation, not just as an internal management accounting memo

marking_guide.competency: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
marking_guide.criterion: Flags that Anya committing to customers ahead of an unapproved capital decision creates a business risk and governance concern that should not be overlooked because of the family relationship

rating_guidance: A nominal competence response notices the plant wide overhead rate might be a problem or recomputes the robotic welding cell's NPV using at least some of the case data, but does not tie the two together or leaves major inputs from the exhibits unused. A reaching competence response performs either the activity based costing recalculation or the capital budgeting analysis reasonably completely using the case's actual figures, but not both to full depth, or completes both but treats them as unrelated exercises. A competent response completes both the ABC recalculation, correctly reallocating overhead using production runs and inspections as the drivers with the given numbers, and the capital budgeting analysis with a defensible NPV, explicitly connects the two by recognizing the equipment addresses the setup driven cost problem in the Custom line, and gives Wade practical advice on financing and on the Anya situation. A response reaching competence with distinction goes further, using the corrected margins to directly inform how Wade should think about the succession valuation multiple, explicitly cautions against over rewarding Custom line volume growth that is not real profit growth, and integrates the financing constraint and the customer commitment issue into a single coherent set of next steps for Wade rather than addressing each requirement as a separate silo.

enabling_focus: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
