title: NorthGrid Analytics: Bridge Financing and Going Concern
case_background:
It is February 2027. You, CPA, are the part time controller (contracted two days per week) at NorthGrid Analytics Inc. (NGA), a Toronto based software startup that builds predictive maintenance software for electrical utility grids. NGA was founded in 2023 by two engineers, Priya Chandrasekaran (CEO) and Tomas Reyes (CTO), and has raised 3.2 million dollars from angel investors and a seed stage venture fund, Larchwood Capital, which holds 22 percent of NGA's shares and has one board seat.

NGA has 11 employees and has signed pilot contracts with three mid sized utilities, but has not yet converted any pilot into a full paid contract. Cash burn has been higher than budgeted, and as of January 31, 2027, NGA has 210,000 dollars of cash remaining, against average monthly operating expenses of 165,000 dollars, meaning NGA has roughly 1.3 months of runway at the current burn rate. Priya is in active discussions with Larchwood Capital for a 1.5 million dollar bridge financing round (a convertible note that converts to equity at a discount on the next priced round, or repays with interest if no financing round closes within 18 months), and she expects a term sheet within three weeks, but nothing is signed yet.

Your role today involves two things. First, Priya has asked you to prepare a cash flow forecast and a memo assessing whether NGA can continue as a going concern for the note disclosures in the March 31, 2027 fiscal year end financial statements that will be reviewed (not audited) by your firm, Halbrook Chen LLP, since NGA's shareholders agreement requires annual review engagement financial statements. Second, and more sensitively, Tomas has come to you privately and told you that he has been having weekly calls with two of the three pilot utility clients, and he believes at least one pilot, with Meridian Grid Corp, is not going well technically and is unlikely to convert, though the sales team led by Priya's brother in law, who is NGA's VP Sales, has continued describing all three pilots as on track for conversion in board updates and in the financial projections shared with Larchwood Capital as part of the bridge financing discussions.

Tomas is uncomfortable raising this directly with Priya because of the family relationship with the VP Sales, and asks for your advice on what he should do, given that inaccurate pilot status could affect Larchwood's financing decision and NGA's own cash runway planning. You need to prepare the going concern analysis and also decide how to handle what Tomas has told you, given your role preparing information that will go to the board and potentially to Larchwood as part of the bridge round.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: NGA Cash Position and Burn Rate
exhibits.content: Cash balance at January 31, 2027: 210,000 dollars. Average monthly operating expenses (payroll, rent, software hosting, professional fees) over the trailing 3 months: 165,000 dollars, trending upward due to two recent hires. Accounts receivable: 0 dollars (no clients are yet paying, all three engagements are unpaid pilots). Accounts payable and accrued liabilities: 95,000 dollars, including 40,000 dollars owed to a contract development firm, due February 28, 2027. Committed but undrawn financing: none as of the report date; Larchwood term sheet expected but not received. NGA's monthly burn, if unchanged, would exhaust remaining cash by approximately mid March 2027, before the fiscal year end review engagement is even completed.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Pilot Program Status per Sales Reporting (used in board deck and Larchwood discussions)
exhibits.content: Meridian Grid Corp: pilot started September 2026, described in the January 2027 board deck as on track, conversion to a 380,000 dollar annual contract expected Q2 2027. Prairie Electric Cooperative: pilot started November 2026, described as on track, conversion expected Q3 2027, estimated 210,000 dollars annually. Sunhaven Utilities: pilot started December 2026, described as early stage, no conversion estimate given yet. Combined potential annual recurring revenue from pilots described as on track: 590,000 dollars, cited directly in the financial projections shared with Larchwood Capital in the January 2027 bridge financing discussion materials as expected new revenue in fiscal 2028.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 3: Tomas's Technical Notes on the Meridian Grid Pilot (shared with you privately)
exhibits.content: Tomas's notes from his weekly technical calls with Meridian Grid's engineering team: the predictive maintenance model has produced a 61 percent false positive rate on Meridian's transformer failure predictions over the past 6 weeks, well above the 15 percent threshold Meridian's engineering lead has told Tomas is their internal cutoff for considering the pilot successful. Meridian's engineering lead told Tomas in a call on January 20, 2027 that they plan to recommend against conversion when the pilot formally ends in April 2027 unless there is a significant and unlikely improvement in accuracy before then. Tomas has not shared this directly with Priya or the VP Sales, and does not know whether the VP Sales is aware of these technical results or is reporting optimistic status without full visibility into them.

requirements: Prepare an assessment of whether there is significant doubt about NGA's ability to continue as a going concern, and identify the financial statement and disclosure implications for the March 31, 2027 review engagement.
Advise Tomas on the professional and ethical course of action given what he has learned about the Meridian Grid pilot, considering the information's relevance to the Larchwood financing discussions.

Advise Priya, as the person you ultimately report to, on how the going concern situation and the pilot status issue should be addressed together before the bridge financing discussions proceed further.

model_response.understand_issue: You are the part time controller for NGA, reporting primarily to Priya but with review engagement obligations to Halbrook Chen LLP and now a sensitive disclosure from Tomas that touches on the accuracy of information being given to the board and to Larchwood Capital during active financing discussions. The core problem is that NGA has roughly six weeks of cash left with no committed financing, which is a going concern indicator on its own, compounded by the possibility that the revenue pipeline underlying both the board reporting and the bridge financing pitch materially overstates the likelihood of the Meridian Grid conversion. The real ask spans a technical going concern assessment and a judgment call about integrity of information reaching investors.
model_response.analyze_case:
Going concern: NGA has 210,000 dollars of cash against monthly burn of 165,000 dollars and rising, plus 95,000 dollars of current liabilities including a 40,000 dollar payment due February 28, 2027 to a contract developer, all against zero committed revenue and zero signed bridge financing as of the report date. Applying the going concern framework, conditions exist that cast significant doubt: recurring operating losses, negative cash flow, absence of a signed financing commitment despite an expected term sheet, and reliance on unconverted pilots for future revenue. The fact that Priya expects a term sheet within three weeks is a mitigating plan but is not itself sufficient evidence of financing being probable, since nothing is signed and venture financing discussions frequently change terms, get delayed, or fall through, particularly if underlying diligence, which could include Larchwood learning about the Meridian Grid pilot's true status, changes their view. For the March 31, 2027 review engagement, management is responsible for assessing going concern and, if substantial doubt exists that is not fully alleviated by mitigating factors, disclosing the nature of the events and conditions and management's plans in the notes; the practitioner's review report would need to consider whether adequate disclosure has been made, and if going concern doubt exists but is not appropriately disclosed, this would be a basis for a review report modification. Given NGA's cash runway of roughly six to seven weeks from the report date, this is not a remote or theoretical risk, it is the central going concern fact pattern, and the note disclosure needs to describe the specific conditions (limited cash, absence of signed financing, dependency on pilot conversions) rather than generic language.

Meridian Grid pilot issue: the 590,000 dollars of pipeline described as on track in board materials and cited directly in the Larchwood bridge financing discussion materials includes the 380,000 dollar Meridian Grid contract, which Tomas's direct technical evidence, a 61 percent false positive rate against a stated 15 percent client threshold and an engineering lead's stated intent to recommend against conversion, suggests is unlikely to convert. If the VP Sales is reporting on track status without this technical information, this may be an innocent information gap between sales and engineering; if the VP Sales has this information and is not passing it on, this becomes a more serious integrity issue, particularly because the same 590,000 dollars of pipeline is being used in materials presented to Larchwood while NGA is actively raising money from them. Either way, providing financing counterparties with pipeline figures that a member of the founding team has specific technical reason to doubt, without correcting or at least flagging the uncertainty, risks being misleading, and this is compounded by the fact the same numbers likely factor into the valuation or terms Larchwood is contemplating for the bridge note.
model_response.recommendation: Recommend the March 31, 2027 review engagement financial statements include clear going concern disclosure describing NGA's cash position, burn rate, absence of committed financing as of the report date, and management's plans regarding the Larchwood bridge round, since the facts support substantial doubt that is not yet alleviated by a signed commitment; recommend this be prepared now rather than left until year end so Priya is not surprised by it during the financing discussions. Recommend Tomas raise the Meridian Grid technical findings directly and promptly, first with Priya as CEO given the financing timeline urgency, framing it factually around the false positive rate and the engineering lead's stated intent rather than around any accusation toward the VP Sales, and recommend this happen before any further pipeline figures including the Meridian Grid contract are shared with Larchwood, since providing outdated or unsupported figures to an investor NGA is actively negotiating with carries real legal and reputational risk if discovered later. Recommend Priya, once informed, revise the pipeline figures used in the Larchwood materials to reflect a realistic probability weighted view, which may mean removing or heavily discounting the Meridian Grid contract, and recommend this be done even though it will make the pitch to Larchwood less favourable, because presenting inflated pipeline to an investor currently evaluating a financing decision creates risk far greater than a less impressive but honest number. Recommend Priya treat the going concern situation and the pilot status issue as connected, since an investor who later discovers the pipeline was overstated during active financing talks may rescind or reprice the deal, which would worsen the very cash crisis driving the going concern doubt.
model_response.conclusion: NGA faces a genuine going concern issue that needs direct, specific disclosure regardless of how the financing discussions resolve, and Tomas's information about the Meridian Grid pilot is directly relevant to both the going concern assessment and the integrity of what NGA is currently telling its prospective investor. The most important step is for accurate information to reach Priya quickly and for her to correct the Larchwood materials before the round closes, since a financing deal built on overstated pipeline creates far more risk to NGA, its employees, and its existing investors than an honest conversation about a disappointing pilot result.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly identifies specific conditions indicating substantial doubt about going concern using the case's cash, burn rate, and liability figures
Explains that management's expectation of a term sheet is a mitigating plan but not sufficient evidence to eliminate going concern doubt absent a signed commitment
Identifies the review engagement implications, including the need for adequate note disclosure and the practitioner's responsibility to assess that disclosure
Quantifies NGA's runway using the cash balance, burn rate and near term payable to establish the timeline urgency
Connects the pipeline overstatement risk to the terms or viability of the Larchwood bridge financing itself, not just to internal reporting accuracy

marking_guide.competency: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
marking_guide.criterion: Advises Tomas to raise the technical findings directly and promptly rather than staying silent because of the family relationship with the VP Sales

marking_guide.competency: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
marking_guide.criterion: Recommends the pipeline figures given to Larchwood be corrected before the financing closes, explicitly prioritizing accurate investor communication over a more favourable pitch

marking_guide.competency: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
marking_guide.criterion: Frames the concern around the information gap or integrity issue factually, without making unsupported accusations against the VP Sales

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Explicitly connects the going concern assessment and the pilot status issue rather than analyzing them as unrelated requirements

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Distinguishes between an innocent information gap and a possible deliberate omission by the VP Sales, and tailors advice to handle either scenario appropriately
Provides Tomas with a specific, practical approach for raising the issue with Priya given the urgency and sensitivity
Recommends going concern disclosure be prepared proactively and communicated to Priya before it becomes a surprise during the financing process
Positions the writer as taking initiative to ensure accurate information flows to the board and investor rather than passively waiting to be asked

rating_guidance: A nominal competence response notes that NGA is low on cash or that the Meridian pilot sounds troubled, without using the specific runway calculation or the false positive rate and stated client threshold from the exhibits, and gives generic advice to be honest without a concrete recommendation. A reaching competence response completes a reasonable going concern analysis using the cash and burn figures and recognizes the Meridian Grid pilot issue is a problem, but treats the two issues separately or does not clearly recommend correcting the Larchwood materials before financing closes. A competent response correctly identifies substantial going concern doubt with specific reference to the runway calculation and the absence of signed financing, correctly explains the review engagement disclosure implications, and gives Tomas and Priya clear, ethically sound, practical recommendations that explicitly link the pipeline overstatement to the active financing negotiation. A response reaching competence with distinction goes further by explaining how failing to correct the pipeline before the round closes could create legal and reputational exposure beyond the immediate cash crisis, by handling the family relationship sensitivity in a professionally mature way, and by showing the candidate understands that fixing the information given to Larchwood is not just an ethical nicety but is directly protective of NGA's survival given the going concern facts already established.

enabling_focus: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
Communicating
Managing Self
Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
title: Dunmore Family Orchards: Succession and an Estate Freeze
case_background:
It is March 2027. You, CPA, are a senior accountant at Halloway Kestrel LLP and have worked with the Dunmore family for six years. Dunmore Family Orchards Ltd. (DFO) is an apple orchard and cidery operation in the Okanagan Valley, owned entirely by Herb Dunmore, age 68, who founded the business in 1989. DFO has grown steadily and now has an estimated fair market value of 4.6 million dollars, with Herb's adjusted cost base in his common shares at 40,000 dollars, meaning a sale or deemed disposition today would trigger a very large capital gain.

Herb has three adult children: Callum, who has worked in the orchard and cidery operations for 15 years and is the presumptive successor; Bree, who lives in Calgary and works as a marketing consultant with no involvement in DFO; and Nadia, who moved back to the Okanagan two years ago and has recently started helping with DFO's retail and events side on a part time basis, though she also runs her own small photography business.

Herb has asked you to help him implement an estate freeze, a technique he read about, so that future growth in DFO's value accrues to his children rather than to his estate, while he retains control and a fixed value in preferred shares that he can draw against in retirement. Herb wants Callum to eventually control DFO given his operational role, but Herb has not decided how to treat Bree and Nadia fairly, since Bree has no involvement at all and Nadia's involvement is recent and part time. Herb tells you he does not want a freeze structure that locks in an unfair split between the children before he has figured out what fair actually means in this family's situation, and he also mentions, somewhat uncomfortably, that his wife Marlene (the children's mother) passed away in 2019 and left her entire estate to Herb, so all decisions about DFO's future ownership rest with Herb alone.

Separately, Callum has come to you directly, without Herb's knowledge, and mentioned that he is worried Herb is planning to give Bree and Nadia equal shares to his despite their lack of involvement, and Callum wants to know if there is anything he can do to protect his position, given he has worked for below market wages for years on the understanding, never formalized in writing, that he would eventually take over the business. You need to prepare technical advice for Herb on the freeze mechanics and tax consequences, while being mindful of your professional obligations given Callum has approached you separately and DFO, not any individual family member, is technically your firm's client.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: DFO Financial and Ownership Summary
exhibits.content: DFO current estimated fair market value: 4.6 million dollars, based on a preliminary valuation using a capitalized earnings approach on average EBITDA of 620,000 dollars over the past three years. Herb's adjusted cost base in DFO common shares: 40,000 dollars (100 percent ownership, 1,000 common shares issued at incorporation in 1989). Herb's age: 68. DFO has no other shareholders and no outstanding debt to shareholders. Herb draws a salary of 145,000 dollars annually plus dividends of approximately 80,000 dollars in a typical year. Callum's current salary: 58,000 dollars annually, below the estimated market rate of 85,000 to 95,000 dollars for an orchard operations manager with his experience, a gap Callum has accepted for years on the understanding he would eventually take over.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Family Involvement and Herb's Notes
exhibits.content: Callum: full time, 15 years, manages all orchard and cidery production operations, holds a provincial cidery operator certification DFO relies on. Bree: no operational involvement, lives in Calgary, has expressed interest in remaining a passive shareholder for family reasons but not in working at DFO. Nadia: part time since 2025, manages retail shop and farm gate events, generating approximately 210,000 dollars of DFO's 2.9 million dollars total annual revenue, also runs her own separate photography business generating roughly 35,000 dollars annually for her personally. Herb's handwritten notes shared with you: I want Callum to run this place and eventually control it, that is not in question. What I have not decided is whether Bree and Nadia get an equal share of the freeze preferred value, a smaller share, or something tied to future involvement, and I do not want to be talked into a fast answer before I have thought this through properly.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 3: Preliminary Freeze Structure Herb Read About
exhibits.content: Herb's proposed structure, based on an article he read: Herb exchanges his 1,000 common shares of DFO for fixed value redeemable retractable preferred shares with a redemption value equal to the current fair market value of 4.6 million dollars, using the section 85 rollover provisions to avoid immediate tax on the exchange. New common shares would then be issued to a family trust or directly to the children for nominal value, so future growth in DFO's value accrues to the children's common shares. Herb wants the preferred shares to carry a dividend entitlement he can draw on in retirement and wants to retain voting control through the preferred shares or a separate class of special voting shares until he is ready to fully step back.

requirements: Explain the tax mechanics and consequences of the proposed estate freeze for Herb, including the role of the section 85 rollover and what it does and does not accomplish.
Advise Herb on considerations for allocating the common share growth among Callum, Bree, and Nadia given their different levels of involvement, without making the decision for him.
Advise on how to respond to Callum's approach to you directly, addressing the professional and ethical considerations given DFO is the firm's client.

model_response.understand_issue: You are the family's long standing accountant, engaged by DFO but with deep personal relationships across the family, being asked by Herb to explain and help implement an estate freeze, while separately being approached in confidence by Callum about his own position. The real technical question is how a freeze works and what tax result it achieves; the real judgment question is how to advise on a fair allocation among three children with very different involvement without overstepping into a family decision that is Herb's alone to make; and the real professional question is how to handle Callum's approach without compromising your obligations to DFO as the client or inappropriately taking sides in a family matter.
model_response.analyze_case:
Freeze mechanics: an estate freeze using a section 85 rollover allows Herb to exchange his 1,000 common shares, with an adjusted cost base of 40,000 dollars and fair market value of 4.6 million dollars, for fixed value preferred shares with a redemption value equal to 4.6 million dollars, electing an amount that allows Herb to defer recognizing the accrued capital gain of approximately 4,560,000 dollars at the time of the exchange, rather than triggering it immediately. This is a deferral, not an elimination, of tax; the gain remains embedded in the preferred shares and will be realized eventually, whether on redemption, sale, or on Herb's death when the preferred shares are deemed disposed of at fair market value, subject to any relief available at that time such as the lifetime capital gains exemption on qualified small business corporation shares or qualified farm property, which DFO as a working orchard likely qualifies for, potentially sheltering up to the applicable exemption limit per individual if structured and timed appropriately, something that should be confirmed and possibly planned around before the freeze rather than after. New common shares issued to the children for nominal value then capture all future growth in DFO's value above 4.6 million dollars, so if DFO grows to 7 million dollars in ten years, that 2.4 million dollars of growth accrues to whoever holds the new common shares, not to Herb's estate, which is the core purpose of a freeze, to cap the value subject to tax in Herb's hands at death and shift future growth to the next generation.

Allocation among children: this is fundamentally a family and governance decision, not a pure technical tax question, but there are structural options worth explaining to Herb rather than deciding for him. A family trust holding the new common shares, with Callum, Bree, and Nadia as beneficiaries, would let Herb defer the final allocation decision, potentially for up to 21 years given the trust's deemed disposition rules, while still achieving the freeze's tax deferral purpose today, giving Herb time to observe how Nadia's involvement develops and to decide fairly rather than being forced into a permanent split now. Alternatively, if Herb wants to allocate directly and immediately, he could weight the allocation toward Callum given his 15 years of operational contribution and the below market compensation he has accepted, while giving Bree and Nadia smaller or non voting participation reflecting their more limited or more recent involvement, but this is Herb's judgment to make based on family values and fairness as he defines it, not something you should determine on his behalf. It is worth noting to Herb that Callum's below market compensation over many years, if never documented, could itself become a source of future family dispute or even a legal claim if Callum feels his contribution was not recognized, which is a reason to consider documenting Callum's role and any informal understanding now, separate from the freeze mechanics themselves.

Callum's approach: DFO, not Herb individually and not any one child, is the firm's client, and you owe your duty of confidentiality regarding DFO's affairs and Herb's planning to the engagement, not to Callum personally in this context. Callum approaching you privately creates a risk of being drawn into a family conflict where you would be perceived as advising one family member against the interests or wishes of another, which threatens your objectivity and the trust the whole family needs to have in you as DFO's advisor. You should not disclose Herb's specific planning intentions to Callum, and you should not give Callum individual advice about protecting his position, since that would go beyond DFO's engagement and could constitute advising a party whose interests may not align with DFO's or Herb's, creating a conflict.
model_response.recommendation: Recommend proceeding with the section 85 rollover freeze as a sound mechanism to cap Herb's tax exposure at death and cap the value in his estate, while explicitly confirming DFO's eligibility for the lifetime capital gains exemption on qualified farm property before finalizing amounts, since this could materially reduce the ultimate tax cost and should shape the timing and elected amounts used. Recommend Herb consider a family trust to hold the new common shares rather than allocating directly and immediately to the three children, giving him time to observe Nadia's evolving role and to make a considered allocation decision consistent with his own stated wish not to be rushed, while still achieving the freeze's tax benefits today. Recommend Herb separately document Callum's historical compensation arrangement and any understanding about future control, independent of the freeze structure, to reduce the risk of future family dispute regardless of how the share allocation is ultimately decided. On Callum, recommend you decline to discuss Herb's planning details or give Callum individual advice, and instead suggest Callum raise his concerns directly with Herb or, if the family wants, in a facilitated family meeting where all three children and Herb can discuss expectations openly, possibly with independent legal counsel for Callum if he wants advice specific to his personal position, since that would be outside DFO's engagement scope.
model_response.conclusion: The estate freeze is a sound and standard technique for Herb's situation and can defer significant tax while shifting future growth to the next generation, but the harder and more important decision, how to fairly divide that future growth among three children with very different levels of involvement, is Herb's to make, not yours, and a trust structure can buy him the time he says he wants without sacrificing the tax benefits of acting now. Handling Callum's private approach carefully protects both your professional obligations to DFO as the client and the family relationships that have made this a six year engagement, and keeping firm boundaries there is as important to the family's long term interests as getting the freeze mechanics right.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly explains that a section 85 rollover on a share exchange defers, rather than eliminates, the accrued capital gain, using the case's specific ACB and FMV figures
Explains the freeze mechanism correctly: fixed value preferred shares to Herb, new growth common shares to the next generation, capping future growth outside Herb's estate
Identifies the potential availability of the lifetime capital gains exemption for qualified farm property as relevant to structuring the freeze amount and timing
Identifies a family trust as a mechanism to defer the final allocation decision among the children while still achieving the freeze today, and notes the 21 year deemed disposition rule
Presents allocation options to Herb (direct allocation weighted by involvement vs. trust deferral) without prescribing the family's fairness decision for him
Identifies Callum's undocumented below market compensation as a governance and family risk that should be addressed independent of the freeze mechanics

marking_guide.competency: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
marking_guide.criterion: Correctly identifies DFO as the client and declines to disclose Herb's planning details to Callum or give Callum individual advice that could conflict with the firm's obligations to DFO

marking_guide.competency: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
marking_guide.criterion: Recognizes the risk of being perceived as taking sides in a family conflict and explicitly protects objectivity rather than trying to please both Herb and Callum

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Separates the technical freeze question from the family fairness question as two distinct issues requiring different types of advice
Suggests a constructive path for Callum's concern to be addressed appropriately, such as a facilitated family conversation or independent legal counsel, rather than simply refusing to engage
Explains the freeze mechanics to Herb in terms he can act on, connecting the technical rollover explanation back to his stated goals (control, retirement income, fairness)
Recognizes the boundary of the engagement (advising DFO and Herb) and does not overreach into providing personal estate or family mediation advice beyond professional competence

rating_guidance: A nominal competence response describes an estate freeze in general terms without using Herb's specific ACB and fair market value figures, or does not recognize that Callum's approach raises a professional boundary issue at all. A reaching competence response correctly explains the freeze mechanics and the rollover deferral using the case numbers, but either does not identify the farm property capital gains exemption consideration or handles the Callum issue superficially, for example simply saying to remain neutral without explaining why or what to do next. A competent response correctly explains the section 85 rollover deferral mechanics with the specific figures, identifies the capital gains exemption as a planning consideration, presents rather than prescribes the family allocation options including the trust deferral idea, and correctly identifies and addresses the client identification and confidentiality issue raised by Callum's private approach. A response reaching competence with distinction integrates all three requirements into a coherent whole, for example explicitly linking the documentation of Callum's compensation history to reducing the very dispute risk that caused Callum to approach the writer in the first place, and demonstrates sophisticated judgment about professional boundaries by suggesting a constructive path forward for the family rather than simply declining to help Callum.

enabling_focus: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
Collaborating
Communicating
Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
title: Riverbend Community Foodbank: Restricted Funds and a Funder Ultimatum
case_background:
It is May 2027. You, CPA, are the treasurer on the volunteer board of directors of Riverbend Community Foodbank (RCF), a registered charity in Kamloops that provides food hampers and a hot meal program to about 1,400 households monthly. You joined the board eight months ago and were asked to take on the treasurer role given your accounting background, working alongside RCF's part time bookkeeper and its executive director, Farrah Kowalski, who has led RCF for six years and is well regarded in the community.

RCF's fiscal year end is June 30. In its June 30, 2026 financial statements, RCF reported total revenue of 1.42 million dollars, including a 300,000 dollar grant from the provincial Ministry of Social Development, restricted specifically for RCF's hot meal program, and a 150,000 dollar grant from the Riverbend Rotary Foundation, restricted for a planned kitchen equipment upgrade. In preparing for the upcoming June 30, 2027 year end, you have discovered that approximately 95,000 dollars of the Ministry grant, intended for hot meal program food and staffing costs, was instead used in the fall of 2026 to cover a shortfall in RCF's general operating budget after hamper program costs came in higher than budgeted due to inflation in food prices. Farrah explains that she viewed this as a temporary internal loan between programs, intending to repay the hot meal program from general donations once the year end campaign closed, and that all the money went to legitimate RCF programming, just not the specific program it was designated for.

Separately, the Rotary Foundation grant of 150,000 dollars for kitchen equipment has not yet been spent; RCF still has the full amount sitting in its general bank account, commingled with operating funds, and the kitchen upgrade has been delayed because Farrah has been focused on the hot meal program budget issue. The Rotary Foundation's program officer has emailed Farrah asking for a spending update, noting that Rotary's policy requires unspent restricted grants to be used within 18 months of receipt or returned, and that 18 months from the December 2025 grant date falls in June 2027, this month.

The board chair has asked you, given your CPA background, to review the situation before the June 30, 2027 audit begins (RCF's bylaws require an annual audit once revenue exceeds 1 million dollars, which happened for the first time this year) and to recommend how the board should respond, both in terms of the financial statements and in terms of the Ministry and Rotary Foundation relationships, which represent nearly a third of RCF's total funding.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Restricted Grant Details
exhibits.content: Ministry of Social Development grant: 300,000 dollars received August 2026, restricted for hot meal program food and staffing costs for the period September 2026 to August 2027, per the signed grant agreement. Grant agreement clause 7: funds not used for the specified purpose must be returned to the Ministry, and the Ministry reserves the right to require repayment plus request a special audit if misuse is identified. As of April 2027, 205,000 dollars of the 300,000 dollars has been spent on qualifying hot meal program costs; 95,000 dollars was redirected to general operations in October and November 2026; no amount has been repaid to the hot meal program from general donations yet, though Farrah estimates the year end campaign (running May and June 2027) will raise approximately 110,000 dollars in unrestricted donations.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Rotary Foundation Grant and Email
exhibits.content: Rotary Foundation grant: 150,000 dollars received December 2025, restricted for kitchen equipment (a new commercial oven, walk in cooler, and food safety upgrades), per the grant agreement, which requires funds to be used within 18 months of receipt (by June 2027) or returned. Email from Rotary Foundation program officer to Farrah, dated April 15, 2027: We have not received an update on the kitchen equipment project since the grant was issued. As you know, our policy requires funds to be deployed within 18 months or returned to the Foundation for reallocation to other community partners. Please provide a spending update and timeline by May 15, 2027. The full 150,000 dollars remains in RCF's general operating bank account, which had a balance of 268,000 dollars as of April 30, 2027, meaning the restricted funds are not separately identifiable or segregated from operating cash.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 3: RCF Fiscal 2027 Draft Financial Summary (unaudited, year to date through April 30, 2027)
exhibits.content: Total revenue year to date: 1.31 million dollars. Total expenses year to date: 1.29 million dollars. Hamper program costs year to date: 640,000 dollars, up 18 percent from the same period last year due to food price inflation. Hot meal program costs year to date: 205,000 dollars (funded from the Ministry grant as noted). General operating cash balance: 268,000 dollars, which includes the full unspent 150,000 dollar Rotary grant and represents the pool from which the 95,000 dollars was drawn for the hamper program shortfall. RCF has no other outstanding restricted grants at this time. The board has not previously reviewed or approved any use of restricted funds outside their stated purpose; the October and November transfers were made by Farrah without board authorization.

requirements: Assess the financial reporting implications of the Ministry grant fund redirection for the June 30, 2027 audited financial statements, including how the amounts should be presented and disclosed.
Recommend how the board should respond to Farrah's use of restricted funds outside their designated purpose, addressing both the immediate remediation and the governance issue it raises.
Recommend how RCF should respond to the Rotary Foundation's request given the June 2027 deadline and the fact the funds have not been segregated or spent.

model_response.understand_issue: You are the volunteer treasurer, a CPA on a charity board, asked by the board chair to assess a situation where the executive director redirected restricted grant funds without board approval and where a second restricted grant is now at risk of being reclaimed by its funder due to a missed deadline. The audience is the full board, which needs both the technical financial reporting answer and practical governance guidance, given RCF is about to undergo its first audit and nearly a third of its funding comes from these two funders whose trust has now been put at risk.
model_response.analyze_case:
Financial reporting: restricted contributions like the Ministry and Rotary grants should be recognized as revenue in the period the related expenses are incurred, or deferred as a liability until then, under the deferral method typically used by not for profit organizations, or recognized with restricted net asset classification if the organization follows the restricted fund method. Regardless of which method RCF applies, the key issue is that 95,000 dollars of the 300,000 dollar Ministry grant was spent on unrestricted general operations rather than the hot meal program, meaning RCF has effectively used restricted funds for a purpose the resource provider did not authorize, which is a breach of the grant agreement (clause 7), not merely a timing or classification issue. For the June 30, 2027 statements, RCF needs to determine whether this amount remains recoverable through the planned repayment from year end campaign donations, and if repayment has not occurred by year end, the financial statements should reflect a liability to the Ministry for the unspent, misapplied portion, or at minimum robust note disclosure describing the breach, the amount involved, and management's remediation plan, since users of RCF's financial statements, including the Ministry itself and potential future funders, need to understand this is not simply an unspent balance but a contractual breach with repayment or clawback risk. If the year end campaign successfully raises the 110,000 dollars Farrah projects and the hot meal program is made whole by June 30, 2027, the note disclosure can describe the breach and its remediation as resolved, but the breach itself, having occurred, should still be disclosed given its significance to a funder relationship representing a large share of RCF's revenue, and the auditor will likely need to specifically consider this as a matter affecting the audit, potentially including consideration of the Ministry's clause 7 right to require repayment or a special audit as a contingency.

Governance issue: the transfers were made unilaterally by Farrah without board authorization, which is a serious internal control and governance failure independent of whether the underlying intent (a temporary internal loan, to be repaid) was well meaning. A restricted grant is a fiduciary obligation to a third party funder, not an internal RCF resource Farrah has discretion to reallocate, and the board, which is legally responsible for RCF's stewardship of restricted funds, was not even aware this had happened until the treasurer's review surfaced it. This points to a lack of financial controls: restricted funds were not segregated, monitored against budget by program, or subject to any board level oversight that would have caught the redirection when it happened in October and November 2026 rather than seven months later.

Rotary Foundation issue: the 150,000 dollar Rotary grant is fully unspent, commingled in a general account with a balance of only 268,000 dollars, which is concerning both because the funds are not segregated (raising the same control issue as the Ministry situation) and because the 18 month usage deadline falls this month, with no equipment purchased and no clear evidence RCF can execute the kitchen upgrade in the remaining weeks. Given the missed progress and the approaching deadline, Rotary's inquiry needs a direct, honest response rather than a delay tactic, since Rotary explicitly has the right to reclaim the funds for reallocation to other community partners, and a late or evasive response risks damaging the relationship further and potentially the reputational standing RCF needs with other funders.
model_response.recommendation: Recommend the board direct that the 95,000 dollars be treated as a liability owed to the hot meal program or disclosed as an unresolved breach if not fully repaid by June 30, 2027, with clear note disclosure of the facts regardless of repayment status given the significance to a major funder relationship, and recommend the board proactively contact the Ministry before the audit is finalized rather than waiting for the Ministry to discover it independently, since voluntary disclosure paired with a credible remediation plan is far more likely to preserve the relationship than a discovery after the fact. Recommend the board formally address the governance failure with Farrah directly, establishing that restricted funds require board authorization before any reallocation regardless of intent, and recommend implementing a segregated bank account or, at minimum, a restricted fund tracking ledger reconciled monthly and reported to the board, so program level spending against restricted grants is visible in real time rather than discovered after the fact. This should be handled as a serious control lapse requiring correction, while recognizing Farrah's six years of good service and the absence of any evidence of personal benefit, meaning the response should focus on strengthening controls and requiring board oversight going forward rather than assuming bad faith. Recommend RCF respond to the Rotary Foundation immediately and honestly, before the May 15 deadline, providing a realistic timeline for the kitchen equipment purchase, proposing to segregate the remaining funds into a dedicated account immediately as a show of good faith, and directly asking whether Rotary would grant a short extension given a credible near term spending plan, rather than risking a full reclaim by staying silent or providing a vague response.
model_response.conclusion: RCF's mission and community reputation depend on the trust of funders like the Ministry and Rotary Foundation, and the board's response to both situations needs to combine transparent, accurate financial reporting with genuine governance improvements, not just a quiet fix before the audit. Handling this proactively and honestly, even though it is uncomfortable given Farrah's standing and good intentions, gives RCF the best chance of preserving both funder relationships and the credibility it needs going into its first ever audit.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly identifies that the 95,000 dollar redirection is a breach of restricted grant terms requiring liability recognition or clear note disclosure, not simply a timing difference
Explains the restricted contribution recognition principle (deferral until related expense, or restricted fund classification) as the framework for assessing both grants
Identifies that disclosure of the breach and remediation plan is needed even if the funds are repaid before year end, given the significance to a major funder relationship
Identifies the lack of board authorization for the fund transfers as a governance and internal control failure distinct from the good intent behind the transfer
Recommends specific, practical control improvements (segregated accounts, restricted fund tracking, board reporting) rather than only addressing the past breach
Recommends proactive, voluntary disclosure to the Ministry rather than waiting for discovery, and explains why this better protects the relationship

marking_guide.competency: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
marking_guide.criterion: Treats the situation with appropriate seriousness as a fiduciary breach while avoiding an unsupported accusation of bad faith against Farrah given the facts presented

marking_guide.competency: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
marking_guide.criterion: Recommends full, accurate disclosure to Rotary Foundation before the deadline rather than a delay or evasive response

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Addresses the Ministry and Rotary situations as related but distinct issues, each requiring its own timeline sensitive response
Frames recommendations as board level decisions requiring the full board and Farrah's engagement, not unilateral treasurer action
Provides specific, actionable language or approach for the board's communication to both the Ministry and Rotary Foundation
Balances the seriousness of the finding with constructive, forward looking recommendations suitable for a volunteer board audience

rating_guidance: A nominal competence response notices that using restricted funds for another purpose is a problem but does not identify the specific financial reporting consequence (liability or disclosure) or does not separately address the Rotary Foundation deadline issue at all. A reaching competence response identifies both issues are problems and recommends telling the funders, but does not clearly explain the accounting treatment using the deferral or restricted fund framework, or gives generic governance advice without specific, actionable control recommendations. A competent response correctly explains the financial reporting treatment for the Ministry breach including disclosure regardless of repayment, identifies the governance failure in unilateral fund reallocation with concrete control recommendations, and gives a clear, proactive, honest recommended response to both the Ministry and Rotary Foundation grounded in the case's specific deadlines and dollar amounts. A response reaching competence with distinction ties all three requirements together into a single coherent narrative about restoring funder trust through transparency, explicitly reasons through the tradeoff between disclosure risk and discovery risk for both funders, and gives Farrah credit for good intent while still holding firm that board authorization and segregated controls are non negotiable going forward, showing nuanced judgment appropriate for a first time volunteer board treasurer handling a delicate personnel and governance situation.

enabling_focus: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
Communicating
Managing Self
title: Kestrel Ridge Law Group: Partner Independence and a Trust Account Discrepancy
case_background:
It is November 2026. You, CPA, are an audit senior at Voss Iyer LLP, assigned to the annual audit of Kestrel Ridge Law Group LLP (KRLG), a mid sized law firm in Winnipeg with 22 lawyers across real estate, family law, and corporate practice areas. KRLG is required to have its trust accounts and general financial statements audited annually under Law Society rules, and your firm has performed this audit for five years. You have been the senior on this engagement for two years and report to the engagement partner, Alain Fortier.

During fieldwork this month, you discover a 42,000 dollar discrepancy in the trust account reconciliation for October 2026: a client trust ledger shows funds held in trust for a real estate closing that, per your testing, do not reconcile to the trust bank statement, with the bank balance being 42,000 dollars lower than the sum of individual client trust ledger balances. Tracing the discrepancy, you find that in September 2026, 42,000 dollars was transferred from the trust account to KRLG's general operating account, coded in the accounting system as a transfer relating to a different, unrelated real estate file that in fact only required a 8,000 dollar fee transfer at that time, meaning 34,000 dollars in excess of what should have moved was withdrawn from trust.

You also discover that the real estate file the excess funds were coded against is handled by Deborah Whitlock, one of KRLG's four equity partners and the firm's most senior real estate lawyer, who also happens to be married to Alain Fortier, your engagement partner, a fact you did not previously know and only learned this week when another staff member mentioned it in passing. Alain has led this engagement for all five years your firm has served KRLG and has never disclosed the relationship to you or, as far as you can tell from the engagement file, formally addressed it as an independence matter within the firm.

When you raise the trust discrepancy informally with Alain, he tells you he is confident it is a simple coding error by KRLG's bookkeeper and asks you to close the finding out with a standard adjusting entry recommendation rather than escalating it further, noting that KRLG's Law Society trust audit deadline is in nine days and he does not want to delay the file over what he believes is likely an innocent clerical mistake. You are uncertain whether to accept this explanation given the amount, the fact it involves a partner's file, and the independence question you have just learned about regarding Alain and Deborah.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Trust Reconciliation Detail, October 2026
exhibits.content: Sum of individual client trust ledger balances at October 31, 2026: 386,000 dollars, across 14 active client files. Trust bank statement balance at October 31, 2026: 344,000 dollars. Discrepancy: 42,000 dollars (ledgers exceed bank by this amount, indicating funds were disbursed from the bank account without a corresponding reduction being properly recorded against the correct client ledger, or were recorded against the wrong file). Trust account transfer history for September 2026 shows one transfer of 42,000 dollars from trust to KRLG general account on September 18, 2026, coded to file number RE-2291 (Kowalczyk purchase, handled by Deborah Whitlock), described in the system as legal fees transfer. File RE-2291's engagement letter and invoice on file support a fee of 8,000 dollars for services rendered as of that date, not 42,000 dollars.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: KRLG Partnership and Engagement Background
exhibits.content: KRLG has four equity partners: Deborah Whitlock (real estate, 34 percent partnership interest), Grant Oduya (corporate, 26 percent), Priya Sethi (family law, 22 percent), and Marcus Yaremko (real estate and litigation, 18 percent). Alain Fortier has been the engagement partner on the KRLG audit for Voss Iyer LLP for five consecutive years. Voss Iyer's independence policy requires partners to disclose close personal or family relationships with client personnel annually via an independence declaration form; a review of the firm's independence declaration file for Alain shows no declaration referencing any relationship with KRLG personnel in any of the five years. Alain and Deborah Whitlock have been married since 2015, confirmed by the staff member who mentioned it to you and independently by a public Law Society directory listing showing the same last known address.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 3: Notes from Your Conversation with Alain
exhibits.content: Alain, when you raised the discrepancy: I have looked at this kind of thing before at KRLG, their bookkeeper Janet makes coding errors maybe two or three times a year, always innocent, always caught and fixed. Just write it up as a proposed adjusting journal entry moving the 34,000 dollar excess back into the correct trust ledger and note the bookkeeper will correct the general ledger coding. We are nine days from the Law Society deadline and I do not want to turn this into a bigger issue than it is. You do not need to interview Deborah about it, Janet handles all the trust transfers, not the lawyers directly.

requirements: Assess the audit procedures needed to properly investigate and resolve the 42,000 dollar trust account discrepancy, and evaluate whether Alain's proposed approach is appropriate.
Assess the independence implications of Alain's relationship with Deborah Whitlock for the KRLG audit engagement, including what should have happened and what should happen now.
Recommend how you, as the audit senior, should proceed given Alain's instruction to close out the finding quickly, considering your professional obligations.

model_response.understand_issue: You are the audit senior on a law firm trust account audit who has found a material, unexplained 34,000 dollar excess trust withdrawal on a partner's file, and who has just learned that the engagement partner supervising you has an undisclosed five year marital relationship with that same partner. The real issue is not just how to audit the discrepancy technically, it is that the person directing you to close it out quickly has an independence conflict that may be affecting his judgment about how seriously to investigate a matter involving his spouse's file, and you need to decide how to act appropriately within your firm's structure given you report to Alain but the integrity of the audit and public trust in the Law Society regulated trust account are both at stake.
model_response.analyze_case:
Trust discrepancy: a 42,000 dollar trust transfer against an 8,000 dollar supported fee, a 34,000 dollar unexplained excess, involving client trust funds for a real estate purchase, is a significant and unusual item that warrants full investigation regardless of whose file it involves, particularly given trust accounts hold client funds the firm has a fiduciary and regulatory obligation to safeguard exactly as designated. Accepting a bookkeeper coding error explanation without further evidence is not sufficient audit evidence for an item of this nature and size; appropriate procedures include obtaining and reviewing the client's (Kowalczyk's) file and instructions to confirm what disbursement, if any, was authorized, confirming directly with the client or their lender whether 42,000 dollars or 8,000 dollars was the correct and authorized amount, interviewing both the bookkeeper Janet and Deborah Whitlock (the responsible lawyer on the file) about the transfer, and verifying where the additional 34,000 dollars actually went, whether it was used, for what purpose, and whether it has since been returned to trust. A proposed adjusting entry to simply move the ledger balances back without establishing where the actual cash went does not resolve the substantive question of whether client trust funds were misappropriated, used improperly, or genuinely miscoded, and given this touches a Law Society regulated trust account, a shortfall of this nature, if it reflects an actual cash shortage rather than a pure recording error, could itself be a reportable matter under Law Society rules, which is far more serious than adjusting a journal entry nine days before a deadline.

Independence: Alain's five year undisclosed marital relationship with Deborah Whitlock, an equity partner at the audit client, is a clear and serious independence threat under professional independence standards; a close family relationship (spousal) with an individual in a position to influence the subject matter of the engagement, here a partner whose own file is now central to a trust account discrepancy, creates both a self interest and familiarity threat that should have precluded Alain from serving as engagement partner on this file at all, and certainly should have been disclosed and evaluated under Voss Iyer's own independence policy from the start. The complete absence of any independence declaration referencing this relationship across five years suggests either Alain never disclosed it internally or the firm's monitoring process failed to catch it, either of which is a significant firm level quality control gap requiring escalation beyond just this one finding.

Alain's direction: Alain's instruction to close out the finding as a simple coding error, skip interviewing Deborah, and move quickly given the deadline, is exactly the kind of decision the independence conflict should make you distrust as objective professional judgment, regardless of whether Alain consciously intends to protect his spouse. As the audit senior, you have a professional obligation not to sign off on inadequate audit evidence simply because a superior directs you to, particularly once you are aware of a specific reason (the undisclosed relationship) to question that superior's objectivity on this exact finding.
model_response.recommendation: Recommend the trust discrepancy be fully investigated using the procedures above before any adjusting entry or file closure, regardless of the nine day deadline; if evidence is not available within that window, recommend the firm communicate to KRLG and, if necessary, the Law Society that additional time is needed to resolve a significant unreconciled trust discrepancy, since a rushed conclusion on a matter of this nature carries far more professional risk than a short delay. Recommend you not accept Alain's direction to close the file without further work, and instead escalate the matter within Voss Iyer LLP, first by raising both the substantive finding and the independence concern with your firm's quality control or ethics partner, or an appropriately independent partner outside this engagement, rather than resolving it solely through further discussion with Alain, given his personal connection to the very file in question. Recommend Voss Iyer formally evaluate whether Alain's five year undisclosed relationship with Deborah Whitlock has compromised independence on this engagement historically, which could require a re-evaluation of prior year audit conclusions and, depending on the firm's findings, communication with KRLG and potentially the firm's professional body about the independence lapse, and recommend Alain be removed from the engagement immediately regardless of how the trust discrepancy investigation concludes, since the relationship itself, not just this one finding, is the independence problem. Recommend that once appropriately escalated, a partner independent of the relationship take over supervision of the completion of trust discrepancy procedures, including the interview of Deborah Whitlock that Alain specifically instructed you to skip.
model_response.conclusion: This engagement now has two separate but connected problems, an unresolved and potentially serious trust account discrepancy on a partner's file, and a longstanding undisclosed independence conflict involving the very partner directing how that discrepancy should be handled. Protecting the integrity of the audit and the public interest the trust account audit exists to serve requires escalating both issues within your firm promptly, rather than deferring to Alain's instruction, even though doing so as a senior raising a concern about a partner is professionally uncomfortable and requires real courage.

marking_guide.criterion: Identifies that a simple adjusting entry does not resolve the substantive question of where the 34,000 dollar excess actually went and recommends specific further procedures (client confirmation, file review, interviews)
Recognizes the potential regulatory significance of an unresolved trust account shortfall under Law Society rules, beyond a normal audit misstatement
Explains why accepting an unsupported bookkeeper error explanation is insufficient audit evidence given the size and nature of the item
Correctly frames the trust ledger versus bank reconciliation issue and identifies that the true cash movement, not just the ledger coding, must be established

marking_guide.competency: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
marking_guide.criterion: Correctly identifies the undisclosed marital relationship between Alain and Deborah as a serious independence threat requiring the partner's removal from the engagement

marking_guide.competency: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
marking_guide.criterion: Recommends escalating both the trust finding and the independence issue to someone within the firm independent of Alain, rather than resolving it through further discussion with Alain alone

marking_guide.competency: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
marking_guide.criterion: Recognizes that Alain's specific instruction to skip interviewing Deborah is itself evidence of compromised objectivity, not just an efficiency shortcut

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Connects the independence issue and the trust discrepancy as related problems (the conflict may be shaping how the finding is being handled) rather than analyzing them independently

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Weighs the deadline pressure against the risk of inadequate evidence and concludes the investigation should not be rushed to meet the nine day deadline
Recognizes the difficulty of a senior challenging a partner's instruction and still commits to appropriate escalation rather than deferring due to hierarchy
Identifies a specific, appropriate escalation channel within the firm (ethics or quality control partner, someone independent of the engagement) rather than a vague suggestion to tell someone

Identifies that if the trust shortfall reflects an actual cash misuse rather than a pure coding error, KRLG's own trust liability financial statements would need a corresponding correction, not just an internal adjusting entry

rating_guidance: A nominal competence response notices the discrepancy is unusual or that the relationship is a conflict of interest, but does not connect the two or does not recommend specific further audit procedures beyond generically investigating more. A reaching competence response identifies both the inadequate evidence for the coding error explanation and the independence problem, but treats Alain's instruction as simply something to push back on without a clear escalation path, or does not recognize the potential Law Society regulatory significance of an unresolved trust shortfall. A competent response identifies specific further audit procedures needed to resolve the discrepancy, correctly analyzes the independence threat created by the undisclosed marital relationship and its effect on this specific finding, and recommends a clear, appropriate escalation path within the firm rather than either quietly complying with Alain or trying to resolve the conflict single handedly. A response reaching competence with distinction goes further by recognizing that the independence problem is not limited to this one finding, since it may have compromised five years of prior audit work, by explicitly identifying that Alain's specific instruction to skip interviewing Deborah is itself a red flag of compromised objectivity, and by demonstrating the professional courage and structured judgment needed to escalate a partner level issue as a senior, while keeping the trust account investigation itself appropriately rigorous and not rushed to meet an external deadline.

title: Larkspur Valley Dairy: Biological Assets and a Quota Financing Decision
case_background:
It is August 2026. You, CPA, are an external advisor engaged by Larkspur Valley Dairy Ltd. (LVD), a family owned dairy farm near Woodstock, Ontario operated by second generation owners Gerrit and Anneke Voss. LVD milks 210 cows and holds 195 kilograms of daily butterfat production quota under the provincial supply management system, a significant and valuable asset that governs how much milk LVD is permitted to sell into the regulated market. Gerrit and Anneke have been approached by a neighbouring dairy operation, the Prentice family, who want to sell 25 kilograms of additional quota, which would let LVD expand its herd and milk production by roughly 13 percent.

The Prentice quota is priced at 24,000 dollars per kilogram, a price the provincial milk marketing board's quota exchange has published as the current clearing price, meaning the total purchase price would be 600,000 dollars. LVD's bank, Heartland Farm Credit, has indicated it would finance 80 percent of the quota purchase (480,000 dollars) at a fixed rate of 6.4 percent over 10 years, provided LVD's financial statements demonstrate sufficient debt service capacity, which the bank measures using a minimum debt service coverage ratio of 1.15.

Gerrit and Anneke have also asked you to help them understand how their dairy herd and the quota itself should be valued and presented in LVD's financial statements, since their current bookkeeper has historically recorded the milking herd at original purchase cost with no subsequent remeasurement, and has recorded quota at its original cost when first allocated decades ago under the supply management system's initial free allocation, which was effectively nil, even though LVD has since purchased additional quota at market prices in two prior transactions that were recorded at cost.

Gerrit mentions he has heard from other farmers that dairy cattle and quota should sometimes be shown at fair value in farm financial statements, and he wants to understand what that would mean for LVD's balance sheet and whether it affects how the bank will view the new loan application, especially since a fair value approach could significantly increase LVD's reported asset base and equity given how much quota values have risen since LVD's original allocation.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: LVD Herd and Quota Data
exhibits.content: Milking herd: 210 cows, historically recorded at original purchase cost of 2,450 dollars per cow on average (herd was built up over 15 years through purchases and internal breeding, with internally raised animals recorded at nil cost historically), total carrying value on the books of 514,500 dollars. Independent livestock appraiser's estimate of current fair value less costs to sell for the milking herd: 3,100 dollars per cow, total 651,000 dollars. Existing quota: 195 kilograms, comprised of 140 kilograms from original free allocation (carrying value nil) and 55 kilograms purchased in two prior transactions at a weighted average cost of 19,500 dollars per kilogram, total cost 1,072,500 dollars, current carrying value on LVD's books. Current published quota exchange clearing price: 24,000 dollars per kilogram, meaning existing quota's fair value is approximately 4,680,000 dollars (195 kilograms times 24,000 dollars) against a book value of 1,072,500 dollars.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: LVD Financial Summary, Year Ended June 30, 2026
exhibits.content: Milk sales revenue: 1,850,000 dollars. Operating expenses (feed, labour, veterinary, utilities, quota lease payments): 1,410,000 dollars. EBITDA: 440,000 dollars. Existing long term debt (barn mortgage and equipment loans): 620,000 dollars, annual debt service (principal plus interest) of 98,000 dollars. Current debt service coverage ratio: EBITDA of 440,000 dollars divided by total debt service; with the new quota loan, debt service would increase by approximately 65,500 dollars annually (480,000 dollars amortized over 10 years at 6.4 percent), bringing total annual debt service to approximately 163,500 dollars. Additional milk production from the 25 kilogram quota purchase is projected to add approximately 215,000 dollars of annual revenue and 165,000 dollars of incremental EBITDA once the expanded herd reaches full production, expected to take 8 months to ramp up.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 3: Gerrit's Questions and Bank Discussion Notes
exhibits.content: Gerrit's notes from a call with the Heartland Farm Credit loan officer: The loan officer said they primarily look at debt service coverage ratio and cash flow, not asset fair values, for the lending decision itself, but that a stronger balance sheet showing quota and herd at current market value could help with the overall relationship and potentially support a higher future borrowing base if LVD wants to expand further. The loan officer also asked whether LVD's financial statements are prepared on an accrual basis under a recognized framework, since Heartland's credit policy requires this for loans over 250,000 dollars, and was not sure from LVD's current bookkeeper prepared statements whether they meet this standard given quota and herd have never been remeasured or consistently valued.

requirements: Advise Gerrit and Anneke on the appropriate financial reporting approach for the dairy herd and quota, including whether a fair value approach is appropriate and what it would mean for LVD's financial statements.
Evaluate whether LVD can support the proposed quota purchase financially, including the debt service coverage impact, and advise on the financing decision.
Advise on what LVD needs to do to meet Heartland Farm Credit's financial statement requirements before the loan application proceeds.

model_response.understand_issue: You are an external advisor to Gerrit and Anneke, a farm couple weighing a significant quota purchase that requires bank financing, who also have an underlying financial reporting problem: their herd and quota have never been consistently or appropriately valued, and the bank has flagged that LVD's statements may not meet the accrual, recognized framework standard required for loans of this size. The real ask combines a financial reporting recommendation (fair value versus cost for biological assets and quota), a quantitative financing feasibility assessment, and practical next steps to get LVD's financial statements to where the bank needs them to be before the loan can be approved.
model_response.analyze_case:
Herd and quota valuation: agricultural produce and biological assets like a dairy herd are commonly measured at fair value less costs to sell under frameworks designed for agricultural producers, given the difficulty of tracking historical cost for animals raised internally at effectively nil cost, and this is directly relevant here since LVD's herd includes internally raised animals carried at nil that clearly have real economic value; the independent appraiser's estimate of 3,100 dollars per cow, giving a total fair value of 651,000 dollars against a book value of 514,500 dollars, illustrates the understatement under the cost approach. Quota presents a different and more significant issue: quota is an intangible asset, and while LVD's originally allocated 140 kilograms was received at nil cost and has appropriately been carried at nil under a cost model since no cost was incurred, the 55 kilograms purchased in two later transactions have a real cost of 1,072,500 dollars that should already be on the books at cost, which the case indicates it is. The question of remeasuring quota to fair value, 4,680,000 dollars in total using the current exchange price, is a bigger question, since intangible assets like purchased quota are generally not marked to a fluctuating market fair value under most accounting frameworks unless the specific framework LVD adopts explicitly permits a fair value model for this asset class, which is less commonly available for intangibles than for biological assets like livestock. Gerrit and Anneke need to understand that showing quota at fair value would dramatically inflate LVD's reported equity, from a position where quota is carried far below its economic value, but this is not simply an optional choice to make the balance sheet look better, it depends on which accounting framework LVD adopts and what that framework permits for each asset class, and a herd fair value adjustment and a quota fair value adjustment are not the same accounting question even though both currently understate LVD's true economic position.

Financing feasibility: LVD's current EBITDA of 440,000 dollars against current debt service of 98,000 dollars gives an existing debt service coverage ratio of roughly 4.5 to 1, comfortably above any typical minimum. Adding the new quota loan's approximately 65,500 dollars of annual debt service brings total debt service to approximately 163,500 dollars; using current EBITDA alone (before the new quota's production comes online), the ratio would be 440,000 divided by 163,500, approximately 2.69 to 1, still well above Heartland's 1.15 minimum. Once the new quota's projected incremental 165,000 dollars of EBITDA is fully phased in after 8 months, combined EBITDA of approximately 605,000 dollars against 163,500 dollars of debt service gives a ratio of roughly 3.70 to 1, even stronger. The financing appears well supported on a coverage basis in both the ramp up period and once mature, assuming the production projections are reasonably achievable, though the 8 month ramp up period should be discussed with the bank explicitly since it is the period of lowest coverage relative to the new debt, even though coverage remains comfortably above the covenant even during ramp up using current EBITDA alone.

Meeting bank requirements: the loan officer's comment that statements need to be prepared on an accrual basis under a recognized framework is a real gating item, separate from the fair value question; if LVD's bookkeeper prepared statements have not been consistently applying any single recognized framework, for example mixing cost and nil valuations inconsistently or not applying consistent policies year over year, this needs to be resolved with a qualified accountant preparing or at least reviewing LVD's statements under a defined framework before the loan application can be considered complete, regardless of whether fair value or cost is ultimately chosen for the herd.
model_response.recommendation: Recommend LVD adopt a fair value less costs to sell approach for the dairy herd, consistent with typical agricultural accounting practice, which would better reflect the herd's real economic value including internally raised animals and directly address the inconsistent nil versus cost treatment currently used. Recommend LVD continue carrying purchased quota at cost under a standard intangible asset model unless a specific framework LVD is adopting explicitly supports a fair value model for quota, and recommend Gerrit and Anneke get clear confirmation of which framework LVD will report under before deciding this, since it is not simply their choice to make for optics but depends on the applicable standards; regardless of the measurement model chosen, recommend the financial statements clearly disclose the quota's cost basis alongside its current market value in the notes, since this gives the bank the fuller economic picture the loan officer mentioned wanting without necessarily requiring a change in the primary measurement basis. On the financing decision, recommend proceeding with the quota purchase given the debt service coverage ratio remains strong (above 2.6 to 1 even before full production ramp up) against the 1.15 minimum, but recommend Gerrit and Anneke present the 8 month ramp up assumptions explicitly to the bank with a conservative sensitivity case, since this is the most vulnerable period even though it still clears the covenant comfortably. Recommend LVD engage a qualified accountant to prepare or review a full set of financial statements under one consistently applied recognized framework before submitting the loan application, since the current inconsistent bookkeeper prepared statements do not meet Heartland's stated requirement for loans over 250,000 dollars and could delay or jeopardize the application if not addressed first.
model_response.conclusion: LVD's underlying financial capacity to support the quota purchase looks strong under a reasonable debt service coverage analysis, but Gerrit and Anneke need to resolve the more fundamental financial reporting inconsistency in how the herd and quota have been valued and presented before the bank will treat LVD's statements as a reliable basis for the loan decision. Getting the accounting framework right first, rather than simply restating quota at a favourable fair value, will serve LVD better both for this financing and for its credibility with Heartland going forward.
