marking_guide.criterion: Recommends a fair value less costs to sell approach for the dairy herd (biological asset) and explains why this differs from the historical cost approach currently used, referencing the appraised value versus book value

Distinguishes quota (an intangible asset) from the dairy herd (a biological asset) and correctly identifies that fair value remeasurement is not automatically appropriate for quota the way it may be for the herd

Identifies that the originally allocated quota's nil cost carrying value is appropriate while the purchased quota's cost basis of 1,072,500 dollars should already be reflected, distinguishing the two components
Recommends disclosure of quota's market value in notes as an alternative to full remeasurement if the framework does not support a fair value model
Correctly calculates the existing and pro forma debt service coverage ratios using the case's EBITDA and debt service figures
Explicitly evaluates the ramp up period coverage separately from the fully phased in coverage, using the 8 month timeline
Concludes with a clear, well supported recommendation on whether to proceed with the quota financing
Uses EBITDA and incremental production figures appropriately to project post acquisition financial performance

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Recognizes that the fair value question is not simply an optics choice but depends on the applicable accounting framework LVD adopts
Identifies the bank's accrual/recognized framework requirement as a separate gating issue from the fair value question, and advises resolving it proactively before the loan application
Explains the herd versus quota distinction in terms Gerrit and Anneke, non accountants, can act on
Recommends engaging a qualified accountant to prepare statements under a consistent framework rather than the writer unilaterally fixing the books

rating_guidance: A nominal competence response suggests fair value might be appropriate for the farm's assets generally without distinguishing the herd from the quota, or calculates a debt service coverage ratio incorrectly or without using the case's actual EBITDA and debt figures. A reaching competence response correctly recommends fair value for the herd and does a reasonable debt service coverage calculation, but does not distinguish quota's treatment from the herd's treatment, treating both the same way, or does not identify the bank's separate accrual framework requirement. A competent response correctly distinguishes the herd (appropriate for fair value less costs to sell) from the purchased quota (appropriately at cost under a standard intangible model, with market value disclosure as an alternative to remeasurement), calculates both the ramp up and mature debt service coverage ratios correctly using the case data, and identifies the separate need to prepare statements under a consistent recognized framework before the loan application proceeds. A response reaching competence with distinction integrates all three pieces into a single practical action plan for Gerrit and Anneke, explicitly explains why treating quota the same as the herd would be a framework driven error and not simply a preference, and shows sophisticated judgment by presenting the ramp up sensitivity to the bank proactively as a credibility building step rather than something to avoid mentioning.

enabling_focus: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
Communicating
Leading
title: Brackenfield Construction: Percentage of Completion and a Change Order Dispute
case_background:
It is December 2026. You, CPA, are the controller at Brackenfield Construction Ltd. (BCL), a mid sized commercial general contractor in Hamilton, Ontario. BCL's fiscal year end is December 31, and BCL's financial statements are audited annually by Colville Reyes LLP because BCL's bonding company, a requirement for BCL to bid on larger commercial projects, requires audited statements as a condition of maintaining BCL's surety bonding capacity.

BCL is currently 68 percent complete, based on costs incurred to date, on a 14.2 million dollar fixed price contract to build a mixed use retail and office building for a developer client, Thornbury Properties Inc. BCL uses the percentage of completion method to recognize revenue on its long term contracts, consistent with industry practice for fixed price construction contracts where progress can be reliably measured. The project has been profitable to date, with BCL's original budgeted gross margin of 11 percent tracking closely to actual results through November 2026.

In October 2026, a significant dispute arose with Thornbury over a change order. BCL performed 620,000 dollars of additional structural steel reinforcement work that BCL's project manager states was required due to a design deficiency in Thornbury's structural engineer's drawings, discovered after excavation began. Thornbury's position, communicated in writing by their VP of Development, is that the reinforcement was BCL's responsibility to identify during the original bid process and that Thornbury will not approve or pay the 620,000 dollar change order. BCL's project manager and legal counsel believe BCL has a strong claim, since BCL's counsel has reviewed the original drawings and believes the deficiency was clearly an error in Thornbury's engineer's design, not something a reasonable contractor bidding the project could have identified, but no resolution has been reached, and Thornbury has not paid any portion of the 620,000 dollars, which BCL has already incurred as actual cost.

BCL's construction accountant has included the full 620,000 dollars as both a cost incurred and as expected contract revenue in the percentage of completion calculation for the Thornbury project, on the basis that BCL's legal counsel is confident of recovery. Your engagement partner, Priya Nazari, has flagged this treatment for you to assess as part of your controller level review before Colville Reyes LLP begins fiscal 2026 audit fieldwork in January, since a full 620,000 dollars of unresolved, disputed revenue is a meaningful percentage of the project's total remaining margin and could affect BCL's reported profit and, in turn, its bonding capacity calculation with the surety company.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Thornbury Contract Financial Summary, November 2026
exhibits.content: Original contract price: 14,200,000 dollars. Approved change orders to date (unrelated to the disputed steel reinforcement): 310,000 dollars, bringing approved contract value to 14,510,000 dollars. Disputed steel reinforcement change order: 620,000 dollars, not approved by Thornbury, currently included in both revenue and cost in BCL's percentage of completion calculation. Costs incurred to date (including the disputed 620,000 dollars): 9,850,000 dollars. Estimated total costs to complete the project (including the disputed 620,000 dollars): 14,485,000 dollars, giving an estimated total project gross margin of approximately 4.3 percent if the disputed amount is included as both revenue and cost with no separate profit margin applied to it (BCL applied the change order at cost recovery only, no markup, given the uncertainty). If the disputed 620,000 dollars is excluded entirely from both revenue and cost, estimated total costs to complete would be 13,865,000 dollars against contract revenue of 14,510,000 dollars, giving a gross margin of approximately 4.4 percent.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Legal Counsel Opinion Letter Excerpt
exhibits.content: Letter from BCL's legal counsel, Halversen Woolf LLP, dated November 20, 2026: Based on our review of the structural engineering drawings issued by Thornbury's engineer of record and our comparison to industry standard bid documentation practices, it is our opinion that the reinforcement deficiency identified after excavation began was not reasonably discoverable during the bid process and represents an error in the issued for construction drawings, not a scope item BCL should have priced into its original bid. We believe BCL has a good claim for recovery of the 620,000 dollars but note that Thornbury has not agreed to this position, no formal dispute resolution process (mediation or arbitration, as provided for under the contract) has yet been initiated, and litigation or arbitration outcomes are inherently uncertain. We would characterize BCL's likelihood of success, if the matter proceeds to formal dispute resolution, as more likely than not, but this is not a guarantee, and timing of resolution could extend six months to two years given the contract's arbitration clause.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 3: Bonding Company Requirement Extract
exhibits.content: BCL's surety bonding agreement with Continental Surety Group requires audited annual financial statements and includes a working capital and profitability covenant used to determine BCL's aggregate bonding capacity (the total dollar value of projects BCL can bid and be bonded for at one time). A significant reduction in reported gross margin on an in progress project could reduce BCL's calculated bonding capacity for the following year, which BCL's VP of Operations has flagged as a concern given BCL is planning to bid on two new projects in Q1 2027 that would require close to BCL's current maximum bonding capacity.

requirements: Assess whether the 620,000 dollar disputed change order should be included in BCL's percentage of completion revenue and cost estimates for the fiscal 2026 financial statements, and explain the basis for your conclusion.
Identify the financial statement disclosure considerations for the Thornbury dispute, regardless of how the change order is ultimately treated.
Advise BCL's management on how to manage the tension between the accounting treatment, the bonding capacity implications, and the ongoing negotiation or dispute resolution with Thornbury.

model_response.understand_issue: You are the controller at BCL, asked by the engagement partner to assess a significant judgment call before year end audit fieldwork begins: whether a disputed, unapproved 620,000 dollar change order should be included in the percentage of completion revenue recognition for a major project, given the outcome affects reported profit and, through that, BCL's bonding capacity for future bids. The real question is a claims recognition judgment under the percentage of completion framework, balanced against BCL's genuine legal position and the practical business consequences of getting the accounting conservative or aggressive, communicated to management who has real business reasons to want the more favourable treatment.
model_response.analyze_case:
Claims recognition: under percentage of completion accounting for long term contracts, additional consideration from claims (amounts a contractor seeks to collect from a customer for costs not included in the contract price, such as customer caused delays or errors) should only be recognized as contract revenue when specific, generally quite restrictive, conditions are met: the claim must be probable of recovery and reliably measurable, negotiations must have reached an advanced stage, there is a legal basis for the claim, and the amount can be reasonably estimated based on the contract terms and supporting documentation. Here, Thornbury has explicitly rejected the claim in writing, no formal dispute resolution process has even begun, and BCL's own legal counsel characterizes the outcome as more likely than not rather than as a stronger standard like probable or virtually certain, with a wide six month to two year potential resolution timeline. This falls meaningfully short of the threshold typically required to recognize claim revenue: a good faith legal opinion of a favourable outcome, without an advanced stage of negotiation or agreement in principle with the customer, is not the same as revenue being probable and reliably measurable. This means the 620,000 dollars should not currently be included as contract revenue, even though the cost was genuinely incurred and can remain in costs incurred to date; recognizing the cost without matching revenue is consistent with the framework's treatment of contract costs that relate to unapproved claims, expensed as incurred until recovery is established, rather than deferred or matched with unearned revenue.

Quantifying the impact: excluding the disputed 620,000 dollars from revenue while the cost remains incurred (unless BCL separately assesses whether some portion of the 620,000 dollars in costs should instead be excluded from the percentage of completion cost base entirely, treated instead as a receivable or contingent asset pending recovery, which would be the more conservative and arguably more technically correct approach since the cost was not contemplated in the original contract scope) meaningfully changes the picture from the case's own math: including the disputed amount gives an estimated 4.3 percent overall project margin, while excluding it entirely from both revenue and cost gives 4.4 percent, showing that how this is treated has a real, quantifiable effect on BCL's reported profitability on this project, not just a technical footnote matter.

Disclosure: regardless of how the 620,000 dollars is ultimately reflected in revenue and cost recognition, the existence of a significant unresolved contract dispute, the amount in question, the fact Thornbury has rejected it in writing, and management's assessment of the likely outcome and range of possible resolution timing, are all matters that should be disclosed, likely as a contingency or a significant contract related uncertainty in the notes to BCL's fiscal 2026 financial statements, since users, including the surety bonding company reviewing these statements, need visibility into a matter of this size and uncertainty affecting an in progress major contract.
model_response.recommendation: Recommend BCL not include the disputed 620,000 dollars as contract revenue in the fiscal 2026 percentage of completion calculation given the claim does not yet meet the threshold for claims recognition (no advanced stage of negotiation, formal rejection by the customer, no dispute resolution process yet initiated), and recommend BCL and its auditors specifically discuss whether the 620,000 dollars of costs incurred should remain in the percentage of completion cost base or be separately tracked as a contingent receivable pending recovery, since either treatment needs to be applied consistently and disclosed clearly, and this is a judgment BCL should not make unilaterally without the auditor's input given the audit is about to commence. Recommend clear note disclosure describing the nature of the Thornbury dispute, the amount, the status (rejected, not yet in formal dispute resolution), and management's assessment, without overstating the certainty of recovery given legal counsel's own more likely than not characterization rather than a stronger assurance. On managing the tension with bonding capacity, recommend BCL's management resist the temptation to include the disputed revenue simply to protect the Q1 2027 bonding capacity for the new bids, since presenting the financial statements based on business need rather than the appropriate accounting judgment risks the audit opinion and the surety relationship far more than a modestly lower but well supported and defensible margin; recommend instead that BCL proactively discuss the situation, including its plans for Q1 2027 bidding, directly with Continental Surety Group, since sureties generally respond better to transparency about a known, well documented, actively pursued claim than to being surprised by a dispute discovered later, and recommend BCL pursue formal dispute resolution with Thornbury promptly given the contract's arbitration clause, since faster resolution, whichever direction it goes, is more valuable to BCL's bonding capacity planning than an extended dispute sitting unresolved on the books.
model_response.conclusion: BCL has a genuinely strong legal position on the Thornbury change order, but a strong legal opinion is not the same as revenue that meets the recognition threshold for a disputed, unapproved claim, and management's understandable desire to protect bonding capacity should not drive the accounting conclusion. The better path for BCL is accurate, well disclosed financial statements paired with proactive communication to the surety and prompt pursuit of formal dispute resolution with Thornbury, which protects both BCL's credibility with its auditor and surety and its actual chances of recovering the 620,000 dollars.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly identifies the specific criteria for claims recognition under percentage of completion accounting and applies them to the case facts (rejection in writing, no advanced negotiation stage, legal opinion below a probable/virtually certain standard)
Concludes the 620,000 dollars should not be recognized as revenue given the facts, while distinguishing this from whether the cost remains in the cost base
Quantifies the margin impact of including versus excluding the disputed amount using the case's own figures (4.3 percent versus 4.4 percent)
Identifies that the treatment is a significant judgment that should be discussed with the auditors before fieldwork rather than decided unilaterally by BCL
Recommends specific note disclosure of the dispute's nature, amount, and status regardless of the recognition conclusion reached

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Correctly distinguishes between a strong legal opinion and the higher/different threshold required for accounting recognition of a disputed claim

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Connects the accounting judgment to the bonding capacity consequence without letting the business consequence override the technical conclusion

marking_guide.competency: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
marking_guide.criterion: Explicitly recommends against recognizing revenue to protect bonding capacity, prioritizing appropriate accounting over the favourable business outcome management wants

marking_guide.competency: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
marking_guide.criterion: Avoids overstating certainty of recovery in the recommended disclosure language, consistent with counsel's actual characterization
Recommends proactive communication with the surety about the dispute rather than waiting for it to be discovered through the audited statements
Advises management on pursuing formal dispute resolution promptly as part of managing the broader business consequence, showing initiative beyond the pure accounting question

Recommends the auditors specifically assess whether the disputed cost should remain in the percentage of completion cost base or be reclassified as a contingent receivable, flagging it as a matter for audit discussion rather than a closed question

rating_guidance: A nominal competence response concludes the change order should or should not be recognized without applying specific claims recognition criteria to the case facts, or ignores the bonding capacity and disclosure dimensions entirely. A reaching competence response correctly identifies that claims recognition has a threshold and that the facts here are weak (rejection, no formal process), but may not distinguish the revenue treatment from the cost treatment, or does not quantify the margin impact, or gives only generic disclosure advice. A competent response correctly applies the claims recognition criteria to conclude the 620,000 dollars should not be recognized as revenue, addresses the cost base question as a related but distinct judgment, recommends appropriate disclosure, and advises management against letting the bonding capacity concern drive the accounting conclusion, while suggesting proactive surety communication. A response reaching competence with distinction shows sophisticated judgment by explicitly distinguishing a favourable legal opinion from the accounting recognition threshold, quantifies the specific margin impact using the case numbers to make the materiality concrete, and ties together the accounting conclusion, the disclosure, and a practical business recommendation (prompt dispute resolution, proactive surety conversation) into a single coherent path forward that serves BCL's genuine interests better than the more aggressive treatment management may have hoped for.

enabling_focus: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
Communicating
Adding Value
Managing Self
title: Windward Cove Resort: Impairment and a Strategic Pivot Decision
case_background:
It is January 2027. You, CPA, are the finance manager at Windward Cove Resort Inc. (WCR), a mid sized lakeside resort in Muskoka, Ontario, owned by a group of five private investors led by managing partner Colette Renaud. WCR operates a 64 room lodge, a marina with 40 boat slips, and a conference centre that historically drew corporate retreat bookings. WCR has a September 30 fiscal year end and is preparing its fiscal 2026 year end financial statements, which are subject to a review engagement under WCR's shareholder agreement.

WCR's conference centre segment, which includes a dedicated 12,000 square foot building, has struggled since 2023, as corporate travel budgets shifted toward smaller, more local meeting venues and virtual conferencing reduced demand for large multi day retreats. The conference centre generated 480,000 dollars of revenue in fiscal 2026, down from 1.1 million dollars in fiscal 2022, and has operated at a loss for the past three fiscal years, with a fiscal 2026 operating loss of 210,000 dollars before an allocation of shared resort overhead.

Colette has asked you to assess whether the conference centre building and related equipment, carried on WCR's books at a net book value of 2.85 million dollars, is impaired, given the sustained losses. Separately, and connected to the same underlying issue, Colette and two of the other four investors want to pivot the conference centre space into a wellness and spa retreat concept, targeting a growing market of individual and small group wellness travellers rather than corporate conferences, which would require an estimated 950,000 dollars of renovation investment and result in the conference centre's existing specialized audiovisual and catering equipment, with a combined net book value of 340,000 dollars, becoming largely obsolete for the new concept.

The other two investors, brothers Devon and Marcus Whitfield, who together hold 30 percent of WCR's shares, are opposed to the pivot, arguing WCR should instead sell the conference centre building and the adjacent 4 acres of lakefront land it sits on separately, since a local developer has informally indicated interest in the range of 3.2 million dollars for that portion of the property, which the Whitfields believe is a better and lower risk use of capital than an unproven wellness retreat concept, especially given WCR already carries 1.9 million dollars of mortgage debt secured against the whole property. You need to complete the impairment assessment for the year end statements and provide analysis to help the ownership group evaluate the two competing strategic paths.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Conference Centre Segment Financial Data
exhibits.content: Fiscal 2026 revenue: 480,000 dollars (down from 1.1 million in fiscal 2022, 820,000 in fiscal 2024, 590,000 in fiscal 2025). Fiscal 2026 direct operating costs (staffing, utilities, maintenance specific to the conference centre): 690,000 dollars, giving a direct operating loss of 210,000 dollars before shared resort overhead allocation. Building and equipment net book value at September 30, 2026: building 2,510,000 dollars, specialized audiovisual and catering equipment 340,000 dollars, total 2,850,000 dollars. Building's estimated remaining useful life: 22 years. Management's revenue and cost projections if the conference centre continues in its current corporate retreat format: revenue expected to decline a further 8 to 12 percent annually for at least 3 more years based on the observed trend, with continued operating losses projected throughout, and undiscounted future cash flows over the building's remaining useful life estimated at approximately 1.95 million dollars, below the current net book value of 2,850,000 dollars.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Wellness Retreat Pivot Proposal
exhibits.content: Estimated renovation cost: 950,000 dollars, to be financed through a combination of 400,000 dollars from existing WCR cash reserves and a proposed 550,000 dollars addition to the existing mortgage facility, subject to lender approval. Projected wellness retreat revenue once ramped up (estimated 18 months to stabilize): 1.35 million dollars annually, with projected direct operating costs of 780,000 dollars, giving projected annual operating income of 570,000 dollars, a significant improvement over the current conference centre's losses. The existing specialized audiovisual and catering equipment, net book value 340,000 dollars, would be sold or scrapped for an estimated combined 45,000 dollars, as it is not suited to the wellness retreat concept. WCR's cost of capital: 8.5 percent.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 3: Land Sale Alternative and Whitfield Position
exhibits.content: Informal indication of interest from a local developer for the conference centre building and 4 acres of adjacent lakefront land: approximately 3.2 million dollars, though the Whitfields acknowledge this is not a firm offer and would require a formal appraisal, severance approval from the local municipality to sever the 4 acres from WCR's larger property, and completion of a sale process estimated to take 9 to 14 months. WCR's current mortgage: 1.9 million dollars outstanding, secured against the full resort property including the conference centre building and land; a partial sale would likely require lender consent and could trigger a partial mortgage discharge or renegotiation. Devon Whitfield's written comment to the ownership group: We have already lost money on this building for three straight years chasing a corporate market that is not coming back, and I am not comfortable adding renovation risk and more debt on top of a concept none of us have run before, when a cash sale removes the risk entirely and lets us pay down debt and reinvest in the parts of WCR that already work, the lodge and marina.

requirements: Assess whether the conference centre building and equipment are impaired for the fiscal 2026 year end financial statements, including the accounting basis for your conclusion.
Evaluate the wellness retreat pivot proposal against the land sale alternative from a financial and strategic perspective.
Advise Colette on how to bring the ownership group, including the dissenting Whitfield brothers, to a resolution on this decision.

model_response.understand_issue: You are WCR's finance manager, asked by the managing partner to complete a required impairment assessment for year end reporting purposes and, connected to the same facts, to provide analysis supporting a real strategic decision the five owner group is divided over. The audience is Colette directly but ultimately the full ownership group, including two investors who disagree with her preferred direction, so the real ask is both a technical accounting conclusion and an objective strategic comparison that can help move a divided ownership group toward a decision rather than simply endorsing one investor's preference.
model_response.analyze_case:
Impairment assessment: for long lived assets like the conference centre building and equipment, impairment testing under an asset group recoverability approach compares the asset group's carrying amount to the undiscounted future cash flows expected from its use, and if carrying amount exceeds those undiscounted cash flows, the asset is impaired and written down to fair value. Here, management's own projections show undiscounted future cash flows of approximately 1.95 million dollars over the building's remaining useful life, against a carrying amount of 2.85 million dollars, meaning the recoverability test fails and an impairment loss must be recognized, measured as the excess of carrying amount over the asset's fair value (which would need to be separately estimated, potentially using the 3.2 million dollar land and building indication as one reference point, though that figure includes land value and reflects an alternative use, a sale, rather than the assets continuing use, so it cannot be used directly without adjustment). This is a required accounting conclusion independent of which strategic path the ownership group ultimately chooses, since the impairment test is based on continuing to use the assets in their current form (the corporate conference format) generating the projected declining cash flows, and the sustained three year loss trend and management's own projection of continued decline are themselves the objective evidence supporting impairment.

Strategic comparison: the wellness retreat pivot projects annual operating income of 570,000 dollars once stabilized (revenue 1.35 million less operating costs 780,000), a substantial improvement over the current segment's 210,000 dollar loss, but requires a 950,000 dollar investment, an 18 month stabilization period during which returns are uncertain, and additional mortgage debt of 550,000 dollars on top of WCR's existing 1.9 million dollars already secured against the property, plus loss of the 340,000 dollar equipment's value beyond its 45,000 dollar scrap or resale estimate. Applying a rough payback and return lens: the 950,000 dollar investment against a projected 570,000 dollar annual operating income (before considering the ramp up period and before financing costs) suggests roughly a two year payback once stabilized, and at WCR's 8.5 percent cost of capital, a project generating this level of annual income relative to investment would likely show a strongly positive net present value if the projections hold, but the case gives no indication these wellness retreat projections have been independently validated or benchmarked against comparable operations, unlike the land sale figure which, while informal, is at least an external market indication. The land sale alternative offers 3.2 million dollars, well above both the impaired carrying amount and likely above the 1.95 million dollar continuing use value, providing immediate capital to pay down the 1.9 million dollar mortgage and reinvest in the lodge and marina segments the Whitfields note are already performing well, but carries its own risks and uncertainties: the 3.2 million dollars is informal, not a firm offer, the municipal severance process could fail or be delayed well beyond the estimated 9 to 14 months, and completing the sale removes WCR's optionality on that portion of the property permanently.

Governance dynamic: this is a decision with real financial stakes for all five owners and a genuine, reasonable difference of opinion, not a situation where one side is clearly right; Colette's preferred pivot has higher potential upside but meaningfully higher execution risk and additional leverage, while the Whitfields' preferred sale is lower risk and provides immediate liquidity but depends on an unconfirmed price and an uncertain approval process, and permanently forecloses the upside if the wellness concept would have worked.
model_response.recommendation: Recommend WCR recognize an impairment loss on the conference centre building and equipment in the fiscal 2026 statements, using the undiscounted cash flow shortfall as the trigger and estimating fair value, potentially informed by an independent appraisal given the informal 3.2 million dollar indication cannot be used directly without adjusting for the land component and the change in use it assumes, to determine the specific write down amount; recommend this proceed regardless of which strategic direction is chosen, since it reflects the segment's performance under its current use as required by the accounting framework. On the strategic decision, recommend the ownership group not choose between the two paths based on incomplete information: recommend obtaining a formal appraisal and confirming the municipal severance feasibility and timeline before treating the 3.2 million dollar land sale figure as reliable, and recommend independently stress testing the wellness retreat revenue and cost projections, ideally against comparable operator benchmarks, before relying on the 570,000 dollars of projected annual operating income, since neither figure should be treated as confirmed as currently presented. Recommend Colette convene a structured ownership discussion, using a shared, mutually agreed set of validated assumptions (once the appraisal and severance inquiry and the benchmarked projections are available) rather than continuing to debate based on each side's preferred but unverified numbers, and recommend the group explicitly discuss risk tolerance and the mortgage capacity implications of each path, since the debt consequences differ meaningfully (paying down debt under a sale versus adding debt under a pivot) and this may be as important to some owners as the return potential itself.
model_response.conclusion: The conference centre is impaired under its current use regardless of which strategic path WCR pursues, and that write down should proceed at year end independent of the ownership debate. The choice between the wellness pivot and the land sale is a legitimate, close strategic call between higher risk upside and lower risk certainty, and Colette's best path to resolving the disagreement with the Whitfields is grounding the debate in validated numbers, an appraisal, confirmed severance feasibility, and benchmarked wellness projections, rather than each side continuing to argue from its own preferred but unverified assumptions.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly applies the recoverability test comparing carrying amount to undiscounted future cash flows and concludes impairment exists using the case's 1.95 million and 2.85 million figures
Recognizes the impairment measurement requires estimating fair value, and appropriately cautions that the 3.2 million dollar land sale figure cannot be used directly without adjustment
Recognizes the impairment conclusion applies regardless of which strategic direction is ultimately chosen, since it reflects current use
Compares the wellness pivot and land sale alternatives using specific figures from both exhibits (570,000 projected operating income vs. 3.2 million sale price, financing/debt implications of each)
Identifies that both alternatives rest on unverified assumptions (informal price indication, unbenchmarked wellness projections) and recommends validation steps before deciding
Applies a return or payback style analysis to the wellness pivot investment using the case's cost of capital and projected figures
Identifies the differing debt/leverage implications of the two paths (paying down the mortgage vs. adding to it) as a decision relevant factor

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Does not simply recommend one strategic path but frames a structured process for the ownership group to reach a validated decision

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Separates the mandatory accounting conclusion (impairment) from the discretionary strategic decision (pivot vs sell) as two distinct matters
Provides Colette with a specific, practical approach for engaging the dissenting Whitfield brothers constructively rather than overriding them
Identifies that neither side's current numbers are validated and proactively recommends specific due diligence steps (appraisal, severance confirmation, benchmarking) beyond what either side has done
Maintains objectivity between the two ownership factions rather than favouring Colette's preference simply because she is the requester

rating_guidance: A nominal competence response asserts the building might be impaired without applying the recoverability test using the case's specific undiscounted cash flow and carrying amount figures, or simply picks one strategic side without comparing both alternatives using case data. A reaching competence response correctly applies the impairment recoverability test and reaches the correct conclusion, and discusses both strategic alternatives, but may not recognize that the impairment conclusion is independent of the strategic choice, or may not flag that both alternatives rest on unverified figures. A competent response correctly performs the impairment recoverability test, appropriately cautions on measuring fair value using the land sale figure without adjustment, compares both strategic alternatives quantitatively using the case's actual numbers including debt implications, and gives Colette a structured, objective approach to resolving the ownership disagreement rather than simply validating her preferred outcome. A response reaching competence with distinction shows sophisticated judgment by clearly separating the mandatory accounting conclusion from the discretionary strategic decision, explicitly identifies that both the wellness projections and the land sale price are unverified and proposes specific validation steps for each, and crafts a governance process for the ownership group that treats the Whitfields' concerns as legitimate rather than an obstacle to be managed around.

enabling_focus: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
Adding Value
Communicating
title: Meridian Health Partners: Clinic Acquisition Due Diligence
case_background:
It is April 2027. You, CPA, are a senior associate at Castellane Bergeron Advisory, engaged by Meridian Health Partners Inc. (MHP), a growing operator of 9 physiotherapy and rehabilitation clinics across southern Ontario, to perform financial due diligence on a proposed acquisition. MHP's CEO, Owen Marchetti, has agreed to a letter of intent to acquire Lakeshore Physiotherapy Group (LPG), a 3 clinic operation in the Niagara region, for a purchase price of 2.4 million dollars, structured as 1.8 million dollars cash at closing and 600,000 dollars in an earnout payable over 2 years contingent on LPG achieving specified revenue targets. The purchase price was based on a multiple of 4.2 times LPG's reported trailing twelve month EBITDA of 571,000 dollars, which LPG's owner, Renata Kowalski, provided to MHP directly during preliminary negotiations, before your firm was engaged to verify the underlying figures.

During your review of LPG's financial records, you identify several matters. First, LPG's reported EBITDA includes 145,000 dollars of revenue from a block billing arrangement with a regional workers compensation board for a caseload of injured workers; your review of the underlying contract shows this arrangement expired in December 2026 and was not renewed, with the workers compensation board's regional office confirming to you directly, with LPG's consent, that the caseload has been reassigned to a competing clinic starting January 2027, meaning this revenue will not recur in future periods despite being included in the trailing twelve month figure Renata provided.

Second, you find that LPG's reported EBITDA does not include an add back for approximately 95,000 dollars of Renata's total compensation that appears to be above a reasonable market rate for a working owner clinic director performing Renata's actual duties, based on comparable executive compensation benchmarks for similar sized private clinic operations that your firm's valuation team provided; if LPG is normalizing EBITDA for a new owner who would pay a market rate general manager instead, this suggests EBITDA should be adjusted upward, the reverse effect of the WCB revenue issue.

Third, you discover that one of LPG's three clinics, the St. Catharines location, has a lease expiring in August 2027, five months from the anticipated May 2027 closing date, and the landlord has verbally indicated to Renata, confirmed in an email you obtained during diligence, that renewal rent would increase from the current 4,200 dollars monthly to approximately 6,800 dollars monthly given current market rates in the area, a fact not reflected anywhere in LPG's financial projections shared with MHP. Owen has asked you to complete the diligence report and, given the letter of intent's exclusivity period expires in three weeks, wants your preliminary view quickly on whether the deal terms need to be revisited.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: LPG Trailing Twelve Month EBITDA as Presented by Renata
exhibits.content: Total revenue: 2,890,000 dollars, including 145,000 dollars from the WCB block billing arrangement described above. Total operating expenses before EBITDA adjustments: 2,502,000 dollars, including Renata's total compensation (salary plus benefits plus bonus) of 215,000 dollars. Reported EBITDA: 388,000 dollars. Renata's presentation to MHP included one add back not reflected in the 388,000 dollars above: a one time 183,000 dollars addback for renovation costs at the Niagara Falls clinic incurred in 2026, which LPG's records confirm were indeed non recurring facility improvement costs, bringing Renata's presented trailing twelve month EBITDA to 571,000 dollars (388,000 plus 183,000), the figure used to negotiate the 4.2 times multiple and 2.4 million dollar purchase price.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Owner Compensation Benchmark and WCB Confirmation
exhibits.content: Castellane Bergeron valuation team's benchmark for a market rate general manager/clinic director role, based on comparable private multi site physiotherapy operations in Ontario of similar size and revenue: 120,000 dollars total annual compensation. Renata's actual total compensation as reported in Exhibit 1: 215,000 dollars, a difference of 95,000 dollars above the benchmark. Written confirmation from the regional workers compensation board's contracting office, dated March 28, 2027, obtained with Renata's consent as part of diligence: The block billing arrangement with Lakeshore Physiotherapy Group expired December 31, 2026 and was not renewed; the associated caseload has been reassigned to an alternate provider effective January 1, 2027 and there are no plans to reinstate this arrangement with Lakeshore in the foreseeable future.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 3: St. Catharines Lease Email and Clinic Level Detail
exhibits.content: Email from the St. Catharines landlord's property manager to Renata, dated February 10, 2027, shared with you during diligence: With your current lease expiring August 31, 2027, we wanted to give you advance notice that based on current market rates in the area, we would expect to propose a renewal rate in the range of 6,700 to 6,900 dollars monthly, up from your current 4,200 dollars monthly rate. St. Catharines clinic annual revenue: 940,000 dollars, representing approximately 33 percent of LPG's total revenue, with estimated annual EBITDA contribution of approximately 195,000 dollars before considering the higher rent. The current 4,200 dollar monthly rent (50,400 dollars annually) is included in LPG's reported operating expenses as presented in Exhibit 1; the increase to approximately 6,800 dollars monthly (81,600 dollars annually) would add approximately 31,200 dollars of annual rent expense not reflected in any projection shared with MHP.

requirements: Recalculate LPG's normalized trailing twelve month EBITDA, incorporating appropriate adjustments for the matters identified during diligence, and assess the impact on the proposed 4.2 times EBITDA purchase price.
Assess the implications of the St. Catharines lease renewal for the acquisition, including how it should be addressed in the transaction.
Advise Owen on how MHP should respond given the exclusivity period deadline, including what should be renegotiated and how to raise these findings with Renata.

model_response.understand_issue: You are the diligence advisor to MHP's CEO, Owen, who has agreed to a purchase price based on a seller provided EBITDA figure that your work has now shown includes both an overstatement (non recurring WCB revenue) and an understatement (excess owner compensation that would not transfer to a new owner), plus an unrelated lease risk not reflected anywhere in the deal terms. The real ask is to produce a normalized, defensible EBITDA figure that should actually drive the purchase price, quantify the specific dollar impact of the lease issue, and give Owen practical, time sensitive advice on renegotiating with Renata before the exclusivity period expires, in a way that is fact based rather than adversarial given the parties still need to close a deal together.
model_response.analyze_case:
EBITDA normalization: starting from Renata's presented 571,000 dollars, which already appropriately includes the 183,000 dollars addback for genuinely non recurring 2026 renovation costs, two further adjustments are needed. First, the 145,000 dollars of WCB block billing revenue included in the trailing twelve months should be removed, since the underlying contract has definitively expired and been reassigned to a competitor with written confirmation there are no plans to reinstate it, meaning this revenue, and its associated profit contribution, will not recur for a new owner; assuming this revenue carried a margin roughly consistent with LPG's overall EBITDA margin (388,000 divided by 2,890,000, approximately 13.4 percent) would understate the removal since block billing arrangements typically carry lower incremental costs once the base clinic infrastructure exists, so a more conservative and defensible approach is to remove the full 145,000 dollars from revenue and only add back the directly avoidable incremental costs, if any can be identified; absent specific evidence of material incremental costs tied only to the WCB caseload, the full 145,000 dollars should be treated as a reduction to normalized EBITDA. Second, Renata's compensation should be adjusted to reflect the 95,000 dollar excess over the 120,000 dollar market benchmark, added back to EBITDA since a new owner would only need to pay a market rate general manager going forward, this being a standard normalization adjustment for owner operated business valuations. Combining these: normalized EBITDA equals 571,000 dollars less 145,000 dollars (WCB) plus 95,000 dollars (compensation normalization) equals 521,000 dollars, a net reduction of 50,000 dollars from the figure used to negotiate price. Applying the same 4.2 times multiple to the normalized 521,000 dollars gives an implied purchase price of approximately 2,188,200 dollars, roughly 212,000 dollars lower than the agreed 2.4 million dollars, before even considering the lease issue.

Lease issue: the St. Catharines clinic, representing roughly a third of LPG's total revenue and an estimated 195,000 dollars of EBITDA contribution, faces a rent increase from 50,400 dollars to approximately 81,600 dollars annually starting September 2027, four months after the anticipated May 2027 closing, adding approximately 31,200 dollars of annual expense not reflected in any figure shared with MHP or embedded in the purchase price negotiation. This further reduces the going forward normalized EBITDA that MHP would actually realize post acquisition, from approximately 521,000 dollars to approximately 489,800 dollars once the higher rent is in place, and it is a known, quantifiable, near term item, not a speculative risk, given the written landlord communication.
model_response.recommendation: Recommend MHP present Renata with the normalized EBITDA calculation of approximately 521,000 dollars (before the lease increase) or approximately 489,800 dollars if annualizing the higher rent going forward, both meaningfully below the 571,000 dollars used to set the 2.4 million dollar price, and recommend MHP renegotiate the purchase price downward using the same 4.2 times multiple applied to the normalized figure, which implies a price in the range of approximately 2.06 to 2.19 million dollars depending on which EBITDA base is used, rather than accepting the original 2.4 million dollars. Recommend the St. Catharines lease specifically be addressed as a closing condition or a specific purchase price adjustment, since it is a known, quantified, near term cash flow reduction rather than a general business risk, and recommend MHP also confirm the lease renewal terms directly with the landlord before closing given the current information is based on an informal email rather than a signed renewal, since the actual terms could differ. Recommend raising all three findings with Renata directly and factually, presenting the underlying evidence (the WCB confirmation letter, the compensation benchmark, the landlord email) rather than simply asserting a lower value, since a transparent, evidence based renegotiation is more likely to preserve the deal and the relationship than an unexplained lower offer, and recommend Owen do this promptly given the three week exclusivity deadline, since running out the clock without addressing these findings would leave MHP negotiating from a weaker position or force a rushed decision at the deadline.
model_response.conclusion: The diligence has surfaced real, evidence based reasons the original 2.4 million dollar price, built on an unadjusted seller provided EBITDA figure, overstates what MHP would actually be acquiring, and Owen has both the facts and the time, if he acts promptly within the exclusivity window, to renegotiate to a price that reflects LPG's true normalized earning power. Handling this transparently with Renata, grounded in the specific evidence gathered, gives MHP the best chance of closing a fair deal rather than either overpaying or losing the opportunity entirely.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly removes the 145,000 dollars of non recurring WCB revenue from normalized EBITDA, explaining why it does not represent recurring earning power
Correctly adds back the 95,000 dollars of excess owner compensation as a normalization adjustment, explaining the market rate replacement rationale
Combines both adjustments to arrive at a normalized EBITDA figure (approximately 521,000 dollars) and correctly recalculates the implied purchase price using the 4.2 times multiple
Incorporates the St. Catharines lease increase's annual cash flow impact (approximately 31,200 dollars) into the going forward earnings assessment

Appropriately relies on and cites the specific diligence evidence (WCB confirmation letter, compensation benchmark, landlord email) to support each adjustment rather than asserting conclusions without support
Notes the landlord's indication is informal (email, not a signed renewal) and recommends confirming final lease terms before closing

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Recognizes that the WCB and compensation adjustments move in opposite directions and nets them appropriately rather than only presenting the negative adjustment

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Treats the lease issue as a distinct, quantifiable deal term to negotiate (price adjustment or closing condition) rather than a vague risk factor

marking_guide.competency: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
marking_guide.criterion: Recommends a transparent, evidence based approach to raising the findings with Renata rather than a one sided or non disclosed lower offer
Gives Owen a clear, quantified renegotiation position (specific revised price range) rather than only qualitative concerns
Addresses the time pressure of the exclusivity deadline explicitly in the recommended course of action
Identifies the compensation normalization adjustment even though it was not explicitly flagged as an issue by MHP, going beyond the two problems Owen was already aware existed

rating_guidance: A nominal competence response identifies that the WCB revenue or the lease issue reduces the value of the deal, without quantifying the specific dollar adjustments using the case's actual figures, and does not address the compensation normalization at all. A reaching competence response correctly quantifies at least one of the three adjustments (WCB removal, compensation add back, or lease impact) using case figures, but does not net all three together into a single normalized EBITDA and revised price recommendation. A competent response correctly calculates all three adjustments, nets the WCB removal against the compensation add back to reach a normalized EBITDA around 521,000 dollars, incorporates the lease impact into the go forward assessment, and gives Owen a specific, evidence based renegotiation position along with practical advice on timing given the exclusivity deadline. A response reaching competence with distinction shows sophisticated judgment by recognizing the compensation adjustment moves in the opposite direction from the WCB adjustment and correctly nets both rather than only emphasizing the negative finding, explicitly separates the lease issue as a distinct negotiable deal term (price adjustment versus closing condition) from the EBITDA normalization exercise, and frames the entire renegotiation approach around transparent evidence sharing with Renata that preserves the possibility of closing a fair deal rather than simply justifying a lower offer.

enabling_focus: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
Communicating
Collaborating
title: Prairie Grain Partners: Succession, Storage Losses, and a Family Dispute
case_background:
Prairie Grain Partners (PGP) is a family-owned grain farming operation near Swift Current, Saskatchewan, growing wheat, canola, and lentils on 6,400 owned and leased acres. The business was built over 35 years by Walter Dorchuk, now 68, who wants to retire within two years and transfer the operation to his two adult children, Nadia and Corey. Nadia, 39, has managed the agronomy and field operations for twelve years. Corey, 34, moved back to the farm eighteen months ago after a career in commercial banking and now handles grain marketing and equipment financing. You, CPA, are a manager at a regional public accounting firm that has prepared PGP's compilation engagement and corporate tax returns for the past six years. Walter has asked you to help the family think through the transition and to look into a storage problem before harvest begins in six weeks.

PGP operates through a holding structure: Dorchuk Farms Ltd. owns the land and three grain storage bins (combined capacity 42,000 bushels), while a separate operating entity, PGP Cropco Inc., owns the equipment, carries the inventory, and contracts the annual crop insurance. Walter holds 100% of the voting common shares of both companies. Nadia and Corey each hold 20% of a class of non-voting preferred shares issued three years ago as part of an earlier, incomplete estate freeze that Walter's previous accountant set up but never fully documented with an election filed under section 85 of the Income Tax Act. Walter is not sure whether the freeze was properly implemented and is worried about triggering unexpected tax on death if it was not.

Separately, in April a moisture sensor failure in Bin 2 went undetected for nine days during unusually humid weather. When Corey inspected the bin in May he found that roughly 3,100 bushels of the 11,000 bushels of canola stored there had heated and spoiled, and could only be sold as animal feed at a steep discount instead of as food-grade canola. PGP's crop insurance covers field losses (hail, drought, frost) but explicitly excludes post-harvest storage losses caused by equipment malfunction. Corey wants to file an insurance claim anyway, arguing to Nadia that the adjuster "probably won't check the cause too closely if we just say the bin had a structural leak." Nadia is uncomfortable with this and called you directly, without Corey's knowledge, to ask what she should do. She also mentioned that PGP Cropco's year-end inventory count (fiscal year end June 30) is in five weeks and that the spoiled canola is still sitting in Bin 2 valued at cost in the internal records.

PGP Cropco's grain inventory is currently recorded at the lower of cost and net realizable value under the cost formula the company has used for years, weighted average cost, with cost built up from field input costs (seed, fertilizer, fuel, custom spraying) allocated by acre and crop type. Walter mentioned that in past years the bookkeeper has sometimes valued year-end inventory at the estimated selling price under a specified commodity contract instead, because "it's easier and the bank likes seeing it that way for the borrowing base certificate." PGP has a $1.2 million operating line with Prairie Farm Credit secured by inventory and receivables, with a covenant requiring a current ratio of at least 1.25:1, tested at each fiscal year end. Walter forwarded you the unaudited internal trial balance and is anxious that a bad debt covenant breach could happen right as he is trying to negotiate transition financing for Nadia and Corey to buy out his voting shares over five years.

Walter has asked you to (1) look at the estate freeze and flag anything that needs fixing before he sees his lawyer next month, (2) address how the spoiled canola should be handled for both the insurance question and the year-end inventory valuation, and (3) give him your honest read on whether Nadia and Corey are ready to run PGP together, since their disagreement over the insurance claim is the first real conflict he has seen between them. He wants a memo he can share with both children.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: PGP Cropco Inc. - Unaudited Trial Balance Extract, June 30 (draft, before year-end adjustments)
exhibits.content: Cash: $84,000. Accounts receivable (grain contracts): $312,000. Inventory - grain on hand: $1,610,000 (includes 11,000 bushels of canola in Bin 2 at weighted average cost of $11.80/bushel, i.e. $129,800 of the total). Prepaid crop insurance: $46,000. Total current assets: $2,052,000. Bank operating line (Prairie Farm Credit): $1,050,000. Accounts payable and accrued input costs: $472,000. Current portion of equipment loans: $135,000. Total current liabilities: $1,657,000. Current ratio as drafted: 1.24:1. Covenant requirement: 1.25:1, tested June 30 and December 31 each year. Corey's note attached to the file: "If we value the canola at the feed-grade contract price of $14.50 instead of holding it at cost, or if the insurance claim comes through before year end, the ratio clears covenant. Let's hold off finalizing until we know the insurance outcome." Food-grade canola contract price at June 30: $17.20/bushel. Feed-grade (damaged) canola price offered by a local feedlot buyer: $9.10/bushel, cash, buyer to pick up within two weeks.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Crop Insurance Policy Excerpt and Adjuster Email
exhibits.content:
Policy excerpt, Saskatchewan Crop Insurance Corporation, Storage and Equipment Rider: "Coverage under this policy applies to yield loss arising from insured perils occurring prior to harvest, including hail, drought, excess moisture, and frost. This policy does NOT provide coverage for loss, spoilage, or deterioration of grain after harvest, including losses caused by inadequate aeration, sensor or equipment malfunction, or improper storage management, except where such loss is a direct result of physical damage to the storage structure itself (e.g., roof failure, wall breach) caused by an insured peril."

Email from Corey Dorchuk to Nadia Dorchuk, May 14, forwarded to you by Nadia with the subject line 'need your read on this, don't mention I sent it': "Nadia, I talked to the adjuster's office. If we report this as a suspected roof seam leak from the April hailstorm rather than the sensor issue, it fits the rider and we get the claim paid, probably $28-30k after our deductible. The sensor failure is our fault and nobody's going to find that out unless we tell them. I already told the equipment supplier not to send us the sensor fault log. We need the cash for the new grain cart before harvest and I don't want this eating into what we can offer Dad for the buyout. Can you back me up if the adjuster calls?"

exhibits.title: Exhibit 3: Estate Freeze Documentation (as found in the prior year's file)
exhibits.content: Memo dated three years ago from the previous accountant, unsigned, found in the permanent file: "Recommend estate freeze: Walter Dorchuk exchanges his common shares of Dorchuk Farms Ltd. for fixed value preferred shares under section 85, retaining voting control via a special voting preferred class; new common shares issued to Nadia and Corey to capture future growth." Share register as currently maintained by PGP's bookkeeper shows: Walter Dorchuk holds 1,000 common shares (100% voting) of both Dorchuk Farms Ltd. and PGP Cropco Inc. Nadia Dorchuk and Corey Dorchuk each hold 200 Class B non-voting preferred shares of PGP Cropco Inc. only, issued at $50,000 each (paid by promissory note, still outstanding, no interest charged or accrued). No section 85 election form (T2057) is in the file. No corresponding preferred shares were ever issued in Dorchuk Farms Ltd., the entity holding the land, which has appreciated from an estimated $2.1 million to approximately $4.6 million in fair market value since the shares were issued to Nadia and Corey. Walter's note: "I thought this was all done together as one deal. I don't actually know what I signed."

requirements: Prepare a memo to Walter Dorchuk addressing how PGP Cropco should account for the spoiled canola inventory at June 30, including the effect on the covenant calculation, and recommend how the insurance claim situation should be handled.
Assess the status of the estate freeze structure, identify the risks in how it was implemented, and recommend next steps before Walter meets with his lawyer.

Advise Walter on the readiness of Nadia and Corey to jointly lead PGP, using what the case reveals about their approach to this conflict, and identify the ethical obligations you personally face given what Nadia has told you in confidence.

model_response.understand_issue: I am a CPA acting as PGP's long-standing external accountant, asked by Walter Dorchuk, the controlling owner, for a memo he intends to share with both of his children. The real ask has three layers: a technical inventory and covenant question complicated by a fraudulent insurance scheme Corey is proposing, an incomplete and potentially costly estate freeze, and a governance judgment about succession readiness. My audience is Walter, but my work will be read by Nadia and Corey, and Nadia has confided in me privately about conduct that affects PGP's financial statements and a third party (the insurer). I must navigate confidentiality, integrity, and my own independence obligations while giving Walter a candid, evidence-based answer, not simply the answer that keeps the family comfortable.
model_response.analyze_case:
Insurance and inventory issue: Corey's plan to characterize the sensor-driven spoilage as hail damage to the bin structure is insurance fraud, and asking Nadia to corroborate a false cause of loss asks her to participate in it. The policy language in Exhibit 2 is unambiguous that equipment and sensor malfunction is excluded and that only physical structural breach from an insured peril is covered. There is no evidence of roof or wall damage; Corey is proposing to fabricate one. Regardless of PGP's cash needs ahead of harvest, submitting or supporting a false claim exposes PGP, Corey personally, and by extension Nadia if she stays silent, to insurance fraud liability and voids the broader policy. I need to be direct with Walter that this is not a business judgment call, it is a legal and ethical line that cannot be crossed, and that concealing the equipment supplier's fault log compounds the exposure.

On the accounting itself, the 11,000 bushels of canola in Bin 2 cannot remain at the original weighted average cost of $11.80/bushel once it is known to be spoiled. Inventory is carried at the lower of cost and net realizable value. The realistic recovery is the feed-grade cash offer of $9.10/bushel (less any disposal costs, none noted), not the food-grade contract price of $17.20 that no longer applies to this damaged portion, and not a hoped-for insurance recovery, which under ASPE is a contingent gain that should not be recognized until virtually certain, and here should not be pursued at all given the fraud issue. Writing the 3,100 damaged bushels down from $11.80 to $9.10 produces a loss of $2.70 per bushel, $8,370 in total, which needs to flow through cost of sales, not be quietly capitalized or ignored. Corey's suggestion to instead value the canola at $14.50 has no basis since that price was never offered or contracted for this grain and materially overstates a known-impaired asset purely to manage a bank ratio, which is a red flag for management bias and would misstate the financial statements presented to the lender.

Recomputing the current ratio: current assets fall by $8,370 to $2,043,630 (from $2,052,000), current liabilities are unaffected by this adjustment at $1,657,000, giving a ratio of about 1.234:1, still below the 1.25:1 covenant, and worse than the draft 1.24:1 once properly stated versus Corey's proposed $14.50 valuation. PGP is at real risk of a covenant breach independent of the insurance question, and that needs to be flagged to Walter now so he can talk to the lender proactively about a waiver rather than let it surface after the fact tied to a disputed inventory value.

Estate freeze issue: the documentation in Exhibit 3 shows the freeze was only ever half-executed. Walter still holds 100% of the voting common shares of both entities, no section 85 election was filed, and the preferred shares issued to Nadia and Corey exist only in PGP Cropco, the equipment and inventory company, not in Dorchuk Farms Ltd., the entity holding the land that has appreciated by roughly $2.5 million. This means the freeze failed to accomplish its purpose for the asset that matters most, the land, and Walter's estate still bears the full future growth in that land's value along with a large deemed disposition on death under subsection 70(5) if nothing changes. There is also an unpaid, non-interest-bearing shareholder loan from Nadia and Corey's original share subscription that could trigger an income inclusion under the shareholder loan rules or an imputed benefit if it remains outstanding too long. This needs specialist tax and legal attention before Walter proceeds further; my role is to flag the gap and the urgency, not to redesign the freeze myself.
model_response.recommendation:
On the canola, PGP Cropco should write the 3,100 damaged bushels down to $9.10 per bushel net realizable value, recognizing an $8,370 loss in the June 30 financial statements, and sell to the feed buyer promptly since the offer is time-limited. Walter should not submit or support any insurance claim that mischaracterizes the cause of loss; the correct and only defensible action is to either not file a claim given the exclusion, or file honestly disclosing the sensor failure and accept it will likely be denied. The equipment supplier's fault log should be requested and retained, not suppressed. Walter should have a direct conversation with Corey making clear this is not open for negotiation, and should consider whether Corey's willingness to propose this reflects a broader risk-tolerance gap that needs addressing before handing him marketing and financial authority. Given the covenant is projected to fall short at roughly 1.234:1 regardless of the insurance outcome, Walter should contact Prairie Farm Credit now, before June 30, to discuss the shortfall transparently and explore a temporary waiver or an injection of additional working capital, rather than let the bank discover it through the covenant certificate.

On the freeze, Walter should meet with his lawyer and an independent tax advisor as soon as possible to complete a proper freeze of Dorchuk Farms Ltd., likely requiring a fresh valuation of the land, a section 85 rollover, and formal share attribute documentation, and to resolve the outstanding shareholder loans, ideally by having Nadia and Corey repay them or having the loans formally forgiven with the resulting tax consequences considered. This should happen before, not after, any further transition steps.

On succession readiness, this conflict is a legitimate warning sign, not a reason to abandon the plan. Nadia showed good judgment and integrity by refusing to go along with the claim and by seeking outside advice discreetly; Corey showed poor judgment under financial pressure. Walter should use this as a concrete, low-stakes-relative-to-later opportunity to set clear decision rights and an escalation process for disagreements before the buyout, rather than assume the siblings will resolve conflicts like this on their own once he steps back.
model_response.conclusion: PGP faces a near-term covenant shortfall that exists independent of the insurance dispute and must be addressed honestly with the lender, an incomplete estate freeze that leaves Walter's largest asset unprotected from future tax exposure, and a live integrity test between the two people who are about to inherit control of the business. None of these can be resolved with a shortcut; each requires Walter to act now, before harvest and before the ownership transition accelerates, using proper professional advice on the tax and legal side and a firm, values-based conversation with his children on the operational side.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly identifies the lower of cost and net realizable value issue and calculates the write-down using the feed-grade price, not a fabricated or insurance-contingent value
Recomputes the current ratio with the correct inventory adjustment and identifies the covenant is breached regardless of the insurance outcome
Rejects Corey's proposal to value inventory at $14.50 and explains why this would misstate the financial statements
Identifies that the estate freeze was never completed for Dorchuk Farms Ltd. and that Walter's exposure to tax on death remains largely unaddressed
Flags the outstanding non-interest-bearing shareholder loans as a separate tax risk requiring specialist attention
Assesses succession readiness using specific evidence from the case rather than generic commentary
Recommends Walter proactively engage the lender about the covenant shortfall rather than wait for discovery

marking_guide.competency: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
marking_guide.criterion: Unambiguously identifies Corey's proposal as insurance fraud and recommends against it regardless of cash flow pressure

marking_guide.competency: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
marking_guide.criterion: Recognizes the tension in having received confidential information from Nadia and addresses it appropriately in the response to Walter without breaching confidence inappropriately

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Separates the accounting question from the insurance ethics question and addresses both with distinct, well-reasoned conclusions
Frames the memo appropriately for an audience that will include both children, balancing candor with tact
Recommends a concrete governance mechanism (decision rights, escalation process) for Nadia and Corey rather than a vague call to 'communicate better'
Recognizes the professional boundary of not redesigning the freeze personally and instead recommends qualified legal and tax specialists
Advises Walter to address Corey's conduct directly as an owner-leader responsibility rather than avoid the confrontation

rating_guidance: A nominal-competence response notices the canola is damaged and mentions a write-down but does not tie the number to the correct net realizable value source, misses the covenant math entirely, or treats Corey's insurance proposal as a minor communication problem rather than fraud. A reaching-competence response gets the write-down calculation right and flags the freeze as incomplete but stops at describing the issues without connecting the covenant shortfall to a concrete recommendation for Walter to approach the lender, and gives only a soft caution on the insurance issue. A competent response correctly quantifies the write-down and the resulting covenant breach, clearly states the insurance proposal is fraudulent and must not proceed, identifies the specific gap in the freeze (land entity never addressed, no section 85 filing), and gives Walter an honest, evidence-based assessment of succession readiness. A response demonstrating competence with distinction does all of that and additionally integrates the threads, for example linking Corey's ethical lapse under financial pressure to a broader governance recommendation about decision rights before the buyout, addresses the shareholder loan issue as an additional tax exposure most candidates will miss, and handles the confidentiality tension from Nadia's private disclosure with genuine professional judgment rather than ignoring it.

enabling_focus: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
Communicating
Adding Value
Leading
title: Harbourview Property Group: Lease Classification and an Expansion Financing Decision
case_background:
Harbourview Property Group Inc. (HPG) owns and manages six mixed-use commercial buildings in Halifax, Nova Scotia, with a combined 480,000 square feet of retail and office space. HPG is privately owned by three partners and reports under ASPE. You, CPA, are the newly hired Director of Finance, having started four weeks ago after eleven years in public practice. The managing partner, Elaine Furlong, has asked you to prepare a memo ahead of Thursday's partner meeting, where the partners will decide whether to proceed with a seventh building, a $9.4 million acquisition of a light-industrial property called Bayers Lake Distribution Centre, currently 100% leased to a single tenant, a regional grocery distributor, under a lease with eleven years remaining.

HPG's bank, Maritime Commercial Trust, has offered two financing structures for the acquisition. Option A is a conventional 20-year mortgage at 6.1% fixed, requiring a 25% down payment ($2.35 million) and secured only against the new property, with a debt service coverage ratio (DSCR) covenant of 1.30x tested annually on HPG's consolidated results. Option B is a 10-year mortgage at 5.4% fixed with only a 15% down payment ($1.41 million) but cross-collateralized against two of HPG's existing buildings, and carries a DSCR covenant of 1.40x plus a loan-to-value covenant of 70% that would be tested using annual third-party appraisals of all three pledged properties, not just the new one.

Separately, Elaine has flagged an accounting question that has been bothering her. HPG's largest existing tenant, a national pharmacy chain occupying 62,000 square feet at the Spring Garden Road building, recently renegotiated its lease. The new twelve-year term includes a fixed annual base rent, but also gives the tenant an option to purchase the entire building at the end of year eight for $6.2 million, a price HPG's own appraiser estimates will be roughly 55% of the building's expected fair value at that time given current market trends in the neighbourhood. HPG's bookkeeper has been recording this lease the same way as all of HPG's other tenant leases, as an operating lease with rent recognized straight-line over the term, but Elaine is not sure that treatment still holds given the purchase option and wants your view before it affects the year-end statements that go to the bank under HPG's existing covenants.

HPG's most recent fiscal year end (December 31) shows consolidated EBITDA of $4.1 million and existing annual debt service (principal and interest) across all current mortgages of $2.6 million, for a current DSCR of 1.577x. The Bayers Lake acquisition would add its own EBITDA contribution of roughly $780,000 annually from the existing lease, but also adds new debt service. Elaine also mentioned, almost in passing, that one of the three partners, Desmond Okafor, has a 50% ownership stake in a numbered company that would act as the leasing broker on the Bayers Lake deal, earning a $141,000 commission on the transaction if it closes, a fact that has not yet been raised formally with the other two partners.

Elaine wants your memo to (1) work out and compare the DSCR impact of Option A versus Option B for the Bayers Lake acquisition and recommend which financing structure the partners should choose, (2) advise on how the Spring Garden Road pharmacy lease should be classified and what that means for HPG's financial statements and existing covenants, and (3) tell her how to handle the Desmond Okafor conflict of interest before Thursday's meeting.

HPG's existing six buildings collectively carry two mortgages already registered against Spring Garden Road and Dartmouth Crossing, both currently unencumbered by any cross-collateralization arrangement, which is part of why the partners have historically preferred single-asset security whenever possible. Elaine mentioned that outside legal counsel has not yet reviewed either financing term sheet in detail, and that Thursday's meeting is intended to reach a preliminary decision so an offer can be submitted to the seller before a competing bidder, rumoured to be a regional REIT, potentially moves first. She wants your memo to stand on its own, since Desmond has a habit of dominating partner discussions with strong opinions, and she would like the other two partners to walk in already grounded in the numbers rather than reacting only to whatever he presents verbally at the table.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Bayers Lake Financing Options Summary
exhibits.content: Purchase price: $9,400,000. Existing lease EBITDA contribution: $780,000 per year, fixed for the eleven remaining years of the lease term. Option A: 25% down ($2,350,000), 20-year mortgage on remaining $7,050,000 at 6.1% fixed, estimated annual debt service (blended principal and interest, level payment) of approximately $615,000. Security: Bayers Lake property only. DSCR covenant: 1.30x, tested on consolidated HPG results annually. Option B: 15% down ($1,410,000), 10-year mortgage on remaining $7,990,000 at 5.4% fixed, estimated annual debt service of approximately $980,000 (shorter amortization drives higher payments despite the lower rate). Security: Bayers Lake plus Spring Garden Road and the Dartmouth Crossing building. DSCR covenant: 1.40x.
