Additional loan-to-value covenant: 70%, tested annually via third-party appraisal of all three pledged properties. HPG's current consolidated EBITDA: $4,100,000. HPG's current existing annual debt service across all mortgages: $2,600,000.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Spring Garden Road Pharmacy Lease Terms (excerpt)
exhibits.content: Tenant: National pharmacy chain (existing tenant since building opened). Space: 62,000 square feet of a 62,000 square foot single-tenant building (pharmacy occupies the entire building). Term: 12 years, commencing this fiscal year. Base rent: fixed at $18.50/sq ft annually, no escalation clause. Purchase option: tenant may purchase the building for $6,200,000 at the end of year 8 of the lease, exercisable at tenant's sole discretion with 180 days notice. HPG's appraiser's current estimate of the building's fair value at the end of year 8: approximately $11,300,000, based on current cap rate trends for single-tenant pharmacy-anchored retail in the Spring Garden corridor. Estimated fair value of the building today: $8,900,000. Estimated remaining useful economic life of the building at lease inception: 30 years. HPG's bookkeeper's note in the file: 'Recorded same as all our other tenant deals, straight-line operating lease, no change needed since it's still just a rental agreement until they exercise, if ever.'

exhibits.title: Exhibit 3: Partner Ownership and Related Party Note
exhibits.content: HPG Inc. ownership: Elaine Furlong 40%, Desmond Okafor 35%, Priya Bhandari 25%. HPG's partnership agreement, Section 9.2: 'No partner shall enter into or cause the Company to enter into any transaction in which that partner or a related party of that partner holds a financial interest without first disclosing the interest in writing to the other partners and obtaining approval by a majority of the disinterested partners.' Email from the Bayers Lake seller's agent to Elaine, forwarded by the file clerk: 'Confirming Coastal Leasing Solutions Ltd. will act as broker of record on this transaction, commission of 1.5% of purchase price payable at closing, standard terms.' A quick search by the file clerk of the Nova Scotia corporate registry, attached to the email, shows Coastal Leasing Solutions Ltd. is 50% owned by D. Okafor and 50% owned by an unrelated third party. No disclosure of this ownership has been made to Priya Bhandari or circulated with the Thursday meeting materials.

requirements: Calculate and compare the consolidated DSCR under Option A and Option B for the Bayers Lake acquisition, and recommend which financing option the partners should select, addressing the covenant risk of each.
Advise on the appropriate lease classification for the Spring Garden Road pharmacy lease and explain the financial statement and covenant implications of your conclusion.
Advise Elaine on how to handle the Desmond Okafor conflict of interest before Thursday's partner meeting.

model_response.understand_issue: I am the newly hired Director of Finance reporting to the managing partner of a private, ASPE-reporting real estate company, preparing a memo for a partner decision meeting in three days. Three distinct questions are being asked of me: a financing structure comparison with real covenant risk consequences, a lease classification judgment that affects existing bank covenants on unrelated properties, and an undisclosed related-party conflict of interest involving one of the three decision-makers at the very meeting where the financing decision will be made. My job is to give Elaine numbers she can rely on and a clear, professionally grounded recommendation on the governance issue, since she is new to relying on me and this is a credibility-defining first assignment.
model_response.analyze_case:
DSCR comparison: current consolidated EBITDA is $4.1 million against current debt service of $2.6 million, a baseline DSCR of 1.577x. Under Option A, EBITDA rises by the Bayers Lake contribution of $780,000 to $4.88 million, and debt service rises by approximately $615,000 to $3.215 million, giving a projected consolidated DSCR of about 1.518x, comfortably above the 1.30x covenant with roughly 17% headroom. Under Option B, EBITDA is the same $4.88 million, but debt service rises by approximately $980,000 to $3.58 million, giving a projected DSCR of about 1.363x, which falls short of the higher 1.40x covenant, meaning Option B would breach its own DSCR covenant at closing even before any vacancy or rent shortfall in the three cross-collateralized buildings is considered. Option B is also riskier structurally because it cross-collateralizes Spring Garden Road and Dartmouth Crossing, exposing buildings unrelated to this acquisition to default risk from Bayers Lake, and adds a loan-to-value covenant tested against all three properties, compounding the exposure precisely at a time when Spring Garden Road's own lease classification is in question. The lower down payment in Option B preserves $940,000 of cash today, but that benefit is outweighed by the thin covenant cushion and the cross-collateralization risk, especially given HPG's DSCR position deteriorates further if the Spring Garden Road lease turns out to require reclassification.

Spring Garden Road lease classification: under ASPE Section 3065, a lease is a capital lease if, among other criteria, it is reasonably assured that the lessee will obtain ownership by the end of the lease term, or the lease term is for a major part of the asset's economic life, or the present value of minimum lease payments amounts to substantially all of the fair value of the asset. Here the tenant holds a purchase option at the end of year 8 for $6.2 million against an appraised expected fair value at that date of roughly $11.3 million, a strike price at only about 55% of expected fair value. This is a deeply in-the-money bargain purchase option; a rational tenant would almost certainly exercise it, which makes it reasonably assured that ownership will transfer. That alone is sufficient to require capital lease treatment, and the analysis is reinforced by the lease term covering a major part of the asset's remaining economic life relative to the point ownership is expected to transfer. The bookkeeper's continuation of straight-line operating lease treatment is not supportable once the purchase option economics are examined; this is not simply 'a rental agreement until exercised,' the accounting standard requires an assessment of what is reasonably assured to happen, not what has already happened. Reclassifying to a capital lease would remove the building's carrying value (or the relevant portion) from HPG's investment property and instead recognize a net investment in the lease, changing the pattern and composition of income recognized and altering the balance sheet in ways that affect any existing covenants tied to Spring Garden Road, including its use as collateral under Option B, which makes getting this right before Thursday even more important.

Related party conflict: Desmond Okafor holds a 50% interest in Coastal Leasing Solutions Ltd., the broker earning a $141,000 commission on the very transaction being voted on, and this has not been disclosed to Priya Bhandari as the partnership agreement's Section 9.2 requires. This is a clear governance breach in progress, not a hypothetical risk, since the meeting materials have already been circulated without disclosure.
model_response.recommendation:
The partners should proceed with Option A. It carries a comfortable covenant cushion (roughly 1.52x against a 1.30x covenant), whereas Option B's projected DSCR of roughly 1.36x would not even meet its own 1.40x covenant threshold at closing, a breach rather than merely a thin cushion. Option A also avoids putting two unrelated, currently unencumbered buildings at risk, and the additional $940,000 upfront cash required is not, on these numbers, worth the added structural and covenant risk, particularly given uncertainty is already being introduced by the Spring Garden Road lease question.

The Spring Garden Road pharmacy lease should be reclassified from operating to capital lease treatment for the current fiscal year, given the bargain purchase option. Elaine should have this formally assessed and quantified before year end, communicate proactively with any lender relying on HPG's covenants that reference Spring Garden Road, and ensure the reclassification is reflected before the building is potentially pledged as collateral under any financing option, since an inaccurate representation of the property's lease status to a lender would be a serious integrity issue.

On the conflict of interest, Elaine must ensure Desmond's ownership in Coastal Leasing Solutions Ltd. is disclosed in writing to Priya Bhandari and formally recorded before Thursday's vote, consistent with Section 9.2 of the partnership agreement. Desmond should recuse himself from voting on the Bayers Lake acquisition given his direct financial interest in it closing, and the meeting should not proceed on the current materials until this disclosure has happened, since voting on an undisclosed related-party transaction would taint the decision regardless of whether the underlying deal is sound.
model_response.conclusion: HPG can proceed with the Bayers Lake acquisition on sound footing only if it selects the more conservative Option A financing, corrects the Spring Garden Road lease classification before it understates HPG's obligations to its lenders, and resolves the undisclosed conflict of interest before any partner vote occurs. Addressing the numbers without addressing the governance breach would leave HPG exposed to a tainted decision even if the acquisition itself proves sound.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly calculates projected consolidated DSCR under both Option A and Option B using the given EBITDA and debt service figures
Recommends Option A with quantified covenant cushion comparison rather than a preference based on down payment size alone
Identifies the cross-collateralization risk to unrelated properties under Option B as a structural risk beyond the covenant ratio itself
Applies the ASPE 3065 capital lease criteria and identifies the bargain purchase option as determinative
Quantifies or clearly reasons through why the $6.2 million option price versus $11.3 million expected fair value makes exercise reasonably assured
Connects the reclassification to downstream covenant and collateral implications rather than treating it as an isolated bookkeeping fix
Identifies the undisclosed related-party interest as a breach of the partnership agreement requiring formal disclosure and recusal

marking_guide.competency: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
marking_guide.criterion: Treats the conflict of interest as a required disclosure and voting-integrity issue, not a minor administrative detail

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Addresses all three distinct issues (financing, lease classification, conflict of interest) with linked, non-siloed analysis
Presents the DSCR comparison clearly with supporting figures suitable for a partner-level decision memo
Recommends proactive lender communication about the lease reclassification rather than waiting for it to surface at audit or covenant testing
Gives Elaine, a new supervisor of the candidate, a decisive and actionable recommendation on the governance issue rather than deferring the judgment back to her

rating_guidance: A nominal-competence response calculates a DSCR for only one option or makes an arithmetic error, notes the purchase option exists without concluding on capital versus operating classification, and mentions the broker relationship without identifying it as a required disclosure under the partnership agreement. A reaching-competence response gets both DSCR figures roughly right and picks a financing option but does not explain the cross-collateralization risk, correctly leans toward capital lease treatment but without firmly linking the option price to fair value, and flags the conflict of interest but with a vague recommendation to 'be transparent.' A competent response accurately compares both financing structures with the covenant cushion made explicit, reaches a well-supported capital lease conclusion tied to the bargain purchase option criterion, and gives Elaine a specific, actionable recommendation that Desmond disclose and recuse before the vote. A response demonstrating competence with distinction integrates all three issues, for example noting that Spring Garden Road's reclassification affects its suitability or representations if ever used as loan collateral, and frames the conflict-of-interest recommendation in a way that protects HPG's decision integrity even if the underlying acquisition itself later proves justified.

enabling_focus: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
Communicating
Managing Self
title: ActiveCare Physiotherapy Group: Billing Practices and a Clinic Profitability Review
case_background:
ActiveCare Physiotherapy Group Inc. operates five physiotherapy and rehabilitation clinics across the Greater Toronto Area, employing 34 registered physiotherapists, 12 massage therapists, and support staff. The clinics bill a mix of private extended health insurers, the provincial Workplace Safety and Insurance Board (WSIB) for workplace injury patients, and direct patient payments. You, CPA, are a senior financial analyst in ActiveCare's small head office finance team, reporting to the CFO, Marguerite Tan. ActiveCare is owned by three physiotherapist-founders and is preparing for a possible minority investment from a private equity healthcare fund, Meridian Health Partners, which has completed preliminary due diligence and is now asking pointed questions before finalizing its offer.

Marguerite has asked you to look into two things this week. First, Meridian's due diligence team flagged that ActiveCare's Mississauga clinic shows an unusually high proportion of WSIB billings coded as 'extended treatment, complex injury' (billing code PT-14, reimbursed at $186 per session) compared to ActiveCare's other four clinics, which code the large majority of similar injuries as 'standard treatment' (billing code PT-6, reimbursed at $94 per session). The Mississauga clinic manager, Tobias Reyes, is ActiveCare's highest-billing clinic manager and receives a bonus equal to 8% of any billings above a monthly target, uncapped. Marguerite pulled a sample of ten Mississauga patient files coded PT-14 and, reading the treatment notes herself even though she is not a clinician, thought at least four of them looked like routine soft-tissue injuries with treatment plans similar to what other clinics code as PT-6. She has not raised this with Tobias yet and does not want to accuse him without more evidence, but she is worried about what Meridian's diligence team will conclude, and separately worried about WSIB fraud exposure regardless of the deal.

Second, and unrelated to the coding question, Marguerite wants a proper contribution margin analysis of all five clinics, because Meridian's offer is structured to value ActiveCare partly on a multiple of clinic-level EBITDA, and the current allocation of shared head-office costs (billing staff, marketing, IT, the CFO's own office) across the five clinics has never been revisited since it was set up four years ago using a simple headcount-based allocation. Marguerite suspects this understates the profitability of the newer, smaller Oakville clinic, which has grown quickly but carries a headcount-based share of overhead that may no longer reflect its actual usage of shared services, and overstates the profitability of the original, larger Scarborough clinic. Meridian's proposed valuation multiple would be applied per clinic if ActiveCare later wants to sell or close underperforming locations, so getting the allocation basis right matters beyond just this one negotiation.

ActiveCare reports under ASPE. The clinics recognize revenue from insurers at the time treatment is provided, net of an allowance for expected claim denials, which has historically run at about 3% of gross billings across the company but has never been analyzed clinic by clinic. Marguerite wants your recommendation on both issues before her call with Meridian's diligence lead on Friday, including whether the WSIB coding pattern needs to be raised proactively with Meridian rather than waiting for them to find it, and how to fix the overhead allocation for the profitability analysis Meridian has requested.

ActiveCare's three founders have been actively involved in clinical work themselves for most of the company's history, and Marguerite noted this is part of why oversight of individual clinic billing practices has been relatively informal, each clinic manager has historically been trusted to run daily operations, including coding decisions, with limited head-office review beyond monthly revenue reporting. Meridian's diligence team has also asked, separately from the coding question, for a breakdown of how ActiveCare's revenue mix (private insurance, WSIB, direct pay) differs by clinic, since Meridian's investment thesis assumes WSIB referral relationships are a durable growth channel worth paying a premium multiple for, which makes the Mississauga coding question even more sensitive, since it is WSIB billings specifically that are in question at ActiveCare's most WSIB-dependent location.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Clinic Billing and Cost Data (current fiscal year, $000s)
exhibits.content: Scarborough: Revenue $2,840, direct clinic costs (clinician wages, supplies, rent) $1,920, headcount 14 FTE, square footage 6,200. Mississauga: Revenue $2,410, direct clinic costs $1,540, headcount 11 FTE, square footage 4,800, WSIB PT-14 billings represent 61% of WSIB revenue (industry and ActiveCare's other four clinics average 18%). Oakville: Revenue $1,180, direct clinic costs $740, headcount 6 FTE, square footage 2,600, grew 34% year over year. North York: Revenue $1,960, direct clinic costs $1,310, headcount 9 FTE, square footage 4,100. Etobicoke: Revenue $1,540, direct clinic costs $1,050, headcount 8 FTE, square footage 3,400. Total shared head-office costs to allocate: $1,240. Current allocation method: by headcount FTE (48 FTE total across clinics). Head office team's actual driver usage assessment, prepared by Marguerite's team last month but not yet applied: billing/claims processing effort correlates with number of insurance claims processed (WSIB claims require roughly 2.3x the administrative time of a standard private insurance claim due to WSIB's separate reporting requirements); IT and marketing costs correlate more closely with revenue than headcount.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: WSIB Billing Code Definitions and Sample File Notes
exhibits.content:
WSIB Fee Schedule excerpt: 'PT-6 Standard Treatment: applies to soft tissue, sprain/strain, and post-surgical injuries following a standard recovery trajectory, reimbursed at $94/session. PT-14 Extended Treatment, Complex Injury: applies only where the treating physiotherapist has documented a complicating factor such as multiple-site injury, pre-existing condition significantly affecting recovery, or a documented failure to progress after 8 standard sessions, reimbursed at $186/session. Coding to PT-14 without supporting clinical documentation of a qualifying complicating factor constitutes a billing misrepresentation under WSIB provider agreements and may result in clawback, provider audit, or removal from the approved provider list.'

Marguerite's handwritten summary of the four flagged Mississauga files: File 1: 'Lower back strain, lifting injury, no other conditions noted, coded PT-14 from session 1, no documented complicating factor in the file.' File 2: 'Shoulder sprain, coded PT-14 from session 3 after only 2 prior sessions at PT-6, note says patient reported the pain 'wasn't improving as fast as hoped', no formal failure-to-progress documentation per the 8-session standard.' File 3: 'Similar lifting injury to File 1, coded PT-14 throughout, file looks almost identical in treatment plan to a PT-6-coded file from North York clinic for a comparable injury.' File 4: 'Knee injury with a genuinely documented pre-existing arthritis condition noted by the physiotherapist, complicating factor appears legitimate and well documented, correctly coded PT-14.'

exhibits.title: Exhibit 3: Tobias Reyes Compensation Structure and Recent Email
exhibits.content: Clinic manager bonus plan, all five clinics: base salary plus 8% of monthly billings in excess of an assigned target, uncapped, paid monthly. Mississauga's assigned monthly target: $175,000 (the lowest of the five clinics per Marguerite, set two years ago before Tobias joined). Mississauga's actual average monthly billings over the last six months: $201,000, generating bonus payments to Tobias averaging roughly $2,080/month above his base. Email from Tobias to the Mississauga front desk lead, forwarded to Marguerite anonymously by a former employee: 'For WSIB files, if there's any ambiguity at all on complexity, code it PT-14 and I'll deal with it if WSIB ever asks. We're leaving money on the table otherwise and our bonus structure rewards volume not perfect coding.' The email is dated four months ago.

requirements: Analyze the WSIB billing coding pattern at the Mississauga clinic, assess whether it represents a real risk to ActiveCare, and recommend how Marguerite should handle it, including whether and how to raise it with Meridian.

Recommend a more appropriate basis for allocating shared head-office costs across the five clinics, recalculate clinic-level contribution margin using your recommended allocation, and explain how this changes the profitability picture Meridian will see.
Advise Marguerite on any broader control weaknesses this situation reveals about ActiveCare's compensation structure and billing oversight.

model_response.understand_issue: I am a senior financial analyst reporting to ActiveCare's CFO during a live investment negotiation, where the counterparty's diligence team has already flagged an anomaly I now have direct evidence supports a real concern. Marguerite needs a technically sound overhead reallocation for the valuation conversation, but the more urgent and higher-stakes issue is that the billing pattern points to probable insurance fraud tied directly to a manager's uncapped bonus incentive, discovered days before a diligence call. My response has to give her a defensible position for Friday and be honest that this is not simply a modeling question, it is a disclosure and integrity question that could affect the deal and ActiveCare's standing with WSIB regardless of the deal's outcome.
model_response.analyze_case:
WSIB coding issue: the fee schedule in Exhibit 2 is explicit that PT-14 requires documented clinical justification, a complicating factor, and that coding without it is a billing misrepresentation with real consequences, clawback, audit, or removal from the provider list. Of the four sampled files, three show clear red flags: File 1 has no complicating factor documented at all; File 2 was upgraded to PT-14 after only two prior sessions based on a subjective patient comment, well short of the required eight-session failure-to-progress threshold; File 3 is materially indistinguishable from a PT-6-coded file at another clinic. Only File 4 shows a properly documented, legitimate complicating factor. Combined with Mississauga's PT-14 rate of 61% against a 18% average elsewhere in the company, and Tobias's own email instructing staff to code ambiguous cases as PT-14 and that he would 'deal with it if WSIB ever asks,' this is not an isolated documentation gap, it is a pattern consistent with deliberate upcoding driven by his uncapped, revenue-linked bonus. This creates real exposure: WSIB clawback of overbilled amounts, provider audit, potential removal from the WSIB approved provider list which would be severe given WSIB revenue is material to Mississauga, and reputational and legal risk for ActiveCare as the corporate billing entity.

Given this, the issue must be raised proactively with Meridian rather than left for their diligence team to find independently, since Meridian already flagged the anomaly and any attempt to let it pass unaddressed would be a material omission discovered later, damaging trust in the whole transaction and potentially exposing ActiveCare to a claim of misrepresentation in the deal documents. It also needs to be investigated and corrected on its own merits independent of the deal, since the WSIB exposure exists regardless of whether Meridian invests.

Overhead allocation: the current headcount-based allocation does not reflect actual cost drivers. Total shared costs are $1,240K across 48 FTE, or about $25.8K per FTE under the current method. But the case identifies that billing and claims administrative effort is driven by claim volume and mix, not headcount, with WSIB claims requiring roughly 2.3x the effort of standard private claims, and that IT and marketing correlate with revenue. Mississauga's WSIB-heavy billing mix means its true administrative burden is understated under a flat headcount allocation, while Oakville's fast revenue growth on a small headcount base means it is likely absorbing less than its fair share of IT and marketing cost under headcount allocation, flattering its apparent contribution margin less than it should, or conversely, Scarborough's larger headcount may be overallocated its share of revenue-driven costs relative to its actual revenue share. A more defensible allocation would split shared costs into components: a claims-processing component allocated by weighted claim volume (adjusting WSIB claims by the 2.3x factor), and an IT/marketing component allocated by revenue share, rather than a single blended headcount driver. This is directly relevant to Meridian's proposed clinic-level EBITDA multiple, since a distorted allocation could lead to a mispriced valuation of individual clinics if ActiveCare or Meridian later considers divesting or investing further in specific locations.
model_response.recommendation:
Marguerite should treat the Mississauga coding pattern as a confirmed, high-priority issue, not a suspicion to sit on. She should commission a proper clinical audit of all Mississauga PT-14 files by a qualified physiotherapist reviewer (not just her own reading of the notes), quantify the extent of potential overbilling, and be prepared to self-report to WSIB and make any required repayment if the audit confirms a pattern, since voluntary disclosure is treated far more favourably than discovery. She should raise the issue proactively with Meridian's diligence lead on Friday, framing it as an issue ActiveCare identified, is actively investigating, and is remediating, rather than waiting for Meridian to raise it again, since Meridian already has enough information to suspect something is wrong. Tobias's bonus structure and the specific instruction in his email need to be addressed directly, likely including a compensation plan redesign that removes the uncapped incentive to maximize billing codes and adds appropriate coding-accuracy safeguards, such as periodic independent chart audits across all clinics, not just Mississauga.

On overhead allocation, Marguerite should move to a two-driver model, claim-volume-weighted allocation for billing and claims administration and revenue-based allocation for IT and marketing, and rerun the clinic-level contribution margins on that basis before presenting profitability figures to Meridian, clearly documenting the change in methodology and the reasoning so it withstands diligence scrutiny.
model_response.conclusion: ActiveCare faces two connected problems: a billing integrity issue at its highest-billing clinic that must be investigated and disclosed regardless of the Meridian deal, and an overhead allocation methodology that no longer reflects how the business actually operates and would distort the very valuation Meridian is trying to establish. Addressing both proactively protects ActiveCare's credibility with Meridian, its standing with WSIB, and the integrity of the profitability data driving the transaction.

marking_guide.criterion: Evaluates the sample of four patient files against the PT-14 documentation criteria and correctly concludes three of four lack supporting justification
Connects the coding pattern, the compensation structure, and Tobias's email into a coherent fraud risk assessment rather than treating them as separate facts
Identifies headcount as a poor cost driver for claims administration and revenue as a better driver for IT/marketing, and proposes a two-driver reallocation
Recalculates or clearly directionally explains how contribution margins shift for at least Mississauga and Oakville under the improved allocation
Recognizes the potential need to adjust the revenue allowance for expected claim denials/clawbacks given the identified overbilling risk

marking_guide.competency: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
marking_guide.criterion: Recommends proactive disclosure to Meridian rather than waiting to be asked, and recommends self-reporting to WSIB if the audit confirms the pattern

marking_guide.competency: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
marking_guide.criterion: Does not minimize or excuse the billing pattern based on Tobias being a high performer

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Recommends an independent clinical review rather than relying on Marguerite's own non-clinical reading of the files as sufficient evidence
Frames the Meridian disclosure recommendation in a way that is honest but constructive for an active negotiation
Recognizes the limits of a financial analyst's clinical expertise and recommends appropriately qualified review rather than overstepping
Recommends a broader periodic chart-audit control across all five clinics rather than a Mississauga-only fix

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Distinguishes the two-driver overhead reallocation issue from the billing integrity issue rather than conflating them into one recommendation

rating_guidance: A nominal-competence response notices the PT-14 rate is unusually high but does not review the sample files against the actual coding criteria, and proposes only a generic overhead reallocation without linking it to the specific drivers described in the case. A reaching-competence response reviews the files and correctly flags some as unsupported but does not tie this to Tobias's compensation structure or his email as corroborating evidence, and proposes a directionally better allocation without full justification. A competent response builds a complete fraud risk picture from the file review, the billing statistics, and the compensation incentive, recommends proactive disclosure to both WSIB and Meridian, and produces a properly justified two-driver overhead reallocation. A response demonstrating competence with distinction does all of that while also recognizing the compensation plan itself as the root control weakness requiring redesign, and explicitly connects the allocation fix to how it will affect Meridian's clinic-level valuation approach, not just historical reporting.

enabling_focus: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
Communicating
Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
Adding Value
title: Northern Roast Coffee Co.: Franchise Fee Recognition and a Struggling Franchisee
case_background:
Northern Roast Coffee Co. Inc. is a Canadian coffee shop franchisor based in Winnipeg, with 46 franchised locations across Manitoba, Saskatchewan, and northwestern Ontario, plus 3 corporately owned flagship stores. You, CPA, are the Controller, reporting to the CEO, Bethany Kroeker, who founded the company eleven years ago. Northern Roast is a private company reporting under ASPE and is in the process of renewing its $3.5 million operating facility with its bank, Assiniboine Credit Union, which requires audited annual financial statements as a condition of the facility.

Northern Roast charges new franchisees an initial franchise fee of $45,000, payable $15,000 on signing the franchise agreement and the remaining $30,000 upon store opening, plus an ongoing royalty of 6% of gross sales and a 2% national marketing fund contribution. The initial fee is intended to cover Northern Roast's site selection support, initial training (a mandatory two-week program at head office), and access to proprietary recipes and the supply chain relationship with Northern Roast's coffee roaster. Historically, the company has recognized the full $45,000 initial fee as revenue at the time the $15,000 signing payment is received, on the reasoning that 'the fee is non-refundable once signed.' This year, six new franchise agreements were signed, and Bethany wants your view on whether this continues to be appropriate, since the company's auditor, a different firm than yours (Northern Roast switched auditors last year), sent a preliminary planning memo flagging franchise fee revenue recognition as a significant risk area for this year's audit, given that of the six new agreements, only two stores had actually opened by year end, three are still mid-buildout, and one signed franchisee has not yet started any site work at all, six months after signing.

Separately, Bethany has a harder problem. One of Northern Roast's longest-standing franchisees, Devan Marchetti, who operates the Portage Avenue location and has been with Northern Roast for eight years, is three months behind on royalty payments totalling $18,400, and has told Bethany privately that the store is barely breaking even because a new corporate-owned Northern Roast flagship store opened four blocks away last year, drawing away foot traffic. Devan is asking Bethany for a temporary royalty reduction to 4% for twelve months and forgiveness of a portion of the outstanding $18,400. Bethany is sympathetic, Devan trained many of Northern Roast's other franchisees informally over the years and is well respected in the franchise network, but she is also aware that Northern Roast's standard franchise agreement has no provision for royalty reduction, and that granting one informally to Devan without a documented, consistently applied policy could create a problem if other struggling franchisees find out and demand the same treatment, or if the auditor or bank asks why a receivable was written down informally outside any documented policy.

Northern Roast's franchise agreements are otherwise fairly standard for the sector, and Bethany noted that the company has generally prided itself on a collaborative relationship with its franchise network, including an annual franchisee conference and a Franchise Advisory Council made up of five elected franchisee representatives that is supposed to be consulted on decisions materially affecting existing locations. Bethany also mentioned, somewhat defensively, that the decision to open the new corporate flagship store near Devan's location was hers, made without going through Northern Roast's own internal site-approval policy that requires a minimum one-kilometre buffer from any existing franchise location unless the franchisee is consulted first, which did not happen here. She wants your help thinking through both the accounting questions and how to handle Devan fairly without setting a precedent that undermines the franchise system, and is candid that she feels partly responsible for the position Devan now finds himself in.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Franchise Agreement Terms and Status of Six New Franchisees
exhibits.content: Initial franchise fee: $45,000 ($15,000 on signing, $30,000 on store opening), non-refundable once paid. Obligations owed by Northern Roast under the fee: site selection support, two-week head office training program (must be completed before store opening), proprietary recipe and branding manual access, and initial roaster supply agreement setup. Ongoing royalty: 6% of gross sales, billed monthly. Marketing fund contribution: 2% of gross sales. Status of the six agreements signed this fiscal year: Franchisee A (Brandon, MB): signed and opened, training completed, full $45,000 received. Franchisee B (Regina, SK): signed and opened, training completed, full $45,000 received. Franchisee C (Saskatoon, SK): signing payment received ($15,000), store 70% built, training scheduled for next month, opening expected in 6 weeks. Franchisee D (Thunder Bay, ON): signing payment received ($15,000), store 40% built, training not yet scheduled. Franchisee E (Kenora, ON): signing payment received ($15,000), store 25% built. Franchisee F (Portage la Prairie, MB): signing payment received ($15,000), no site work started six months after signing, franchisee has gone quiet on communications from head office.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Devan Marchetti Royalty Account and Portage Avenue Location Financials
exhibits.content: Portage Avenue location, trailing twelve months: Gross sales $412,000 (down from $498,000 the prior year, a 17.3% decline). Royalties owed at 6%: should be $24,720 for the period; actual royalties paid: $6,320, with $18,400 currently in arrears across the last three months. Devan's estimated store-level costs (rent, labour, COGS, utilities): approximately $398,000 annualized on current sales, leaving an estimated pre-royalty operating margin of only about $14,000, before the $24,720 in royalties and 2% marketing fund contribution owed, meaning the store is effectively operating at a loss once obligations to Northern Roast are included. Distance from Devan's location to the new corporate flagship: 0.6 km. Northern Roast's internal site policy, Section 4.1: 'No new corporate or franchised location shall be approved within 1.0 km of an existing Northern Roast location without prior consultation with the affected franchisee(s) and sign-off from the Franchise Advisory Council.' No record of consultation with Devan or Council sign-off exists in the site approval file for the new flagship.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 3: New Auditor's Planning Memo Excerpt
exhibits.content: Planning memo from Northern Roast's incoming external auditor, addressed to the audit committee: 'We have identified franchise fee revenue recognition as a significant risk area for the current year audit. Based on our preliminary review, Northern Roast recognizes the full initial franchise fee upon receipt of the signing payment, regardless of whether performance obligations such as training delivery and store opening support have been substantially completed. Under ASPE, revenue from franchise fees should generally not be recognized until the franchisor has substantially performed or satisfied all material services and conditions related to the sale, which for an initial franchise fee typically does not occur until the franchisee's store has opened and initial training and support obligations have been delivered. We will be testing this area closely and it may result in a proposed adjustment.'

requirements: Assess Northern Roast's current franchise fee revenue recognition policy against ASPE and recommend the appropriate treatment for each of the six new franchise agreements, quantifying any required adjustment.

Advise Bethany on how to handle the Devan Marchetti situation, addressing both the royalty arrears and his request for a temporary reduction, in a way that is fair and does not undermine the franchise system.
Identify the governance issue raised by the new flagship store's location and recommend how Bethany should address it.

model_response.understand_issue: I am the Controller of a franchisor preparing for its first audit under a new external auditor who has already flagged the exact revenue recognition policy the company has used for years as a significant risk, right as the company needs clean audited statements to renew its bank facility. Bethany is asking me to solve a technical accounting problem with real covenant and audit consequences, a franchisee relations problem she is personally conflicted about, and a governance lapse she caused herself by approving the new flagship store outside policy. My job is to give her a defensible accounting position before the audit begins and a way to treat Devan fairly without creating an ungoverned precedent.
model_response.analyze_case:
Franchise fee revenue recognition: the auditor's memo correctly states the ASPE principle, initial franchise fee revenue should not be recognized until the franchisor has substantially performed the material services tied to the fee, generally site support, training, and store opening. Recognizing the full $45,000 at signing, when the $30,000 second instalment is not even due or paid until opening, and when training, a mandatory and material obligation, has not been delivered, does not reflect substantial performance. Applying this to the six agreements: Franchisee A and B have opened and completed training, so the full $45,000 for each is properly recognized, a total of $90,000. Franchisee C has only paid the $15,000 signing fee, training has not yet occurred and the store is not open, so none of even the $15,000 received should be recognized as revenue yet, it should be deferred revenue (a liability) until opening and training are complete, likely in about six weeks. The same logic applies to Franchisee D, E, and F, each of whom has paid $15,000 with no training completed and no store opened. That means $60,000 of signing fees received this year across C, D, E, and F (4 x $15,000) has likely already been recognized as revenue under the old policy but should instead be reclassified to deferred revenue, a liability, until the substantial-performance threshold is met. Franchisee F is a particular concern beyond timing, since no site work has started after six months and the franchisee has gone quiet, raising doubt about whether the store will open at all; if management concludes the agreement is unlikely to be completed, the $15,000 may need to be assessed for recoverability or potential refund obligation rather than simply deferred indefinitely. Overall, this points to a correction of roughly $60,000 of previously recognized revenue for the four incomplete agreements, moved to deferred revenue, which will reduce current-year revenue and net income and needs to be communicated to Assiniboine Credit Union proactively given the facility renewal is in progress, since a lender should not be surprised by a revenue restatement during a live renewal process.

Devan Marchetti: the numbers in Exhibit 2 support that Devan's concern is real, not simply underperformance; sales are down 17.3% and the store is effectively operating near breakeven or a loss once full royalty and marketing obligations are included, and the timing coincides with the new corporate flagship opening 0.6 km away, inside the 1.0 km buffer that the company's own policy is meant to protect against, and which was approved without the required consultation or Council sign-off. Bethany has a direct personal and organizational responsibility here, this was not simply market competition, it was a policy breach by leadership that materially harmed a long-standing franchisee. That context matters for how the arrears and the reduction request should be handled; treating Devan as an ordinary delinquent account without acknowledging Northern Roast's own role in the situation would be neither fair nor accurate.

Governance issue: Section 4.1 exists precisely to prevent this kind of harm and protect the trust franchisees place in the system when they invest in a location. Bypassing it, even by the CEO, undermines the credibility of the entire franchise network, especially since other franchisees will eventually learn how Devan's situation arose.
model_response.recommendation:
Northern Roast should correct its franchise fee revenue recognition policy prospectively and for the current year audit, recognizing initial fees only once training is complete and the store has opened, reclassifying the roughly $60,000 associated with Franchisees C, D, E, and F to deferred revenue, and separately assessing Franchisee F for recoverability given the lack of any progress after six months. Bethany should proactively disclose this correction to Assiniboine Credit Union as part of the facility renewal discussion rather than let it surface as an audit adjustment during the process.

On Devan, rather than an informal, one-off concession, Bethany should establish a documented hardship or franchisee-relief policy with clear, objective criteria (for example, demonstrated sales decline linked to a Northern Roast-caused factor such as a policy breach, applied consistently to any franchisee who qualifies), so that any relief given to Devan is principled and defensible rather than personal or arbitrary. Given Northern Roast's own policy breach materially contributed to Devan's situation, some accommodation, whether a temporary royalty reduction, a structured repayment plan for the $18,400 arrears, or both, is reasonably justified, but it should be documented, time-limited, and tied to specific conditions, and communicated in a way that does not invite indiscriminate claims from unrelated franchisees.

On governance, Bethany should acknowledge the Section 4.1 breach to the Franchise Advisory Council, reinforce the site-approval policy for all future locations including corporate ones, and consider whether Devan is owed a direct, documented explanation and apology as part of rebuilding trust.
model_response.conclusion: Northern Roast needs to correct its franchise fee recognition before the audit proceeds, given the auditor has already identified it as a significant risk, and needs to resolve the Devan Marchetti situation through a principled, documented policy rather than an ad hoc concession, particularly since the company's own governance failure contributed to his losses. Handling both transparently protects Northern Roast's credibility with its lender, its auditor, and its franchise network.

marking_guide.criterion: Applies the substantial performance principle under ASPE to franchise fee revenue and correctly identifies which of the six agreements qualify for recognition
Quantifies the approximately $60,000 reclassification to deferred revenue for the four incomplete agreements
Identifies Franchisee F's stalled progress as a recoverability concern distinct from the timing issue affecting the other three
Recommends proactive disclosure of the revenue correction to the lender given the active facility renewal
Uses the Portage Avenue store-level margin data to assess whether Devan's hardship claim is substantiated rather than accepting or rejecting it without analysis

marking_guide.competency: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
marking_guide.criterion: Identifies the site policy breach as a Northern Roast-caused harm to Devan, not simply normal competitive pressure, and factors this into the recommendation

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Recommends a documented, criteria-based relief policy rather than an informal one-off concession to avoid setting an ungoverned precedent
Presents the revenue correction in terms suitable for both the auditor conversation and the lender conversation
Recommends reinforcing the site-approval policy going forward, not just resolving the current instance
Recommends Bethany formally acknowledge the Section 4.1 breach to the Franchise Advisory Council rather than resolving it privately with Devan alone
Assesses Franchisee F's stalled agreement for recoverability rather than assuming deferral alone resolves the accounting question
Frames the relief policy so it can be communicated consistently across the whole franchise network without inviting indiscriminate claims

rating_guidance: A nominal-competence response identifies that some franchise fee revenue may be recognized too early but does not work through which of the six specific agreements qualify or quantify the adjustment. A reaching-competence response correctly identifies most of the four incomplete agreements needing deferral but misses the distinct recoverability concern with Franchisee F, and addresses Devan's request only as a sympathy-based judgment call rather than proposing a structured policy. A competent response accurately applies the substantial performance test to all six agreements, quantifies the correction, connects it to the lender renewal, and proposes a documented relief framework for Devan that accounts for Northern Roast's own policy breach. A response demonstrating competence with distinction does all of that while explicitly linking the governance lapse to the fairness of Devan's treatment, and addresses the forward-looking control fix needed so the site-approval policy is not bypassed again.

enabling_focus: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
Communicating
Managing Self
title: Kestrel Exploration Corp.: Capitalized Exploration Costs and a Going Concern Question
case_background:
Kestrel Exploration Corp. is a junior mineral exploration company listed on the TSX Venture Exchange, holding mineral claims in northern British Columbia targeting a copper-gold porphyry deposit near the Sitgunda property. Kestrel has never generated revenue and has no producing mine; its entire business is exploration, funded through periodic equity financings. You, CPA, are a manager at the public accounting firm engaged to audit Kestrel's financial statements for the fiscal year ended March 31. Fieldwork begins in ten days and the engagement partner, Farah Ahmadi, has asked you to work through two significant risk areas she flagged during planning before the team goes to site.

Kestrel capitalizes all exploration and evaluation expenditures on the Sitgunda property as an intangible exploration asset under the accounting policy choice permitted by the company's chosen framework, IFRS as issued for a venture-listed company, consistent with IFRS 6. At March 31, the capitalized balance is $14.6 million, built up over six years of drilling, geological consulting, and camp costs. This year, Kestrel's 2025 drill program (the tenth phase of drilling on the property) returned results that the company's Vice President of Exploration, in an internal memo to the CEO that was inadvertently included in the file Farah's team was given, described as disappointing, noting the mineralized zone appears to pinch out at depth in the areas tested this year, and that the geological model may need to be significantly revised before recommending further drilling in the current target area. Despite this, Kestrel's public news release describing the same drill results, issued two weeks after the internal memo, states the results continue to support the potential for a significant mineralized system at Sitgunda and that management remains confident in the exploration target. The CEO, Owen Bratcher, has told Farah in the planning meeting that he does not believe any impairment indicator exists and expects the full $14.6 million to remain capitalized.

Separately, Kestrel's cash position at March 31 was $410,000, against budgeted cash needs for the coming twelve months of approximately $3.1 million (mostly a planned eleventh drill phase and general and administrative costs). Kestrel has no debt and no revenue-generating operations. Management's cash flow forecast assumes a $3.5 million equity financing will close in June, two months after year end, based on a term sheet from a private placement lead investor. That term sheet, however, includes a condition precedent that the eleventh drill phase must first confirm continuity of the mineralized zone at depth in at least two additional drill holes, the same target area the VP's internal memo suggests may be geologically unsupported based on this year's results. If the financing does not close, Kestrel has no other committed source of funding and would run out of cash within approximately two months of year end.

Farah has asked you to prepare a memo addressing whether the capitalized exploration asset shows impairment indicators under IFRS 6 and how the going concern basis of preparation should be assessed and disclosed, given what the file reveals about the disconnect between the internal geological assessment and the public messaging and financing plan.

Kestrel's board consists of five directors, three of whom, including Owen, are also executives or founding shareholders, and two of whom are described in the company's public filings as independent. Farah noted in planning that Kestrel's audit committee has historically met only twice a year and has not, in prior years, requested detailed briefings on exploration results directly from technical staff, relying instead on management's summarized reporting. This is Farah's firm's second year on the Kestrel engagement, having taken over after the prior auditor resigned for reasons that were never fully documented in the predecessor's working papers made available on transition, which Farah flagged as a modest additional planning consideration but not, on its own, a reason for particular concern at the time.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Internal VP Exploration Memo (inadvertently included in audit file)
exhibits.content: Memo from Priya Nazarian, VP Exploration, to Owen Bratcher, CEO, dated March 28: 'Owen, final assays are in from all 14 holes of the 2025 program. I want to be direct with you before we talk messaging. Grades and widths in this year's holes are materially below the block model estimates we built after the 2023 program, and more importantly, holes SK-25-11 and SK-25-13, which targeted the down-dip extension we were counting on for next year's resource update, both came back essentially barren below 280 metres. This is consistent across both holes and is not what we expected. My honest geological read is that the mineralized zone appears to pinch out at depth in the areas we tested this year, and our geological model may need to be significantly revised before we'd recommend further drilling in the current target area. I know this is difficult timing given the financing discussions, but I don't think we should be telling investors the down-dip target is confirmed when the data says the opposite. Happy to walk you through the sections whenever works.'

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Public News Release Excerpt (issued two weeks after the internal memo)
exhibits.content: 'Kestrel Exploration Corp. Announces 2025 Drill Results from the Sitgunda Property. Kestrel is pleased to report results from its 2025, 14-hole drill program at Sitgunda. While results in certain holes were below block model expectations, the program continues to support the potential for a significant mineralized system at Sitgunda, and management remains confident in the exploration target. Kestrel plans a follow-up 11th phase drill program in the current fiscal year to further test the down-dip extension of mineralization. Kestrel is also pleased to report it has received a non-binding term sheet from a private placement investor for gross proceeds of up to $3.5 million, expected to close in June, subject to customary conditions.'

exhibits.title: Exhibit 3: Cash Flow Forecast and Financing Term Sheet Condition
exhibits.content: Kestrel cash on hand at March 31: $410,000. Forecast cash requirements for the twelve months following year end: Phase 11 drill program $2,100,000; general and administrative costs $780,000; property claim maintenance fees $220,000; total $3,100,000. Management's forecast assumes the $3.5 million private placement closes in June and no other financing is currently committed. Term sheet excerpt, Section 3(b), Conditions Precedent: 'Closing of the Financing is conditional upon the Company's Phase 11 drill program confirming, to the reasonable satisfaction of the Investor, continuity of the down-dip mineralized zone in at least two additional drill holes beyond the 2025 program results.' Without the financing, management estimates Kestrel's existing cash of $410,000, less committed near-term payables of approximately $95,000, would fund operations for approximately eight to nine weeks from year end.

requirements: Assess whether an impairment indicator exists for the capitalized Sitgunda exploration asset under IFRS 6 and recommend how management and the auditor should respond.
Assess Kestrel's ability to continue as a going concern and recommend the appropriate financial statement treatment and disclosure.
Identify the professional and ethical issues raised by the disconnect between the internal geological memo and the public news release, and recommend how you as the auditor should respond.

model_response.understand_issue: I am an audit manager preparing for fieldwork on a junior exploration company with a large capitalized exploration asset, a thin cash position, and financing plans conditioned on exactly the geological outcome the company's own technical expert says is now in doubt. The audience is my engagement partner, who needs a defensible risk assessment before the team is in the field, but the underlying issue is broader than accounting mechanics, it touches whether management's public disclosure is honest and whether the company can even continue operating. I need to apply IFRS 6 impairment indicators correctly, apply going concern judgment rigorously despite management's optimism, and think through my firm's professional responsibility given evidence of a potential public disclosure problem.
model_response.analyze_case:
Impairment indicators under IFRS 6: the standard requires specific impairment indicators be assessed for exploration and evaluation assets, including whether the entity's right to explore has expired or will expire without renewal, whether substantive expenditure on further exploration is neither budgeted nor planned, whether exploration has not led to the discovery of commercially viable quantities and the entity has decided to discontinue activities in the area, and whether sufficient data indicates the carrying amount is unlikely to be recovered in full through successful development or sale. The internal memo is direct evidence bearing on the third and fourth indicators: Kestrel's own VP of Exploration has concluded the down-dip target that underpins the future exploration plan appears geologically unsupported based on this year's results, and has recommended the geological model be revised before further drilling in the current target area, which is a strong signal that the carrying amount built up around that target may not be recoverable. This is precisely the kind of internal, contemporaneous technical evidence that should trigger management to perform a formal impairment assessment. Owen's assertion in the planning meeting that no impairment indicator exists is not consistent with his own VP's written technical conclusion, and cannot simply be accepted; the audit team needs to obtain and evaluate management's impairment analysis, involve a qualified auditor's expert in geology if needed, and consider whether some portion of the $14.6 million carrying value requires write-down, likely including the incremental costs specifically associated with the down-dip target area tested this year.

Going concern: Kestrel has $410,000 in cash against a $3.1 million twelve-month cash need, with no revenue and no committed financing other than a non-binding term sheet whose closing condition depends on Phase 11 confirming continuity of the exact mineralized zone the VP's own memo says appears to pinch out. This creates a circular and highly precarious funding picture: the financing needed to fund further drilling is itself conditional on a favourable drilling outcome that the company's internal technical expert already doubts based on this year's data. Without the financing, cash runs out within roughly two months of year end. These facts represent material uncertainties that cast significant doubt on Kestrel's ability to continue as a going concern, and management's forecast, built on an unqualified assumption the financing will close, does not appear to adequately reflect the risk inherent in the term sheet's own condition precedent. This needs rigorous professional skepticism given management's evident optimism bias.

Disclosure and ethics issue: the public news release, issued after the internal memo, characterizes the results as continuing to support the exploration target and expresses confidence, without disclosing that the company's own VP concluded the down-dip target appears geologically unsupported and recommended revising the model. This is a material omission in continuous disclosure that could mislead investors, including the private placement investor relying on Phase 11 results to satisfy a financing condition, and existing shareholders. This raises serious questions about management integrity that affect the overall audit risk assessment, not just the two specific balances discussed above.
model_response.recommendation:
The engagement team should require management to perform and document a formal impairment assessment of the Sitgunda exploration asset before or during fieldwork, incorporating the VP's technical findings, and should independently evaluate, with a qualified geological expert if necessary, whether a write-down of some portion of the $14.6 million carrying amount, particularly costs tied to the down-dip target, is required. Owen's verbal assurance that no indicator exists should not be accepted without independent corroboration given it directly contradicts his own VP's written conclusion.

On going concern, the team should challenge management's cash flow forecast, specifically the assumption that the $3.5 million financing will close given its condition precedent is tied to a geological outcome already in doubt, and should conclude that a material uncertainty related to going concern exists requiring appropriate disclosure in the financial statements, and likely an emphasis of matter or material uncertainty paragraph in the auditor's report, depending on the adequacy of management's disclosure.

On the disclosure disconnect, this should be escalated immediately to the engagement partner and, given it touches continuous disclosure obligations under securities law and potential public misrepresentation, likely needs to involve Kestrel's audit committee directly, with the firm considering its reporting obligations and whether this affects overall engagement risk and the integrity of representations the team can rely on from management for the remainder of the audit.
model_response.conclusion: The audit team is looking at more than a routine capitalized-asset test; the same underlying geological finding calls into question the exploration asset's carrying value, the going concern basis of the financial statements, and the integrity of a public disclosure investors and a financing counterparty are relying on. All three need to be addressed together, with heightened professional skepticism given management's demonstrated optimism bias in the face of contrary internal evidence.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly applies the IFRS 6 impairment indicators to the facts, particularly the VP memo's implications for indicators (c) and (d)
Concludes that management's assertion of no impairment indicator cannot be accepted without independent evaluation given the contradictory internal evidence
Identifies the going concern material uncertainty using the specific cash shortfall and financing condition precedent facts
Recognizes the circularity between the financing condition and the doubted geological target as heightening going concern risk beyond a simple cash runway calculation
Recommends involving a qualified expert or independent evaluation rather than relying solely on management representation
Evaluates the reliability of the private placement as a funding source given its non-binding nature and condition precedent

marking_guide.competency: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
marking_guide.criterion: Identifies the disconnect between the internal memo and the public news release as a potential misleading disclosure issue, not just an accounting technicality

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Applies professional skepticism to management's verbal assurances given they are directly contradicted by documentary evidence in the file
Presents the interconnection between the impairment, going concern, and disclosure issues clearly for the engagement partner
Recommends appropriate escalation of the disclosure issue rather than attempting to resolve it unilaterally
Recognizes the audit committee's limited historical engagement with technical exploration findings as a contributing governance weakness
Distinguishes which portion of the $14.6 million carrying amount is most directly implicated by the down-dip target findings rather than treating the whole balance uniformly

rating_guidance: A nominal-competence response notes the VP memo exists and mentions impairment or going concern in general terms without applying the specific IFRS 6 indicators or quantifying the cash shortfall against forecast needs. A reaching-competence response correctly identifies that an impairment indicator likely exists and that going concern is a risk, but treats them as two separate, unconnected findings and does not address the public disclosure issue substantively. A competent response applies the IFRS 6 criteria specifically, reaches a well-supported going concern conclusion using the cash flow and financing condition facts, and identifies the disclosure disconnect as a serious issue requiring escalation. A response demonstrating competence with distinction connects all three issues as manifestations of the same underlying fact pattern, explicitly identifies the circular logic between the financing condition and the doubted geological target, and reasons through the implications for overall audit risk and the reliability of management representations going forward, not just the current year's balances.

enabling_focus: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
Communicating
Leading
Adding Value
title: Lakeshore Boutique Hotels: Performance Metrics and a Succession Tax Question
case_background:
Lakeshore Boutique Hotels Inc. operates four independent boutique hotels in Ontario cottage country (Muskoka, Prince Edward County, Collingwood, and Haliburton), totalling 168 rooms, positioned as premium leisure destinations with average daily rates well above regional chain hotels. The company is owned entirely by its founder, Rosalind Vance, age 61, who built the business over twenty-two years and is now planning to retire and transition ownership to her son, Julian, who has worked in hotel operations for six years but only two of them at Lakeshore. You, CPA, are engaged as an external advisor to help Rosalind evaluate hotel-level performance ahead of the transition and to flag any tax issues she should raise with her lawyer.

Rosalind is concerned that the four hotels look profitable on a simple revenue-minus-expense basis but she suspects the picture is misleading because each property has very different room counts, seasonality, and cost structures, and the general managers are compensated on a bonus tied to raw net income, which she worries encourages decisions that boost this year's number at the expense of the property's condition and guest experience longer term. She specifically flagged the Haliburton property, where net income is the highest of the four hotels this year, but where Rosalind personally toured the property last month and found deferred maintenance she had not authorized, including a general manager who postponed a $180,000 roof replacement flagged as urgent by the company's facilities engineer eighteen months ago, and instead spent discretionary budget on additional weekend event marketing that boosted this year's bookings and, by extension, the general manager's bonus.

Rosalind wants a proper comparison of the four hotels using RevPAR (revenue per available room) and GOPPAR (gross operating profit per available room), standard hotel industry metrics, rather than raw net income, and wants your view on whether the deferred roof replacement should factor into how Haliburton's performance is really being judged this year.

On the tax side, Rosalind's estate plan currently has all of her Lakeshore shares passing to Julian and her daughter, Priya, equally on her death, split 50/50, even though Priya has never worked in the business and has said she wants no operational role, only investment value. Rosalind's lawyer has suggested this could be restructured now, while Rosalind is alive, using an estate freeze so that Julian receives growth-class common shares reflecting his active involvement going forward, while Priya receives fixed value preferred shares reflecting the value already built, but Rosalind is unsure how this interacts with the lifetime capital gains exemption she was told she might be able to use on qualified small business corporation shares, since Lakeshore's balance sheet includes a $2.4 million investment portfolio built up over the years from retained hotel profits, held in a separate holding company, that she has never used for hotel operations and has been advised might jeopardize the qualified small business corporation status of her operating company shares if not addressed before any sale or freeze transaction.

Rosalind wants your help identifying the true performance picture across the four hotels including how the Haliburton situation should be viewed, and wants you to flag, in plain terms she can bring to her lawyer, what the investment portfolio issue could mean for her tax planning.

Julian has been general manager of the Collingwood property for the past two years and has expressed interest in eventually overseeing all four hotels once Rosalind steps back, though he has not yet been involved in any head-office financial reporting or general manager oversight decisions. Rosalind mentioned she has not yet decided whether Julian should take over responsibility for the general manager bonus structure and facilities oversight as part of the transition, or whether she should retain some board-level involvement even after formally stepping back from daily operations, and would value your perspective on that as well once the more immediate performance and tax questions are addressed.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Hotel-Level Operating Data (current fiscal year)
exhibits.content: Muskoka: 52 rooms, revenue $4,680,000, operating expenses (excl. GM bonus and one-time items) $3,350,000, net income $1,330,000, average occupancy 71%. Prince Edward County: 38 rooms, revenue $3,120,000, operating expenses $2,240,000, net income $880,000, average occupancy 68%. Collingwood: 44 rooms, revenue $3,740,000, operating expenses $2,690,000, net income $1,050,000, average occupancy 74%. Haliburton: 34 rooms, revenue $2,890,000, operating expenses $1,780,000, net income $1,110,000, average occupancy 79% (highest of the four, up from 69% last year following the increased event marketing spend). Deferred roof replacement at Haliburton: $180,000 project, flagged as urgent by the facilities engineer's report eighteen months ago, not included in this year's operating expenses since it was postponed, not cancelled; engineer's report notes continued delay risks water damage escalating repair cost to an estimated $310,000-$400,000 if not addressed before next winter.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: GM Bonus Plan and Facilities Engineer's Note
exhibits.content: General manager bonus plan, all four properties: 10% of property-level net income, paid annually, no adjustment for deferred capital maintenance or asset condition. Facilities engineer's original memo, eighteen months ago, to the Haliburton general manager and copied to Rosalind's former operations VP (who has since left the company): 'Roof assessment at Haliburton confirms active leaking in the east wing, recommend replacement within the next 12 months to avoid structural and guest-facing water damage. Estimated cost $180,000 at current pricing.' No record exists of this memo being escalated to Rosalind directly at the time; her operations VP's departure appears to have left the item unresolved. Rosalind's note to you: 'I only found out about this because I happened to walk the property myself and saw the ceiling stains in the east wing hallway.'

exhibits.title: Exhibit 3: Lakeshore Holding Company Structure and Investment Portfolio
exhibits.content: Corporate structure: Lakeshore Boutique Hotels Inc. (the operating company, owns and runs all four hotel properties) is wholly owned by Vance Holdings Inc. (the holding company), which Rosalind wholly owns personally. Vance Holdings Inc. holds a $2.4 million portfolio of marketable securities and GICs, accumulated over twelve years from dividends paid up from the operating company, and does not conduct any hotel operations itself. Total fair market value of Vance Holdings Inc. (operating company plus investment portfolio): approximately $14.8 million. Estimate of the operating company's own fair value (hotels and directly related operating assets only): approximately $12.4 million. Lawyer's preliminary note to Rosalind: 'The qualified small business corporation share test requires that substantially all (generally interpreted as 90%) of the fair market value of the corporation's assets be used principally in an active business carried on primarily in Canada, at the time of the transaction in question. This needs to be reviewed given the investment portfolio's size relative to total company value before we proceed with any freeze or eventual sale planning.'

requirements: Calculate RevPAR and GOPPAR-style metrics for the four hotels and use them, along with the Haliburton facts, to give Rosalind a more accurate picture of comparative property performance than raw net income provides.
Advise Rosalind on the general manager bonus structure and what the Haliburton situation reveals about it.

Explain, in terms suitable for Rosalind to bring to her lawyer, how the investment portfolio in Vance Holdings Inc. could affect her qualified small business corporation planning, and recommend next steps.

model_response.understand_issue: I am an external advisor engaged by the founder-owner of a four-property hotel business who needs an objective read on operating performance ahead of a family succession, plus a plain-language translation of a technical tax issue her lawyer has raised. Rosalind's real concern is that her current bonus structure and reporting are rewarding short-term optics over the underlying health of the properties, and that she may be walking into a tax planning trap she does not fully understand. My job is to produce metrics that reveal what raw net income hides, connect that to a concrete governance recommendation, and give her an accurate but accessible explanation of the qualified small business corporation issue so she can have an informed conversation with her lawyer.
model_response.analyze_case:
Performance comparison: raw net income ranks Haliburton highest at $1,110,000, ahead of Collingwood ($1,050,000), even though Haliburton is by far the smallest property at 34 rooms. RevPAR, revenue per available room, corrects for size: dividing revenue by room count gives Muskoka $4,680,000 / 52 = approximately $90,000 per room, Prince Edward County $3,120,000 / 38 = approximately $82,105 per room, Collingwood $3,740,000 / 44 = approximately $85,000 per room, and Haliburton $2,890,000 / 34 = approximately $85,000 per room. On a net-income-per-room basis (a simplified GOPPAR proxy given the data available), Muskoka is $1,330,000 / 52 = approximately $25,577 per room, Prince Edward County $880,000 / 38 = approximately $23,158, Collingwood $1,050,000 / 44 = approximately $23,864, and Haliburton $1,110,000 / 34 = approximately $32,647, which still shows Haliburton ahead on a per-room basis. However, this figure is not comparable once the deferred $180,000 roof replacement is factored in: that cost was avoided this year, not eliminated, and the engineer's report indicates delay could increase the eventual cost to $310,000-$400,000. If even the original $180,000 is treated as a cost that should properly have been incurred this year, Haliburton's adjusted net income falls to roughly $930,000, or about $27,353 per room, which brings it much closer to Muskoka and changes its relative ranking materially, and this ignores the risk of further cost escalation and guest experience damage from continued leaking. Haliburton's occupancy jump from 69% to 79% is also explained directly by the additional event marketing spend that substituted for the roof project, meaning this year's strong headline numbers were bought in part by deferring a known, urgent liability rather than by genuine operational improvement.

GM bonus structure: paying 10% of raw net income with no adjustment for deferred capital maintenance creates a direct incentive for exactly what happened at Haliburton, a general manager rationally maximizing a bonus by deferring an urgent, previously flagged capital expense and redirecting the freed-up budget into revenue-generating marketing. This is not necessarily deliberate misconduct by the general manager acting alone, since there was a governance failure too, the engineer's warning was never escalated to Rosalind after the operations VP departed, but the bonus design made the decision financially rational regardless of intent, which is the deeper structural problem.

Qualified small business corporation issue: for Lakeshore's shares (held through Vance Holdings Inc.) to qualify, substantially all, generally 90%, of the fair market value of the corporation's assets must be used principally in an active business carried on primarily in Canada at the relevant time. Vance Holdings Inc. is valued at approximately $14.8 million in total, of which the $2.4 million investment portfolio is not used in the active hotel business at all. That portfolio represents roughly 16.2% of total value ($2.4M / $14.8M), which on its face fails the 90% active-asset test at the holding company level, meaning shares of Vance Holdings Inc. as currently structured would likely not qualify for the lifetime capital gains exemption, jeopardizing a substantial tax benefit Rosalind was hoping to use, on the order of hundreds of thousands of dollars in tax depending on the gain realized and the exemption amount available at the time of any sale or freeze.
model_response.recommendation:
