Rosalind should evaluate hotel performance using RevPAR and a properly adjusted net-income-per-room metric that reinstates deferred but necessary capital costs like the Haliburton roof, rather than raw net income, and should specifically treat this year's Haliburton results as materially overstated given the deferred maintenance and the escalating repair risk. The roof should be authorized for immediate replacement given the engineer's warning that delay could nearly double or more than double the eventual cost.

The general manager bonus plan should be redesigned across all four properties to incorporate a capital maintenance or asset-condition component, for example withholding or clawing back a portion of bonus for known, unaddressed capital items flagged by facilities assessments, so that general managers are not financially rewarded for deferring necessary investment. Rosalind should also fix the reporting gap that let the engineer's warning go unescalated for eighteen months, for example by requiring facilities reports above a dollar threshold to route directly to her or the board rather than only to the property-level operations VP role.

On the tax issue, Rosalind should tell her lawyer and accountant that the $2.4 million investment portfolio in Vance Holdings Inc. appears to put the qualified small business corporation status of her shares at real risk given it represents roughly 16% of total company value, well above the 10% non-active-asset threshold implied by the 90% test. Before any freeze or sale transaction, she should explore purifying the corporate structure, for example by distributing or moving the investment portfolio out of Vance Holdings Inc. into a separate entity she owns directly, well in advance of the transaction, since these purification steps generally need time and careful tax planning to execute without triggering unwanted tax consequences themselves.
model_response.conclusion: Lakeshore's apparent top performer this year, Haliburton, is not really outperforming once deferred maintenance and the flawed bonus incentive behind it are accounted for, and Rosalind needs to fix both the immediate roof risk and the structural bonus design before she can trust property-level comparisons. At the same time, her succession and tax planning cannot proceed safely until the investment portfolio issue in Vance Holdings Inc. is addressed, since it currently threatens to disqualify her shares from the tax relief her whole plan depends on.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly calculates RevPAR (or equivalent revenue-per-room) for all four properties
Adjusts Haliburton's net income for the deferred roof cost and recalculates a comparable per-room profitability figure
Identifies the occupancy increase at Haliburton as directly linked to the marketing spend that substituted for the roof project
Correctly identifies and applies the approximately 90% active-asset test for qualified small business corporation shares to the given fair values
Quantifies the investment portfolio's approximate percentage of total company value and concludes it likely fails the test as structured
Recommends purification steps be considered well in advance of any freeze or sale transaction
Identifies the GM bonus plan's design as the root cause enabling the deferred maintenance decision
Recommends a specific redesign of the bonus plan to incorporate asset condition or capital maintenance

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Uses case facts to distinguish genuine operational improvement from performance achieved by deferring known obligations
Explains the qualified small business corporation issue in plain, accessible terms suitable for a non-technical business owner to relay to her lawyer
Recommends a reporting/escalation fix so future facilities warnings reach ownership directly rather than only restating the current problem
Connects the operational governance finding and the tax planning finding into a coherent set of pre-succession priorities for Rosalind

rating_guidance: A nominal-competence response calculates RevPAR for the properties but does not adjust for the deferred roof cost, and mentions the investment portfolio without applying the active-asset percentage test to the specific numbers given. A reaching-competence response makes the roof adjustment but does not identify the bonus plan itself as the structural cause, and correctly flags a QSBC risk without quantifying it against the 90% threshold. A competent response recalculates Haliburton's adjusted profitability, links it explicitly to the bonus design, recommends a specific bonus redesign, and correctly quantifies the investment portfolio's roughly 16% share of total value as a likely QSBC failure requiring purification planning. A response demonstrating competence with distinction does all of that while also identifying the escalation/reporting gap that let the engineer's warning go unaddressed for eighteen months as a separate, fixable governance issue, and frames the tax and operational findings as a single coherent pre-succession priority list for Rosalind.

title: Trans-Prairie Freight Lines: A Lease-or-Buy Fleet Decision and a Driver Hours Concern
case_background:
Trans-Prairie Freight Lines Inc. is a long-haul trucking company based in Regina, Saskatchewan, operating a fleet of 62 tractors and 140 trailers hauling agricultural products, manufactured goods, and general freight across the Prairie provinces and into the northern United States. The company is privately owned by two partners, Ilya Semchenko and his sister Katya Semchenko, who together also serve as the two members of the board. You, CPA, are Trans-Prairie's Controller, reporting jointly to both partners.

Trans-Prairie's tractor fleet is aging, with an average age of 7.2 years, and Ilya, who runs daily operations, wants to replace 18 of the oldest tractors this year. He has two options in front of him. Option 1 is to purchase the tractors outright for $196,000 each ($3,528,000 total), financed through the company's existing equipment line at 7.2% over 6 years, with Trans-Prairie retaining the tractors, claiming capital cost allowance, and being responsible for maintenance after the 2-year manufacturer warranty expires. Option 2 is a 6-year full-service operating lease from the tractor manufacturer's captive finance arm, at $3,650/month per tractor ($65,700/month total, $788,400/year), which includes all scheduled maintenance and a guaranteed maximum repair cost cap, with the tractors returned to the lessor at the end of the term with no purchase option.

Katya, who oversees finance and safety compliance, is less focused on the equipment decision and more concerned about something she found while reviewing driver hours-of-service data last week. Federal and provincial regulations limit commercial drivers to 13 hours of driving within a 14-hour on-duty window, followed by a mandatory 10 consecutive hours off duty, tracked through electronic logging devices (ELDs) installed in every tractor. Katya found that for the 14 drivers assigned to Trans-Prairie's busiest lane, the Regina-to-Chicago route, the dispatch scheduling software has, for the past four months, been assigning back-to-back loads with turnaround windows that are only feasible if drivers begin pre-trip inspections and loading activities before their mandatory 10-hour off-duty period has actually elapsed, activities the drivers' informal text messages to dispatch (which Katya has copies of) suggest are being logged as 'off-duty' or 'sleeper berth' time on the ELD even though drivers are performing actual physical work. The dispatch manager responsible for the scheduling, Grant Fedoruk, reports to Ilya, not Katya, and Ilya has told Katya he thinks she is 'overreacting to a scheduling efficiency thing' and that "drivers exaggerate in text messages, it's not like we're telling them to falsify anything."

Katya is worried about both the safety risk of fatigued drivers and the regulatory and reputational risk to Trans-Prairie if a roadside inspection or an audit by the transportation regulator uncovers a pattern of hours-of-service violations enabled, even if not explicitly ordered, by the dispatch scheduling practice. She has asked you, as Controller and someone both partners trust, to help her think through the financial analysis on the tractors (since Ilya wants a decision this month) and separately to help her figure out how to raise the hours-of-service issue in a way that Ilya will actually take seriously, since her first attempt did not land.

Trans-Prairie has never had a formal driver safety committee or a documented escalation process for safety concerns raised outside the regular dispatch chain of command, something Katya has wanted to establish since she took over the safety compliance portfolio eighteen months ago but has not yet prioritized given the demands of day-to-day finance work. The company's insurance renewal, covering fleet liability and cargo, is also due in ten weeks, and Katya noted that a documented hours-of-service issue discovered by the insurer during underwriting review could affect premium pricing or coverage terms, which adds another reason to resolve the scheduling problem before it is discovered externally rather than disclosed proactively.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Lease vs. Buy Financial Data
exhibits.content: Option 1 (Purchase): Total cost $3,528,000 for 18 tractors ($196,000 each). Financed at 7.2% over 6 years through the equipment line, estimated annual debt service (principal and interest) approximately $728,000/year. CCA class 16 (specified tractors for hauling), rate 40% declining balance. Trans-Prairie's marginal tax rate: 27%. Estimated maintenance cost after the 2-year warranty period (years 3-6): approximately $14,000/tractor/year, escalating 5% annually, totalling approximately $252,000 in year 3 rising to about $291,700 by year 6 across the 18 tractors. Estimated resale value of the tractors at end of year 6: approximately $38,000/tractor ($684,000 total). Option 2 (Full-Service Lease): $65,700/month, $788,400/year, for 6 years, fully deductible as an operating expense, includes all scheduled maintenance and a repair cost cap; tractors returned to lessor at end of term, no resale value or ownership retained by Trans-Prairie. No separate financing required, no CCA claimed since Trans-Prairie does not own the assets.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Katya's Notes on Driver Hours and Dispatch Scheduling
exhibits.content: Federal Hours of Service Regulations (commercial vehicle drivers) excerpt: 'A driver shall not drive after having been on duty for 14 hours following 10 consecutive hours off duty, and shall not accumulate more than 13 hours of driving time within that window. Off-duty and sleeper berth time must accurately reflect the driver's actual activity; time spent on any work-related task, including vehicle inspection or loading, must be logged as on-duty time regardless of its duration.' Sample text message from a Regina-Chicago driver to dispatch, forwarded to Katya: 'Grant has me starting pre-trip at 5:40am again but my 10 off doesn't finish till 6:15 per the app. Same as last week. Just doing it off the clock I guess since the load has to leave by 7.' Katya's summary: pattern observed across 11 of the 14 Chicago-lane drivers over the last four months, with an average of approximately 25-40 minutes of apparent off-clock pre-trip activity per turnaround, occurring on average 2-3 times per week per driver. No formal instruction from Grant to falsify logs has been found in writing; the pattern appears to result from unrealistic turnaround scheduling rather than an explicit order to log hours falsely.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 3: Ilya's Response to Katya (email, forwarded to you)
exhibits.content: Email from Ilya to Katya, three weeks ago: 'Katya, I hear you, but I think you're overreacting to a scheduling efficiency thing. The Chicago lane is our most profitable route and Grant is just trying to keep the trucks moving. Drivers exaggerate in text messages, it's not like we're telling them to falsify anything, and nobody's gotten a ticket. Let's not slow down our best lane over a handful of text messages. If it becomes an actual problem I'll deal with it.' Trans-Prairie's most recent roadside inspection summary (provincial regulator, last 12 months): 2 inspections on Chicago-lane tractors, both passed with no hours-of-service violations noted, though neither inspection happened to occur during the specific turnaround windows described in Katya's data.

requirements: Analyze the financial and operational tradeoffs between the purchase and full-service lease options for the 18 tractors, and recommend which option Trans-Prairie should choose.
Assess the hours-of-service and dispatch scheduling issue Katya has identified, including the risk it poses to Trans-Prairie, and recommend what should be done about it.
Advise Katya on how to raise the hours-of-service issue with Ilya more effectively, given her first attempt was dismissed.

model_response.understand_issue: I am the Controller of a small, partner-owned trucking company, trusted by both siblings who jointly govern it, being asked to do two very different things at once: a standard equipment financing analysis Ilya wants resolved this month, and a much more serious regulatory compliance and safety concern Katya has raised and had dismissed. The real challenge is not the tractor math, which is important but routine; it is helping Katya escalate a genuine hours-of-service risk effectively to a co-owner who has already brushed it off once, without letting the more urgent issue get lost behind the more time-pressured equipment decision.
model_response.analyze_case:
Lease versus buy: purchasing costs $3,528,000 upfront, financed at 7.2% over 6 years at roughly $728,000/year in debt service, plus post-warranty maintenance rising from about $252,000 in year 3 to about $291,700 in year 6, offset by CCA tax shields (class 16 at 40% declining balance provides meaningful early-year deductions against Trans-Prairie's 27% tax rate) and an estimated $684,000 resale value retained at the end of year 6. The full-service lease costs a level $788,400/year for six years, fully deductible, with maintenance risk and cost variability transferred to the lessor via the repair cost cap, but Trans-Prairie retains no residual value and no CCA shield. On a purely cash basis, purchasing's annual outlay in the early years (debt service alone, before maintenance ramps up) is lower than the lease payment, but this understates the comparison since it excludes maintenance, financing risk, and ties up the equipment line's capacity that could otherwise support other growth or working capital needs, an especially relevant consideration if Trans-Prairie may want to finance additional trailers or expansion later. The lease provides more predictable, all-in costs and shields Trans-Prairie from the risk of repair costs escalating faster than the 5% annual assumption used in the purchase estimate, which is a meaningful risk on an aging-fleet replacement decision like this one, and better matches monthly cash outflow to monthly revenue generation from the tractors. Purchasing keeps more long-run value with Trans-Prairie through CCA shields and resale value, which likely wins on a net present value basis if Trans-Prairie is confident in its maintenance cost estimate and wants to preserve balance sheet flexibility for other borrowing; the lease wins on cost certainty and preserving the equipment line's borrowing capacity, which may matter more given the safety and compliance risk identified separately, since Trans-Prairie could face costs or constraints from that issue that argue for preserving financial flexibility right now.

Hours-of-service issue: the regulation in Exhibit 2 is explicit that any work-related task, including pre-trip inspection, must be logged as on-duty time regardless of duration, and the pattern Katya documented, drivers beginning pre-trip activity 25-40 minutes before their mandatory 10-hour off-duty period has elapsed, occurring 2-3 times per week across 11 of 14 drivers on the busiest lane for four months, is a systemic hours-of-service violation being effectively enabled by unrealistic dispatch turnaround scheduling, regardless of whether Grant explicitly instructed anyone to falsify logs. Ilya's response, that nobody has been ticketed and it is 'a scheduling efficiency thing,' understates the risk: the two clean roadside inspections did not occur during the specific turnaround windows in question, so they provide little real assurance, and hours-of-service violations carry both a significant regulatory and legal risk (fines, potential loss of operating authority in serious cases, and liability exposure if a fatigue-related accident occurs) and a genuine safety risk to the drivers themselves and the public. This is a control and culture issue rooted in the scheduling system, not an isolated dispatcher decision, since it is happening across the large majority of drivers on that route consistently over four months, which points to a structural turnaround-time problem in how Grant's schedules are built, not individual driver behaviour.
model_response.recommendation:
On the tractors, I recommend the full-service lease, Option 2, primarily because it caps and makes predictable a cost category, maintenance, that carries real escalation risk on an aging fleet replacement, and because it preserves the equipment line's borrowing capacity at a time when Trans-Prairie may need financial flexibility to address the more pressing compliance issue identified separately. If Trans-Prairie's maintenance cost estimates prove reliable and balance sheet capacity is not a near-term constraint, purchasing remains a reasonable alternative given the CCA shield and residual value, and Ilya and Katya should revisit the full net present value comparison with actual quotes once immediate compliance priorities are resolved.

On the hours-of-service issue, Trans-Prairie needs to treat this as a genuine compliance and safety risk requiring immediate action, not a scheduling inefficiency. The Chicago-lane turnaround windows should be reviewed and rebuilt to provide realistic, compliant buffers, Grant should be made aware that the scheduling pattern is producing apparent hours-of-service violations regardless of intent, and Trans-Prairie should consider a proactive internal audit of ELD data against dispatch records across all lanes, not just Chicago, to determine the scope of the issue before a regulator does. Given the safety and legal exposure, this should take priority over the equipment decision timeline if the two compete for management attention this month.

Katya should re-raise the issue with Ilya using the documented pattern, not the earlier informal framing, presenting the specific text message evidence, the four-month pattern across 11 of 14 drivers, and the regulatory definition that leaves no ambiguity that this constitutes a hours-of-service logging problem regardless of intent. Framing it around concrete regulatory and liability exposure, and proposing a specific, low-disruption fix (adjusted turnaround windows) rather than an open-ended concern, is more likely to get a serious response than a general safety worry, and she should consider involving me or another neutral party in presenting the data given her first attempt was dismissed personally.
model_response.conclusion: Trans-Prairie faces a routine but consequential equipment financing decision alongside a much more serious hours-of-service compliance issue that has apparently been building for four months without resolution. The two partners need to address the compliance issue with real urgency, supported by the specific evidence Katya has gathered, while the tractor decision, though real, can be resolved on sound financial grounds without the same time pressure the safety issue demands.

marking_guide.criterion: Compares the purchase and lease options including debt service, maintenance escalation, CCA shield, and residual value, not just headline monthly cost
Reaches a supported recommendation between the two options with explicit tradeoffs (cost certainty vs. retained value/flexibility)
Connects the equipment financing decision to the company's need for financial flexibility given the separate compliance risk
Identifies the maintenance cost escalation risk on an aging fleet as a key differentiator between the two options
Correctly applies the hours-of-service definition to conclude the pre-trip activity constitutes on-duty time regardless of dispatcher intent
Recommends a broader internal audit of ELD data across all lanes rather than treating the Chicago lane as an isolated incident
Correctly assesses that the two clean roadside inspections provide limited assurance given they did not occur during the relevant turnaround windows

marking_guide.competency: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
marking_guide.criterion: Treats the hours-of-service pattern as a genuine safety and compliance issue requiring urgent action, not a minor scheduling inefficiency

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Prioritizes the compliance issue's urgency appropriately relative to the equipment decision timeline
Recommends a specific, evidence-based approach for Katya to re-raise the issue rather than generic advice to 'communicate better'
Recognizes the value of a neutral third party helping present the data given the direct approach was already dismissed
Recommends Grant's dispatch scheduling process be reviewed structurally rather than treating the pattern as a personnel issue with one dispatcher

rating_guidance: A nominal-competence response compares the two tractor options on payment amount alone without addressing maintenance risk or CCA, and describes the driver hours pattern without concluding whether it actually violates the regulation. A reaching-competence response makes a reasonable equipment recommendation with some tradeoff discussion, and correctly flags the hours-of-service pattern as a likely violation but offers only generic advice on how Katya should raise it again. A competent response thoroughly compares both options including maintenance escalation and CCA shield, reaches a well-supported recommendation, correctly applies the regulatory definition to conclude a violation pattern exists regardless of intent, and gives Katya a concrete, evidence-based approach for re-raising the issue. A response demonstrating competence with distinction does all of that while connecting the two issues, for example noting the lease option better preserves financial and management flexibility for addressing the more urgent compliance problem, and recommends a broader audit of scheduling practices across all lanes rather than treating Chicago as an isolated case.

Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
Communicating

title: Fernwood Craft Brewing Co.: Inventory Costing, an Expansion Loan, and a Related-Party Supply Deal
case_background:
Fernwood Craft Brewing Co. Inc. is a craft brewery in Nelson, British Columbia, producing 14 year-round and seasonal beer styles distributed to restaurants, liquor retailers, and its own taproom. Fernwood is privately owned, with founder and CEO Marcus Whitfield holding 65% of shares and his business partner, Sana Okafor, holding the remaining 35%; Sana also serves as head brewer. You, CPA, are engaged as Fernwood's part-time controller, working two days a week, and report to Marcus.

Fernwood is seeking a $650,000 expansion loan from Kootenay Community Credit Union to add two new fermentation tanks and expand canning capacity, which would let Fernwood take on a large new grocery chain distribution contract it has been offered. The credit union requires audited financial statements (this is Fernwood's first audit; it has only had review engagements in prior years) and will apply a debt service coverage covenant of 1.35x once the loan is in place. Fernwood reports under ASPE.

Two issues have come up as you prepare for the transition to an audit. First, Fernwood values its beer inventory (raw materials, work in process across various fermentation stages, and finished goods) using a simplified standard cost that has not been updated in three years, despite malt and hop costs rising significantly due to supply chain pressures, and despite Fernwood having added several new, more hop-intensive seasonal styles that cost meaningfully more to produce than the flagship lager the standard cost was originally built around. You pulled actual production cost data for the most recent quarter and found that the standard cost understates actual production cost by an average of 22% across the product line, and by as much as 38% for the two most hop-intensive seasonal styles. This means finished goods inventory, and by extension gross margin, has been materially misstated for some time, though the exact cumulative effect has not yet been quantified.

Second, Sana mentioned to you, seemingly without realizing its significance, that Fernwood's primary hop supplier for the past two years, Kootenay Hop Traders Ltd., is 100% owned by her spouse, Dax Okafor, a fact that has never been disclosed to Marcus or documented anywhere in Fernwood's records. Fernwood purchased $284,000 of hops from Kootenay Hop Traders Ltd. in the current fiscal year, representing roughly 60% of Fernwood's total hop purchases, at prices that, based on a quick comparison you did against two other regional hop suppliers' published price lists, appear to run about 9-14% above what comparable hops cost from arm's length suppliers. Sana insists the pricing is fair because Kootenay Hop Traders provides better quality control and more reliable seasonal allocations of scarce specialty hop varieties, but there is no written agreement or documented rationale for the premium anywhere in Fernwood's files.

Marcus has asked you to help him understand what the inventory costing problem means for the numbers going into the loan application and the upcoming first audit, and separately, once you mentioned what Sana told you, asked you directly what he should do about the hop supplier relationship before the loan closes and the audit begins.

Fernwood's taproom, adjacent to the production facility, generates roughly 18% of total revenue and has grown steadily since opening four years ago, and Marcus has mentioned this growth as part of the pitch to the credit union for why the grocery chain distribution contract represents a natural next step rather than overreach. Sana has been Fernwood's head brewer since its founding and is well known in the regional craft beer community for her recipe development, which is part of why Marcus has historically deferred to her judgment on supplier relationships without much independent scrutiny, a dynamic he acknowledged to you feels different now that a bank and an outside auditor are both about to review the same records.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Standard Cost vs. Actual Cost Comparison (most recent quarter)
exhibits.content: Flagship Lager (original product the standard cost was built around): standard cost $1.42/litre, actual cost $1.51/litre, variance +6.3%. Pale Ale: standard cost $1.55/litre, actual cost $1.79/litre, variance +15.5%. Two hop-intensive seasonal IPAs (combined): standard cost average $1.68/litre, actual cost average $2.32/litre, variance +38.1%. Weighted average variance across the full product line: standard cost understates actual cost by approximately 22%. Finished goods inventory at standard cost, current quarter end: $612,000. Estimated finished goods inventory if restated at actual cost: approximately $746,600 (an increase of approximately $134,600, though note this changes the balance sheet inventory figure, not gross margin, which moves in the opposite direction since historically recorded cost of sales has been understated by a corresponding amount across all units sold during the period, not just those remaining in ending inventory).

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Kootenay Community Credit Union Loan Terms
exhibits.content: Loan amount: $650,000, 10-year amortization, 6.8% fixed. Estimated annual debt service: approximately $89,000. Financial statement requirement: audited annual statements, first audit to be for the fiscal year just ended, current review-engagement statements not accepted for the ongoing covenant relationship. Debt service coverage covenant: 1.35x, calculated as EBITDA divided by total annual debt service (existing plus new). Fernwood's most recent EBITDA under the current (understated cost, therefore overstated gross margin) reporting: $412,000. Fernwood's existing annual debt service on prior equipment loans: $118,000. Projected total annual debt service once the new loan closes: $207,000 ($118,000 existing plus $89,000 new). Current DSCR at reported EBITDA: 1.99x, well above covenant.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 3: Hop Purchase Comparison and Supplier Ownership
exhibits.content: Fernwood total hop purchases, current fiscal year: $473,000. Purchases from Kootenay Hop Traders Ltd.: $284,000 (60% of total). Purchases from two other regional suppliers (arm's length): $189,000 combined. Price comparison for three comparable hop varieties Fernwood buys from both Kootenay Hop Traders and at least one arm's length supplier: Variety A: Kootenay $18.40/kg vs. arm's length average $16.20/kg (+13.6%). Variety B: Kootenay $22.10/kg vs. arm's length average $20.15/kg (+9.7%). Variety C: Kootenay $31.50/kg vs. arm's length average $27.60/kg (+14.1%). Kootenay Hop Traders Ltd. corporate registry search: 100% owned by Dax Okafor. Dax Okafor is Sana Okafor's spouse; Sana is Fernwood's 35% shareholder and head brewer, responsible for selecting hop suppliers and negotiating pricing. No written supply agreement, board resolution, or disclosure of the relationship exists in Fernwood's corporate or accounting records.

requirements: Explain the inventory costing problem to Marcus, quantify its effect on Fernwood's reported gross margin and EBITDA, and assess what this means for the loan application and the debt service coverage covenant.
Advise Marcus on the Kootenay Hop Traders related-party issue, including the governance and disclosure steps needed before the loan closes and the audit begins.
Recommend how Fernwood should fix its inventory costing system going forward.

model_response.understand_issue: I am Fernwood's part-time controller reporting to the majority owner, who needs to understand two problems before a first-ever audit and a bank loan close: an inventory costing system that has been materially understating production costs for years, and an undisclosed related-party supply relationship involving the minority owner's spouse at above-market pricing. Marcus is relying on me to quantify the accounting problem honestly, even though it will likely reduce the EBITDA figure the bank is evaluating, and to give him clear direction on the governance problem before it surfaces during the audit rather than after.
model_response.analyze_case:
Inventory costing: the standard cost system was built around the flagship lager and has not been updated in three years despite rising input costs and a shift toward more hop-intensive seasonal styles. The variance data shows this is not a minor rounding issue, the flagship lager itself is only modestly understated at 6.3%, but the newer, more hop-intensive styles are understated by up to 38.1%, and the weighted average understatement across the product line is 22%. This means cost of sales has been understated and gross margin, and therefore EBITDA, overstated for as long as this standard cost has been in use without updates, likely multiple fiscal years given it has not changed in three years while Fernwood's product mix shifted. Restating finished goods inventory at actual cost would increase the reported inventory balance from $612,000 to roughly $746,600, an increase of $134,600, but this does not directly increase EBITDA, in fact the opposite: it confirms that cost of sales during the period was understated by a related amount as goods were sold at the lower standard cost throughout the year, meaning gross margin and EBITDA for the year need to be restated downward to reflect actual production costs, not just the year-end inventory snapshot corrected. Applying the 22% average understatement directionally to the cost of sales embedded in the $412,000 reported EBITDA suggests a meaningfully lower, though not yet precisely quantified without full unit-level data, corrected EBITDA figure. Given the DSCR covenant is calculated at 1.35x and the current reported DSCR is 1.99x, there is likely still a cushion even after correction, but it needs to be properly quantified before the loan closes, both because the incoming auditor will require inventory to be stated at actual cost under ASPE's lower of cost and net realizable value framework, and because representing an inflated EBITDA to the credit union, even unintentionally through a stale standard cost system, risks a covenant surprise or a misrepresentation concern once the audited numbers are available.

Related-party hop supplier: Kootenay Hop Traders Ltd. is wholly owned by Sana's spouse, and Sana, as the shareholder and head brewer responsible for supplier selection, has directed 60% of Fernwood's hop purchases, $284,000, to this related entity at prices running 9.7% to 14.1% above comparable arm's length pricing on the three varieties that can be directly compared. This is a related-party transaction that has never been disclosed to Marcus, the majority owner, and has no supporting documentation or independent rationale on file, only Sana's verbal assertion of superior quality control and allocation reliability, which is plausible but entirely unverified. Under ASPE, related party transactions require specific disclosure, and the incoming auditor will need to identify and evaluate this relationship as part of standard related-party procedures; discovering it during fieldwork rather than having it already identified and addressed by management would be a poor start to Fernwood's first audit and could raise broader questions about what else has not been disclosed. There is also a direct governance concern independent of the audit: Sana has a personal financial interest, through her spouse, in Fernwood continuing to pay premium pricing to this supplier, which is a conflict that should have been disclosed to Marcus and the board (informal as it is) from the start.
model_response.recommendation:
Fernwood should immediately commission a proper restatement of cost of sales and inventory using actual production costs for the current and, if practical, prior comparable periods, so the true EBITDA figure is known before the loan application proceeds and before the auditor begins fieldwork. This figure, even if lower than previously reported, should be shared transparently with Kootenay Community Credit Union rather than presenting the inflated standard-cost-based EBITDA and having it corrected later during the audit, which would be far more damaging to the lending relationship. Given the substantial covenant cushion apparent even before precise restatement (1.99x reported against a 1.35x covenant), Fernwood is likely still positioned to qualify for the loan on properly stated numbers, but this needs to be confirmed with real figures, not assumed.

On the hop supplier relationship, Marcus needs to have this formally disclosed and documented immediately: the relationship between Sana and Dax Okafor, the pricing comparison, and Sana's rationale should all be put in writing, and Fernwood's board (informal as it is between the two owners) should formally evaluate whether the pricing premium is justified by the claimed quality and allocation benefits, ideally through an independent comparison or negotiation to bring pricing closer to arm's length terms, or clear documented justification if a premium is retained. This needs to happen before the audit begins, since the auditor will identify and test this related-party relationship regardless, and it is far better for Fernwood to have already addressed it proactively than to be seen as having concealed it, which could itself raise integrity concerns for both the auditor and the lender.

Going forward, Fernwood should move to an actual or standard cost system updated at least annually, or ideally a normal costing approach that captures actual material cost inputs by product style given the significant cost variation across Fernwood's now-diverse product line, rather than a single standard cost built around one legacy product.
model_response.conclusion: Fernwood cannot take its first audit or its loan application forward on the current numbers or the current undisclosed hop supplier arrangement without real risk of an unpleasant surprise mid-process. Correcting the inventory costing now, disclosing the related-party relationship proactively, and rebuilding the costing system for the future gives Marcus a credible, defensible position with both the auditor and the credit union, and protects the integrity of the ownership relationship between him and Sana.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly explains that the standard cost understatement affects cost of sales/EBITDA for the period, not just the ending inventory balance
Uses the variance data (6.3% to 38.1%, 22% weighted average) to support the conclusion that gross margin has been materially overstated
Identifies the related-party hop purchases as requiring disclosure under ASPE and anticipates auditor testing of the relationship
Applies the DSCR covenant correctly and reasons about whether the likely restated EBITDA still supports loan qualification
Recommends proactive transparency with the lender about the restated figures rather than allowing the correction to surface only during the audit
Recognizes the related-party pricing premium could also carry tax implications (e.g., reasonableness of expenses, potential shareholder benefit considerations) worth flagging for further review

marking_guide.competency: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
marking_guide.criterion: Identifies the undisclosed relationship and pricing premium as a genuine conflict of interest requiring disclosure, not simply accepting Sana's quality justification at face value

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Quantifies the hop pricing premium using the comparable data provided rather than relying on Sana's unverified assertion
Explains the inventory costing issue to Marcus in terms that connect the technical correction to its practical effect on the loan application
Recommends a durable fix to the costing system (updated standard costs or normal costing by product style) rather than only a one-time correction
Recommends the informal board (Marcus and Sana) formally evaluate and document the hop pricing rationale rather than accept Sana's verbal explanation alone
Recognizes the controller's role is to surface and quantify both issues objectively for Marcus rather than resolve the related-party conflict personally

rating_guidance: A nominal-competence response notes that the standard cost is outdated and inventory is understated but does not correctly explain that cost of sales for the whole period, not just ending inventory, is also misstated, and mentions the hop supplier relationship without quantifying the pricing premium. A reaching-competence response calculates the inventory adjustment reasonably but does not fully connect it to the EBITDA and covenant implications, and identifies the related-party issue but recommends only generic disclosure without proposing how to evaluate whether the pricing premium is justified. A competent response correctly explains the period-wide gross margin impact, connects it to the DSCR covenant analysis, quantifies the hop pricing premium from the comparable data, and recommends formal disclosure and evaluation before the audit begins. A response demonstrating competence with distinction does all of that while also recommending proactive transparency with the lender ahead of the audit rather than letting the correction surface as an audit adjustment, and proposes a durable, forward-looking fix to the costing system tailored to Fernwood's now-diversified product line.

Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
