title: Riverbend Community Health Foundation: Restricted Fund Reporting and an Auditor Independence Question
case_background:
Riverbend Community Health Foundation is a registered Canadian charity based in Thunder Bay, Ontario, that raises funds to support equipment purchases, patient support programs, and a mental health outreach initiative at Riverbend Regional Hospital. The Foundation is governed by a volunteer board of 11 directors and operates with a staff of 6, led by Executive Director Colette Fabbri. You, CPA, sit on the Foundation's Finance and Audit Committee as a volunteer director, a role you took on partly because of your professional background, and partly because a close friend's father was treated at Riverbend Regional Hospital's cardiac unit, funded in part by a past Foundation campaign.

The Foundation uses restricted fund accounting under ASNPO (Accounting Standards for Not-for-Profit Organizations) to report separately on its General Operating Fund, its Mental Health Outreach Fund (externally restricted by donors specifically for the mental health program), and its Capital Equipment Fund (externally restricted for hospital equipment purchases). This year, the committee chair asked you to review a matter Colette flagged: the Mental Health Outreach Fund shows a cumulative surplus of $340,000 that has built up over three years because the mental health program has consistently spent less than budgeted, while the General Operating Fund, which covers the Foundation's own administrative costs and fundraising expenses, ran a deficit of $95,000 this year and is projected to run out of unrestricted reserves within about eight months if the trend continues. Colette has proposed transferring $150,000 from the Mental Health Outreach Fund's surplus to the General Operating Fund to cover a shortfall in fundraising staff salaries, arguing that 'the mental health program benefits from having a functioning Foundation behind it, so this is a reasonable allocation of overhead,' and that no individual donor has ever complained about how their gift was used.

Separately, and more urgently, you learned last week, in your capacity as a director rather than through any professional engagement, that the Foundation's external auditor, Voskuijlen & Partners LLP, which has audited the Foundation for the past nine years, recently engaged Colette's spouse, Renaud Fabbri, as a part-time bookkeeper for the audit firm's own internal accounting function, a role unrelated to the Foundation audit itself but within the same firm that signs the Foundation's audit opinion. This arrangement was not disclosed to the board or the Finance and Audit Committee before it began, and you learned of it only because Renaud mentioned it casually to your spouse at a social event.

The committee chair has asked you to prepare a memo for the next Finance and Audit Committee meeting addressing whether the proposed fund transfer is appropriate, and, given what you have learned, whether there is an auditor independence concern the committee needs to address before Voskuijlen & Partners LLP begins this year's audit fieldwork, which is scheduled to start in three weeks.

Riverbend Community Health Foundation was established eighteen years ago and has grown its annual fundraising from under $200,000 to approximately $2.1 million today, funding several major hospital equipment purchases and, more recently, the Mental Health Outreach Program launched three years ago in response to a documented gap in regional youth mental health services. The Foundation's bylaws require an annual audited financial statement to be presented at the Foundation's public annual general meeting, attended by donors, hospital staff, and community members, and Colette has emphasized in past board communications how important donor trust is to the Foundation's continued growth, since a large share of annual giving comes from repeat donors who specify program-level restrictions on their gifts rather than giving to general operations, a pattern the Foundation's own donor stewardship materials actively encourage by highlighting specific program outcomes rather than the Foundation's overall operating needs.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Fund Balances and Donor Restriction Language
exhibits.content: Mental Health Outreach Fund: cumulative surplus $340,000, built from three years of actual spending below approved program budget (program spent $410,000 against a $750,000 budget over the three-year period combined). General Operating Fund: current-year deficit $95,000; unrestricted net assets remaining $63,000, projected to be exhausted within approximately 8 months at the current burn rate absent additional revenue or cost reduction. Capital Equipment Fund: balance $212,000, fully committed to a defibrillator replacement project already approved by the hospital foundation liaison committee. Donor restriction language, standard gift agreement used for Mental Health Outreach Fund donors: 'This gift is restricted exclusively to the direct costs of Riverbend Regional Hospital's Mental Health Outreach Program, including counsellor salaries, program materials, and program-specific outreach activities. Funds may not be used for the Foundation's general administrative or fundraising costs.'

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Colette's Proposal Email
exhibits.content: Email from Colette Fabbri to the Finance and Audit Committee chair: 'I'd like the committee to consider approving a $150,000 transfer from the Mental Health Outreach Fund surplus to General Operating to cover our fundraising staff shortfall this year. The mental health program benefits from having a functioning Foundation behind it, so I see this as a reasonable allocation of overhead that the program should bear its share of. We have never had a donor complain about how their gift was used, and the program still has plenty of surplus left even after the transfer. Without this, I don't know how we keep the fundraising team fully staffed through the rest of the year, which honestly puts all three funds at risk long term since fundraising is what replenishes everything.'

exhibits.title: Exhibit 3: Auditor Engagement and Independence Background
exhibits.content: Voskuijlen & Partners LLP has served as Riverbend Community Health Foundation's external auditor for nine consecutive years, providing an annual audit opinion required under the Foundation's bylaws and as a condition of certain major grant funding. The firm's engagement letter, standard language: 'The firm maintains independence in accordance with the CPA Code of Professional Conduct throughout the period of the engagement and the period covered by the financial statements.' Renaud Fabbri's role, as described informally: part-time bookkeeper (approximately 10 hours/week) supporting Voskuijlen & Partners LLP's own internal accounts payable and payroll function, a role with no direct involvement in any client engagement, including the Riverbend engagement. Renaud is married to Colette Fabbri, the Foundation's Executive Director, who is a member of Foundation management with primary responsibility for the information and representations provided to the auditors during the Riverbend engagement. No disclosure of this arrangement has been made to the Finance and Audit Committee or the board by either Voskuijlen & Partners LLP or Colette.

requirements: Assess whether Colette's proposed $150,000 transfer from the Mental Health Outreach Fund to the General Operating Fund is appropriate under ASNPO restricted fund accounting principles, and recommend an alternative approach to the General Operating Fund's shortfall if the transfer is not appropriate.

Assess whether Renaud Fabbri's employment at Voskuijlen & Partners LLP creates an auditor independence concern, and recommend what the Finance and Audit Committee should do about it before fieldwork begins.

As a director who learned of the auditor relationship informally and who also has a personal connection to a past Foundation-funded program, reflect on how you should handle bringing this forward given your own position.

model_response.understand_issue: I am a volunteer director on the Finance and Audit Committee of a charitable foundation, asked to prepare a memo covering a restricted fund transfer proposal from the Executive Director and a separate, more serious auditor independence issue I learned about socially rather than through any board process. My audience is the Finance and Audit Committee, which needs a technically sound answer on the fund transfer under donor restriction rules, but the auditor independence issue is the more urgent matter given fieldwork starts in three weeks, and I need to think carefully about my own role in raising it given how I learned of it and my personal connection to the Foundation's work.
model_response.analyze_case:
Fund transfer proposal: the donor restriction language for the Mental Health Outreach Fund is explicit and unambiguous, gifts are restricted exclusively to direct program costs and may not be used for the Foundation's general administrative or fundraising costs. Colette's proposed $150,000 transfer would use externally restricted donor funds to cover fundraising staff salaries, which is precisely the use the restriction language prohibits. Under ASNPO, externally restricted contributions must be accounted for and used in accordance with the restrictions placed on them by the donor; a foundation cannot unilaterally redirect restricted funds to cover unrelated operating costs simply because no donor has complained, since the absence of a complaint does not create authority to override a written restriction, and donors gave with a specific, documented expectation. This would misstate the financial statements if recorded as an internal transfer without donor consent and would represent a breach of fiduciary duty to the donors who restricted those funds, exposing the Foundation to reputational harm and possibly regulatory scrutiny from the Canada Revenue Agency's charities directorate if the misuse of restricted donations came to light. The surplus itself likely also needs review, three years of consistent material underspending against budget, $410,000 actual against $750,000 budgeted, may itself need explanation to donors or a decision about whether to expand the program's scope, seek donor consent to reallocate for a related purpose within the mental health mandate, or communicate the surplus transparently, but none of that authorizes redirecting the funds to unrelated general operating costs.

The General Operating Fund shortfall is a real and separate problem that needs its own solution: options include a dedicated administrative or operations fundraising campcampaign explicitly seeking unrestricted gifts, cost reduction in the fundraising function, seeking board-approved emergency unrestricted funding, or exploring whether a modest, clearly disclosed and donor-consented administrative fee could be built into future restricted gift agreements, none of which involve simply reallocating existing restricted surplus without authorization.

Auditor independence: the CPA Code of Professional Conduct requires that a firm and its personnel be independent, in fact and appearance, of an audit client, and this extends to close family relationships between firm personnel and client management with significant influence over financial information provided to the auditor. Here, Renaud Fabbri, though not directly on the Riverbend engagement, is employed by the same firm that signs the Foundation's audit opinion, and is married to Colette Fabbri, the Executive Director, a member of Foundation management directly responsible for financial information and representations to the auditor. Even though Renaud's role is confined to the firm's internal accounts and not the client engagement itself, this is the kind of close family relationship between firm personnel and client management that creates, at minimum, a significant threat to independence in appearance, and firm policies and the CPA Code generally require such relationships to be identified, evaluated, and where necessary addressed through safeguards, such as excluding Renaud from any proximity to the engagement, additional independent review of the engagement, or in some cases declining to continue the relationship. The fact that this was never disclosed to the committee compounds the concern, since it suggests either the firm's own independence evaluation process failed to catch it or the firm did not consider it disclosure-worthy, neither of which is reassuring given fieldwork begins in three weeks.
model_response.recommendation:
The committee should decline Colette's proposed $150,000 transfer as presented, since it would misuse externally restricted donor funds contrary to the explicit gift agreement language, and should direct Colette to bring forward alternative solutions to the General Operating Fund shortfall that do not involve unauthorized use of restricted funds, such as a targeted unrestricted fundraising appeal or a cost review of the fundraising function. The Mental Health Outreach Fund's accumulated surplus should be reviewed separately, potentially through renewed donor communication about program scope or options to expand the program within its restricted purpose, but any use of those funds must remain within the donor restriction.

On the auditor relationship, the committee should raise this directly and promptly with Voskuijlen & Partners LLP's engagement partner before fieldwork begins in three weeks, requesting a formal explanation of how the firm's independence policies addressed, or failed to address, this relationship, and confirmation of what safeguards are in place. Depending on the firm's response, the committee should consider whether the safeguards are adequate or whether the relationship represents an unremediated independence threat requiring the firm to withdraw from this year's engagement, particularly given the nine-year tenure already raises separate familiarity considerations worth reviewing. This should not wait until fieldwork begins.

On my own role, I should bring this forward promptly and formally to the full committee rather than informally, documenting how I learned of it, and should recognize that my personal connection to a past Foundation-funded program does not create a conflict that prevents me from raising a legitimate governance concern, but I should be transparent about that connection if it is ever relevant to the discussion, and should let the committee, not me alone, decide the ultimate course of action regarding the auditor relationship.
model_response.conclusion: Riverbend's Finance and Audit Committee faces a restricted fund proposal that would breach donor trust and ASNPO principles if approved as presented, and a genuine, currently undisclosed auditor independence concern that needs to be resolved before fieldwork begins in three weeks. Both issues call for the committee to act on principle over convenience, protecting donor intent on one side and audit integrity on the other.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly applies ASNPO restricted fund principles to conclude the proposed transfer violates the donor restriction
Identifies the three-year underspending pattern in the Mental Health Outreach Fund as a separate issue requiring donor communication, distinct from authorizing the transfer
Correctly identifies the close family relationship between firm personnel and client management as an independence threat requiring evaluation under the CPA Code
Recommends the committee proactively engage the engagement partner for an explanation and evidence of safeguards before fieldwork begins
Considers whether the firm's nine-year tenure adds a compounding familiarity consideration worth separate review
Proposes a credible alternative path for the General Operating Fund shortfall that does not rely on unauthorized restricted fund use

marking_guide.competency: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
marking_guide.criterion: Firmly concludes the transfer as proposed should not be approved despite the operational pressure it would relieve

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Separates the fund transfer issue from the auditor independence issue with distinct, appropriately prioritized analysis given the fieldwork timeline
Presents both issues in a way appropriate for a volunteer board committee audience
Recommends bringing the informally learned information to the full committee formally and promptly rather than letting it sit or handling it alone
Reflects candidly on the candidate's own personal connection to the Foundation's work and concludes it does not disqualify raising the concern, while committing to transparency about it
Recommends the committee, not the candidate alone, ultimately decide on the auditor relationship response

rating_guidance: A nominal-competence response notes the transfer might not be allowed because of the donor restriction but does not explain why under ASNPO, and mentions the auditor's spouse works at the audit firm without concluding whether this actually creates an independence problem. A reaching-competence response correctly rejects the transfer citing the restriction language and identifies the auditor relationship as a possible concern, but does not propose a concrete alternative for the operating shortfall or a specific next step for addressing the auditor issue before fieldwork. A competent response applies ASNPO principles clearly to reject the transfer, proposes a credible alternative funding path, correctly analyzes the independence threat under the CPA Code, and recommends the committee engage the audit firm directly before fieldwork begins. A response demonstrating competence with distinction does all of that while also addressing the three-year underspending pattern as its own governance issue meriting donor communication, considers the compounding effect of the firm's long tenure, and thoughtfully reflects on the candidate's own position and obligations as a director who learned sensitive information informally.

enabling_focus: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
Communicating
Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
Adding Value
title: Solstice Solar Installations Inc.: Revenue Recognition Over Time and the Clean Technology Tax Credit
case_background:
Solstice Solar Installations Inc. designs and installs commercial rooftop and ground-mount solar power systems across Alberta, primarily for warehouses, agricultural operations, and light industrial customers. Contracts typically range from $180,000 to $1.4 million and take four to nine months to complete from signed contract to system commissioning. You, CPA, are Solstice's newly promoted Finance Manager, reporting to the CFO, Whitney Lachance. Solstice is privately owned by three investors and reports under ASPE, though the company is considering a transition to IFRS within the next two years in anticipation of a possible future public listing, which has made Whitney more attentive to getting revenue recognition right now rather than having to restate later.

Currently, Solstice recognizes revenue on each installation contract using the completed contract method, recording no revenue until the system passes final commissioning and is handed over to the customer, even though contracts typically span two to three fiscal quarters and Solstice invoices customers progressively at defined milestones (25% on signing, 35% at equipment delivery, 25% at substantial completion of installation, 15% at commissioning). Whitney has asked you to review whether completed contract remains appropriate, since she has noticed that Solstice's reported revenue and profit swing significantly between quarters depending on how many large projects happen to reach commissioning in a given quarter, which does not reflect the steady pace of actual work being performed, and she is concerned this could look problematic to a future IFRS auditor or public market investor even though it has not caused any covenant issues yet under Solstice's current bank facility.

Separately, and more time-sensitive, Solstice's largest contract this year, a $1.4 million, 620-panel installation for a large poultry processing facility, was structured with the customer specifically to allow the customer to claim the federal Clean Technology Investment Tax Credit, a refundable credit equal to 30% of the capital cost of eligible clean technology property, including solar photovoltaic equipment, provided specific conditions are met, including that the property must be acquired and become available for use by the claiming taxpayer. Solstice's sales team, eager to close the deal before the customer's fiscal year end, promised the customer in a signed letter that the system would be 'available for use no later than March 15,' to ensure the customer could claim the credit in its current tax year, while the actual, more realistic project schedule Solstice's own operations team has built internally shows commissioning is not expected until approximately April 28, six weeks after the promised date, due to a documented equipment delivery delay from Solstice's inverter supplier that the sales team was aware of before signing the letter to the customer.

Whitney has asked you to (1) assess whether Solstice should move to percentage-of-completion revenue recognition for its installation contracts, and (2) address the poultry facility timeline problem, since the customer is now relying on the March 15 date for its own tax filing position and the sales team's commitment does not match Solstice's actual internal schedule.

Solstice was founded six years ago and has grown from a two-person operation to a company of 34 employees, including its own in-house engineering and installation crews rather than relying on subcontractors, which Whitney considers a competitive advantage for schedule reliability, making the poultry facility situation especially frustrating to her given it resulted from a sales commitment rather than an actual field execution failure. Solstice's three investors, two of whom sit on a quarterly advisory board rather than a formal board of directors, have been pressing management for more predictable quarterly reporting ahead of any future capital raise, which is part of why Whitney raised the revenue recognition question now rather than waiting until closer to an actual IFRS transition.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Contract Structure and Recent Project Data
exhibits.content: Standard Solstice contract terms: fixed-price, milestone billing (25% signing / 35% equipment delivery / 25% substantial completion / 15% commissioning), typical duration 4-9 months, detailed engineering specifications agreed at signing with defined scope of work and change order process for scope changes. Current accounting policy: completed contract method, no revenue recognized until commissioning. Sample of 5 contracts completed this fiscal year showing quarterly revenue distortion: Q1 revenue $340,000 (1 small project commissioned), Q2 revenue $1,890,000 (2 large projects commissioned), Q3 revenue $0 (no commissioning), Q4 revenue $1,650,000 (1 large project plus 1 medium project commissioned), despite Solstice's field crews working at a relatively steady pace of 6-9 active projects across all quarters. Solstice's project costing system tracks actual costs incurred to date against total budgeted contract cost for each project with reasonable reliability, used internally for job costing and crew scheduling even though not currently used for revenue recognition.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Clean Technology Investment Tax Credit Rules (excerpt, as summarized by Solstice's tax advisor)
exhibits.content: 'The federal Clean Technology Investment Tax Credit provides a refundable credit equal to 30% of the capital cost of eligible clean technology property, including solar photovoltaic equipment, acquired by a taxable Canadian corporation. To claim the credit for a given taxation year, the property must have become available for use by the taxpayer in that year, determined under the same general available-for-use rules applicable elsewhere in the Income Tax Act, generally the time at which the property is first used by the taxpayer for the purpose of earning income, or, for a solar installation, generally when the system is functionally complete and capable of generating electricity for the taxpayer's use, consistent with commissioning. A property that becomes available for use after a taxpayer's year end cannot support a credit claim for the prior year even if substantially complete, subject to limited exceptions not applicable here.'

exhibits.title: Exhibit 3: Internal Schedule vs. Sales Team Commitment Letter
exhibits.content: Solstice operations team's internal project schedule for the poultry facility contract, prepared using the actual delivery date confirmed by the inverter supplier: equipment delivery revised to February 20 (originally scheduled for January 10), installation substantially complete by April 10, commissioning and available for use by April 28. Email from Solstice's inverter supplier to the Solstice project manager, dated six weeks before the sales team's letter to the customer: 'Unfortunately we need to push your allocation back, current lead time for your inverter model has extended to 14 weeks due to a component shortage, revised delivery now February 20 at the earliest.' Signed letter from Solstice's VP Sales to the poultry facility customer, dated after the supplier's delay email: 'We are pleased to confirm your system will be available for use no later than March 15, allowing you to claim applicable tax credits in your current fiscal year.' Customer's fiscal year end: March 31. Customer's finance team has indicated they are relying on the March 15 date to support their Clean Technology Investment Tax Credit claim on their current-year tax return.

requirements: Assess whether Solstice should adopt percentage-of-completion revenue recognition for its installation contracts and explain how this would change the reported revenue pattern shown in Exhibit 1.

Assess the poultry facility timeline problem, including the risk to the customer's tax credit claim, and recommend how Solstice should handle it, including any obligation to correct the record with the customer.
Identify the internal control or process weakness this situation reveals and recommend a fix.

model_response.understand_issue: I am the newly promoted Finance Manager at a solar installation company being asked by the CFO to modernize revenue recognition ahead of a possible future IFRS transition, and separately to deal with a live problem where the sales team made a written, dated commitment to a customer that contradicts Solstice's own internal schedule and puts the customer's tax credit claim at real risk. The revenue recognition question is a technical policy improvement; the poultry facility issue is more urgent and carries integrity implications, since Solstice has essentially given the customer information the sales team had reason to know was inaccurate at the time.
model_response.analyze_case:
Revenue recognition: under ASPE, the percentage-of-completion method is appropriate when the outcome of a long-term contract can be estimated reliably, and completed contract is used only when reliable estimation is not possible. Solstice's contracts have defined engineering specifications, fixed pricing, a documented change order process, and a project costing system that reliably tracks actual cost against budgeted cost per project, all of which indicate reliable estimation is achievable. The current completed contract method is producing exactly the kind of distortion Whitney has observed, revenue swinging from $0 in Q3 to $1,890,000 in Q2 despite a steady underlying pace of 6-9 active projects each quarter, which does not represent Solstice's actual operating performance and would look even more problematic under IFRS 15's percentage-of-completion-style requirements for contracts where control transfers over time, which is highly likely to apply to Solstice's custom, site-specific installations. Moving to percentage-of-completion, using costs incurred to date over total estimated contract cost as the measure of progress given the existing costing system supports this reliably, would recognize revenue and margin steadily as work is performed across all active projects each quarter, smoothing the reported results to reflect the actual steady pace of field activity rather than the timing of milestone completions, and would position Solstice well ahead of any future IFRS transition, avoiding a disruptive restatement later.

Poultry facility timeline: this is a serious, time-sensitive problem. The inverter supplier's email confirms Solstice's project manager knew, six weeks before the VP Sales letter was sent, that the realistic delivery date had slipped to February 20, pushing commissioning to approximately April 28. The VP Sales nonetheless wrote to the customer confirming availability for use no later than March 15, specifically to support the customer's tax credit claim, apparently without correcting for the known delay. Under the Clean Technology Investment Tax Credit rules, the property must actually become available for use, generally at commissioning, within the taxpayer's claiming year; a promise from the installer does not change the real facts the CRA would examine, and if the system is not actually commissioned until April 28, after the customer's March 31 fiscal year end, the customer's claim for the credit in its current tax year would not be supportable and could be denied or reassessed later, potentially with penalties, and the customer is currently relying on Solstice's incorrect commitment to file its own tax return. Solstice bears real responsibility here: it made a specific written representation it had reason to know was false at the time it was made, and allowing the customer to proceed with a tax filing based on that representation without correction would compound the harm and raise serious integrity concerns for Solstice, independent of any contractual or reputational consequences that may follow once the customer discovers the actual schedule.
model_response.recommendation:
Solstice should move to percentage-of-completion revenue recognition, using costs incurred to date as a percentage of total estimated contract costs as the basis for measuring progress, supported by the existing project costing system, and should apply this prospectively with appropriate disclosure of the accounting policy change, positioning the company well for its anticipated IFRS transition and giving Whitney and the investors a much more accurate quarter-to-quarter view of performance than the current completed contract method provides.

On the poultry facility, Solstice must correct the record with the customer immediately, not wait until closer to March 15 or hope the schedule somehow accelerates. Whitney and Solstice's leadership should notify the customer in writing as soon as possible that the realistic commissioning date is approximately April 28, explain the inverter delivery delay, and make clear that the March 15 commitment cannot be met, so the customer has the maximum possible time to adjust its own tax filing position, seek advice from its own tax advisor, or explore whether any exception might apply, rather than discovering the shortfall close to or after its own year end. Internally, Solstice should review why its VP Sales made a written commitment that contradicted information already known to the operations team, and address this directly with the sales team as a serious lapse, since making written commitments to customers that the company has reason to know are inaccurate, especially where a customer's tax position depends on it, is a significant integrity failure regardless of sales pressure to close the deal.

The company should also consider whether any other active contracts have similar sales-versus-operations schedule commitment gaps that need to be reviewed proactively before they become the same kind of problem.
model_response.conclusion: Solstice has a legitimate opportunity to improve its revenue recognition to better reflect actual performance and prepare for a future IFRS transition, but the more urgent priority is correcting the poultry facility customer's understanding of the real project timeline before its tax filing position is finalized on inaccurate information Solstice itself provided. Addressing the sales-operations communication gap that allowed this to happen is essential to prevent it from recurring.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly identifies that Solstice's contracts meet the criteria for percentage-of-completion (reliable cost estimation, defined scope) rather than completed contract
Explains how percentage-of-completion would smooth the quarterly revenue pattern shown in Exhibit 1 using the existing project costing data as the basis of measurement
Connects the recommendation to the anticipated future IFRS transition as a proactive benefit
Correctly applies the available-for-use/commissioning concept to conclude the customer's tax credit claim is at risk given the April 28 realistic date falls after the March 31 year end
Recognizes the customer, not Solstice, ultimately bears the tax filing risk, but that Solstice created the reliance issue
Does not treat the timeline issue as purely a scheduling problem but recognizes the financial and legal exposure it creates for both parties

marking_guide.competency: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
marking_guide.criterion: Concludes Solstice has an obligation to correct the record with the customer immediately given the sales team had actual knowledge of the delay before making the commitment

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Uses the specific dated evidence (supplier email predating the sales letter) to establish that the sales team's commitment was made with knowledge of the conflicting schedule
Recommends prompt, direct written communication to the customer rather than delay or a vague caution
Recommends a broader review of other active contracts for similar sales-versus-operations commitment gaps
Recommends internal accountability for the VP Sales given the written commitment contradicted known operational information
Recommends the accounting policy change be adopted prospectively with appropriate disclosure rather than an undocumented informal shift

rating_guidance: A nominal-competence response suggests percentage-of-completion might be better without explaining why Solstice's contracts qualify, and notes the poultry facility date does not match without concluding on the tax credit risk or recommending correction. A reaching-competence response supports the accounting policy change with general reasoning and flags the timeline mismatch as a customer service issue without fully connecting it to the available-for-use tax rule or the sales team's prior knowledge of the delay. A competent response justifies percentage-of-completion using the specific reliable-estimation criteria met by Solstice's contracts, explains the smoothing effect using the Exhibit 1 data, correctly applies the available-for-use concept to conclude the tax credit claim is at risk, and recommends immediate correction of the record with the customer. A response demonstrating competence with distinction does all of that while explicitly using the dated supplier email to establish that the sales team's commitment was made with actual knowledge of the conflicting schedule, treating this as a genuine integrity failure requiring internal accountability, and recommending a broader proactive review of other contracts for the same risk.

enabling_focus: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
Communicating
title: Northgate Fresh Markets Inc.: Inventory Valuation and Year-End Cutoff Risk
case_background:
You, CPA, are the audit senior on the engagement of Northgate Fresh Markets Inc. (Northgate or the Company), a privately held grocery and general merchandise retail chain operating 34 stores across Ontario and Manitoba. Northgate prepares its financial statements in accordance with Accounting Standards for Private Enterprises (ASPE) and is audited annually because its senior lender, Elm Valley Commercial Bank (the Bank), requires audited financial statements as a condition of a $28 million revolving operating credit facility and a $12 million term loan used to finance store renovations. The engagement partner, Priya Nathan, CPA, has asked you to complete fieldwork on inventory and revenue cutoff for the year ended December 31, 2025, and to draft a memo addressing the issues you identify, along with your recommended audit response, for her review before the file is finalized.

Northgate's credit agreement with the Bank contains a financial covenant requiring the Company to maintain a minimum current ratio of 1.40 to 1, tested at each fiscal year end based on the audited financial statements. A covenant breach gives the Bank the right to demand immediate repayment of amounts outstanding under the operating facility, currently drawn to $9.1 million, or to renegotiate pricing and terms, which the CFO, Grant Osei, has told you would be extremely damaging given the Company's ongoing renovation program. This is the first year in three that the covenant calculation has come this close to the threshold, and you know from discussions with Priya that maintaining lender confidence is a top priority for the ownership group, the Osei family, who hold 100 percent of the Company's shares.

Northgate's draft financial statements show revenue of $186.4 million for fiscal 2025, up modestly from $179.2 million in 2024. However, gross margin has declined from 32.1 percent in 2024 to 27.4 percent in 2025, a drop management attributes to competitive pricing actions taken in response to a new discount grocery entrant in the Company's core Ontario markets. You are skeptical that pricing actions alone explain a decline of this magnitude and want to consider whether inventory that should have been written down to net realizable value is instead still being carried at cost.

Inventory is a significant balance: the draft trial balance shows inventory of $14.2 million at December 31, 2025, net of the recorded allowance for obsolescence, compared to $12.6 million in 2024, representing roughly 69 percent of current assets and the single largest current asset on the balance sheet. Northgate carries inventory across three broad categories: perishable food, shelf-stable grocery, and general merchandise, which includes seasonal goods, housewares, and small electronics. General merchandise has historically been the slowest-moving and highest-risk category for obsolescence, since unsold seasonal stock has limited resale value once the relevant season passes.

Management's current allowance for inventory obsolescence, recorded as a contra-asset against inventory, is $180,000, essentially unchanged from $175,000 last year despite the gross inventory balance growing by $1.6 million and the aging profile of general merchandise inventory deteriorating markedly, as set out in the aging report you obtained from the Company's inventory management system (Exhibit 2). You noted that $1.9 million of inventory, almost entirely general merchandise, has been on hand for more than 180 days, up from $0.7 million at the prior year end.

Separately, while performing cutoff testing on the December sales detail, you identified a single large sale recorded on December 29, 2025 to a customer called Coastal Buying Group, a wholesale distributor with no prior transaction history in Northgate's customer master file. The invoice, for $1.2 million, relates to a bulk sale of general merchandise inventory and carries payment terms of net 90 days, compared to Northgate's standard wholesale terms of net 30, and includes a clause granting Coastal Buying Group the right to return any unsold portion of the goods within 120 days of delivery. Shipping records indicate the goods left Northgate's central distribution centre on December 31, 2025, the last business day of the fiscal year. Grant Osei described the transaction to you as a great opportunity to move some slow stock and start a new wholesale relationship. You have not yet been able to reach anyone at Coastal Buying Group to confirm the terms of the arrangement directly, and a basic search did not return an obviously active corporate website for the company (Exhibit 3 sets out the internal correspondence you obtained about this transaction).

Your responsibility, working under Priya's supervision, is to work through the issues using the CPA Way: analyze whether the recorded allowance for inventory obsolescence is adequate under ASPE Section 3031, assess the fraud risk and appropriate accounting treatment for the Coastal Buying Group transaction, quantify the impact of any required adjustments on the draft financial statements, and determine what this means for both the audit opinion and the covenant calculation the Bank will rely on. You should also address what this means for the nature, timing, and extent of further audit procedures required before the file can be finalized, and what needs to be communicated to Priya and, ultimately, to those charged with governance.

exhibits.content:
From: Grant Osei, CFO
To: Denise Chow, Controller

Denise,

Good news, I confirmed the deal with Coastal Buying Group for the general merchandise sitting in the west distribution centre. 1.2 million, and they want net 90 terms plus the right to send back anything they can't move within 120 days, which is a bit longer than we normally give, but honestly it clears a lot of stock off our books before year end and it's a new relationship worth protecting. Let's get this booked and shipped before December 31 if we can. I want this in the year-end numbers.

Grant

From: Denise Chow, Controller
To: Grant Osei, CFO
Date: December 22, 2025, 5:15 PM
Subject: RE: Year-end push - Coastal Buying Group deal

Grant,

Understood, I'll get the invoice prepared. One question before I book it: with a 120-day unconditional return right, should we hold back some of the revenue until the return window closes, or is it fine to record the full 1.2 million as a normal sale right away?

Denise

From: Grant Osei, CFO
To: Denise Chow, Controller
Date: December 22, 2025, 5:41 PM
Subject: RE: Year-end push - Coastal Buying Group deal

Denise,

Book it as a normal sale for the full amount, same as any other wholesale customer. We can deal with any returns if and when they happen next year. I need this in the 2025 numbers, we're right on the line with the bank ratio and I don't want any surprises at the covenant certificate stage. Just get the shipment out by the 31st.

Grant

requirements: Identify and assess the financial reporting and audit risks arising from the inventory obsolescence issue and the Coastal Buying Group transaction, and recommend the appropriate accounting treatment and required adjustments to the draft financial statements.

Recommend the audit procedures you would perform to obtain sufficient appropriate audit evidence over inventory valuation and the Coastal Buying Group transaction, including consideration of fraud risk under CAS 240.
Discuss the implications of your findings for the audit opinion and for Northgate's compliance with its bank covenant, and identify matters to communicate to those charged with governance.

model_response.understand_issue: This is an external audit engagement of a privately held retail company reporting under ASPE, where the audited financial statements directly drive a bank covenant calculation. As audit senior reporting to the engagement partner, I need to work through two related issues identified in year-end fieldwork: whether the recorded inventory obsolescence allowance is adequate under ASPE Section 3031, and whether a large, unusual, last-day-of-year sale to a new customer with generous return and payment terms represents a fraud risk under CAS 240 affecting revenue and receivables. Both issues affect current assets used in the covenant test, so I need to quantify required adjustments, recommend audit procedures, and consider the resulting implications for the audit opinion and communication with governance.
model_response.analyze_case:
Inventory obsolescence: Under ASPE Section 3031, inventory must be measured at the lower of cost and net realizable value, with this comparison made at each reporting date. Net realizable value is the estimated selling price in the ordinary course of business less estimated costs to complete and sell. The aging report shows inventory aged over 180 days, almost entirely general merchandise, has grown from $700,000 at the 2024 year end to $1.9 million at the 2025 year end, nearly a threefold increase, while the recorded allowance has stayed essentially flat at $180,000 versus $175,000 last year. Northgate's own historical clearance sale data shows aged general merchandise recovers, on average, only about 70 percent of cost once moved to clearance pricing, meaning a 30 percent loss is realized on average. Applying that 30 percent loss rate to the $1.9 million of aged inventory produces an estimated required write-down of $570,000 (1,900,000 times 30 percent). Compared to the $180,000 currently recorded, this indicates the allowance is understated by approximately $390,000 (570,000 minus 180,000). This finding is consistent with, and helps explain, the otherwise unexplained decline in gross margin from 32.1 percent to 27.4 percent, since carrying obsolete inventory at cost overstates both inventory and gross margin until the write-down is recorded. Inherent risk here is high: inventory valuation for a multi-category retailer requires significant estimation judgment, and management has a clear incentive, the covenant, not to record a further write-down this year.

Coastal Buying Group and fraud risk under CAS 240: several facts together point to a heightened fraud risk involving improper revenue recognition, commonly described as channel stuffing. The customer is new with no transaction history in the customer master file; the sale of $1.2 million is unusually large relative to a first transaction; payment terms of net 90 (versus the Company's standard net 30) and an unconditional 120-day return right are far more generous than ordinary business terms; the shipment occurred on the very last business day of the fiscal year; and the CFO's own email language, let's get this booked before year end and I don't want any surprises at the covenant certificate stage, signals the transaction's purpose is timing-driven rather than ordinary course. Under ASPE Section 3400, revenue recognition requires that the significant risks and rewards of ownership have transferred to the buyer, that the amount of revenue can be reliably measured, and that collection is reasonably assured. A broad unconditional right of return, extended payment terms, and an unverifiable customer relationship together raise substantial doubt about whether the significant risks and rewards of ownership had genuinely transferred to Coastal Buying Group by year end, and about whether the receivable is collectible at all. This is a classic channel-stuffing fact pattern used to inflate period-end revenue and current assets in order to influence a lender covenant calculation. The combination of apparent management override (the CFO directly negotiating and directing the booking of the transaction), a clear management incentive (covenant pressure), and inability to independently corroborate the counterparty represents a significant fraud risk under CAS 240 requiring an unpredictable, specifically tailored audit response rather than reliance on standard cutoff testing alone.

Combined impact on the covenant: if the $1.2 million sale is reversed, both accounts receivable and revenue decrease by $1.2 million, and if the obsolescence allowance is increased by $390,000, inventory's carrying value decreases further. Both adjustments reduce current assets, and since the Company's current ratio of 1.404 to 1 is already barely above the 1.40 to 1 minimum, these adjustments would very likely push Northgate below the covenant threshold once properly recorded.
model_response.recommendation:
For the obsolescence allowance, I recommend that management record an additional write-down of approximately $390,000, bringing the total allowance to $570,000, consistent with the Company's own three-year historical recovery experience. I would obtain and recalculate the aging report for clerical accuracy, test a sample of aged general merchandise items to subsequent clearance sale prices or, where available, independent liquidator quotes, to corroborate the 70 percent recovery assumption, and inquire whether specific discontinued lines within the aged population require a write-down closer to 100 percent of cost rather than the average rate.

For the Coastal Buying Group transaction, I recommend performing procedures including obtaining and inspecting the signed contract or purchase order, attempting independent third-party confirmation of the receivable and its terms directly with Coastal Buying Group without routing the request through Grant Osei, searching public and corporate registries to verify the counterparty is a legitimate, operating entity, and reviewing subsequent cash receipts and any goods returned in the period after year end through the date of the auditor's report. Based on the facts already obtained, including the inability to confirm an operating business, the absence of any prior history, and the extended unconditional return right, I recommend to the engagement partner that the transaction be reversed for financial reporting purposes: derecognize the $1.2 million of revenue and the related receivable, and return the goods to inventory at cost pending resolution. Given the fraud indicators present, I also recommend expanding procedures to include unpredictable testing of other late-December transactions, heightened professional skepticism toward management representations generally on this file, and a direct discussion of both the transaction and the obsolescence allowance with the CFO, escalating to those charged with governance, ideally including an independent party rather than only the Osei family in their management capacity, if the matter is not satisfactorily resolved.
model_response.conclusion: I recommend Priya discuss both required adjustments with management before the file is finalized. If management agrees to record the additional $390,000 obsolescence write-down and to reverse the Coastal Buying Group transaction, the financial statements would be materially correct and an unmodified opinion could still be considered, subject to satisfactory completion of the expanded fraud-risk procedures with no further findings. If management refuses either adjustment, the resulting misstatements must be evaluated individually and in aggregate against materiality, likely resulting in a qualified opinion, and both the covenant implications and the underlying fraud risk indicators must be communicated in writing to those charged with governance, since the same pressures suggest an elevated risk of further undetected misstatement elsewhere in the engagement.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly applies ASPE 3031 lower of cost and net realizable value test to identify inadequacy of the recorded obsolescence allowance
Recalculates the required additional write-down as approximately $390,000 using the 30 percent historical loss rate applied to the $1.9 million aged inventory balance
Explains the link between the unrecorded write-down and the otherwise unexplained gross margin decline from 32.1 percent to 27.4 percent
Identifies fraud risk indicators specific to the Coastal Buying Group transaction, including new customer, extended payment terms, unconditional return rights, year-end timing, and CFO email language
Applies CAS 240 concepts of management override of controls, incentive or pressure, and rationalization to the fact pattern
Assesses whether the significant risks and rewards of ownership transferred under ASPE 3400 given the unconditional return right, concluding revenue recognition criteria are not met
Recommends independent, non-management-mediated confirmation with Coastal Buying Group
Recommends unpredictable or expanded procedures over other late-period transactions, not just the single identified sale
Recommends the specific adjustment to reverse the $1.2 million revenue and receivable and return the goods to inventory at cost
Quantifies the combined impact of both adjustments on current assets and the 1.40 to 1 covenant ratio
Considers implications for the audit opinion depending on whether management agrees to record the adjustments
Identifies the need to communicate with those charged with governance, ideally involving parties independent of the CFO given override risk

marking_guide.competency: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
marking_guide.criterion: Maintains professional skepticism despite management's business explanation and the commercial importance of the client relationship

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Uses the CPA Way structure, working from specific case facts through to a supported, quantified conclusion
Drafts a clear, well-organized memo appropriate for engagement partner review, distinguishing the two issues while showing their combined effect
Recommends testing a sample of aged inventory items against subsequent sale prices or liquidator quotes to corroborate the 70 percent recovery assumption
Correctly identifies ASPE Section 3400 revenue recognition criteria, rather than IFRS 15, as the applicable framework for this private enterprise
Considers subsequent events, including post year-end returns or cash receipts, as corroborating or contradicting audit evidence

rating_guidance: A not-competent response fails to identify that the obsolescence allowance is inadequate, treats the aging report as description rather than performing the ASPE 3031 lower-of-cost-and-net-realizable-value comparison, and either ignores the Coastal Buying Group transaction or accepts management's explanation without recognizing the fraud risk indicators. A reaching-competence response identifies that inventory may be overstated and that the Coastal Buying Group sale looks unusual, but does not quantify the required $390,000 write-down or the $1.2 million revenue reversal with supporting calculations, and offers only generic procedures such as test inventory or confirm receivables without tailoring them to the specific risks identified. A competent response performs both quantifications correctly, ties the analysis to the correct standards, ASPE 3031 for inventory and ASPE 3400 for revenue, and CAS 240 for fraud, recommends specific tailored procedures including independent confirmation outside management, and connects the findings to the covenant threshold. A competent-with-distinction response goes further: it integrates the two issues to show how both adjustments together push the current ratio below 1.40 to 1, considers audit opinion implications under different management response scenarios, explicitly recommends escalating to parties independent of the CFO given the override risk, and maintains a consistently skeptical, well-reasoned tone that recognizes the broader engagement-level implications of a management team willing to structure a transaction around a covenant deadline.

enabling_focus: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
Adding Value
Communicating
title: Vantage Cloud Solutions Inc.: Revenue Recognition on a First-Year IFRS Audit
case_background:
You, CPA, are the audit senior on the first-year audit of Vantage Cloud Solutions Inc. (Vantage or the Company), a Toronto-based provider of cloud-based human resources and payroll software to mid-market Canadian employers, for the year ended December 31, 2025. Vantage was founded in 2019 and has grown quickly, reporting revenue of $9.4 million for fiscal 2025, up from $6.1 million in fiscal 2024. The Company is backed by institutional venture capital investors who hold preferred shares and require the Company to report under International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS) rather than ASPE, in anticipation of a possible future initial public offering. This is the first year your firm has audited Vantage; the prior year audit, for the year ended December 31, 2024, was performed by Bellwood LLP, a smaller regional firm, which issued an unmodified audit opinion. Vantage changed auditors this year following a competitive proposal process run by the Company's audit committee, which is chaired by one of the institutional investor board representatives.

The engagement partner, Marcus Delacroix, CPA, has asked you to complete detailed testing of revenue recognition for Vantage's enterprise customer contracts, which typically combine a software subscription fee, a one-time implementation and configuration fee, and an ongoing premium support fee, and to consider what the first-year nature of this engagement means for your work on opening balances.

As part of your testing, you selected a representative new enterprise contract for detailed review: an agreement signed March 1, 2025 with Meridian Manufacturing Group, a mid-size manufacturer, for use of Vantage's HR and payroll platform. Under the contract, Meridian is charged a subscription fee of $480,000 over a 24-month term, or $20,000 per month, for access to the platform; a one-time implementation fee of $120,000 for data migration and system configuration, delivered over the first three months of the contract and completed in May 2025; and a premium support fee of $80,000 over the same 24-month term, or approximately $3,333 per month, for enhanced service-level response times. The total contract value is $680,000. Access to the platform, per the contract, begins on the March 1, 2025 effective date, with implementation work running concurrently over the following three months.

In reviewing the Company's revenue recognition working papers, you noted that Vantage's controller recognized the entire $120,000 implementation fee as revenue immediately in May 2025 upon completion of the configuration work, treating it as a separately billed service recognized at a point in time rather than over the subscription term. The subscription fee and the support fee are each being recognized on a straight-line basis over the 24-month contract term. You reviewed the implementation scope description in the contract and in supporting project documentation and noted that the work consists of standard data migration and configuration using Vantage's own built-in configuration tools; there is no evidence of any modification to the underlying software code, and Meridian's employees cannot access or use the platform in any functional capacity without the completed configuration.

You raised this treatment with the controller, Aisha Bello, who explained that recognizing the implementation fee upfront is Vantage's standard practice, applied consistently to all enterprise contracts, and was also the practice used and accepted by the predecessor auditor, Bellwood LLP, in the prior year audit. In discussing the population of similar contracts with Ms. Bello, you learned that Vantage signed 18 similar enterprise contracts with implementation components during fiscal 2025, and that a total of $1,850,000 of implementation fees was recognized as revenue upfront across those contracts during the year (Exhibit 2 sets out further detail). You selected a sample of five of these contracts, including Meridian, for detailed testing and found the same fact pattern and the same accounting treatment applied in each case.

Marcus has calculated overall materiality for the engagement at $470,000, based on 5 percent of revenue, given that Vantage is a growth-stage company where revenue is likely to be the metric of greatest interest to the institutional investors and any future IPO market. Vantage's draft statement of comprehensive income shows a net loss of approximately $2.1 million for fiscal 2025 before consideration of any audit adjustments, and its draft statement of financial position shows deferred revenue of $3.6 million at December 31, 2025.

Your responsibility, working under Marcus's supervision, is to work through the CPA Way to determine the correct revenue recognition treatment for the Meridian contract and the broader population of similar contracts under IFRS 15, quantify any required adjustment, consider what CAS 510 requires of you as auditor on this first-year engagement with respect to opening balances and the predecessor auditor's prior work, and recommend the procedures and next steps needed before the file can be finalized.
