exhibits.title: Exhibit 3: Correspondence Regarding Predecessor Auditor and Opening Balances
exhibits.content:
From: Marcus Delacroix, Engagement Partner
To: You, Audit Senior
Date: February 3, 2026
Subject: Bellwood LLP file access and opening balances

After obtaining the Company's written authorization, I reached out to the engagement partner at Bellwood LLP to request access to their prior year working papers as part of our procedures on opening balances for this first-year engagement. Bellwood has agreed to provide access. I have not yet reviewed the file myself. Please follow up once you have looked at the revenue recognition working papers in detail, since if the same implementation fee treatment was used in the prior year, this could affect opening deferred revenue and opening retained earnings, not just the current year numbers. Keep in mind Vantage's investors are expecting clean numbers heading toward a possible IPO process next year, and the audit committee chair has already asked me informally how the transition from Bellwood is going.

From: Aisha Bello, Controller
To: You, Audit Senior
Date: February 5, 2026
Subject: RE: Implementation fee treatment, prior year

Hi, to answer your question, yes, we used the exact same approach in fiscal 2024 that we used this year, recognizing implementation fees upfront on completion for every enterprise contract with an implementation component. Bellwood reviewed a sample of these contracts as part of the 2024 audit and did not propose any adjustment. Total implementation fees recognized upfront in fiscal 2024 across all enterprise contracts were approximately $640,000, on total fiscal 2024 revenue of $6.1 million. We have used this same revenue recognition approach since we introduced implementation services in 2022. Let me know if you need anything else from the 2024 contract files.

requirements: Determine, with supporting analysis under IFRS 15, the correct revenue recognition treatment for the Meridian Manufacturing Group contract and the broader population of similar enterprise contracts, and quantify the required adjustment.

Discuss the implications of this issue for your work as auditor on this first-year engagement, including the requirements of CAS 510 with respect to opening balances and your consideration of the predecessor auditor's prior work.
Recommend the audit procedures and next steps required before the engagement can be finalized, and identify matters to communicate to those charged with governance.

model_response.understand_issue: This is a first-year external audit engagement of a private, IFRS-reporting SaaS company preparing for a possible future IPO, where the predecessor auditor's work and consistency of accounting policy are directly relevant. As audit senior reporting to the engagement partner, I need to determine the correct revenue recognition treatment for a multi-element software contract under IFRS 15, quantify the resulting adjustment for both the sample contract and the broader population, address what CAS 510 requires regarding opening balances given the same policy was applied in the prior year audited by a predecessor firm, and recommend next steps and governance communication before the file can be finalized.
model_response.analyze_case:
Applying the IFRS 15 five-step model to the Meridian contract, the first step, identifying the contract, is straightforward: a signed agreement exists with commercial substance and identified payment terms. The critical issue is step two, identifying the performance obligations. A promised good or service is a distinct performance obligation only if it is both capable of being distinct, meaning the customer can benefit from it either on its own or together with other readily available resources, and distinct within the context of the contract, meaning it is not highly interrelated with other promises or a significant input into a combined output. The implementation service here is arguably capable of being distinct in isolation, since a third-party consultant could in theory perform similar data migration work. However, it is not distinct within the context of this contract: the implementation work consists of standard data migration and configuration using Vantage's own toolset, involves no modification of the underlying software, and Meridian cannot obtain any functional benefit from the subscription without the configuration being completed. The implementation service is therefore an input into a single combined output, a functioning, configured HR and payroll platform accessible to Meridian's employees, and should be combined with the subscription as a single performance obligation rather than recognized separately upon completion. The premium support fee represents a separate, distinct stand-ready obligation to provide priority service over the term and should continue to be recognized on a straight-line basis over the 24-month contract period, which is consistent with how the Company is already recognizing it.

Recalculating revenue on the correct basis: total contract consideration of $680,000 (480,000 subscription plus 120,000 implementation plus 80,000 support), recognized straight-line over the 24-month term beginning March 1, 2025, produces a combined monthly rate of $28,333.33 (680,000 divided by 24). From March 1 through December 31, 2025 is 10 months, so cumulative revenue that should be recognized at December 31, 2025 is $283,333 (10 times 28,333.33). Vantage instead recognized $353,333 on this contract (120,000 upfront implementation plus 200,000 subscription plus 33,333 support), an overstatement of $70,000 on this single contract.

Extrapolating to the population: the sample of five contracts, including Meridian, all exhibited the identical fact pattern and error. The $70,000 error represents 58.3 percent of the $120,000 implementation fee on the Meridian contract (70,000 divided by 120,000). Applying that same ratio to the $1,850,000 of implementation fees recognized upfront across all 18 similar contracts during fiscal 2025 produces a projected misstatement of approximately $1,079,000 (1,850,000 times 58.3 percent), which substantially exceeds the engagement partner's calculated overall materiality of $470,000. This indicates the revenue recognition error is material to the fiscal 2025 financial statements taken as a whole, not an isolated issue limited to the Meridian contract, and correcting it would increase Vantage's reported net loss from approximately $2.1 million to roughly $3.2 million, a substantial change likely to be of significant interest to the institutional investors ahead of any IPO process.

CAS 510 and opening balances: because this is Vantage's first audit by our firm, CAS 510 requires us to obtain sufficient appropriate audit evidence about whether the opening balances contain misstatements that materially affect the current period financial statements, and whether appropriate accounting policies reflected in the opening balances have been consistently applied. Ms. Bello confirmed the identical upfront-recognition policy was applied to enterprise contracts in fiscal 2024, when approximately $640,000 of implementation fees were recognized upfront on fiscal 2024 revenue of $6.1 million, and that Bellwood LLP did not propose any adjustment. This is not merely a current year issue: it indicates a policy applied incorrectly and consistently since 2022, meaning opening deferred revenue and opening retained earnings at January 1, 2025 are also likely misstated. Under IAS 8, this represents a correction of a prior period error, since the performance obligations under these contracts were consistently misidentified, not a change in accounting estimate, and a correction of a material prior period error is required to be applied retrospectively, restating opening retained earnings and the relevant comparative figures.
model_response.recommendation: I recommend the engagement team propose an adjustment to defer $70,000 of previously recognized revenue on the Meridian contract specifically, and extend testing beyond the initial sample of five contracts, ideally to the full population of 18 similar fiscal 2025 contracts, to precisely quantify rather than merely project the total required current-year adjustment, since the projected misstatement of approximately $1,079,000 already exceeds materiality and needs to be either corrected or evaluated as an uncorrected misstatement in aggregate with any other identified items. I recommend obtaining and reviewing Bellwood LLP's fiscal 2024 working papers on revenue recognition, now that access has been granted, to identify the total population of fiscal 2024 contracts affected and to quantify the resulting opening balance misstatement at January 1, 2025, consistent with our CAS 510 responsibilities. Where the fiscal 2024 error is also material, I recommend the Company restate the January 1, 2025 opening retained earnings and deferred revenue balances and related comparative disclosures under IAS 8, rather than only correcting fiscal 2025. I recommend further procedures including inspection of a broader sample of implementation scope descriptions and statements of work to confirm none of the 18 contracts involve any actual software customization that might change the performance obligation analysis for a subset of contracts, recalculation of the deferred revenue continuity schedule on the corrected basis, and obtaining an updated management representation addressing revenue recognition policies and the prior period error. Given the significance of the change to reported net loss and its relevance to the institutional investors and a possible future IPO, I recommend this matter, including both the current year adjustment and the prior period restatement, be discussed directly with management and communicated in writing to those charged with governance, including the audit committee, well before the file is finalized.
model_response.conclusion: Subject to completing the extended population testing and confirming the precise fiscal 2024 opening balance impact, I recommend Marcus communicate to the audit committee that the implementation fee revenue recognition policy applied since 2022 is inconsistent with IFRS 15 and requires correction, including a retrospective restatement of opening balances at January 1, 2025 under IAS 8. If management agrees to record both the current year adjustment and the opening balance restatement, and no further issues arise from the extended population testing, an unmodified opinion on the fiscal 2025 financial statements remains achievable. If management disagrees or the corrected misstatement is not properly reflected, this would need to be evaluated for a modified opinion, and the systemic, multi-year nature of the error should also inform a reassessment of the overall risk of material misstatement across the remainder of the engagement.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly identifies that the implementation service is not distinct within the context of the contract under IFRS 15 step two, requiring combination with the subscription performance obligation
Applies the IFRS 15 five-step model explicitly, addressing contract identification, performance obligations, transaction price, allocation, and recognition pattern
Recalculates correct cumulative revenue recognized at December 31, 2025 on the Meridian contract as $283,333, using the combined $28,333.33 monthly rate over 10 months
Quantifies the Company's recorded revenue of $353,333 on the Meridian contract and the resulting $70,000 overstatement
Projects the sample error rate of 58.3 percent across the population of 18 similar contracts to estimate a projected misstatement of approximately $1,079,000
Compares the projected misstatement to calculated overall materiality of $470,000 and concludes the error is likely material to the financial statements as a whole
Applies CAS 510 to the first-year audit, addressing the need for sufficient appropriate evidence about opening balances and consistency of accounting policy application
Recommends reviewing predecessor auditor Bellwood LLP's working papers, now that access has been granted, as a specific opening balance procedure
Identifies that the identical revenue recognition policy was applied in fiscal 2024, meaning opening deferred revenue and opening retained earnings are also likely misstated
Applies IAS 8 to conclude this is a correction of a prior period error requiring retrospective restatement, rather than a change in accounting estimate
Recommends extending testing to the full population of 18 contracts, or otherwise precisely quantifying rather than only projecting the required adjustment
Recommends discussing the required correction and prior-period restatement with those charged with governance given IPO and investor reporting implications
Recognizes the premium support fee as a distinct stand-ready performance obligation properly recognized straight-line over the service period
Considers whether the systemic, multi-year nature of the error indicates a broader risk of material misstatement requiring a reassessment of the audit approach, not just a one-off adjustment
Recommends a practical remediation beyond the audit adjustment itself, such as updated revenue recognition policy documentation, relevant ahead of a future IPO process

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Structures the response using the CPA Way, moving from specific contract facts through standard-based analysis to a supportable, quantified conclusion
Presents the technical IFRS 15 and IAS 8 analysis clearly enough that a non-accounting audience, such as the audit committee, could understand the implication for reported results
Discusses implications for the audit opinion under different scenarios of management agreement or disagreement, given the systemic nature of the error

rating_guidance: A not-competent response fails to recognize that the implementation fee should be combined with the subscription as a single performance obligation, either accepting the Company's upfront recognition without analysis or asserting a different treatment without applying the IFRS 15 distinct-within-the-context-of-the-contract criterion, and does not identify any opening balance implication. A reaching-competence response identifies that the implementation fee treatment looks aggressive and that first-year opening balance work is relevant, but does not correctly recalculate the $283,333 correct revenue figure or the resulting $70,000 error, and does not extend the analysis to the broader population or the prior year. A competent response correctly applies the IFRS 15 five-step model to conclude the performance obligations should be combined, accurately recalculates both the correct and recorded revenue amounts and the resulting adjustment, projects the population-level misstatement and compares it to materiality, and correctly identifies the CAS 510 opening balance implication given the same policy was used in the Bellwood-audited prior year. A competent-with-distinction response goes further: it explicitly applies IAS 8 to characterize the issue as a prior period error requiring retrospective restatement rather than a policy change, integrates the current year and opening balance analyses into a single coherent conclusion about the risk of material misstatement across the whole engagement, and tailors its recommended governance communication to the IPO context in a way that reflects genuine professional judgment rather than a mechanical recitation of the standards.

enabling_focus: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
Communicating
title: Northstar Exploration Corp: Going Concern and E&E Asset Impairment
case_background:
You, CPA, are the audit senior on the engagement team for Northstar Exploration Corp (Northstar or the Company), a junior mineral exploration company listed on the TSX Venture Exchange. Northstar has no revenue-generating mining operations. It holds mineral claims across three properties in northern Ontario and reports under IFRS, with exploration and evaluation costs capitalized under IFRS 6. The Company has never declared a dividend and has funded its activities entirely through equity issuances and, more recently, short-term loans from its founder and CEO, Marcus Feld.

The engagement partner, Priya Anand, has asked you to complete fieldwork for the December 31, 2025 year-end audit and to draft memos on the two most significant risk areas before the file goes to review: going concern, and the recoverability of capitalized exploration and evaluation (E&E) costs. Materiality for the engagement has been set at $175,000 based on 1 percent of total assets, with performance materiality of $131,000.

At December 31, 2025, Northstar reported a cash balance of $1,200,000. Cash used in operating activities has run at approximately $350,000 per month over the second half of fiscal 2025, driven primarily by ongoing drilling contractor costs, geologist salaries, and TSXV listing and corporate compliance costs. At the current burn rate, the Company's cash on hand would be exhausted in approximately 3.4 months, or roughly mid-April 2026, well before the anticipated completion of a further equity financing. Northstar has three mineral properties. The Redfern Property, its most advanced claim, carries a capitalized E&E balance of $4,800,000 and has produced encouraging assay results this year, including several high-grade gold intercepts that support continued capitalization. The Silver Creek Property carries a capitalized E&E balance of $2,100,000; a 22-hole drill program completed in October 2025 returned results significantly below the Company's internal exploration targets, with most holes intersecting only trace mineralization and no hole exceeding the Company's own internally defined economic cutoff grade. The Blue Mountain Property, an early-stage claim acquired in 2024, carries a capitalized E&E balance of $900,000 and has not yet been drill-tested. Total capitalized E&E assets are $7,800,000, representing approximately 65 percent of Northstar's total assets of $12,000,000.

Management, led by CFO Elena Ruiz, has prepared a going concern note for inclusion in the financial statements. The note discloses the negative operating cash flows, the limited cash runway, and describes a financing plan under which the Company intends to complete a non-brokered private placement of up to $5,000,000 in units, targeted to close by the end of Q1 fiscal 2026. As at the date you are performing fieldwork (February 20, 2026), the Company has received signed subscription agreements totaling $1,400,000 from existing shareholders, including $400,000 from CEO Marcus Feld personally, but has not yet closed the financing or filed the required TSXV exchange bulletin. Management's cash flow forecast, which assumes the full $5,000,000 raise closes on schedule and assumes exploration spending on Silver Creek is reduced to near zero for the next twelve months, shows the Company remaining solvent through December 2026. If the financing does not close, or closes for a lesser amount, management's forecast shows Northstar running out of cash within the 3 to 4 month window noted above.

During your fieldwork you also learned, through discussion with the VP Exploration, Dana Whitfield, that the technical report on Silver Creek prepared by the Company's independent qualified person (a National Instrument 43-101 requirement) recommends no further drilling on the property in its current configuration and suggests the claims may be more valuable if optioned or sold to a third party rather than continuing self-funded exploration. Elena Ruiz has told you she does not believe any write-down is necessary because the Company still holds the mineral rights and intends to keep the claims in good standing by paying the annual assessment fees.

Partner Priya Anand wants your memo to address: (1) whether the going concern basis of preparation remains appropriate and whether a material uncertainty related to going concern exists requiring disclosure, and the impact on the audit opinion; and (2) whether indicators of impairment exist under IFRS 6 for the Silver Creek E&E asset, and if so, what audit evidence is needed regarding management's assessment. She has asked you to draft the technical memo using the CPA Way.

Cash Flow Summary, Year Ended December 31, 2025

Note: The above cash flow statement excludes the subsequent private placement discussed in Exhibit 3, which had not closed as at the date of this draft.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Excerpt from Northstar's Independent Technical Report on the Silver Creek Property (prepared by qualified person, dated November 30, 2025)
exhibits.content:
Summary of 2025 Drill Program Results, Silver Creek Property

A 22-hole, 4,100 metre diamond drill program was completed at Silver Creek between July and September 2025, targeting an interpreted extension of gold-bearing structures identified in historical exploration. Of the 22 holes drilled, 19 returned only trace to weakly anomalous gold values below 0.3 grams per tonne over narrow intervals, and 3 holes returned no significant mineralization. No hole intersected mineralization exceeding the Company's internally applied 1.0 gram per tonne economic cutoff over a minimum true width of 2 metres, which is the threshold management has historically used in assessing whether a zone warrants continued investment.

Based on these results, in the qualified person's opinion, the currently defined target area at Silver Creek does not present a compelling case for further Company-funded drilling in its present configuration. The qualified person recommends that the Company either (a) conduct a substantial reinterpretation of the geological model before committing further exploration capital, which is not expected to be completed within the next 12 months given current staffing, or (b) pursue an option or sale of the property to a third-party operator with a different exploration thesis or greater risk tolerance. The qualified person notes that Northstar's 2026 exploration budget, as most recently approved by the board on December 10, 2025, allocates no further drilling capital to Silver Creek and reallocates the majority of available exploration spending to the Redfern Property.

The Company retains 100 percent of its mineral claims in good standing at Silver Creek. Annual assessment work and claim maintenance fees required to keep the claims in good standing are estimated at $28,000 per year, a small fraction of the $2,100,000 currently capitalized.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 3: Email from Elena Ruiz, CFO, to Priya Anand, dated February 18, 2026
exhibits.content:
Priya,

Attached is our updated cash flow forecast reflecting the private placement. As you will see, we have subscription agreements in hand for $1,400,000, including $400,000 from Marcus personally, and we are in active discussions with two institutional investors who have each verbally indicated interest in the $1,500,000 to $2,000,000 range. We are targeting a first closing by March 15, 2026 and expect to reach the full $5,000,000 by the end of Q1. Marcus has also indicated he is prepared to advance a further $300,000 short-term loan if needed to bridge us through closing, on the same terms as the existing $500,000 loan.

On Silver Creek, I want to be clear that we are not planning to write anything off. We still hold the claims, we are current on our assessment work obligations, and gold prices have actually strengthened since the drill results came in, which supports the view that the ounces in the ground are still worth pursuing eventually, even if not this year. The board redirected the 2026 budget to Redfern because that is where the near-term news flow is, not because we think Silver Creek has no value. I would rather keep the asset on the books at cost and revisit it when we have capital to do a proper geological reinterpretation, as the qualified person suggested.

Let me know what else you need from us for the going concern note. We think our current wording, which mirrors last year's, is sufficient.

Elena

requirements: Using the CPA Way, assess whether Northstar's use of the going concern basis of preparation remains appropriate, and evaluate the adequacy of management's proposed going concern disclosure and its impact on the auditor's report.
Evaluate whether indicators of impairment exist for the Silver Creek E&E asset under IFRS 6, and recommend the audit procedures required to conclude on management's impairment assessment.

Recommend the specific audit procedures the team should perform over the CEO loan and subsequent private placement subscriptions, given their relevance to both going concern and related party considerations.

model_response.understand_issue: This is a year-end external audit of a TSXV-listed junior exploration company under IFRS. As audit senior, I am preparing a technical memo for the engagement partner on two interrelated risk areas: whether the going concern basis of preparation remains appropriate under CAS 570 given a 3.4 month cash runway and an unclosed financing plan, and whether the Silver Creek E&E asset requires an impairment trigger assessment under IFRS 6 following disappointing drill results. Both issues affect the audit opinion and require specific evidence about management's assumptions and subsequent events.
model_response.analyze_case:
Going concern (CAS 570). The audit risk model points to elevated risk here: inherent risk is high because junior explorers with no revenue are structurally dependent on external financing, and Northstar's own cash flow data confirms this, with $1,200,000 cash on hand against a $350,000 monthly burn, implying exhaustion of cash by mid-April 2026 absent new financing. This is a strong indicator of events or conditions that may cast significant doubt on the entity's ability to continue as a going concern under CAS 570. Management has prepared a mitigating plan, a $5,000,000 private placement, but as at the fieldwork date only $1,400,000 is firmly subscribed, none of it has closed, and $400,000 of the signed subscriptions come from the CEO himself, a related party whose participation does not represent new external capital in the same sense as an arm's length investor and should be evaluated with professional skepticism regarding its reliability as mitigation. The remaining $3,600,000 rests on verbal indications of interest from two institutional investors, which is not sufficient audit evidence of a committed source of financing. Management's forecast also assumes exploration spending at Silver Creek drops to near zero, which is at least partially corroborated by the board-approved 2026 budget in Exhibit 2, lending some support to that specific assumption, but the financing assumption remains the weaker leg of the plan. Under CAS 570, when events or conditions are identified, the auditor evaluates whether a material uncertainty exists after considering management's plans. Given the size of the funding gap relative to firmly committed financing, and the short runway, I conclude a material uncertainty related to going concern exists and requires disclosure; if management's note in the financial statements adequately describes the principal events, the funding shortfall, and the risk, an unmodified opinion with a Material Uncertainty Related to Going Concern paragraph is appropriate; if the disclosure is inadequate, a qualified or adverse opinion would be required. The current draft note, per Elena's email, mirrors last year's wording and has not been updated for the fact that only 28 percent of the targeted raise is firmly committed at this late date, which is a deficiency the team must raise with management before forming a final view on disclosure adequacy.

IFRS 6 impairment indicator (Silver Creek). IFRS 6 does not require capitalized E&E assets to be tested for impairment under IAS 36 in the same manner as other assets, but it does require an entity to assess E&E assets for impairment when facts and circumstances suggest the carrying amount may exceed recoverable amount, and it specifically lists indicators including a substantive expenditure on further exploration in the specific area is neither budgeted nor planned, and exploration for and evaluation of mineral resources in the specific area have not led to the discovery of commercially viable quantities and the entity has decided to discontinue such activities in the specific area. Both indicators are present at Silver Creek: the qualified person's report recommends no further Company-funded drilling in the current configuration, and the board-approved 2026 budget confirms no further drilling capital is allocated to the property. Management's assertion that gold prices have strengthened and the claims remain in good standing does not overcome these two specific triggers under IFRS 6; maintaining claims in good standing at a nominal $28,000 annual cost is not equivalent to substantive planned exploration expenditure, and price strength alone does not offset a technical report concluding the currently drilled target area is not economically compelling. This is a significant risk requiring specific audit attention because management has strong incentive, given the going concern pressure, to avoid recognizing a write-down that would further weaken the balance sheet ahead of a planned financing.
model_response.recommendation:
On going concern, I recommend the team obtain and evaluate management's detailed cash flow forecast through at least twelve months from the reporting date, corroborate the $1,400,000 in signed subscriptions to executed agreements and bank confirmations of funds received, and obtain third-party evidence, such as executed term sheets or correspondence, for the two institutional investors before giving any credit to the remaining $3,600,000; verbal interest alone should receive no weight. We should confirm the $500,000 existing CEO loan terms and the availability of the further $300,000 bridge loan through a signed commitment letter, since related party support is relevant but requires separate corroboration of Marcus Feld's actual capacity to fund it. We should request management revise the going concern note to reflect the actual status of financing as at the report date, including the shortfall, and discuss required wording with Priya Anand before the note is finalized. Subsequent events procedures should be performed through the date of the auditor's report to capture any financing closing updates.

On Silver Creek, I recommend the team formally document that impairment indicators exist under IFRS 6 and request management perform and document an impairment assessment. Given the qualified person's report already exists as external, objective evidence, the team should evaluate whether the carrying amount of $2,100,000 is recoverable, which for a property with no near-term planned work and no defined resource is unlikely to be supportable, and press management on why no write-down has been proposed. We should evaluate the disclosed cutoff grade methodology with our internal specialist and consider whether the entire $2,100,000, or a portion reflecting residual option/sale value, is the appropriate carrying amount going forward. Any proposed adjustment should be quantified against performance materiality of $131,000 and aggregated with other identified misstatements.
model_response.conclusion: Both matters point to significant risks requiring escalation to the engagement partner before the report is finalized. If management updates the going concern note to reflect the true state of financing and the note adequately discloses the material uncertainty, an unmodified opinion with a Material Uncertainty Related to Going Concern paragraph is the likely outcome; inadequate disclosure would require a qualified or adverse opinion. Separately, absent a supportable recoverable amount for Silver Creek, we should propose a write-down; management's refusal to record one, if it persists, is itself a matter for further discussion with those charged with governance and may affect the opinion if the amount is material and the difference is not corrected.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly identifies that events or conditions exist casting significant doubt on going concern under CAS 570 and cites the standard
Evaluates the reliability of management's mitigating financing plan by distinguishing firmly committed subscriptions from verbal institutional interest and related-party (CEO) subscriptions
Concludes on whether a material uncertainty exists and correctly links this to the appropriate audit opinion outcome (Material Uncertainty Related to Going Concern paragraph vs. qualified/adverse)
Identifies the two specific IFRS 6 impairment indicators present at Silver Creek (no budgeted further exploration; QP recommendation to discontinue in current configuration)
Recommends specific procedures to corroborate the private placement (bank confirmation, executed agreements, third-party term sheets) rather than accepting management representation alone
Recommends procedures over the CEO loan and related bridge financing consistent with CAS 550 related party considerations
Quantifies or references materiality ($175,000) and performance materiality ($131,000) when discussing the potential Silver Creek adjustment
Correctly applies IFRS 6 (not IAS 36) as the relevant impairment framework for E&E assets and explains why IFRS 6's indicator-based test differs from a full IAS 36 test
Evaluates management's counterarguments (gold price strength, claims in good standing) and explains why they do not overcome the IFRS 6 indicators
Correctly distinguishes E&E asset treatment for Redfern (continue capitalizing, supported by positive results) from Silver Creek (impairment indicator present)

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Structures the response using Understand, Analyze, Recommend, Conclude and reaches a defensible, evidence-based conclusion rather than a neutral list of considerations

marking_guide.competency: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
marking_guide.criterion: Applies professional skepticism to management's assertion that no write-down is needed, given management's incentive to preserve balance sheet strength ahead of financing
Memo is written for the engagement partner audience with clear, prioritized recommendations rather than an unfocused recitation of facts
Recommends subsequent events procedures through the date of the auditor's report specifically addressing the financing closing
Recognizes the going concern note as currently drafted (mirroring prior year) is likely inadequate and requires revision before disclosure adequacy can be assessed
Notes that maintaining mineral claims in good standing at nominal cost is not equivalent to substantive planned exploration expenditure under IFRS 6

rating_guidance: A Competent (C) response identifies both the going concern material uncertainty and the IFRS 6 impairment indicators at Silver Creek, applies the correct standards (CAS 570, IFRS 6) by name, uses the specific case figures (the 3.4 month runway, the $1,400,000 of $5,000,000 firmly subscribed, the two specific IFRS 6 indicators quoted from the standard), and reaches a defensible conclusion on the audit opinion. A Reaching Competence (RC) response identifies the issues and cites relevant standards but treats the financing plan or the impairment indicators generically, without distinguishing committed from uncommitted financing or without applying the specific IFRS 6 indicator language to Silver Creek's facts, or omits materiality quantification. A Not Competent (NC) response misidentifies the framework, for example applying a full IAS 36 impairment test instead of IFRS 6's indicator-based approach, treats management's verbal institutional interest as sufficient evidence, or fails to reach a conclusion on the audit opinion impact. A Competent with Distinction (CD) response goes further by explicitly weighing the CEO's related-party subscription differently from arm's length capital, ties the going concern funding shortfall and the Silver Creek impairment together as compounding risks that both reflect management's incentive pressure heading into a financing round, and gives specific, prioritized next-step procedures rather than a generic audit program.

enabling_focus: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
Communicating
title: Delacroix Construction Group: Percentage-of-Completion Estimates and Related Party Leasing
case_background:
You, CPA, are the audit senior on the engagement for Delacroix Construction Group Ltd (Delacroix or the Company), a privately owned mid-size construction company based in Manitoba that undertakes commercial and institutional building contracts across Western Canada. Delacroix reports under ASPE, is owned 80 percent by founder and CEO Robert Delacroix and 20 percent by a passive family trust, and is audited annually because its senior lender, a Canadian chartered bank, requires audited financial statements under the terms of a $6,000,000 operating line of credit and a covenant requiring a minimum current ratio of 1.2 to 1 and a maximum debt to equity ratio of 2.0 to 1.

The engagement partner, James Okonkwo, has asked you to complete fieldwork for the fiscal year ended December 31, 2025 audit. Materiality has been set at $210,000 based on 5 percent of normalized pre-tax income, with performance materiality of $157,000. Delacroix accounts for its long-term fixed-price construction contracts using the percentage-of-completion method under ASPE Section 3400, recognizing revenue based on the proportion of costs incurred to date relative to total estimated contract costs.

The largest contract in progress at year end is the Northgate Medical Centre project, a fixed-price contract with a total contract price of $18,400,000. At the prior year end, December 31, 2024, the Company had incurred costs of $6,300,000 against an original total estimated cost to complete the project of $15,000,000, resulting in 42.0 percent completion and cumulative revenue recognized of $7,728,000, with cumulative gross profit recognized of $1,428,000. During fiscal 2025, the project encountered unexpected subsurface soil conditions requiring additional shoring and foundation work, along with several change orders related to mechanical system upgrades that were not yet priced into a formal contract amendment as at year end. As at December 31, 2025, costs incurred to date on the project total $11,960,000. In early December 2025, the project manager revised the total estimated cost to complete the contract upward from the original $15,000,000 to $16,800,000, reflecting the soil conditions and anticipated costs of the mechanical change orders. This revision was made during the final weeks of the fiscal year, after the controller had already prepared a preliminary year-end forecast based on the original $15,000,000 estimate, and it was communicated to you informally by the project manager rather than through a documented change order log entry.

Separately, in reviewing the general ledger for related party activity, you identified that Delacroix leases a fleet of six pieces of heavy equipment, including two excavators and a tower crane, from Marlow Equipment Rentals Ltd, a company wholly owned by Sandra Delacroix, Robert Delacroix's spouse. Delacroix paid Marlow Equipment Rentals a total of $840,000 in equipment lease payments during fiscal 2025, all of which is charged to cost of contracts and allocated across active projects, including $145,000 charged directly to the Northgate Medical Centre project. You obtained quotes from two independent equipment rental companies for comparable equipment and lease terms, which indicate a market rate for the same fleet would be approximately $620,000 for the year, suggesting Delacroix is paying Marlow Equipment Rentals roughly 35 percent above market rates. Robert Delacroix has told the controller that the arrangement with Marlow has been in place for eight years and that Sandra reinvests the lease income into newer equipment that benefits Delacroix's projects, and that he does not consider the pricing to be a related party issue since it has never been questioned by the bank in prior years. The controller, who reports to Robert, has not historically included Marlow Equipment Rentals on the related party disclosure schedule provided to the audit team, and it was your CAS 315 risk assessment discussion with operations staff, not the controller's schedule, that surfaced the relationship.

Partner James Okonkwo wants your memo to address: (1) the appropriateness and reliability of the revised cost-to-complete estimate on the Northgate Medical Centre contract and the resulting percentage-of-completion revenue recognition, including the risk that the estimate was manipulated to manage reported earnings, and (2) the related party transaction with Marlow Equipment Rentals, including disclosure requirements and the risk that the audit team was not made aware of the relationship through normal channels. He has asked you to draft the technical memo using the CPA Way, quantifying the accounting impact where possible.
