The revised $16,800,000 estimate, received from the project manager on Dec 8, 2025, was used in the final trial balance instead.'

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Email chain between Robert Delacroix (CEO) and the Controller, dated December 9-11, 2025
exhibits.content:
From: Robert Delacroix
To: Controller
Date: December 9, 2025
Subject: Northgate numbers

I saw the draft forecast showing over $2.7 million cumulative profit recognized on Northgate. That can't be right given what we've actually spent versus what's left to do out there, especially with the soil issues. Please get updated numbers from the project manager before we finalize anything for the bank. We need the covenant numbers to make sense too.

From: Controller
To: Robert Delacroix
Date: December 10, 2025
Subject: RE: Northgate numbers

Spoke with the PM. He's revised total estimated cost to complete to $16,800,000 to reflect the soil conditions and the mechanical change orders that are still being priced with the mechanical sub. This brings percent complete down to about 71% and current year gross profit to a loss of roughly $289,000 on the project, which is a big swing from the earlier forecast. I want to flag that the mechanical change order pricing is still an estimate; we don't have signed pricing from the sub yet, so there's a range here depending on how that lands.

From: Robert Delacroix
To: Controller
Date: December 11, 2025
Subject: RE: RE: Northgate numbers

Understood, use the PM's number for now. We'll true it up next year once the change order is signed. Let's make sure this doesn't put us offside on the covenant, run the ratios once you have the final trial balance and let me know before it goes to the auditors.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 3: Related Party Lease Comparison and Controller's Related Party Schedule (as provided to audit team)
exhibits.content:
Delacroix Construction Group Ltd
Related Party Disclosure Schedule (as provided by controller, dated Jan 15, 2026)

1. Loan from R. Delacroix (shareholder) to the Company, $250,000, unsecured, non-interest bearing, no fixed repayment terms.
2. Management fees paid to R. Delacroix Holdco, $180,000 for the year.

[Marlow Equipment Rentals Ltd was not included on this schedule.]

Auditor's independent market comparison (obtained during fieldwork, February 2026):

                                Delacroix/Marlow      Independent      Independent
                                (actual, 2025)         Quote A          Quote B
Annual equipment lease cost         $840,000           $610,000          $630,000
(6 units: 2 excavators, 1 tower
crane, 3 support vehicles,
comparable specifications and
term)

Average independent quote: $620,000. Excess paid to Marlow Equipment Rentals over average independent market rate: $840,000 - $620,000 = $220,000 (approximately 35% above market).

Of the $840,000 total, $145,000 was charged directly to the Northgate Medical Centre contract as equipment cost, forming part of the $11,960,000 costs incurred to date used in the percentage-of-completion calculation in Exhibit 1.

Corporate registry search confirms Marlow Equipment Rentals Ltd is 100% owned by Sandra Delacroix, spouse of CEO Robert Delacroix.

requirements: Using the CPA Way, assess the reliability of the revised cost-to-complete estimate on the Northgate Medical Centre contract and evaluate the risk that the estimate was used to manage reported earnings, quantifying the accounting impact.

Evaluate the related party transaction with Marlow Equipment Rentals Ltd, including whether it has been appropriately identified and disclosed, and recommend audit procedures in response to the risk that management did not fully disclose the relationship to the audit team.
Recommend how the team should respond to the identified risks in planning further audit procedures and in communicating with those charged with governance.

model_response.understand_issue: This is a year-end external audit of a private construction company under ASPE, with a bank lender relying on the audited statements for covenant compliance. As audit senior, I must assess two connected risks for a technical memo to the engagement partner: whether the year-end revision to estimated costs to complete on the Northgate contract is a reliable, supportable estimate or reflects earnings management under ASPE 3400, and whether the Marlow Equipment Rentals related party lease was appropriately identified, priced, and disclosed under CAS 550.
model_response.analyze_case:
Percentage-of-completion estimate reliability (ASPE 3400). The percentage-of-completion calculation is arithmetically verified in Exhibit 1: at $11,960,000 costs incurred against the revised $16,800,000 total estimated cost, the contract is 71.19% complete, cumulative revenue recognized is $13,099,000, and cumulative gross profit recognized is $1,139,000, down from $1,428,000 cumulative at the prior year end. This produces a current year loss of $289,000 on the contract, a sharp reversal from the $1,282,000 current-year profit the preliminary forecast would have shown under the original $15,000,000 estimate. Under the audit risk model, inherent risk is elevated because percentage-of-completion accounting inherently depends on management estimates of costs to complete, which CAS 540 identifies as an area prone to management bias, and because Exhibit 2 shows the CEO directly prompted the controller to revisit the estimate specifically because the preliminary profit figure looked too high relative to actual progress and in the context of a bank covenant concern, not because of an independent, documented change order process. This sequence, profit looks too high, CEO asks for it to be revised down, controller obtains a new number informally from the project manager without a signed change order or documented basis, is a classic fraud risk indicator under CAS 240 relating to management override of controls and manipulation of accounting estimates, even though in this instance the direction of the adjustment reduced rather than inflated profit, which itself is unusual and worth exploring further, since a covenant-driven motive could point either direction depending on whether the concern was breaching a profitability-linked covenant or, conversely, wanting to avoid an implausibly large one-time profit spike that invites lender scrutiny. Critically, the controller's own email flags that the mechanical change order pricing underlying part of the $16,800,000 revised estimate is not yet supported by signed subcontractor pricing, meaning the estimate itself has a documented, unresolved uncertainty. This is a significant risk requiring an estimate confirmed only by verbal PM input and unsigned subcontractor pricing, evaluated during a period of heightened CEO attention to the reported outcome, warrants specific substantive procedures rather than reliance on management's estimate as presented.

Related party transaction (Marlow Equipment Rentals, CAS 550). The relationship meets the definition of a related party under ASPE, as Marlow is 100% owned by the CEO's spouse. The transaction is a significant risk for two reasons. First, pricing: the independent market comparison in Exhibit 3 shows Delacroix paid $840,000 against an average independent quote of $620,000, a $220,000 excess, approximately 35% above market, which is not a transaction in the normal course of business at terms equivalent to an arm's length transaction and requires specific ASPE related party disclosure of the relationship, the transaction amount, and the measurement basis used. Second, and more concerning from a risk assessment standpoint, the relationship was not on the controller's related party schedule provided to the audit team, and was instead identified independently by the audit team through discussion with operations staff during CAS 315 risk assessment procedures. This is an indicator that management's related party identification process, and by extension the completeness of the representations we will rely on, cannot be trusted without independent corroboration, which is consistent with CAS 550's emphasis that related party relationships and transactions pose an inherent fraud risk due to the potential for undisclosed side arrangements and non-arm's length pricing that management, rather than independently disclosing, must be actively tested against. Robert Delacroix's explanation, that the arrangement predates current scrutiny and has never previously been questioned by the bank, does not address the disclosure completeness gap or the pricing variance and should be treated with professional skepticism given his direct financial interest, through his spousal relationship, in Marlow's revenue.
model_response.recommendation:
On the Northgate estimate, I recommend the team obtain the underlying support for the revised $16,800,000 total estimated cost, including the project manager's detailed cost-to-complete workup, any available subcontractor correspondence on the mechanical change order even if unsigned, and evidence of the soil conditions cost impact such as the geotechnical report and any resulting change order documentation with the client. We should interview the project manager directly, independent of the controller, to corroborate the basis for the revision and assess whether it aligns with the physical progress observed. A site visit or discussion with the site superintendent to corroborate percent complete based on physical observation, not just cost incurred, would provide useful corroborating evidence. Given the CEO's direct involvement in prompting the revision documented in Exhibit 2, we should extend our discussion with those charged with governance, ideally involving an audit committee member or the passive trust's representative if available, rather than relying solely on discussions with the CEO and controller. We should sensitize the estimate to a reasonable range, for example testing the impact if the mechanical change order settles higher or lower than currently estimated, and assess whether the resulting range of gross profit remains within performance materiality of $157,000.

On the related party transaction, I recommend the team formally document the Marlow relationship, obtain the actual lease agreement and payment history for the full year, and confirm whether any other undisclosed related party arrangements exist by expanding inquiries to other operations and site personnel, not only the controller. We should recommend management correct the related party disclosure schedule and ensure Delacroix's notes to the financial statements disclose the Marlow relationship, the $840,000 transaction amount, and a description of the measurement basis (which given the market comparison, is not at an amount that approximates the exchange amount at arm's length). We should discuss with James Okonkwo whether the $220,000 pricing excess, while not itself a misstatement requiring adjustment for an expense that was legitimately incurred, together with the completeness gap in the related party schedule, elevates our overall assessment of management integrity and control environment risk for the remainder of the engagement.
model_response.conclusion: Both issues require escalation to the engagement partner and likely a discussion with those charged with governance before the file can be finalized. If the Northgate cost-to-complete revision, after our substantive testing, is found to be reasonably supportable, the revised percentage-of-completion figures in Exhibit 1 can stand, but if support is lacking, we should propose an adjustment and consider the implications for other estimates across the Company's contract portfolio. The Marlow related party transaction requires corrected disclosure regardless of pricing conclusions, and the fact that it was not volunteered by the controller should factor into our overall fraud risk and management representation reliability assessment for the balance of the audit.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly recalculates percent complete (71.19%), cumulative revenue ($13,099,000), and current year gross profit/(loss) ($(289,000)) and verifies internal consistency against prior year figures
Identifies the estimate revision as a significant risk / fraud risk indicator under CAS 240 given the CEO-prompted, informally-documented, unsigned-support nature of the revision

Recognizes the unusual direction of the estimate change (reducing rather than inflating profit) and reasons about possible motives (covenant management, smoothing) rather than assuming manipulation is always profit-inflating
Recommends specific procedures to corroborate the revised cost-to-complete estimate independent of the controller (PM interview, site visit, geotechnical/change order documentation)
Identifies Marlow Equipment Rentals as a related party under CAS 550 and recognizes it was omitted from the controller's related party schedule
Quantifies the pricing variance ($220,000 / ~35% above the $620,000 average independent quote) using the case exhibits
Connects the undisclosed related party relationship to overall management representation reliability and control environment risk assessment
Recommends sensitivity/range testing of the estimate against performance materiality of $157,000
Recommends expanding related party inquiries beyond the controller to operations/site personnel
Correctly applies ASPE 3400 percentage-of-completion mechanics (revenue = contract price x percent complete based on cost-to-cost)
Identifies the ASPE related party disclosure requirements applicable to the Marlow transaction (relationship, amount, measurement basis)
Recognizes the bank covenant context (current ratio 1.2:1, debt to equity 2.0:1) as relevant to understanding management's motivation and to assessing disclosure/opinion implications

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Structures the response using Understand, Analyze, Recommend, Conclude, integrating both issues rather than treating them as unrelated

marking_guide.competency: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
marking_guide.criterion: Applies professional skepticism to both the CEO's explanation for the estimate revision and his explanation for the Marlow pricing arrangement
Memo is clearly organized for the partner audience, with quantified conclusions rather than vague concerns
Recommends communication with those charged with governance given the CEO's direct involvement in prompting the estimate change

rating_guidance: A Competent (C) response correctly recalculates the percentage-of-completion figures, identifies both the estimate reliability risk and the related party disclosure gap, cites CAS 240, CAS 550, and ASPE 3400 accurately, and quantifies both the $289,000 current-year swing and the $220,000 pricing variance. A Reaching Competence (RC) response identifies the two issues and applies the right standards generically but does not fully connect the CEO's email involvement to a fraud risk conclusion, or quantifies only one of the two issues, or misses that the related party schedule omission itself is evidence relevant to overall audit risk. A Not Competent (NC) response miscalculates the percent complete or gross profit figures, treats the estimate revision as routine without considering the CEO-prompted context, or fails to identify Marlow as a related party at all. A Competent with Distinction (CD) response additionally reasons carefully about the unusual direction of the estimate change (profit-reducing rather than profit-inflating) and its possible link to covenant management rather than assuming a single simplistic manipulation narrative, explicitly ties the related party disclosure omission to a broader reassessment of management representation reliability for the rest of the engagement, and proposes concrete, independent corroboration procedures (PM interview, site visit, expanded staff inquiries) rather than generic testing language.

enabling_focus: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
Communicating
Managing Self
title: Northbridge Industrial Products Inc.: Group Audit and Warranty Provision
case_background:
You, CPA, are the senior on the audit engagement of Northbridge Industrial Products Inc. (Northbridge or the Company), a Canadian-controlled manufacturer of hydraulic components headquartered in Cambridge, Ontario. Northbridge is privately owned by three founding families, but it carries $58,000,000 of long-term debt under a syndicated credit facility and is required under its lending covenants to deliver audited consolidated financial statements prepared in accordance with IFRS within 90 days of year end. Your firm, Sennett Reyes LLP, has audited Northbridge for eleven years. The current year end is December 31, 2026.

Five years ago, Northbridge established a wholly owned manufacturing subsidiary, Northbridge Vietnam Ltd. (NVL), in Bien Hoa, Vietnam, to lower labour costs for a subset of the Company's hydraulic valve and cylinder product lines. NVL manufactures components that are shipped to Cambridge for final assembly and sold directly to regional distributors across Southeast Asia. For the year ended December 31, 2026, Northbridge's consolidated revenue was $185,000,000 and NVL's standalone revenue, translated to Canadian dollars, was $40,700,000, representing 22.0 percent of consolidated revenue. NVL also holds 26 percent of consolidated inventory and 19 percent of consolidated property, plant and equipment. Consolidated income before tax for the year is $13,900,000.

Because Sennett Reyes LLP does not maintain a Vietnamese practice office, the group engagement partner engaged Tran & Partners, an independent accounting firm in Ho Chi Minh City with prior experience auditing manufacturing subsidiaries of Canadian and United States groups, to act as component auditor for NVL. This is the second year Tran & Partners has performed work over NVL for group reporting purposes. Last year's file review by the group team identified no significant concerns overall, but the group team specifically noted that Tran & Partners' documentation of fraud risk inquiries with NVL management was thinner than the group team expected, and this point was communicated back to Tran & Partners at the time.

You are responsible, under the group engagement partner's direction, for three things: first, determining whether NVL is a significant component requiring an audit of its complete financial information under CAS 600; second, finalizing the group instruction letter to Tran & Partners, specifying component materiality, the scope of work required, significant risks to be communicated, and reporting deadlines; and third, evaluating Tran & Partners' work papers and communications once received, including deciding whether the group team needs to be directly involved in reviewing NVL's audit evidence rather than relying solely on a summary report. You have calculated group (overall) materiality at 5 percent of consolidated income before tax, or $695,000, which you have rounded to $700,000 for planning purposes, consistent with the basis used in prior years given Northbridge's stable, income-generating operating history and the lending syndicate's use of the audited statements to monitor covenant compliance.

A second, unrelated matter has also arisen at year end. Northbridge's TitanFlow 400 Series hydraulic valve assembly, one of the Company's higher-margin product lines, carries an 18-month manufacturer's warranty against defects in workmanship and materials. In late October 2026, Northbridge's quality assurance department identified a batch of defective elastomer seals purchased from a new supplier, Delkor Seal Technologies, that had been installed in TitanFlow units manufactured and shipped between January and October 2026. Laboratory testing conducted by Northbridge's own engineering group in November confirmed that the affected seals degrade prematurely under sustained hydraulic pressure, causing fluid leakage and eventual valve failure. At December 31, 2026, 22,000 TitanFlow units sold during the trailing 18 months remain within their warranty period. Prior to the discovery, Northbridge's warranty model assumed a 1.5 percent claim rate for the TitanFlow line based on five years of claims history; management's engineering group now estimates, based on accelerated life testing of the affected seal batch, that the claim rate for units built during the affected window will rise to 9.5 percent. The average cost of a warranty repair, including parts, labour, and field technician travel, is $1,000 per unit.

Northbridge's controller has recorded a warranty provision balance of $2,340,000 at December 31, 2026, calculated using the historical 1.5 percent claim rate applied to the current year's expanded product base, consistent with the methodology used in the prior year, when the closing provision balance was $2,100,000. The controller's draft note disclosure states that the increase from the prior year reflects normal growth in the TitanFlow product line and does not reference the seal defect. You have been asked to assess whether the warranty provision is fairly stated and to draft your findings and recommended adjustments, if any, for the audit senior manager's review, along with your group audit planning conclusions regarding NVL, before the file is submitted for partner review next week. The engagement partner has also flagged that the credit syndicate's covenant compliance certificate, due at the same time as the audited statements, is sensitive to any late-identified adjustment, so your analysis needs to be well supported before it is raised with the client.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Draft Consolidated Financial Statement Excerpts (unaudited)
exhibits.content:
Northbridge Industrial Products Inc., Draft Consolidated Statement of Income (excerpt), year ended December 31, 2026, in Canadian dollars, unaudited. Revenue $185,000,000 (2025: $168,200,000), comprising Canadian operations $144,300,000 and NVL revenue, intercompany shipments eliminated, $40,700,000. Cost of sales $138,750,000. Gross profit $46,250,000. Income before tax $13,900,000.

Draft Note 14, Warranty Provision. The Company provides warranties on substantially all products sold, generally for periods of 12 to 24 months. A provision is recognized based on historical claims experience applied to units sold that remain within the warranty period at year end. Continuity of the provision for the year: balance, January 1, 2026, $2,100,000; warranty costs paid during the year, $(1,860,000); provision charged to income during the year, $2,100,000; balance, December 31, 2026, $2,340,000. Draft note wording (unaudited): 'The increase in the current year provision reflects normal growth in the TitanFlow 400 Series product line and is consistent with the Company's five-year historical claims experience.'

Draft Note 3, Group Structure and Segment Information. NVL, a wholly owned subsidiary incorporated in Vietnam, manufactures hydraulic valve and cylinder components for shipment to the Company's Cambridge, Ontario facility and for direct sale to regional distributors. At December 31, 2026, NVL represents the following percentages of consolidated totals: revenue 22.0 percent, inventory 26 percent, property, plant and equipment 19 percent. NVL's underlying financial records are prepared under Vietnamese accounting standards and are translated to IFRS and to Canadian dollars for consolidation purposes by NVL's local finance team, with the translation reviewed by the Company's corporate controller. NVL has no other related party relationships with Northbridge beyond the parent-subsidiary ownership and intercompany shipments at cost plus an agreed markup.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Draft Group Audit Instructions to Component Auditor
exhibits.content:
Sennett Reyes LLP, draft group audit instructions to component auditor, addressed to Tran & Partners, Ho Chi Minh City, Vietnam, re: audit of Northbridge Vietnam Ltd. for the year ended December 31, 2026, for group reporting purposes.

This letter confirms the scope of work required for group reporting purposes in connection with our audit of the consolidated financial statements of Northbridge Industrial Products Inc. (1) Scope of work: to be confirmed by the group team, either a full audit of NVL's complete financial information or specified audit procedures on certain accounts. (2) Component materiality: to be determined; group materiality has been set at $700,000 for planning purposes. (3) Reporting deadline: your report and completed group reporting package are required no later than February 20, 2027, to allow the group team sufficient time for review before the target audit opinion date of March 10, 2027. (4) Significant group-wide risks to be communicated to your team: revenue recognition cut-off for shipments to Canada near year end; inventory existence and valuation, including obsolescence tied to discontinued Canadian product lines; translation of NVL's local currency financial statements; and related party transactions with the Canadian parent, including intercompany shipment pricing. (5) Fraud risk: please document specific inquiries made of NVL management and, separately, those charged with governance at NVL, regarding their assessment of the risk of fraud, including any known or suspected instances of fraud, and how management has addressed the risk of management override of controls. Note per prior year file review: last year's documentation of these inquiries was insufficient; please expand documentation this year and discuss directly with the group engagement manager if any indicators are identified. (6) Please communicate immediately, rather than waiting for your final report, any identified misstatements above component performance materiality (to be inserted), any identified fraud or suspected fraud, and any scope limitations encountered. Group materiality, component materiality, and component performance materiality are to be finalized and inserted by the group engagement team prior to sending this letter.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 3: Internal Email Correspondence, TitanFlow Seal Defect
exhibits.content:
From Priya Anand, VP Operations, to Marc Delisle, Controller, dated November 18, 2026, subject TitanFlow seal issue, engineering results. Marc, following up on our call: engineering has finished the accelerated life testing on the Delkor seal batch. Units with the Delkor seals installed are failing at a much higher rate than our normal TitanFlow warranty experience. The test rig ran the equivalent of about 14 months of field use on 40 sample units, and we saw seal degradation and fluid leakage in roughly 9 to 10 percent of the sample. Engineering's formal written estimate, attached, is a 9.5 percent expected claim rate for units built with the affected seal lots, versus our normal 1.5 percent baseline for TitanFlow. We shipped TitanFlow units with Delkor seals from January through October this year before we caught it and switched suppliers back to Meridian Elastomers in late October. Procurement records show all units shipped in that window, and only that window, used the Delkor seals. Combined with the rest of the 18-month warranty population, that puts about 22,000 units currently within the warranty period covered by this analysis, per the sales team's unit count. Average repair cost per the field service team, including parts, labour, and technician travel for a valve replacement in the field, is running about $1,000 per unit based on claims processed so far under the defect. I know year end reporting is tight but wanted engineering's numbers in your hands before the audit team asks. Priya.

From Marc Delisle, Controller, to Priya Anand, dated November 19, 2026, subject RE: TitanFlow seal issue, engineering results. Thanks Priya. I will keep this on file. We will revisit the warranty provision methodology for next year's model. For this year I think our existing 1.5 percent claims-history-based provision is fine since it has been reliable historically; the higher rate is really more of a forward-looking planning number for engineering and procurement rather than something that changes this year's accounting. Marc.

requirements: Using the CPA Way, determine whether NVL is a significant component under CAS 600 and identify the group audit procedures the group engagement team should perform, including the content of your group instruction letter to Tran & Partners (including your determination of component materiality).

Assess whether Northbridge's warranty provision at December 31, 2026 is fairly stated in accordance with IAS 37, recommend any adjustment, and describe the audit procedures you would perform to obtain sufficient appropriate audit evidence over the revised estimate.

model_response.understand_issue: You are the audit senior on Northbridge, a privately owned, IFRS reporting manufacturer with a foreign subsidiary, NVL, and lending covenants that require audited consolidated financial statements. Two technical matters require analysis: group audit scoping and oversight of the component auditor, Tran & Partners, under CAS 600, and evaluation of a warranty provision estimate under IAS 37 following the year end discovery of a defective seal batch. The audience is the audit manager and engagement partner, and ultimately the lending syndicate relying on the audited statements. You are required to determine NVL's significance and design group instructions and procedures, and to evaluate management's warranty estimate for reasonableness, recommend any adjustment, and design procedures to substantiate the revised accrual.
model_response.analyze_case:
NVL represents 22.0 percent of consolidated revenue, 26 percent of consolidated inventory, and 19 percent of consolidated property, plant and equipment, comfortably above the thresholds commonly used to identify a component whose financial information is individually significant to the group under CAS 600. Because NVL's financial information is likely to include amounts individually significant to the group, CAS 600 requires the group engagement team to be involved in NVL's risk assessment and to determine the type of work performed on NVL's financial information; given NVL's size, the group team should require Tran & Partners to perform a full audit of NVL's complete financial information using component materiality, not merely specified procedures on selected accounts. Component materiality must be set lower than group materiality to reduce, to an appropriately low level, the probability that the aggregate of uncorrected and undetected misstatements across all components exceeds group materiality; using a common starting point of roughly 75 percent of group materiality gives component materiality of approximately $525,000, subject to professional judgment about aggregation risk across the rest of the group.

The prior year note that Tran & Partners' fraud inquiry documentation was thin is a specific risk indicator requiring follow up this year. Under CAS 600, the group engagement team must obtain an understanding of the component auditor, including competence and independence, and must be sufficiently involved in the component auditor's risk assessment procedures, including inquiries about fraud risk factors and management override of controls at the component. Given the identified prior year deficiency, the group instruction letter should explicitly require Tran & Partners to expand documentation of fraud inquiries with NVL management and those charged with governance, and the group team should plan a work paper review, whether remotely or through a visit, rather than relying solely on Tran & Partners' summary report, consistent with the heightened involvement CAS 600 expects for significant components with a known prior deficiency.

On the warranty provision, under IAS 37 a provision is recognized only when there is a present obligation, legal or constructive, as a result of a past event, it is probable that an outflow of resources will be required to settle the obligation, and a reliable estimate can be made. Northbridge has a present legal obligation under its warranty terms for the 22,000 TitanFlow units still within the 18-month warranty period at year end; an outflow is probable given the confirmed engineering test results showing seal degradation; and the amount, while an estimate, can be reliably estimated using the Company's own accelerated life testing data. IAS 37 further requires the provision to reflect the best estimate of the expenditure required to settle the obligation as at the reporting date, using the most current, most reliable information available, which necessarily includes information obtained after the reporting period about conditions that existed at year end.

The controller's $2,340,000 provision, based on the pre-defect 1.5 percent historical claim rate, is not the best available estimate because it disregards evidence Northbridge itself generated, its own engineering testing completed in November 2026, about a condition that existed in the units at the time they were manufactured and sold, before year end. This is not a new obligation arising after year end; it is evidence that refines the estimate of an obligation that already existed at December 31, 2026, and it must be reflected in the year end estimate rather than deferred to next year's model, contrary to Marc Delisle's stated position in Exhibit 3. Applying the revised claim rate: incremental units at risk equal 22,000 multiplied by the 8.0 percentage point increase in claim rate (9.5 percent minus 1.5 percent), equal to 1,760 units, multiplied by the $1,000 average repair cost, equal to $1,760,000 of additional provision required. The total appropriate provision is therefore $2,340,000 plus $1,760,000, equal to $4,100,000, meaning the draft financial statements understate the warranty provision by $1,760,000. This understatement exceeds group materiality of $700,000 by a wide margin and is material, both on its own and because of the misleading note disclosure that omits any reference to the known defect.
model_response.recommendation:
For the group audit, I recommend the group engagement partner conclude that NVL is a significant component under CAS 600 and instruct Tran & Partners to perform a full-scope audit of NVL's complete financial information for group reporting purposes, using component materiality of approximately $525,000 and a component performance materiality of roughly 75 percent of that amount, approximately $394,000. The group instruction letter in Exhibit 2 should be finalized to insert these materiality figures and to specify: the significant group-wide risks already listed, including revenue cut-off, inventory valuation, currency translation, and related party pricing; an explicit requirement that Tran & Partners document specific fraud inquiries with NVL management and those charged with governance and communicate any identified fraud risk factors to the group team without delay; and a reporting deadline that allows the group team adequate time to review Tran & Partners' work papers before the group opinion is finalized. Given NVL's significance and last year's documentation gap, I recommend the group team hold a planning discussion directly with Tran & Partners on fraud risk assessment and review selected NVL work papers, particularly on inventory existence and valuation and the fraud inquiry documentation, rather than relying solely on a summary memorandum from the component auditor.

For the warranty provision, I recommend proposing an audit adjustment increasing the provision by $1,760,000, from $2,340,000 to $4,100,000, and revising the note disclosure to describe the Delkor seal defect, the revised 9.5 percent claim rate assumption, and the population of units affected. Audit procedures should include: obtaining and testing the engineering department's accelerated life testing report supporting the 9.5 percent claim rate; corroborating that rate, where possible, against actual claims experience emerging in early 2027 as a subsequent events procedure; independently recalculating the provision using the 22,000 unit population and the $1,000 average repair cost; agreeing the 22,000 unit population to sales and shipment records for the trailing 18-month warranty window and confirming the affected units were shipped within the January to October 2026 Delkor seal window; testing the $1,000 average repair cost to a sample of actual warranty claims paid to date under the defect; and inquiring of legal counsel regarding any threatened claims, disputes, or potential recovery from Delkor Seal Technologies.
model_response.conclusion: NVL meets the definition of a significant component under CAS 600 given it represents 22.0 percent of consolidated revenue and comparable proportions of inventory and property, plant and equipment, requiring a full-scope audit by Tran & Partners under group instructions specifying component materiality of approximately $525,000 and explicit expanded fraud-inquiry documentation, with direct group team review of key work papers given last year's noted deficiency. Separately, the warranty provision as drafted understates Northbridge's obligation by $1,760,000, a material misstatement under IAS 37 because known post-year-end engineering evidence about a condition existing at year end was not reflected in the estimate. The audit team should propose an adjustment to $4,100,000 with revised disclosure. If management corrects both matters and Tran & Partners' completed work supports an unmodified opinion on NVL, an unmodified audit opinion on Northbridge's consolidated financial statements remains appropriate.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly identifies NVL as a significant component under CAS 600 based on the quantitative data provided (22.0% of consolidated revenue)
Calculates group materiality (approximately $700,000) and derives a defensible component materiality lower than group materiality (e.g., approximately $525,000)
Identifies that a full audit of NVL's complete financial information, not merely specified procedures, is required given NVL's significance
Identifies the prior year fraud-inquiry documentation gap as a specific risk requiring follow-up under CAS 600's requirements for group team involvement in a component's risk assessment
Recommends group team involvement beyond relying solely on the component auditor's summary report (e.g., work paper review or direct discussion)
Identifies intercompany or related party transactions between Northbridge and NVL as an area requiring group-level procedures on consolidation
Correctly applies the three IAS 37 recognition criteria (present obligation, probable outflow, reliable estimate) to the warranty provision
Correctly reasons that the post year-end engineering evidence relates to a condition existing at year end and must be reflected in the year end estimate rather than treated as a future-year matter
Recalculates the required incremental provision accurately (22,000 units x 8.0 percentage points x $1,000 = $1,760,000)
Concludes the total appropriate provision is $4,100,000 and correctly quantifies the $1,760,000 understatement
Concludes the misstatement is material relative to group materiality of approximately $700,000
Identifies that the draft note disclosure omitting the known defect is also deficient and recommends revised disclosure
Designs specific, relevant procedures to test the revised warranty estimate (recalculation, testing claim rate and cost inputs, subsequent claims data, legal inquiry)

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Uses the CPA Way structure and integrates both issues into a coherent overall conclusion on the audit opinion
Response is well organized, uses appropriate audit and accounting terminology, and would be understandable to the engagement manager or partner
Prioritizes the two required issues appropriately given the time available, addressing both without excessive detail on lower-risk areas
Recognizes NVL's inventory (26%) and property, plant and equipment (19%) concentration as additional support for significance beyond revenue alone
Notes that the provision estimate should use the Company's own most current and reliable information (the accelerated life testing) consistent with the IAS 37 best estimate requirement

rating_guidance: A Competent (C) response correctly identifies NVL as a significant component under CAS 600 using the quantitative data provided, calculates group and component materiality on a defensible basis, and specifies concrete group instruction letter content including fraud-inquiry follow-up tied to the prior year deficiency. On the warranty issue, a C response applies all three IAS 37 recognition criteria, correctly reasons that the November engineering evidence must be reflected in the year end estimate because it relates to a condition existing at year end, and recalculates the $1,760,000 incremental provision with correct arithmetic, concluding the misstatement is material. A Competent with Distinction (CD) response goes further: it explicitly links required group team involvement to the specific prior year documentation gap rather than generic CAS 600 language, addresses intercompany or related party risk on consolidation, proposes revised disclosure in addition to the adjustment, and reaches a single, well reasoned conclusion on the overall audit opinion. A Reaching Competence (RC) response identifies the two issues and the relevant standards but makes a computational or conceptual error, such as omitting the reliable-estimate criterion, using group materiality where component materiality was required, or failing to quantify the misstatement. A Not Competent (NC) response fails to identify CAS 600 or IAS 37 as relevant, ignores the numerical data provided, or does not reach a recommendation on either issue.

enabling_focus: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
Communicating
Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
title: Maple Grove Family Services: Review Engagement and Restricted Contributions
case_background:
You, CPA, are engaged by Maple Grove Family Services (Maple Grove or the Society), a registered Canadian charity headquartered in Kitchener, Ontario, that provides emergency shelter, counselling, and family reunification programs to individuals experiencing homelessness. Maple Grove's board of directors, all volunteers, has engaged your firm, Osei Bergman LLP, to perform a review engagement on the Society's financial statements for the year ended June 30, 2026, in accordance with Canadian Standard on Review Engagements (CSRE) 2400, Engagements to Review Historical Financial Statements. Maple Grove's financial statements are prepared in accordance with Canadian accounting standards for not-for-profit organizations (ASNPO), Part III of the CPA Canada Handbook. This is the third year your firm has performed the review; a review engagement, rather than an audit, has historically been sufficient to satisfy the reporting requirements of Maple Grove's two largest funders, the Region of Waterloo and a private family foundation, neither of which requires an audit under its current funding agreement with the Society.

For the year ended June 30, 2026, Maple Grove's contributions and other revenue totalled $3,200,000, comprising $2,100,000 of unrestricted operating donations and government grants, $250,000 of program fee revenue, and $850,000 of donor-restricted contributions received during the year specifically for the Northgate Shelter Expansion Project, a capital campaign to add 24 emergency beds and a new intake wing to the Society's existing shelter on Northgate Avenue. The Northgate campaign was publicly launched in September 2025, following board approval, with a fundraising goal of $1,000,000. Donors were told, both in campaign materials and in individual donor acknowledgment letters, that their contributions would be used exclusively for the design, permitting, and construction costs of the shelter expansion.

Maple Grove's accounting policy note states that the Society follows the deferral method of accounting for contributions under ASNPO Section 4410, under which restricted contributions related to expenses of future periods are deferred and recognized as revenue in the period in which the related expenses are incurred. This is consistent with the approach Maple Grove has used in prior years and is an acceptable alternative to the restricted fund method also permitted under Section 4410.

During your review engagement planning, you obtained a schedule prepared by Maple Grove's part-time bookkeeper, Realene Kowalski, summarizing Northgate project activity for the year: $850,000 of restricted contributions received, and $620,000 charged against the project in the general ledger, leaving a bookkeeper-calculated deferred contribution balance of $230,000 at June 30, 2026. However, when you performed analytical procedures comparing the Northgate project ledger detail to the construction contractor's progress billings and the campaign's board-approved budget, you noted that $75,000 of the $620,000 charged to the project relates to catering, venue rental, and printed materials for the Society's annual fall fundraising gala, an event unrelated to the physical construction of the shelter expansion, held in November 2025. When you raised this with Realene in an inquiry call, she explained that she had been instructed by the outgoing executive director, who left the Society in January 2026, to code a portion of the gala costs to the Northgate project because that year's gala program featured a fundraising appeal specifically for Northgate. Realene was not able to point to any board resolution or written donor communication authorizing gala costs to be funded from the restricted campaign contributions.

You are aware that a review engagement under CSRE 2400 provides only limited assurance, expressed as negative assurance, and consists primarily of inquiry and analytical procedures rather than the tests of controls, corroborating third party confirmations, and detailed substantive testing that would be performed in an audit. Nonetheless, whether donor-restricted contributions have in fact been used for their donor-intended purpose, and whether the related ASNPO accounting is correct, are matters you are required to consider in forming your review conclusion, and the situation also raises broader questions about Maple Grove's governance and stewardship of restricted funds following the recent change in executive leadership. The engagement manager has asked you to document your analysis of the deferred contribution accounting and the gala cost coding issue, describe the review procedures, as distinct from audit procedures, that you would perform to address this matter, and draft the conclusion you would communicate on whether Maple Grove's financial statements are free of material misstatement to the extent required for a review engagement, ahead of a board audit and finance committee meeting next week at which the new interim executive director will be present. The finance committee chair has indicated the board wants a clear, documented explanation before that meeting, given the change in leadership and the sensitivity of any issue involving restricted donor funds.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Draft Statement of Operations and Note Excerpts (unaudited)
exhibits.content:
Maple Grove Family Services, draft Statement of Operations (excerpt), year ended June 30, 2026, in Canadian dollars. Revenue: unrestricted donations and government grants $2,100,000; program fee revenue $250,000; contributions recognized as revenue, restricted, Northgate Shelter Expansion Project, $620,000; total revenue $2,970,000. Note: total restricted contributions received in the year, before deferral, were $850,000; $230,000 of this amount remains reflected in deferred contributions on the draft statement of financial position below rather than in the total revenue figure above.

Draft Statement of Financial Position (excerpt). Deferred contributions, Northgate Shelter Expansion Project: $230,000.

Draft Note 2, Significant Accounting Policies (excerpt). Revenue recognition: the Society follows the deferral method of accounting for contributions, as permitted under the Canadian accounting standards for not-for-profit organizations, Section 4410. Restricted contributions for expenses of future periods are deferred and recognized as revenue in the period in which the related expenses are incurred. Unrestricted contributions are recognized as revenue when received or receivable if the amount can be reasonably estimated and collection is reasonably assured.

Draft Note 9, Deferred Contributions. Contributions received for the Northgate Shelter Expansion Project are restricted by the donors for design, permitting, and construction costs of the Society's planned 24-bed shelter expansion. Balance, July 1, 2025: nil. Contributions received during the year: $850,000. Amounts recognized as revenue during the year: $(620,000). Balance, June 30, 2026: $230,000. The Northgate campaign has a total fundraising goal of $1,000,000, of which $850,000 has been received to date. Construction is expected to be completed during the 2027 fiscal year.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Northgate Project Ledger Detail and Contractor Progress Billing Summary
exhibits.content:
Maple Grove Family Services, Northgate Shelter Expansion Project general ledger detail, year ended June 30, 2026, prepared by R. Kowalski, bookkeeper. Charges recorded to the project: architectural design fees, Ferro Wynn Architects, August 2025, $85,000; building permit application fee, City of Kitchener, September 2025, $12,000; site survey and geotechnical report, Bramwell Engineering, October 2025, $28,000; fall gala catering, Riverside Catering Co., November 2025, $41,000; fall gala venue rental, Kitchener Event Centre, November 2025, $22,000; fall gala printed materials and signage, QuickPrint Kitchener, November 2025, $12,000; construction progress billings, six draws between December 2025 and June 2026, Harlow Construction Group, $420,000. Total charged to the Northgate project ledger: $620,000.

Contractor progress billing summary, Harlow Construction Group, per signed construction contract dated August 2025 with a total contract value of $780,000: Draw 1, December 2025, site preparation, $65,000; Draw 2, January 2026, foundation, $70,000; Draw 3, February 2026, framing, $75,000; Draw 4, April 2026, roofing and building envelope, $80,000; Draw 5, May 2026, mechanical and electrical rough-in, $65,000; Draw 6, June 2026, interior framing, $65,000. Total billed to date: $420,000, agreeing to the construction draws recorded in the ledger detail above.

Note prepared during review fieldwork: design, permitting, survey, and construction draws recorded against the project total $545,000 (calculated as $85,000 plus $12,000 plus $28,000 plus $420,000). The three gala-related line items above, totalling $75,000, do not appear on the contractor's billing schedule or in any other construction-related vendor record reviewed for the project.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 3: Email Correspondence and Board Minutes Excerpts
exhibits.content:
Email from Realene Kowalski, bookkeeper, to David Okonkwo, executive director (former), dated November 25, 2025, subject coding the gala costs. David, quick question on the fall gala invoices. Since the appeal at the gala was for the Northgate expansion, do you want me to code the catering, venue, and printing costs to the Northgate project instead of general fundraising expense? Just want to make sure it is coded right for year end.

Reply from David Okonkwo to Realene Kowalski, dated November 26, 2025, subject RE: coding the gala costs. Yes, go ahead and code a portion of it to Northgate since that is what we were raising money for that night. Doesn't need to go to the board, it's just an internal coding question.

Note: David Okonkwo's employment with Maple Grove Family Services ended January 15, 2026. No related item appears in the board minutes for the November 2025, January 2026, or March 2026 board meetings.

Excerpt, minutes of the Maple Grove Family Services board of directors meeting, September 10, 2025. Moved by A. Ferreira, seconded by T. Singh: that the board approve the public launch of the Northgate Shelter Expansion capital campaign with a fundraising goal of $1,000,000, to be used exclusively for architectural design, permitting, and construction costs of the 24-bed shelter expansion and new intake wing, and that all Northgate campaign donor communications confirm this restricted use of funds. Carried.

Excerpt, minutes of the Maple Grove Family Services board of directors meeting, March 18, 2026, finance committee report. The finance committee reported that the Northgate campaign has raised $850,000 to date against the $1,000,000 goal and that construction is proceeding on schedule. No concerns were raised regarding use of campaign funds.

requirements: Using the CPA Way, evaluate whether Maple Grove's accounting for the Northgate restricted contributions and the related deferred contribution balance is in accordance with ASNPO, including the effect of the $75,000 gala cost coding issue, and recommend any correction.

Describe the review procedures you would perform under CSRE 2400 to address this matter, contrasting them with what an audit would require, and draft the conclusion you would communicate regarding the financial statements as a whole.

model_response.understand_issue: You are performing a CSRE 2400 review engagement, not an audit, for Maple Grove, a registered charity reporting under ASNPO. The board and the Society's two main funders are the intended users and have historically accepted limited assurance. Two intertwined issues arise: whether the $850,000 Northgate restricted contribution and the resulting deferred contribution balance are correctly accounted for under the deferral method in ASNPO Section 4410, and whether $75,000 of costs charged to the project were actually used for the donor-restricted purpose. You are required to analyze the accounting, describe the applicable review, as opposed to audit, procedures, and draft the review conclusion for the board meeting.
model_response.analyze_case:
Maple Grove has elected the deferral method under ASNPO Section 4410 for restricted contributions. Under this method, a restricted contribution related to expenses of a future period is initially recognized as a liability, deferred contributions, and is recognized as revenue only in the period or periods in which the related expenses are incurred. This is distinct from the restricted fund method, under which a restricted contribution is recognized as revenue directly in a restricted fund in the period received, provided a restricted fund has been established for that purpose. Maple Grove's consistent use of the deferral method is an acceptable accounting policy, but it also means the full $850,000 received cannot simply be recognized as revenue in the year of receipt; only the portion for which the related, eligible project expenditures have actually been incurred should be recognized as revenue, with the remaining balance carried forward as a deferred contribution liability on the statement of financial position.

Applying the bookkeeper's schedule at face value, $620,000 of expenditures were incurred against the project, leaving $230,000, calculated as $850,000 minus $620,000, properly deferred. However, the analytical procedures performed identified that $75,000 of the $620,000 relates to gala catering, venue rental, and printed materials, costs of a general fundraising event, not costs of designing, permitting, or constructing the shelter expansion as described to donors in the campaign materials and individual acknowledgment letters and as approved by the board in the September 2025 minutes. These gala costs do not meet the definition of expenses related to the restricted purpose for which the Northgate contributions were solicited and accepted; recognizing revenue against them, or treating them as a valid use of the restricted funds, is not supported by the evidence obtained. There is no board resolution or documented donor communication authorizing this reallocation, and the explanation obtained from the bookkeeper, that she was instructed verbally by an executive director who has since left the organization, is not persuasive support for proper authorization. This also raises a governance and stewardship concern: restricted donor funds appear to have been used, without board approval, for a purpose donors did not authorize, which could expose Maple Grove to reputational risk with major donors and to scrutiny regarding use of funds consistent with its charitable purposes.

Correcting for the $75,000, only $545,000, calculated as $85,000 plus $12,000 plus $28,000 plus $420,000, of expenditures qualify as eligible project costs incurred against the restricted contribution, as this amount agrees to the contractor's progress billing schedule and other construction-related vendor records in Exhibit 2. Under the deferral method, the deferred contribution balance at June 30, 2026 should therefore be $850,000 minus $545,000, equal to $305,000, which is $75,000 higher than the bookkeeper's calculated $230,000, and revenue recognized against the Northgate contribution for the year should be $545,000, not $620,000. As drafted, revenue is overstated by $75,000 and the deferred contribution liability is understated by $75,000. Quantitatively, $75,000 represents approximately 2.5 percent of Maple Grove's actual total revenue on the draft Statement of Operations of $2,970,000, which by itself may appear modest against typical materiality benchmarks; however, given the qualitative sensitivity of restricted donor funds for a registered charity, where donor trust, funder relationships, and the risk of a use-of-funds complaint are significant to the users of these financial statements, I would treat this matter as material regardless of the quantitative threshold applied.
model_response.recommendation:
I recommend that Maple Grove correct the Northgate project accounting to reclassify the $75,000 of gala costs out of the project and into general fundraising expense, which reduces revenue recognized on the Northgate contribution from $620,000 to $545,000 and increases the deferred contribution liability from $230,000 to $305,000 at June 30, 2026. I would also recommend that the note disclosure describing restricted contributions be revised to separately present a continuity schedule of Northgate campaign activity, showing the opening deferred balance, contributions received, eligible expenses recognized as revenue, and the closing deferred balance, so that a reader can see how the restricted funds were used during the year.

Under CSRE 2400, the practitioner primarily performs inquiry of management and analytical procedures designed to identify plausible relationships among financial and non-financial data, and does not perform tests of controls, does not typically confirm balances with third parties, and does not perform the extent of substantive testing an audit would require, such as independent verification of contractor billings or detailed vouching of a full population of transactions. Here, my review procedures should include: inquiring of the interim executive director and the board treasurer about the authorization process for using restricted campaign funds, and requesting any board minutes discussing the Northgate campaign's use of funds beyond what has already been reviewed; performing an analytical comparison of the Northgate project ledger detail to the construction contractor's progress billing summary in Exhibit 2 to identify amounts, such as the gala costs, that do not correspond to construction-related billings; and reading the campaign's donor materials and acknowledgment letters to corroborate the donor-restricted purpose communicated at the time funds were solicited. If, after these review procedures, management does not agree to correct the $75,000 misclassification, I would need to consider whether this causes me to believe the financial statements are not, in all material respects, in accordance with ASNPO, which would require a qualified review engagement conclusion describing the matter, rather than the standard unmodified negative assurance conclusion.
model_response.conclusion: Maple Grove's use of the deferral method for the Northgate restricted contributions is an acceptable ASNPO accounting policy, but the $75,000 of gala costs improperly charged to the project understates deferred contributions and overstates revenue recognized during the year, a matter I consider material given the donor-restricted nature of the funds and the governance concern raised by the lack of board authorization for the reallocation. Assuming management agrees to correct the deferred contribution balance to $305,000 and revenue recognized to $545,000, and revises the related note disclosure, I would be able to issue the standard CSRE 2400 review engagement report, providing negative assurance that nothing has come to my attention that causes me to believe the financial statements are not, in all material respects, in accordance with ASNPO. If the correction is not made, a qualified review engagement conclusion, describing the matter and its effect, would be required instead.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly identifies the engagement as a CSRE 2400 review engagement providing limited, negative assurance, not an audit
Correctly contrasts review procedures (inquiry, analytical procedures) with audit procedures (tests of controls, third party confirmations, detailed substantive testing)

Correctly explains the deferral method under ASNPO Section 4410, including that unspent restricted contributions are carried as a deferred contribution liability and recognized as revenue as related expenses are incurred
Correctly distinguishes the deferral method from the restricted fund method permitted under Section 4410
Identifies through analytical comparison to the contractor billing schedule that $75,000 of gala costs do not correspond to construction-related expenditures
Recalculates the deferred contribution balance correctly as $305,000 ($850,000 minus $545,000) after removing the $75,000
Recalculates revenue recognized on the Northgate contribution correctly as $545,000
Identifies the lack of board authorization or written documentation for the gala cost reallocation as insufficient evidential support for the accounting treatment

marking_guide.competency: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
marking_guide.criterion: Identifies the governance and stewardship concern, that restricted donor funds were used without board approval for an unauthorized purpose, and its implications for donor trust and the charity's obligations
Assesses materiality appropriately, considering both the quantitative amount (approximately 2.5% of total revenue) and the qualitative sensitivity of donor-restricted funds
Designs specific review procedures tailored to this issue (inquiry of interim executive director and treasurer, analytical comparison to contractor billings, reading donor campaign materials)
Correctly concludes that if the misstatement remains uncorrected, a qualified review engagement conclusion, not an unmodified one, would be required
Correctly describes the negative assurance wording expected in an unmodified CSRE 2400 review engagement report
Recommends a revised note disclosure presenting a continuity schedule of the Northgate deferred contribution balance

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Uses the CPA Way structure and reaches an integrated conclusion connecting the accounting correction to the review engagement conclusion
Presents the analysis clearly, in language appropriate for a board audit and finance committee audience
Appropriately scopes the response to the two required issues without extending into unrelated review matters

Recognizes that even though a review provides only limited assurance, evidence obtained, such as the analytical variance identified, must still be followed up and cannot be dismissed merely because audit-level procedures were not performed

rating_guidance: A Competent (C) response correctly identifies the engagement as a CSRE 2400 review, contrasts inquiry and analytical procedures with audit-level procedures, and correctly applies the ASNPO deferral method to recalculate both the deferred contribution balance, $305,000, and revenue recognized, $545,000, after removing the $75,000 of gala costs, reaching a conclusion on whether an unmodified or qualified review report is appropriate depending on correction. A Competent with Distinction (CD) response additionally develops the governance and donor-stewardship implications of using restricted funds without board authorization, explicitly ties the analytical procedure result, the variance against contractor billings, to the practitioner's obligation to follow up even within a limited-assurance engagement, and integrates the qualitative materiality argument, donor trust and stewardship risk, rather than relying on the quantitative percentage alone. A Reaching Competence (RC) response identifies the review engagement standard and the deferral method concept but makes an error, such as miscalculating the deferred balance, describing review procedures using audit-level language like third party confirmations, or failing to reach a clear conclusion on the report type. A Not Competent (NC) response treats the engagement as an audit, fails to apply ASNPO Section 4410 concepts, or ignores the $75,000 gala cost variance entirely.

enabling_focus: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
Communicating
title: Northgate Ridge Developments: Revenue Cutoff and Management Override Risk
case_background:
Northgate Ridge Developments Ltd. (NRD) is a private, owner-managed real estate development company based in Kelowna, British Columbia, specializing in mid-rise residential condominium projects. NRD prepares its financial statements in accordance with Accounting Standards for Private Enterprises (ASPE) and has a December 31 fiscal year end. You, CPA, are the senior on the audit engagement for the year ended December 31, 2025, reporting to the engagement partner, Priya Nathwani.

NRD is 100 percent owned by its founder and president, Marcus Delaine, who has served as the sole director and controls all significant decisions. NRD's primary financing is a $12,000,000 revolving construction and term credit facility with Highland Trust Bank, renewed annually. The credit agreement contains a financial covenant requiring NRD to maintain net income before tax of at least $1,800,000 for each fiscal year, tested annually on delivery of the audited financial statements. A covenant breach entitles Highland Trust Bank to demand immediate repayment of the outstanding facility balance of $9,400,000, or to require Marcus to provide additional personal guarantees and collateral over his personal residence.

During planning, the engagement team learned that Marcus is finalizing a divorce settlement that requires a lump sum equalization payment of $2,400,000 to his former spouse by March 31, 2026. Marcus mentioned to Priya, informally, that he is also exploring a sale of a majority interest in NRD to a regional developer, Coastline Living Group, and that preliminary discussions have referenced a purchase price calculated as a multiple of NRD's audited pre-tax earnings. These two personal pressures both create a strong incentive for Marcus to maximize reported net income before tax for the year ended December 31, 2025.

Priya set overall materiality for the engagement at $100,000, based on 5 percent of budgeted net income before tax of $2,000,000, with performance materiality set at $70,000, given the covenant is tested directly against net income before tax and the users (Highland Trust Bank and any prospective purchaser) are highly sensitive to that specific figure.

NRD's most significant active project is The Aurora, a 68 unit condominium building in downtown Kelowna. Under NRD's revenue recognition policy for condominium sales, applied consistent with ASPE guidance on the transfer of risks and rewards, revenue on a unit sale is recognized when all significant risks and rewards of ownership have transferred to the buyer, defined by NRD's own accounting policy as the later of (a) the municipality issuing final occupancy approval for the unit, and (b) closing, meaning title transfer and receipt of purchase funds net of any unsatisfied financing condition. Prior to satisfaction of these conditions, deposits received are recorded as deferred revenue, a liability.

During year end fieldwork you identified three large manual journal entries posted between December 28 and December 30, 2025, recording revenue and a related receivable on the sale of 14 Aurora units to a single purchaser, Meridian Residential Holdings Inc., for total proceeds of $3,200,000. The entries were prepared and posted by NRD's controller, Denise Okafor, at Marcus's direct instruction, and debited Accounts Receivable, Aurora Development, and credited Condominium Sales Revenue. Cost of sales of $2,080,000, representing 65 percent of revenue and consistent with NRD's historical gross margin on Aurora units, was also recognized, with a corresponding release of work in progress inventory.

NRD's pre-adjustment net income before tax for the year, as recorded, is $2,050,000, which exceeds the $1,800,000 covenant threshold by $250,000. Your review of the underlying closing file for the Meridian transaction found that the City of Kelowna's final occupancy certificate for the relevant floors of The Aurora was not issued until January 15, 2026, and that the sale and purchase agreement with Meridian contained an unwaived financing condition that, per the agreement's own terms, was not satisfied until January 9, 2026, when Meridian's lender issued a firm mortgage commitment. Legal title did not transfer until closing occurred on January 15, 2026. Based on these facts, neither of NRD's own revenue recognition criteria was met as of December 31, 2025.

When you raised the timing of the journal entries against the occupancy certificate date with Denise, she was unusually quick to agree that the paperwork had probably just come in a bit late, and offered to provide a signed closing statement, but was unable to produce the actual occupancy certificate or the mortgage commitment letter when asked directly, and twice rescheduled a follow up meeting to review the file with you. Denise has been NRD's controller for eleven years and has generally been viewed by prior audit teams as reliable and cooperative.

If the $3,200,000 revenue and $2,080,000 cost of sales relating to the Meridian transaction are reversed, NRD's net income before tax would decrease by the resulting gross margin of $1,120,000, from $2,050,000 to $930,000, a figure well below the $1,800,000 covenant threshold and well in excess of the $100,000 overall materiality level, meaning it would trigger a reportable covenant breach to Highland Trust Bank.

Priya has asked you to document your understanding of the fraud risk, evaluate the appropriate accounting treatment for the Meridian transaction under ASPE, and design and perform audit procedures responsive to the risk of management override of controls in accordance with CAS 240.

exhibits.content:

The Meridian transaction (14 units, $3,200,000 revenue, $2,080,000 cost of sales, $1,120,000 gross margin) is the only revenue item posted after December 15, 2025 relating to unit sales for which the audit file does not yet contain an executed closing statement or occupancy certificate.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Email correspondence, December 27, 2025
exhibits.content:
From: Marcus Delaine, President
To: Denise Okafor, Controller
Sent: December 27, 2025, 6:41 PM
Subject: Aurora, Meridian units, please get this booked

Denise,

I need the Meridian deal on the books before year end. We are close on financing with them and I do not want to give Highland Trust any reason to look sideways at us this year, not with the lawyers on the settlement breathing down my neck already. Coastline is also asking for our year end numbers before they will move forward on the purchase discussion, so the stronger the number, the better position we are in on both fronts.

Go ahead and record the sale and the receivable for the 14 units at $3.2 million, same margin as usual. I know occupancy on that floor has not been signed off yet by the city and Meridian's bank has not given final approval, but everyone involved is telling me this is a formality at this point and it will all be signed by mid January. Substance over form, right? We both know this deal is happening.

Get it in before the 31st and let me know once it is done.

Marcus

---

From: Denise Okafor, Controller
To: Marcus Delaine, President
Sent: December 28, 2025, 8:15 AM
Subject: RE: Aurora, Meridian units, please get this booked

Marcus,

Understood, I will get the entries posted today. I will hold the supporting documents in a pending file until the occupancy certificate and the financing approval actually come through so we have something on file for the auditors.

Denise

exhibits.title: Exhibit 3: Auditor's summary of the Meridian closing file
exhibits.content:
Prepared by: Audit senior
Subject: Meridian Residential Holdings Inc., 14 units, The Aurora

Documents requested from Denise Okafor on January 20, 2026:
1. Fully executed sale and purchase agreement with Meridian Residential Holdings Inc.
2. City of Kelowna final occupancy certificate for floors 6 through 9 of The Aurora
3. Meridian's mortgage commitment letter from its lender
4. Closing statement and evidence of receipt of funds

Documents obtained:
1. Sale and purchase agreement, dated November 3, 2025, section 8.2, states the agreement is conditional on the purchaser obtaining, on or before the closing date, a firm mortgage commitment satisfactory to the purchaser, and that closing shall not occur until this condition is satisfied or waived in writing.
4. Closing statement, dated January 15, 2026, confirming legal title transferred and full purchase funds of $3,200,000 were received into NRD's trust account on that date.

Documents not yet obtained from Denise despite two requests (January 20 and January 27, 2026): the occupancy certificate and the mortgage commitment letter. Denise stated on January 27 that she believed both documents were finalized before year end but that she would need to check with the site superintendent and with Meridian's lawyer.

Independent corroboration obtained directly by the audit team:
- City of Kelowna building permits department, online records portal, confirms occupancy certificate for floors 6 through 9 of The Aurora was issued January 15, 2026.
- Written confirmation received directly from Meridian's lender, Coastal Pacific Credit Union, confirms the firm mortgage commitment to Meridian was issued January 9, 2026, and was not final or unconditional before that date.

Conclusion pending discussion with engagement partner: based on independently obtained evidence, neither the occupancy condition nor the financing condition in the Meridian agreement was satisfied as of December 31, 2025, and legal title did not transfer until January 15, 2026.

requirements: Identify and assess the fraud risk present in this engagement, applying CAS 240, and explain why the facts indicate a risk of management override of controls.
Evaluate the appropriate accounting treatment for the Meridian transaction under ASPE and quantify the required adjustment to net income before tax and to the covenant calculation.

Recommend specific audit procedures the team should perform in response to the identified risks, including procedures responsive to the manual journal entries and the reporting implications if management declines to record the adjustment.

model_response.understand_issue: This is an external audit engagement of a private, owner-managed company reporting under ASPE, addressed to engagement partner Priya Nathwani. The candidate is asked to act as audit senior and (1) assess a fraud risk under CAS 240 arising from management override indicators surrounding a specific condominium sale, (2) determine the correct ASPE revenue recognition treatment for that sale and quantify the resulting adjustment to net income before tax and the bank covenant calculation, and (3) recommend audit procedures responsive to the assessed risk, including how to respond if management resists the required correction.
model_response.analyze_case:
Applying the fraud triangle from CAS 240, all three elements are present. Incentive and pressure: Marcus faces a $2,400,000 divorce equalization payment due March 31, 2026, and is in preliminary sale discussions with Coastline Living Group where the purchase price is expected to be calculated as a multiple of audited pre-tax earnings; separately, NRD's $9,400,000 outstanding facility with Highland Trust Bank is conditional on maintaining net income before tax of at least $1,800,000. All three create direct, personal, and immediate financial incentives to overstate 2025 net income before tax. Opportunity: Marcus is the sole shareholder and sole director of NRD, giving him unilateral authority to direct accounting entries with no independent board oversight, and the controller, Denise, reports directly to him and complied with his instruction without independent verification of the underlying conditions. This is a textbook opportunity for management override, since the person able to override controls is also the person under personal pressure. Rationalization is evident in Marcus's own words in Exhibit 2 (substance over form, we both know this deal is happening), which reflects a rationalization that the timing difference is immaterial to the economics rather than a genuine accounting judgment.

CAS 240 paragraph 47 establishes a rebuttable presumption that there is a risk of fraud in revenue recognition. Nothing in this file rebuts that presumption; if anything, the facts confirm it. CAS 240 also requires the auditor to presume a risk of management override of controls exists in every audit, given management's unique ability to manipulate accounting records and override controls that otherwise appear to be operating effectively.
