rating_guidance: A competent (C) response must correctly quantify the fuel expense anomaly (approximately $2,096,000), connect it to the internal audit sample as corroborating rather than resolving evidence, correctly identify the going concern indicators including the customer loss percentage and covenant risk, and conclude that a material uncertainty exists with an appropriate discussion of audit report implications. A response reaching CD additionally recognizes the interaction between the IFRS 16 transition and the DSCR covenant (that moving lease expense into depreciation and interest increases reported EBITDA, which affects the very ratio management is relying on to claim compliance), and explicitly identifies that management's full-year 1.27x DSCR calculation does not match the trailing-twelve-month basis actually specified in the note agreement, which is the specific reasoning gap in management's own projection that a strong response must catch rather than simply repeating management's stated conclusion. A response capped at RC identifies the three issues exist and proposes generic procedures (recompute leases, test fuel transactions, evaluate going concern) without the specific quantification or without catching the trailing-twelve-month covenant mismatch. An NC response accepts management's fuel card explanation or its full-year DSCR compliance claim at face value, fails to identify the segregation-of-duties gap as a fraud risk factor, or omits a conclusion on going concern and the audit report entirely, since the case is specifically designed to test whether the candidate independently re-derives the covenant and fuel figures rather than accepting management's own framing of them.

enabling_focus: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
Adding Value
Communicating
title: Northern Fabrication Ltd: Make-or-Buy Decision for Piston Rod Assemblies
case_background:
Northern Fabrication Ltd (NFL) is a privately owned manufacturer of hydraulic cylinders based in Nisku, Alberta. NFL supplies cylinders to oil and gas service companies and heavy construction equipment dealers across Western Canada. The company was founded twenty-two years ago by machinist Doug Kowalchuk, who still owns 70 percent of the shares, with the remaining 30 percent held by his two adult children, who both work in the business. Doug is nearing retirement and has asked you, CPA, an external consultant, to review a sourcing decision that will affect the plant's cost structure well into the succession period.

NFL currently manufactures the piston rod assembly, a core component in every cylinder it builds, entirely in-house on a dedicated production line. Annual production volume is 20,000 units, matching current cylinder output. The plant operations manager, Priya Nandwani, recently received an unsolicited quote from a Saskatchewan-based precision parts supplier, Wheatland Precision Machining, offering to supply the identical piston rod assembly, built to NFL's exact specifications and tolerances, at a delivered price of 38.00 dollars per unit plus freight of 1.50 dollars per unit. Priya is skeptical that buying could possibly be cheaper than making, since NFL's own cost accounting system shows a full absorption cost per unit of 46.00 dollars. Doug has asked you to determine whether the numbers actually support switching to Wheatland, and if so, what NFL should do with the capacity that would be freed up.

You met with the plant controller, who walked you through the piston rod assembly's cost card and confirmed that the fixed overhead applied to each unit is a plant-wide allocation covering supervision, equipment leases, insurance on the dedicated machinery, and a share of the building lease. Of the total fixed overhead currently assigned to the piston rod line, the controller confirmed that the supervisor's salary and the leased CNC lathe used exclusively for this line would be eliminated entirely if production stopped, since the supervisor would be let go and the lathe lease would not be renewed. The remaining fixed overhead assigned to the line represents a share of general plant occupancy costs and corporate administrative salaries that would continue to be incurred and simply reallocated across NFL's other product lines if the piston rod line were shut down.

Priya has also raised an important operational point. The piston rod assembly line currently occupies a dedicated bay that uses roughly 8,000 machine hours of capacity per year. If NFL stops making the component, that bay and its equipment footprint would become available. Priya has identified two possible uses for the freed capacity. The first is to introduce a new stabilizer bracket product line that NFL's sales team believes it can sell to an existing customer, generating additional annual contribution margin of 150,000 dollars using that same freed capacity, with no additional fixed costs required since existing supervisory staff on other lines could absorb the oversight. The second option, favoured by Doug because it involves no execution risk, is to simply sublease the vacated bay to an adjacent tenant, a small hydraulic hose distributor, for a firm annual rent of 60,000 dollars, with no additional cost or effort required from NFL.

Doug is also concerned about qualitative factors. Wheatland Precision Machining is a smaller company than NFL and Doug worries about supplier reliability, particularly during winter months when Saskatchewan highway conditions can disrupt freight schedules. NFL currently maintains only three days of piston rod inventory because the assemblies are bulky and expensive to store. Doug has asked whether NFL should negotiate a minimum inventory buffer requirement or a penalty clause into any supply agreement with Wheatland before committing. He has also asked you to comment on whether NFL's 22 years of in-house manufacturing expertise on this component represents a strategic capability worth protecting, separate from the pure cost comparison, given that some of NFL's cylinder designs are custom-engineered around tight rod tolerances that only NFL's own machinists currently understand well.

Doug has engaged you to prepare a report addressed to him as the majority owner. He wants a clear, numbers-based recommendation on whether NFL should continue making the piston rod assembly in-house or switch to Wheatland Precision Machining, incorporating the best use of any freed capacity, along with your assessment of the key qualitative and succession-related risks he should weigh before signing a multi-year supply agreement.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Piston Rod Assembly Cost Card, Current In-House Production
exhibits.content: The following per-unit cost data was extracted from NFL's standard costing system for the piston rod assembly, based on annual production and sales volume of 20,000 units. Direct material consists of forged steel rod stock, seals, and fittings, costing 18.00 dollars per unit. Direct labour, covering machining, welding, and finishing performed by two dedicated line workers, costs 12.00 dollars per unit at NFL's blended shop rate. Variable manufacturing overhead, which includes machine power, cutting tools, and lubricants that vary directly with units produced, is 6.00 dollars per unit. Fixed manufacturing overhead applied to the piston rod line totals 200,000 dollars annually, which on a base of 20,000 units works out to 10.00 dollars per unit, bringing total full absorption cost to 46.00 dollars per unit. The controller confirmed the 200,000 dollars of annual fixed overhead breaks down as follows: the line supervisor's salary and benefits are 78,000 dollars, and the lease on the dedicated CNC lathe used only for this line is 42,000 dollars, for a combined avoidable total of 120,000 dollars that would disappear entirely if the line were shut down. The remaining 80,000 dollars represents an allocated share of general plant occupancy costs, property taxes, and corporate administrative salaries that are incurred regardless of whether the piston rod line operates and would simply be reallocated to NFL's other two product lines if this line closed.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Wheatland Precision Machining Supply Quote
exhibits.content: Wheatland Precision Machining has provided a written quote to supply 20,000 piston rod assemblies per year, manufactured to NFL's engineering drawings and quality specifications, at a unit price of 38.00 dollars, FOB Wheatland's Saskatoon plant. Freight to NFL's Nisku facility, based on current trucking rates for this weight and volume, is estimated at 1.50 dollars per unit, bringing the delivered cost to 39.50 dollars per unit. The quote is valid for an initial three-year term with annual price adjustments capped at the change in the Statistics Canada machinery and equipment producer price index. Wheatland has agreed to maintain a rolling two-week inventory buffer of finished assemblies at its own cost if NFL commits to a minimum annual purchase volume of 18,000 units. Wheatland's quality certification includes ISO 9001 registration and a stated defect rate of under 0.5 percent based on its existing automotive parts customers, though it has not previously supplied the oil and gas equipment sector.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 3: Capacity Reallocation Options if Piston Rod Production Is Discontinued
exhibits.content: If NFL stops in-house production of the piston rod assembly, the dedicated production bay and its 8,000 annual machine hours of capacity become available for other use. Option one, proposed by the sales team, is to launch a new stabilizer bracket product for an existing heavy equipment dealer customer. Based on a signed letter of intent for 6,000 units per year at a contribution margin of 25.00 dollars per unit, this option is projected to generate additional annual contribution margin of 150,000 dollars. No incremental fixed costs are required since the freed bay and its equipment can be repurposed with only minor tooling changes, and existing supervisory staff on NFL's other two lines have confirmed capacity to absorb oversight of the new product without adding headcount. Option two is to sublease the vacated bay, including racking and utility hookups, to Prairie Hose and Fitting Distributors, an adjacent tenant that has expressed firm interest in expanding into the space. Prairie Hose has offered a five-year sublease at a fixed annual rent of 60,000 dollars, with no capital investment, staffing, or execution risk required from NFL. Doug has noted that option one carries execution risk, since the letter of intent is not a binding purchase contract and the sales forecast could fail to materialize, whereas option two is contractually guaranteed.

requirements: Using relevant costing, determine whether Northern Fabrication Ltd should continue manufacturing the piston rod assembly in-house or purchase it from Wheatland Precision Machining, and quantify the financial impact of your recommendation.
Recommend how NFL should use the production capacity that would be freed up if it switches to Wheatland, incorporating the opportunity cost into your overall make-or-buy analysis.
Identify and discuss the key qualitative and succession-related risks Doug should consider before committing to a multi-year supply agreement with Wheatland.

model_response.understand_issue: Doug Kowalchuk, NFL's majority owner, has engaged me as external CPA consultant to advise on a make-or-buy decision for the piston rod assembly ahead of his retirement. The audience is Doug, and the ask is threefold: a relevant-cost-based recommendation on making versus buying the component, a recommendation on the best use of freed capacity if NFL switches suppliers, and an assessment of the qualitative and succession-related risks before NFL signs a multi-year agreement with a new, sector-unproven supplier.
model_response.analyze_case:
The full absorption cost of 46.00 dollars per unit that concerns Priya is misleading for this decision because it includes 80,000 dollars of unavoidable fixed overhead, 4.00 dollars per unit, that will be incurred whether or not NFL makes the piston rod assembly. A make-or-buy decision must be based only on relevant, avoidable costs. Of the 200,000 dollars in total fixed overhead applied to the line, only the 120,000 dollars covering the supervisor's salary (78,000 dollars) and the dedicated lathe lease (42,000 dollars) would actually disappear if production stopped. On a base of 20,000 units, that avoidable fixed cost is 120,000 divided by 20,000, or 6.00 dollars per unit.

The relevant cost to make is therefore: direct material 18.00, direct labour 12.00, variable overhead 6.00, and avoidable fixed overhead 6.00, totalling 42.00 dollars per unit. The relevant cost to buy is Wheatland's delivered price of 38.00 plus freight of 1.50, totalling 39.50 dollars per unit. Buying is cheaper by 42.00 minus 39.50, or 2.50 dollars per unit. Over 20,000 units, this is a direct annual cost saving of 2.50 times 20,000, or 50,000 dollars, before considering what happens to the freed capacity.

The freed capacity decision must be layered on top of this 50,000 dollar saving as an opportunity cost of continuing to make in-house, or equivalently, as an incremental benefit of switching to buy. Comparing the two options in Exhibit 3: the stabilizer bracket option generates 150,000 dollars of additional annual contribution margin, while the sublease option generates a guaranteed 60,000 dollars per year. On a purely expected-value basis, the stabilizer bracket option is worth 90,000 dollars more per year (150,000 minus 60,000), so it should be selected as the higher-value use of the freed capacity, and its 150,000 dollars becomes part of the relevant benefit of switching to Wheatland.

Combining both effects, the total annual financial benefit of switching to Wheatland and redeploying the freed capacity into the stabilizer bracket line is 50,000 dollars (direct cost saving) plus 150,000 dollars (opportunity benefit from freed capacity), for a total of 200,000 dollars per year in NFL's favour, versus continuing to make the piston rod assembly in-house. Even under the more conservative sublease option, the total benefit would still be 50,000 plus 60,000, or 110,000 dollars annually, which remains strongly favourable to switching. Under either capacity-use scenario, the buy decision dominates the make decision on financial grounds alone. I would flag to Doug, however, that the stabilizer bracket's 150,000 dollar figure rests on an unsigned letter of intent, so there is execution risk that the sublease option does not carry; a sensitivity check shows that the breakeven point between the two capacity options is exactly 40 percent of forecast (60,000 dollars of contribution margin, equal to the sublease amount); above that threshold the bracket line remains the higher-value choice, but below it the sublease becomes superior, so Doug should not treat the 150,000 dollar figure as guaranteed the way he can treat the sublease rent.
model_response.recommendation:
I recommend that NFL discontinue in-house production of the piston rod assembly and switch to Wheatland Precision Machining, generating a direct relevant cost saving of approximately 50,000 dollars per year (2.50 dollars per unit on 20,000 units). I further recommend that NFL use the freed production bay and 8,000 machine hours to launch the stabilizer bracket product line rather than subleasing the space, since the projected 150,000 dollars in additional annual contribution margin exceeds the guaranteed 60,000 dollars sublease rent by 90,000 dollars, even after allowing for the execution risk inherent in an unsigned letter of intent. Combined, switching suppliers and redeploying the capacity into the bracket line is projected to improve NFL's annual results by approximately 200,000 dollars relative to continuing the status quo.

Before signing, I recommend NFL negotiate two protective terms into the Wheatland agreement given the qualitative risks Doug raised. First, NFL should insist on a minimum finished-goods inventory buffer beyond the two-week buffer already offered, and preferably a contractual penalty clause tied to on-time delivery performance, given winter freight risk on the Saskatchewan-Alberta corridor and NFL's currently thin three-day internal buffer. Second, because Wheatland has no prior track record supplying the oil and gas equipment sector, I recommend a phased transition, retaining a small in-house fallback capability or a six-month dual-sourcing overlap period, rather than dismantling the in-house line immediately, so that NFL can validate Wheatland's quality and tolerance performance on safety-critical cylinder components before fully committing.

I recommend the sales team convert the stabilizer bracket letter of intent into a signed purchase agreement before NFL finalizes its capacity redeployment plan, since the 150,000 dollar benefit depends on that volume materializing.
model_response.conclusion: This decision arrives at a pivotal moment for NFL as Doug transitions the business toward his children. Switching to Wheatland and redeploying capacity into the stabilizer bracket line strengthens NFL's financial position by roughly 200,000 dollars annually while also diversifying the product portfolio the next generation will inherit, though Doug should manage the transition carefully to protect NFL's quality reputation and safety-critical tolerances during the supplier changeover.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly identifies that the 80,000 dollars of allocated fixed overhead is unavoidable and irrelevant to the make-or-buy decision
Correctly calculates avoidable fixed overhead per unit as 120,000 divided by 20,000 units equals 6.00 dollars
Correctly computes relevant make cost of 42.00 dollars per unit (18.00 plus 12.00 plus 6.00 plus 6.00)
Correctly computes relevant buy cost of 39.50 dollars per unit including freight
Correctly calculates the 50,000 dollar annual direct cost saving from buying (2.50 dollars times 20,000 units)
Recognizes the freed capacity represents an opportunity cost/benefit that must be layered onto the direct cost comparison
Correctly compares the 150,000 dollar bracket option against the 60,000 dollar sublease option and selects the higher-value alternative
Aggregates total financial benefit of switching (approximately 200,000 dollars) combining direct savings and opportunity benefit
Performs a sensitivity or risk-adjustment discussion on the unsigned letter of intent for the bracket line
Recognizes the strategic value of in-house tolerance expertise on custom cylinder designs as a factor beyond pure cost
Identifies supplier reliability and winter freight risk as a qualitative factor requiring contractual protection
Recommends a specific, actionable protective term such as an inventory buffer or delivery penalty clause
Recommends a phased transition or dual-sourcing period to mitigate quality risk on a sector-unproven supplier

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Structures the analysis using the CPA Way, clearly separating relevant from irrelevant costs before reaching a conclusion
Provides a specific, quantified, actionable recommendation rather than a generic discussion of make-or-buy theory
Report is addressed appropriately to Doug as majority owner, in clear language suited to a non-accountant business owner
Does not double-count the freed capacity benefit or incorrectly include unavoidable fixed costs in the relevant cost of making
Connects the recommendation to the succession planning context Doug raised

rating_guidance: A CD response correctly isolates avoidable versus unavoidable fixed overhead, calculates both the direct 50,000 dollar saving and the opportunity cost of freed capacity, combines them into a single properly reasoned total benefit figure of approximately 200,000 dollars, and layers in a genuine risk-adjusted discussion of the unsigned letter of intent rather than treating both capacity options as equally certain. A C response performs the core relevant costing correctly (identifying the 42.00 versus 39.50 dollar comparison and the 50,000 dollar saving) and recommends a capacity use, but may not fully integrate the opportunity cost into a single combined benefit figure or may treat the two capacity options with equal confidence. An RC response attempts the relevant costing but makes an error such as including unavoidable fixed overhead in the comparison, omitting freight, or failing to connect the capacity decision to the make-or-buy decision at all, or discusses qualitative risk only in generic terms without linking it to NFL's specific facts. An NC response relies solely on the misleading 46.00 dollar full absorption cost without any relevant-costing adjustment, or fails to address the capacity question, or provides recommendations unsupported by any calculation.

enabling_focus: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
Adding Value
title: Maritime Furniture Co: Outdoor Product Line Discontinuance Decision
case_background:
Maritime Furniture Co (MFC) is a mid-sized furniture manufacturer headquartered in Dartmouth, Nova Scotia, producing solid wood furniture sold through independent retailers across Atlantic Canada and Quebec. MFC operates three product lines from a single manufacturing facility: Dining, which produces dining tables, chairs, and hutches; Office, which produces desks, bookcases, and filing cabinets; and Outdoor, which produces cedar patio sets, Adirondack chairs, and garden benches. You, CPA, are the newly hired controller, reporting directly to the CEO, Marion Albrecht, who founded the company eighteen years ago.

At the most recent quarterly board meeting, one of MFC's outside directors, a retired retail executive, pointed to the segmented income statement and noted that the Outdoor line has reported a net loss in each of the past three years. She suggested the board should seriously consider discontinuing Outdoor and reallocating its capacity and working capital to the two profitable lines. Marion is hesitant. Outdoor was the company's founding product line, cedar patio furniture is what built MFC's original retailer relationships in the 1990s, and several long-standing retail accounts still place their largest combined orders when Outdoor products are bundled with Dining products in the same shipment. Marion has asked you to prepare a rigorous financial analysis of whether discontinuing Outdoor actually makes sense, rather than relying on the segmented net income figures alone, which she suspects may be distorted by how corporate overhead is allocated across the three lines.

You pulled MFC's most recent annual segmented income statement, reproduced in Exhibit 1, which allocates common fixed costs, covering the CEO's office, corporate accounting, and shared warehouse security and insurance, to each product line based on relative sales dollars. You confirmed with the plant manager which costs are truly traceable to each line (meaning they would disappear if that line were discontinued) versus which are common costs that would simply be reallocated to the remaining two lines if Outdoor were dropped. Outdoor's traceable fixed costs include its dedicated cedar drying kiln lease, a line supervisor, and specialized outdoor-furniture assembly jigs. You also learned that Outdoor occupies a standalone 8,000 square foot warehouse bay adjacent to the main plant, separate from where Dining and Office products are stored and shipped.

MFC's VP of sales, Ahmed Farooq, raised a complication. He confirmed that approximately 15 percent of Dining line revenue comes from combined orders where retailers purchase Dining and Outdoor products together in the same seasonal order, often to fill a single delivery truck efficiently. Ahmed believes that if Outdoor products were no longer available, some retailers might shift a portion of that combined-order Dining business to a competitor who offers a fuller catalogue, though he could not quantify this risk precisely and described it only as a moderate concern affecting perhaps a handful of MFC's mid-sized accounts.

Separately, the facilities manager mentioned that if the Outdoor line's standalone warehouse bay were vacated, a local landscaping supply company operating next door has already expressed informal interest in leasing the space, and the facilities manager believes a market rent in the range of 50,000 dollars annually is realistic based on comparable industrial lease rates in the Dartmouth area, though nothing has been formally offered yet.

Marion has asked you to prepare an analysis for the board that goes beyond the misleading net income figures on the segmented statement, quantifies the true financial impact of discontinuing the Outdoor line using relevant costing principles, incorporates the potential warehouse sublease opportunity, and gives the board a clear recommendation along with an honest discussion of the customer-retention risk that Ahmed flagged, since Marion does not want the board to make a purely mechanical decision that damages a founding relationship with MFC's original retail customer base.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Segmented Annual Income Statement (most recent fiscal year)
exhibits.content: MFC's segmented income statement for the most recent fiscal year, in dollars, is as follows. Dining line: sales revenue 2,400,000, variable costs 1,440,000, contribution margin 960,000, traceable fixed costs 300,000, allocated common fixed costs 200,000, segment net income 460,000. Office line: sales revenue 1,800,000, variable costs 1,080,000, contribution margin 720,000, traceable fixed costs 250,000, allocated common fixed costs 150,000, segment net income 320,000. Outdoor line: sales revenue 900,000, variable costs 630,000, contribution margin 270,000, traceable fixed costs 280,000, allocated common fixed costs 100,000, segment net income negative 110,000. Company total: sales revenue 5,100,000, variable costs 3,150,000, contribution margin 1,950,000, traceable fixed costs 830,000, allocated common fixed costs 450,000, total net income 670,000. Common fixed costs of 450,000 dollars are allocated to each line based on its relative share of total company sales revenue, and consist of the CEO's office and corporate salaries, corporate accounting and IT, and shared warehouse security and insurance covering the entire facility complex.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Outdoor Line Traceable Fixed Cost Detail
exhibits.content: The plant manager confirmed the 280,000 dollars of traceable fixed costs assigned to the Outdoor line would be entirely eliminated if the line were discontinued. This amount consists of the following: the lease on the dedicated cedar drying kiln, used exclusively for outdoor furniture lumber preparation, at 95,000 dollars annually; the Outdoor line supervisor's salary and benefits at 88,000 dollars annually; depreciation and maintenance on specialized outdoor-furniture assembly jigs and weatherproofing equipment at 47,000 dollars annually; and seasonal marketing and trade show costs specific to the outdoor patio category at 50,000 dollars annually. None of these costs would continue or be redirected to Dining or Office if Outdoor were discontinued. Separately, the Outdoor line occupies a standalone 8,000 square foot warehouse bay, physically separate from the Dining and Office storage and shipping areas, with its own loading dock. This warehouse bay is not included in the 280,000 dollars above; its cost is embedded within the allocated common fixed cost pool as part of overall facility costs.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 3: Combined-Order Sales Data and Warehouse Sublease Interest
exhibits.content: The VP of sales confirmed that of the 2,400,000 dollars in annual Dining line revenue, approximately 15 percent, or 360,000 dollars, comes from orders where a retailer purchases both Dining and Outdoor products in the same shipment, typically to reach a full truckload for shipping efficiency. Of MFC's 40 active retail accounts, 6 mid-sized accounts account for the substantial majority of this combined-order volume. Ahmed estimates, based on informal conversations with these accounts, that if Outdoor products became unavailable, it is possible but not certain that some portion of these 6 accounts' Dining purchases could shift to a competing supplier offering a broader catalogue, though he was unable to provide a specific dollar estimate or probability and characterized the risk as moderate rather than severe. Separately, the facilities manager reports informal interest from Coastal Landscape Supply, an adjacent tenant, in leasing the Outdoor line's standalone 8,000 square foot warehouse bay if it becomes vacant, with a realistic market rent estimated at 50,000 dollars annually based on comparable Dartmouth-area industrial lease rates, though no formal lease offer has been made.

requirements: Using relevant costing principles, determine the true financial impact on MFC's total net income of discontinuing the Outdoor product line, including the potential warehouse sublease opportunity.
Recommend whether MFC's board should discontinue the Outdoor line, keep it, or pursue an alternative course of action, supporting your recommendation with your quantitative analysis.
Discuss the customer-retention risk associated with the combined Dining and Outdoor orders and how it should factor into the board's final decision.

model_response.understand_issue: Marion Albrecht, MFC's CEO, has asked me as controller to prepare a board-facing analysis of whether to discontinue the Outdoor product line, which reports an accounting loss under the segmented income statement. The board needs a relevant-costing analysis that separates avoidable from unavoidable costs, incorporates the warehouse sublease opportunity, and honestly weighs the customer-retention risk tied to combined Dining and Outdoor orders, since the segment loss figure alone is not a reliable basis for this decision.
model_response.analyze_case:
The segmented income statement's reported Outdoor net loss of 110,000 dollars is not the correct basis for a discontinuance decision because it includes 100,000 dollars of allocated common fixed costs that will not disappear if Outdoor is dropped; they will simply be reallocated to Dining and Office, meaning total company common fixed costs remain 450,000 dollars either way. The relevant question is only what happens to total company profit if Outdoor's revenue and its own contribution margin disappear, weighed against what traceable fixed costs are actually avoided.

If Outdoor is discontinued, the company loses Outdoor's contribution margin of 270,000 dollars (900,000 sales minus 630,000 variable costs). Against this lost contribution margin, the company avoids the 280,000 dollars of traceable fixed costs detailed in Exhibit 2, all of which the plant manager confirmed would be entirely eliminated with no redirection to the other lines. The net effect of dropping Outdoor, before considering the warehouse, is a lost contribution margin of 270,000 dollars offset by avoided fixed costs of 280,000 dollars, producing a net improvement to company profit of 280,000 minus 270,000, or 10,000 dollars.

This 10,000 dollar improvement is a genuine but very small margin, and it does not yet include the warehouse sublease. Because the standalone 8,000 square foot Outdoor warehouse bay's cost is embedded in the common overhead pool rather than in the 280,000 dollars of traceable costs already counted, subleasing it produces an incremental benefit on top of the 10,000 dollar figure. If Coastal Landscape Supply's informal interest converts into a signed lease at the estimated 50,000 dollars annual rent, the total improvement to company profit becomes 10,000 plus 50,000, or 60,000 dollars annually.

A sensitivity check is warranted given how thin the 10,000 dollar core figure is. If any single traceable cost item in Exhibit 2 turns out to be only partially avoidable, for example if the 47,000 dollars of specialized jig depreciation continues for a period during wind-down, the core decision could flip to a net loss from discontinuing. Similarly, the combined-order risk in Exhibit 3 works in the opposite direction: 360,000 dollars of Dining revenue is placed jointly with Outdoor orders by 6 mid-sized accounts, and even a partial loss of that Dining revenue, for example 10 percent of the 6 accounts' Dining purchases shifting to a competitor, would represent a meaningful dollar loss to Dining's own contribution margin (Dining's contribution margin ratio is 960,000 divided by 2,400,000, or 40 percent, so even 36,000 dollars of lost Dining revenue at that margin would erase 14,400 dollars of contribution margin) that is not captured anywhere in the 60,000 dollar headline figure. Given the analysis produces only a marginal 10,000 to 60,000 dollar range of benefit, and the combined-order risk is real but unquantified, this is a genuinely close decision rather than a clear-cut one.
model_response.recommendation:
I recommend the board not treat this as a simple discontinue-or-keep decision given how thin the core financial margin is. On a strict relevant-costing basis, discontinuing Outdoor and reallocating the warehouse bay to Coastal Landscape Supply improves company profit by approximately 60,000 dollars annually, but this benefit is small relative to the unquantified risk that 6 mid-sized accounts representing 360,000 dollars of combined-order Dining revenue could partially shift to a competitor.

I recommend MFC pursue a middle path before making a final decision: negotiate the Coastal Landscape Supply sublease to a signed offer to convert that 50,000 dollars from an estimate into a firm number, and have Ahmed directly survey the 6 at-risk accounts to quantify, even roughly, what portion of their Dining business is genuinely contingent on Outdoor availability, since a loss of more than roughly 60,000 dollars of Dining contribution margin from these accounts (which corresponds to about 150,000 dollars of at-risk Dining revenue, roughly 42 percent of the 360,000 dollar combined-order Dining revenue from the 6 accounts, at the 40 percent margin) would be enough to erase the entire projected benefit of discontinuing Outdoor.

If the sublease is confirmed and the account survey shows the retention risk is genuinely low, I recommend proceeding with discontinuance. If the survey reveals that a meaningful share of the 6 accounts' combined orders is truly contingent on Outdoor, I recommend the board instead pursue cost reduction within Outdoor itself, for example renegotiating the kiln lease or evaluating whether the specialized jigs could be shared with Dining, rather than discontinuing a founding product line for a financial benefit this narrow and this uncertain.
model_response.conclusion: This decision sits at the intersection of pure numbers and MFC's founding identity and customer relationships. Given the financial case for discontinuing Outdoor is marginal at best, roughly 10,000 to 60,000 dollars, and the customer-retention risk Ahmed flagged is real but currently unquantified, the board should gather the two missing pieces of information, a firm sublease offer and a real account-level retention estimate, before making an irreversible decision about a line that has been part of Marion's company since its founding.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly identifies that the 100,000 dollars of allocated common fixed cost is not avoidable and excludes it from the discontinuance analysis
Correctly calculates Outdoor's contribution margin of 270,000 dollars (900,000 minus 630,000)
Correctly identifies the 280,000 dollars of traceable fixed costs as fully avoidable per the plant manager's confirmation
Correctly computes the core net benefit of discontinuance as 10,000 dollars (280,000 avoided minus 270,000 lost contribution margin)
Correctly incorporates the 50,000 dollar warehouse sublease as an incremental benefit separate from the traceable fixed costs already counted
Arrives at the combined total benefit of approximately 60,000 dollars annually
Calculates Dining's contribution margin ratio (40 percent) and applies it to quantify the potential dollar impact of lost combined-order Dining revenue
Identifies the breakeven point at which lost Dining contribution margin from the retention risk would offset the entire benefit of discontinuing Outdoor
Recognizes that the sublease is only an informal expression of interest, not a signed commitment, and flags this uncertainty
Recognizes the decision is financially marginal rather than presenting a false sense of precision
Discusses the founding-product-line and long-standing customer relationship dimension as a qualitative factor for the board
Recommends a specific actionable next step, such as converting the sublease to a firm offer or surveying at-risk accounts, rather than a final yes or no with no path to more certainty

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Follows the CPA Way structure, clearly separating relevant from irrelevant costs before forming a recommendation
Goes beyond the segmented income statement's misleading net loss figure to surface the true, much smaller, financial stakes involved
Does not double count the warehouse cost within both the traceable fixed cost pool and the sublease benefit
Presents the analysis in a board-appropriate format with a clear, decision-useful recommendation

rating_guidance: A CD response correctly performs the full relevant-costing analysis (270,000 dollars lost contribution margin against 280,000 dollars avoided fixed cost, yielding a 10,000 dollar core benefit), correctly layers in the separate 50,000 dollar sublease benefit for a combined 60,000 dollar figure, and critically recognizes that this margin is thin enough that the unquantified combined-order retention risk could plausibly reverse the decision, then translates that risk into an actual breakeven calculation using Dining's contribution margin ratio. A C response gets the core relevant-costing numbers right (the 10,000 dollar figure and the 50,000 dollar sublease) and reaches a reasonable recommendation, but may not push through to a full breakeven quantification of the retention risk. An RC response attempts the analysis but makes an error, such as including the allocated common fixed costs as if avoidable, treating the sublease rent as already included in the 280,000 dollars, or failing to distinguish traceable from common costs at all, or discusses the retention risk only qualitatively without any attempt to size it. An NC response relies on the misleading segmented net loss of 110,000 dollars as the basis for the recommendation, or ignores the sublease and retention risk entirely.

title: Silver Birch Senior Living Inc: Zero-Based Budgeting Pilot for Dietary Services
case_background:
Silver Birch Senior Living Inc (SBSL) operates five retirement residences across southwestern Ontario, providing independent living and assisted living accommodations to approximately 850 residents in total. SBSL has grown through two acquisitions over the past six years and has historically prepared its annual operating budget using a simple incremental approach, where each department's prior-year actual spending is increased by a standard inflation factor to set the new budget. The company's new chief financial officer, Renata Oduya, joined four months ago from a hospital network where zero-based budgeting was standard practice, and she has become concerned that SBSL's incremental method is allowing cost creep to go unexamined year after year.

Renata has engaged you, CPA, a management accounting consultant, to run a pilot zero-based budgeting exercise on one department before recommending whether to roll the approach out company-wide. She selected dietary services, which covers meal planning, food purchasing, kitchen labour, and a supplementary nutrition program for residents with specific dietary needs, because it is SBSL's second-largest controllable operating cost after direct care staffing and because the department has shown a persistent pattern of exceeding its incrementally set budget in recent years without a clear explanation.

Under SBSL's current incremental method, the dietary services budget for the current fiscal year was set by taking last year's actual spending of 2,200,000 dollars across all five residences and applying the company's standard 3 percent inflation escalator, producing a current-year budget of 2,266,000 dollars. Renata pulled the year-to-date actual spending trend and, based on the first nine months annualized, dietary services is on pace to finish the year at approximately 2,350,000 dollars, an overrun of roughly 84,000 dollars against the incremental budget. When Renata asked the dietary services director, Colm Whitfield, to explain the overrun, he could only point to general comments about food price inflation and staffing pressures, without being able to break the increase down by specific cause. Renata found this unsatisfactory and believes it demonstrates exactly why the incremental method is inadequate: it never requires anyone to justify spending from the ground up, so genuine cost drivers get buried inside a single inflated lump sum.

You worked with Colm and SBSL's operations team to rebuild the dietary services budget from zero, starting with the actual underlying activity drivers rather than last year's spending total. You confirmed the current standard meal cost per resident meal served, the total annual meal volume across all five residences, the fixed cost of dietary supervision and management, and the cost of the supplementary nutrition program that serves residents on physician-directed dietary plans. Renata has asked you to compare the resulting zero-based budget figure to both the current incremental budget and the trending actual spend, and to explain, using the activity-driver buildup, both why the ZBB figure differs from the incremental figure and why an unexplained gap still remains between the ZBB figure and the trending actual, since she wants to bring a concrete finding to the board rather than a vague statement that ZBB is generally a better practice.

Renata has also asked for your professional judgment on whether SBSL should roll zero-based budgeting out to all departments company-wide next year, given that the pilot required a meaningful time investment from both you and Colm's team, or whether a more limited approach, such as applying full ZBB only to the largest or most volatile cost categories on a rotating multi-year cycle, would deliver most of the benefit at a fraction of the effort.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Current Incremental Budget and Year-to-Date Actual Trend, Dietary Services
exhibits.content: SBSL's dietary services budget for the current fiscal year, prepared under the company's standard incremental method, was calculated as follows: prior year actual spending of 2,200,000 dollars across all five residences, increased by the company's standard 3 percent inflation escalator applied uniformly regardless of department-specific cost drivers, producing a current-year approved budget of 2,266,000 dollars (2,200,000 multiplied by 1.03). Nine months of actual year-to-date spending data show dietary services has spent 1,762,500 dollars through the first three quarters. Annualizing this run rate (1,762,500 divided by 9, multiplied by 12) projects full-year spending of approximately 2,350,000 dollars, representing an overrun against the incremental budget of approximately 84,000 dollars, or 3.7 percent over budget. Colm Whitfield, the dietary services director, has attributed the overrun in general terms to food price inflation and staffing cost pressures but has not been able to provide a specific breakdown by activity or cost driver.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Zero-Based Budget Activity Driver Data, Dietary Services
exhibits.content: Working from actual operating data rather than prior-year spending, the following activity drivers were confirmed across all five residences for the upcoming budget period. Total meals served annually is projected at 450,000 meals, based on current resident census and standard meal frequency per resident. The current standard variable cost per meal served, covering raw food ingredients, kitchen consumables, and variable kitchen labour directly tied to meal volume, is 4.50 dollars per meal, confirmed against recent supplier pricing and current unionized kitchen wage rates. This produces a variable meal cost component of 450,000 meals multiplied by 4.50 dollars, equal to 2,025,000 dollars. Fixed dietary supervision and management costs, covering the dietary services director, head chefs at each of the five residences, and administrative support, are confirmed at 180,000 dollars annually, a cost level that does not vary with meal volume within the relevant range. The supplementary nutrition program, which provides specialized meal plans, nutritional supplements, and dietitian consultation time for residents on physician-directed dietary plans, is confirmed at 95,000 dollars annually based on current program enrollment. The total zero-based budget, built up from these three components (2,025,000 plus 180,000 plus 95,000), is 2,300,000 dollars.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 3: Notes on Remaining Variance and Company-Wide ZBB Rollout Considerations
exhibits.content: Comparing the three figures: the incremental budget is 2,266,000 dollars, the zero-based budget is 2,300,000 dollars, and the trending actual is 2,350,000 dollars. Renata's operations team investigated the gap between the ZBB figure and the trending actual and identified two contributing factors not captured in the standard activity-driver buildup: unplanned overtime premium pay in dietary kitchens, estimated at approximately 30,000 dollars year to date on an annualized basis, driven by unfilled kitchen staff vacancies at two of the five residences, and higher-than-budgeted food waste, estimated at approximately 20,000 dollars annually based on a recent internal waste audit at one residence, attributed to inconsistent portion control practices. Separately, on the question of company-wide rollout, Renata's finance team estimates that a full zero-based budget exercise across all of SBSL's roughly twelve operating departments would require approximately 400 consulting and internal staff hours in total, compared to the approximately 35 hours spent on the dietary services pilot alone, and Renata has asked whether a rotating approach, applying full ZBB to roughly one-third of departments each year on a three-year cycle focused first on the largest and most volatile cost categories, would be a more practical alternative to a full annual company-wide rebuild.

requirements: Prepare the zero-based budget for dietary services from the activity driver data provided, and compare it to both the current incremental budget and the trending actual spend, explaining the source of each variance.
Recommend specific management actions SBSL should take to close the remaining gap between the zero-based budget and the trending actual spend.
Recommend whether SBSL should roll out zero-based budgeting company-wide, apply it on a rotating cycle, or take another approach, supporting your recommendation with a cost-benefit rationale.

model_response.understand_issue: Renata Oduya, SBSL's new CFO, has engaged me to complete a zero-based budgeting pilot on dietary services and report findings to support a board decision on whether to expand ZBB company-wide. The audience is Renata, for use with the board, and the ask is threefold: build and explain the zero-based budget against the incremental budget and trending actual, recommend specific actions to close the remaining unexplained spending gap, and recommend a practical rollout approach for ZBB across SBSL's other departments.
model_response.analyze_case:
The zero-based budget for dietary services, built from actual activity drivers rather than an inflated prior-year total, comes to 2,300,000 dollars, comprised of three components: variable meal cost of 450,000 meals multiplied by 4.50 dollars per meal, equal to 2,025,000 dollars; fixed dietary supervision and management cost of 180,000 dollars; and the supplementary nutrition program cost of 95,000 dollars. Summing these three components, 2,025,000 plus 180,000 plus 95,000, gives the total zero-based budget of 2,300,000 dollars.

Comparing this to the incremental budget of 2,266,000 dollars, the ZBB figure is 34,000 dollars higher (2,300,000 minus 2,266,000). This difference demonstrates the core weakness of the incremental method that Renata suspected: a flat 3 percent escalator applied to a lump-sum prior-year total does not capture SBSL's actual cost structure. The incremental method implicitly assumed dietary costs would grow at the company's general 3 percent inflation rate, but the real driver-based buildup shows costs justified by actual meal volume, current food and labour pricing, and the supplementary nutrition program total 2,300,000 dollars, a growth of 100,000 dollars, or 4.5 percent, over the prior year's actual 2,200,000 dollars, meaningfully faster than the blanket 3 percent assumption. The incremental method was simply the wrong tool to capture this department's true cost drivers, understating the legitimately justified budget by 34,000 dollars.

However, the ZBB figure of 2,300,000 dollars still falls short of the trending actual spend of 2,350,000 dollars by 50,000 dollars (2,350,000 minus 2,300,000). This remaining gap is not a budgeting methodology problem; it represents genuine operational inefficiency that the activity-driver buildup correctly excludes because it is not a legitimate, justified cost. Exhibit 3 identifies the two specific sources: approximately 30,000 dollars of unplanned overtime premium pay caused by unfilled kitchen staff vacancies at two residences, and approximately 20,000 dollars of excess food waste at one residence tied to inconsistent portion control. Together, 30,000 plus 20,000 equals exactly the 50,000 dollar unexplained gap, confirming these two operational issues fully account for the difference between what dietary services should legitimately cost under current activity levels and pricing, and what it is actually trending to spend.

This is precisely the diagnostic value Renata was looking for: rather than Colm's vague explanation of general food and staffing cost pressures, the ZBB exercise isolates a specific 34,000 dollar legitimate budget correction and a specific 50,000 dollar operational inefficiency requiring corrective management action, split between a staffing vacancy problem and a portion control problem.
model_response.recommendation:
I recommend SBSL adopt 2,300,000 dollars as the corrected dietary services budget going forward, replacing the incrementally derived 2,266,000 dollars, since the activity-driver buildup demonstrates this is the legitimately justified cost level given current meal volume, food and labour pricing, and program enrollment.

To close the remaining 50,000 dollar gap between the ZBB figure and the trending actual, I recommend two specific actions tied directly to the two causes identified. For the 30,000 dollars of overtime premium pay, I recommend dietary services prioritize filling the kitchen staff vacancies at the two affected residences on an urgent basis, since sustained overtime premiums are typically a more expensive way to cover a staffing gap than filling the position at a regular rate, and I recommend Renata's team track a monthly vacancy and overtime metric specifically for dietary kitchens going forward. For the 20,000 dollars of excess food waste, I recommend the portion control audit conducted at the one residence be extended to all five residences, with standardized portioning tools and a brief refresher training for kitchen staff, since this issue was only formally identified at a single location and may be present, even if smaller in scale, at the other four.

I recommend against a full company-wide zero-based budget rebuild for the coming year, given the pilot alone consumed approximately 35 hours and a full twelve-department rebuild is estimated at roughly 400 hours, a return that is difficult to justify company-wide when SBSL's largest and most volatile cost categories can be prioritized instead. I recommend a rotating three-year cycle, applying full ZBB to roughly one-third of departments each year, starting with the largest or most cost-volatile remaining departments such as direct care staffing, since this captures most of the diagnostic benefit demonstrated in the dietary pilot at a fraction of the annual effort, while still ensuring every department receives a ground-up review at least once every three years.
model_response.conclusion: The dietary services pilot has given Renata exactly the concrete finding she needed for the board: a 34,000 dollar budget correction attributable to a flawed incremental escalator, and a separately identified, actionable 50,000 dollar operational inefficiency split between staffing vacancies and food waste. A rotating three-year zero-based budgeting cycle, prioritized by cost size and volatility, should let SBSL capture this same diagnostic value across its other departments without the disproportionate effort of a full annual company-wide rebuild.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly calculates the variable meal cost component of 2,025,000 dollars (450,000 meals times 4.50 dollars)
Correctly sums the three ZBB components to arrive at a total zero-based budget of 2,300,000 dollars
Correctly calculates the 34,000 dollar variance between the ZBB budget and the incremental budget
Correctly explains that the incremental method's flat 3 percent escalator does not reflect the department's true underlying cost growth
Correctly calculates the 50,000 dollar remaining gap between the ZBB budget and the trending actual
Correctly ties the 50,000 dollar gap to the two specific operational causes (30,000 overtime plus 20,000 waste) identified in Exhibit 3
Distinguishes between a legitimate budget correction (the 34,000 dollar ZBB versus incremental gap) and genuine operational inefficiency (the 50,000 dollar ZBB versus actual gap)
Recommends a specific, actionable step tied to the overtime/vacancy cause
Recommends a specific, actionable step tied to the food waste/portion control cause
Performs a cost-benefit comparison of the 35 hour pilot versus the estimated 400 hour full company-wide rollout
Recommends a specific rollout approach (e.g., a rotating multi-year cycle) rather than a binary yes or no on full rollout
Frames the recommendation appropriately for a board-level decision on resource allocation for the budgeting process itself

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Follows CPA Way structure, moving logically from building the ZBB figure to diagnosing variances to forming recommendations
Delivers the concrete, decision-useful finding Renata asked for rather than a generic endorsement of zero-based budgeting as a concept
Clearly separates and labels the three distinct dollar figures (incremental, ZBB, trending actual) throughout the response so the board can follow the logic

rating_guidance: A CD response correctly builds the 2,300,000 dollar ZBB figure from the three activity-driver components, correctly explains both variances (the 34,000 dollar gap to the incremental budget as a methodology flaw, and the 50,000 dollar gap to trending actual as genuine operational inefficiency), ties the 50,000 dollar gap precisely to the two named causes, and makes a reasoned, cost-justified recommendation on rollout approach using the 35-hour versus 400-hour comparison. A C response correctly calculates the ZBB figure and both variances but may not fully separate the methodology-flaw explanation from the operational-inefficiency explanation, or may recommend rollout without directly weighing the effort tradeoff. An RC response attempts the ZBB buildup but makes a computational error, such as miscalculating the meal cost component or failing to reconcile the 50,000 dollar gap to its two named causes, or discusses rollout only in general terms. An NC response does not correctly build the zero-based budget from the given drivers, confuses the three budget figures, or fails to distinguish the legitimate cost correction from the operational inefficiency.

title: Coastal Outfitters Ltd: Redesigning Store Performance Measurement Beyond Revenue and Margin
case_background:
Coastal Outfitters Ltd (COL) is a 14-store retail chain based in Victoria, British Columbia, selling outdoor apparel and camping equipment across Vancouver Island and the Lower Mainland. COL has historically measured store performance using two metrics only: total sales revenue and gross margin percentage, both reported monthly to head office. Store managers receive an annual bonus calculated primarily as a percentage of their store's sales revenue growth, with a smaller secondary component tied to gross margin percentage. You, CPA, have recently joined COL's head office finance team as a performance management analyst, reporting to the VP of retail operations, Danielle Foss.

Danielle has become concerned that the current single-dimension bonus structure is producing outcomes that do not actually reflect good store management. She has noticed, anecdotally, that some of COL's highest-revenue stores seem to have chronic staffing and customer service problems, while some smaller-revenue stores appear to run leaner, more disciplined operations, yet the bonus formula consistently rewards the high-revenue stores regardless of these underlying differences. Danielle has asked you to build a case, using real store data, for redesigning COL's store performance measurement system into a proper balanced scorecard covering financial, customer, internal process, and learning and growth perspectives, rather than continuing to rely on revenue and gross margin alone.

To make the case concrete, you selected two comparable COL stores for a detailed comparison: Store A, located in a high-traffic Victoria shopping district, and Store B, located in a quieter Nanaimo neighbourhood plaza. Both stores carry a similar product mix and have similar square footage. Under the current metrics, Store A looks like the stronger performer, with higher revenue and a higher gross margin percentage, and its manager received a substantially larger bonus last year than Store B's manager. However, when you pulled additional operational data, a very different picture emerged. Store A has a customer return rate nearly three times higher than Store B, along with much higher staff turnover and noticeably longer average transaction times, all of which point to service and fit-and-advice problems at the point of sale that are not visible anywhere in the revenue and gross margin figures.

You worked with COL's customer service team to estimate the actual cost of processing a customer return, which includes restocking labour, packaging waste, and a general administrative cost per return transaction, and you have applied this cost estimate to each store's actual return volume to show what the gross margin figures are missing. Danielle wants you to use this analysis to demonstrate, in concrete dollar terms, that Store B may actually be the more profitable and better-managed store once return costs are factored in, and to use this finding as the anchor for a broader balanced scorecard proposal. She has asked for a small set of specific metrics under each of the four balanced scorecard perspectives that COL could realistically start tracking at the store level without a major systems investment, and for a revised bonus structure recommendation that would not have rewarded Store A's manager over Store B's manager last year.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Store A and Store B Reported Financial Performance, Most Recent Fiscal Year
exhibits.content: Store A reported annual sales revenue of 3,000,000 dollars and a gross margin percentage of 42 percent, producing reported gross margin dollars of 1,260,000 (3,000,000 multiplied by 42 percent). Store A processed 40,000 customer transactions during the year. Store B reported annual sales revenue of 2,400,000 dollars and a gross margin percentage of 40 percent, producing reported gross margin dollars of 960,000 (2,400,000 multiplied by 40 percent). Store B processed 32,000 customer transactions during the year. Under COL's current bonus formula, which weights revenue growth at 70 percent and gross margin percentage at 30 percent of the bonus calculation, Store A's manager received a bonus of 18,400 dollars for the year, while Store B's manager received a bonus of 9,600 dollars, reflecting Store A's larger revenue base and higher reported gross margin percentage.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Store-Level Operational Data and Return Cost Analysis
exhibits.content: COL's customer service team confirmed the following operational data for both stores. Store A's customer return rate is 18 percent of transactions, producing 7,200 returns (40,000 transactions multiplied by 18 percent) during the year. Store B's customer return rate is 6 percent of transactions, producing 1,920 returns (32,000 transactions multiplied by 6 percent) during the year. Based on a time-and-cost study conducted by the customer service team, the fully loaded cost of processing a single customer return, covering restocking labour, packaging and shipping waste, point-of-sale transaction handling, and a share of customer service administrative overhead, is 85.00 dollars per return. Applying this cost, Store A's total return processing cost for the year is 7,200 multiplied by 85.00 dollars, equal to 612,000 dollars, while Store B's total return processing cost is 1,920 multiplied by 85.00 dollars, equal to 163,200 dollars. Additional operational metrics show Store A's annual staff turnover rate at 45 percent versus Store B's 15 percent, and Store A's average transaction time (a proxy for the amount of sales floor advice and fitting assistance customers receive before purchase) at 12 minutes versus Store B's 7 minutes.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 3: Notes on Return Drivers and Balanced Scorecard Design Considerations
exhibits.content: The customer service team's exit-interview data on returns indicates that a large majority of Store A's returns are attributed to incorrect sizing or product fit, suggesting customers are not receiving adequate fitting advice or product guidance at the point of sale, consistent with Store A's short average transaction time and high staff turnover, which likely reflects inexperienced or undertrained floor staff working with customers too quickly. Store B's lower return rate, longer transaction time, and lower turnover are consistent with a more experienced, stable staff providing more thorough product and fit guidance before a sale is completed. Danielle has asked that any proposed balanced scorecard metrics be things COL can realistically capture using its existing point-of-sale and HR systems without a major new technology investment, since COL's IT budget for the coming year is constrained. She has also noted that store managers should have line-of-sight influence over any metric used to calculate their bonus, meaning metrics substantially outside a store manager's control, such as regional economic conditions, should not be weighted heavily in the redesigned formula.

requirements: Quantify the hidden cost of customer returns at each store and determine which store, Store A or Store B, is actually more profitable once this cost is factored into the gross margin figures.

Propose a balanced scorecard for Coastal Outfitters with 2 to 3 specific, realistically measurable metrics under each of the four perspectives (financial, customer, internal process, and learning and growth).
Recommend a revised store manager bonus structure that would not have rewarded Store A's manager over Store B's manager last year, and explain how it addresses the flaw in the current formula.

model_response.understand_issue: Danielle Foss, COL's VP of retail operations, has asked me, as performance management analyst, to build a data-driven case for redesigning COL's store performance measurement system, which currently relies only on revenue and gross margin percentage. The audience is Danielle, for use in a broader head office proposal, and the ask is threefold: quantify the true, return-cost-adjusted profitability of Store A versus Store B, propose a practical balanced scorecard with specific metrics COL can capture with existing systems, and redesign the store manager bonus formula so it would not have rewarded Store A's manager over Store B's manager.
model_response.analyze_case:
Under the current metrics, Store A appears to be the stronger store, with 1,260,000 dollars of reported gross margin (3,000,000 dollars sales at 42 percent) against Store B's 960,000 dollars (2,400,000 dollars sales at 40 percent), a 300,000 dollar gap in Store A's favour. But gross margin percentage, as currently calculated, does not capture the cost of processing customer returns, which is a real cash cost to COL and which differs dramatically between the two stores.

Using the confirmed cost of 85.00 dollars per return, Store A's 7,200 returns (18 percent of its 40,000 transactions) cost 7,200 multiplied by 85.00 dollars, equal to 612,000 dollars in return processing costs for the year. Store B's 1,920 returns (6 percent of its 32,000 transactions) cost 1,920 multiplied by 85.00 dollars, equal to 163,200 dollars.

Adjusting each store's reported gross margin for this hidden cost: Store A's adjusted profitability is 1,260,000 dollars minus 612,000 dollars, equal to 648,000 dollars. Store B's adjusted profitability is 960,000 dollars minus 163,200 dollars, equal to 796,800 dollars. Once the true cost of returns is factored in, Store B is actually more profitable than Store A by 796,800 minus 648,000, equal to 148,800 dollars, a complete reversal of the picture given by the headline revenue and gross margin figures, which showed Store A ahead by 300,000 dollars before this adjustment.

The operational data in Exhibit 2 and Exhibit 3 explains why this gap exists. Store A's short 12-minute average transaction time and 45 percent staff turnover are consistent with inexperienced, rapidly rotating floor staff who are not spending enough time on fit and sizing advice before a sale, driving the 18 percent return rate, most of it attributed to fit and sizing issues in the exit-interview data. Store B's longer 7-minute transaction time and much lower 15 percent turnover reflect a more experienced, stable staff providing better pre-sale guidance, producing a far lower 6 percent return rate. The current bonus formula, weighting revenue growth at 70 percent, is effectively paying Store A's manager for driving a high volume of low-quality, high-return sales, while penalizing Store B's manager for a more disciplined, lower-volume but higher-quality operation. This is a direct example of the risk of managing to a single financial metric: it rewarded exactly the behaviour COL should not want more of.
model_response.recommendation:
I recommend COL adopt a balanced scorecard with the following store-level metrics, each realistically capturable through COL's existing point-of-sale and HR systems. Under the financial perspective: return-adjusted gross margin dollars (gross margin less return processing cost, as calculated above) and sales per labour hour. Under the customer perspective: customer return rate as a percentage of transactions, and a simple in-store customer satisfaction score captured through a short point-of-sale receipt survey. Under the internal process perspective: average transaction time as a proxy for the depth of sales floor advice, and inventory shrinkage rate. Under the learning and growth perspective: staff turnover rate, and hours of product knowledge training completed per staff member, both trackable through COL's existing HR system without new technology investment.

I recommend replacing the current 70 percent revenue growth and 30 percent gross margin percentage bonus formula with a formula weighted across at least three of the four perspectives, for example 40 percent return-adjusted gross margin dollars, 30 percent customer return rate improvement, and 30 percent staff turnover reduction, all of which are substantially within a store manager's direct influence, consistent with Danielle's line-of-sight principle. Applying this illustrative formula to last year's data, Store B's materially lower return rate and turnover would have earned it a meaningfully larger share of the customer and learning and growth components than Store A, likely reversing or at minimum significantly narrowing the 18,400 versus 9,600 dollar bonus gap, correcting the current formula's bias toward raw revenue regardless of how it was generated.

I recommend COL pilot the revised scorecard and bonus formula at Store A and Store B specifically for one quarter before a full 14-store rollout, since these two stores provide a natural before-and-after test case to validate that the new formula produces sensible results before committing company-wide.
model_response.conclusion: This analysis shows COL's current single-dimension metrics were masking a 148,800 dollar profitability reversal between its two comparison stores and rewarding the store with worse underlying operational health. A balanced scorecard anchored on return-adjusted profitability, customer and process indicators, and staff stability should give Danielle and COL's store managers a much more accurate, and much harder to game, picture of true store performance going forward.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly calculates Store A's return processing cost of 612,000 dollars (7,200 returns times 85.00 dollars)
Correctly calculates Store B's return processing cost of 163,200 dollars (1,920 returns times 85.00 dollars)
Correctly calculates Store A's return-adjusted profitability of 648,000 dollars
Correctly calculates Store B's return-adjusted profitability of 796,800 dollars
Correctly identifies that Store B is more profitable than Store A once return costs are factored in, a reversal of the headline figures, and quantifies the 148,800 dollar gap
Connects the return rate difference to underlying operational causes (transaction time, staff turnover, fit/sizing issues from exit interviews)
Proposes specific, realistic metrics under the financial perspective beyond raw revenue
Proposes specific, realistic metrics under the customer perspective
Proposes specific, realistic metrics under the internal process perspective
Proposes specific, realistic metrics under the learning and growth perspective
Ensures proposed metrics are capturable with COL's existing systems, consistent with the IT budget constraint noted by Danielle
Redesigns the bonus formula to weight customer and people metrics, not just financial revenue, and explains why this addresses the current formula's flaw
Applies the line-of-sight principle, ensuring bonus metrics are substantially within store manager control
Demonstrates, even qualitatively, how the revised formula would have changed the relative bonus outcome for Store A versus Store B managers last year

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Structures the response using the CPA Way, moving from the numeric reversal finding to the broader scorecard design
Surfaces a decision-relevant, non-obvious insight (the profitability reversal) rather than restating the given data
Presents the scorecard clearly organized under the four standard balanced scorecard perspectives

rating_guidance: A CD response correctly calculates both stores' return processing costs and return-adjusted profitability figures, explicitly identifies and quantifies the 148,800 dollar profitability reversal, ties the operational data (turnover, transaction time, return causes) together into a coherent explanation of why Store A's returns are so much higher, and proposes a genuinely balanced, realistically implementable scorecard and bonus formula that would concretely have changed last year's bonus outcome. A C response correctly performs the return-cost adjustment calculations and identifies that Store B is more profitable, and proposes reasonable scorecard metrics across the four perspectives, but may not fully connect the operational root causes or may propose a bonus formula without showing how it changes the prior year outcome. An RC response attempts the return-cost adjustment but makes a calculation error, or proposes scorecard metrics that are generic and not tailored to COL's specific data and systems constraints, or fails to redesign the bonus formula in a way that actually addresses the line-of-sight and revenue-bias problems. An NC response does not adjust gross margin for return costs at all, continues to treat Store A as the stronger performer, or provides scorecard metrics with no connection to the case facts.
