title: Boreal Resources Group: Transfer Pricing Between the Mining and Smelting Divisions
case_background:
Boreal Resources Group (BRG) is a diversified natural resources company headquartered in Sudbury, Ontario, organized into two autonomous operating divisions evaluated as investment centres: the Mining Division, which extracts and processes nickel ore, and the Smelting Division, which refines ore concentrate into finished nickel products for industrial customers. Each division general manager is compensated primarily on divisional return on investment, and BRG's long-standing corporate policy allows each division to buy and sell freely on the open market, with internal transfers between divisions occurring only when both division managers voluntarily agree to terms. You, CPA, are a management accounting analyst on BRG's corporate finance team, reporting to the CFO, Isabelle Trudeau.

The Smelting Division has historically purchased all of its ore concentrate feedstock from external third-party mining suppliers, currently paying 65.00 dollars per tonne to its primary external supplier under an existing contract that can be adjusted with 60 days notice. The Smelting Division's general manager, Faisal Rahimi, has approached corporate finance because he has learned that BRG's own Mining Division currently has spare processing capacity and could potentially supply concentrate internally, and he wants to know whether an internal transfer would be financially advantageous for BRG as a whole, and if so, what transfer price would be fair to both divisions.

The Mining Division's general manager, Odette Lachance, confirmed that her division's annual processing capacity is 500,000 tonnes of ore concentrate, and current external sales commitments to third-party customers total 400,000 tonnes, sold at a market price of 68.00 dollars per tonne, leaving 100,000 tonnes of unused annual capacity. Odette also confirmed the Mining Division's variable cost of extracting and processing one tonne of concentrate is 42.00 dollars, and the division carries fixed costs of 6,000,000 dollars annually that do not vary with tonnage produced within the relevant range.

Faisal has indicated the Smelting Division needs 80,000 tonnes of concentrate annually to feed its refining operations, well within the Mining Division's 100,000 tonnes of spare capacity. Both general managers have asked corporate finance to facilitate the negotiation, since each is evaluated on divisional ROI and neither wants to agree to a price that would disadvantage their own division's reported performance, even if an internal transfer is clearly better for BRG as a whole. Isabelle has asked you to determine the minimum price Odette's Mining Division should be willing to accept, the maximum price Faisal's Smelting Division should be willing to pay, and to quantify the total value created for BRG as a whole if an internal transfer proceeds at any price within that range, since she wants both general managers to understand that the specific price they agree on only affects how that total value is split between their two divisions, not whether the transfer makes sense for the company overall.

Isabelle has also flagged a governance consideration: BRG's corporate transfer pricing policy states that internal transfers should not be mandated by head office when spare capacity exists, out of respect for divisional autonomy, but she wants your analysis to include a recommendation on whether corporate finance should actively facilitate and encourage this specific negotiation, given the clear company-wide benefit, or whether it should remain entirely hands off and let the two general managers reach their own conclusion independently, since a prior attempt at a different internal transfer two years ago broke down acrimoniously when one general manager felt corporate had put a thumb on the scale in the other division's favour.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Mining Division Cost and Capacity Data
exhibits.content: The Mining Division's total annual processing capacity is 500,000 tonnes of ore concentrate. Current external sales commitments to third-party industrial customers total 400,000 tonnes annually, sold at a confirmed external market price of 68.00 dollars per tonne, generating external sales revenue of 27,200,000 dollars (400,000 multiplied by 68.00). This leaves 100,000 tonnes of unused annual capacity that could be used for either additional external sales, which Odette confirms are not currently available beyond the existing 400,000 tonne commitments due to market demand constraints in the current period, or an internal transfer to the Smelting Division. The variable cost of extracting and processing one tonne of ore concentrate, covering mining labour, blasting and extraction costs, and variable processing costs, is confirmed at 42.00 dollars per tonne. The Mining Division's annual fixed costs, covering site infrastructure, equipment depreciation, and permanent supervisory staff, are confirmed at 6,000,000 dollars, a cost level that does not change with tonnage produced within the current capacity range and would be unaffected by whether the 100,000 tonnes of spare capacity is used or left idle.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Smelting Division Sourcing Requirements and External Alternative
exhibits.content: The Smelting Division requires 80,000 tonnes of ore concentrate annually to feed its refining operations at planned production levels. The Smelting Division currently sources all of its concentrate from an external third-party mining supplier under a contract that can be adjusted with 60 days notice, at a confirmed price of 65.00 dollars per tonne, meaning the Smelting Division's current annual external procurement cost for this volume is 80,000 multiplied by 65.00 dollars, equal to 5,200,000 dollars. Faisal has confirmed the external supplier's concentrate is of comparable quality and specification to what the Mining Division produces, and switching suppliers would not require any changes to the Smelting Division's refining process. Faisal has also confirmed the external supplier has adequate capacity to continue supplying the Smelting Division indefinitely at the current 65.00 dollar price if no internal transfer occurs, so the external option remains a fully available fallback.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 3: Prior Internal Transfer Negotiation Breakdown and Corporate Policy Notes
exhibits.content: Two years ago, BRG attempted a different internal transfer arrangement between two other divisions, which broke down after approximately six weeks of negotiation when one division's general manager publicly stated at a management meeting that corporate finance had pushed the negotiation toward a price favouring the counterparty division, damaging trust in corporate's role as a neutral facilitator. Following that incident, BRG's board affirmed a formal transfer pricing policy stating that internal transfers between autonomous divisions should occur only when both division general managers voluntarily agree, that corporate finance's role is limited to providing objective cost and market data to both parties on request, and that corporate should not mandate a specific transfer price or divisional allocation of the benefit. Isabelle has noted that both Odette and Faisal have signalled they are open to a negotiated arrangement this time and have not expressed the kind of distrust that derailed the prior attempt, but she wants to ensure corporate finance's role in this negotiation stays clearly within the bounds of the board-affirmed policy while still adding genuine value to both general managers.

requirements: Determine the minimum transfer price the Mining Division should accept and the maximum transfer price the Smelting Division should pay, and explain the reasoning behind each boundary.

Quantify the total annual value created for Boreal Resources Group as a whole if the internal transfer proceeds, and explain why the specific negotiated price within the acceptable range does not change this total company-wide benefit.
Recommend the appropriate role for corporate finance in facilitating this negotiation, consistent with BRG's board-affirmed transfer pricing policy and the lessons from the prior breakdown.

model_response.understand_issue: Isabelle Trudeau, BRG's CFO, has asked me, as corporate finance analyst, to support a transfer pricing negotiation between the Mining Division's Odette Lachance and the Smelting Division's Faisal Rahimi. The audience is Isabelle, for use in facilitating the two general managers, and the ask is threefold: establish the minimum and maximum acceptable transfer price boundaries, quantify the total company-wide value creation from an internal transfer independent of where within that range the price lands, and recommend corporate finance's appropriate role given the board's autonomy-respecting transfer pricing policy and the acrimonious breakdown of a prior negotiation.
model_response.analyze_case:
The standard transfer pricing rule sets the minimum acceptable transfer price for the selling division at its variable cost per unit plus any opportunity cost of displaced external sales. Here, the Mining Division has 100,000 tonnes of spare capacity, and Odette has confirmed the existing 400,000 tonne external sales commitment is already the maximum available in the current market, meaning supplying the Smelting Division's 80,000 tonnes from spare capacity would not displace any external sales. The opportunity cost of the transfer is therefore zero, and the Mining Division's minimum acceptable transfer price is simply its variable cost of 42.00 dollars per tonne. Below this price, the Mining Division would be losing money on each tonne transferred; at exactly 42.00 dollars, the division is no better and no worse off than leaving the capacity idle, since the 6,000,000 dollars of fixed costs are unaffected either way.

The maximum acceptable transfer price for the Smelting Division is set by its best available external alternative, which is the confirmed external supplier price of 65.00 dollars per tonne. Faisal would have no financial reason to pay more than 65.00 dollars internally when an equivalent-quality external source is available indefinitely at that price. This establishes a negotiating range of 42.00 dollars to 65.00 dollars per tonne, a spread of 23.00 dollars per tonne, within which any agreed price leaves both divisions better off than their respective fallback positions (idle capacity for Mining, the external 65.00 dollar contract for Smelting).

The total value created for BRG as a whole, independent of exactly where within this range the two managers settle, is calculated by comparing the company's total cost structure with and without the transfer. Without an internal transfer, BRG's Smelting Division pays 80,000 tonnes multiplied by 65.00 dollars, equal to 5,200,000 dollars to an external party, while the Mining Division's spare capacity sits idle earning nothing beyond its own already-fixed costs. With the internal transfer, BRG's Mining Division incurs incremental variable cost of 80,000 tonnes multiplied by 42.00 dollars, equal to 3,360,000 dollars, to produce the concentrate Smelting needs, and no cash leaves the BRG consolidated entity at all, since the transfer price is simply an internal allocation between two divisions of the same parent company. The total value created for BRG as a whole is therefore 5,200,000 dollars (avoided external cash outflow) minus 3,360,000 dollars (incremental internal variable cost), equal to 1,840,000 dollars annually, which is exactly the spread of 23.00 dollars per tonne (65.00 minus 42.00) multiplied by 80,000 tonnes.

Critically, this 1,840,000 dollars of total value is fixed and does not change based on where within the 42.00 to 65.00 dollar range the two managers negotiate; the transfer price only determines how that fixed 1,840,000 dollars of company-wide benefit is split between the Mining Division's reported margin and the Smelting Division's reported cost savings on their respective ROI calculations. For example, at a price of 53.50 dollars (the midpoint), the Mining Division earns incremental contribution of 53.50 minus 42.00, or 11.50 dollars per tonne, times 80,000 tonnes, equal to 920,000 dollars, while the Smelting Division saves 65.00 minus 53.50, or 11.50 dollars per tonne, times 80,000 tonnes, equal to 920,000 dollars, and 920,000 plus 920,000 equals the same 1,840,000 dollars total regardless of the specific split.
model_response.recommendation:
I recommend corporate finance present both Odette and Faisal with the full 42.00 to 65.00 dollar negotiating range and the 1,840,000 dollar total company-wide value figure, making clear to both general managers that the specific price they agree on is purely a distributional question between their two divisions and does not change whether the transfer benefits BRG overall, since it always will within this range. I recommend suggesting a reasonable starting point, such as the midpoint of 53.50 dollars, which splits the benefit evenly, purely as a negotiation anchor rather than a mandated figure, leaving Odette and Faisal free to negotiate their own final split.

Consistent with the board-affirmed transfer pricing policy, I recommend corporate finance's role remain strictly limited to providing objective, verified cost and market data (the 42.00 dollar variable cost, the 65.00 dollar external alternative, and the capacity confirmation) equally and transparently to both general managers at the same time, and explicitly declining to advocate for a specific price or to communicate privately with only one party about pricing strategy, given how directly this mirrors the dynamic that caused the prior negotiation to break down over a perception of corporate favouritism.

I recommend Isabelle personally communicate to both Odette and Faisal, before the negotiation begins, that corporate finance's role in this instance is purely informational and that final agreement rests entirely with them, reinforcing the credibility of corporate's neutrality given the history of the prior incident, and that any final agreed price and volume commitment be documented in a short internal service agreement so both divisions' ROI reporting reflects the arrangement consistently.
model_response.conclusion: This transfer creates a genuine 1,840,000 dollar annual benefit for BRG as a whole, entirely independent of how Odette and Faisal choose to split it between their two divisions' reported results. By supplying objective data transparently to both general managers and staying rigorously neutral on the specific price, consistent with the board's autonomy-respecting policy and the hard lesson from the prior breakdown, corporate finance can help both divisions capture this value without repeating the trust breakdown that derailed BRG's last internal transfer attempt.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly identifies the minimum transfer price as the Mining Division's variable cost of 42.00 dollars per tonne
Correctly reasons that opportunity cost is zero because spare capacity exists and no external sales are displaced
Correctly identifies the maximum transfer price as the Smelting Division's external alternative of 65.00 dollars per tonne
Correctly establishes the full negotiating range of 42.00 to 65.00 dollars per tonne
Correctly calculates total company-wide value created of 1,840,000 dollars annually using the avoided-external-cost-minus-incremental-internal-cost method
Correctly demonstrates, using at least one example price point, that the split between divisions changes but the total 1,840,000 dollars does not
Correctly reconciles the 1,840,000 dollar total to the 23.00 dollar per tonne spread times 80,000 tonnes
Recognizes fixed costs of 6,000,000 dollars are irrelevant to the transfer pricing decision since they are unaffected by the transfer
Recommends a corporate finance role limited to objective data provision, consistent with the board-affirmed autonomy policy
Explicitly connects the recommended corporate role to the lessons from the prior negotiation breakdown
Suggests a reasonable negotiation anchor or starting point without mandating a specific price
Recommends transparent, equal, simultaneous communication of data to both general managers to preserve perceived neutrality

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Structures the analysis using standard transfer pricing theory (minimum equals variable cost plus opportunity cost, maximum equals best external alternative)
Frames recommendations in a way that respects both general managers' autonomy and incentivizes voluntary agreement rather than directing an outcome
Presents the negotiation range and total value logic in terms accessible to both operationally focused general managers

rating_guidance: A CD response correctly derives both boundaries of the negotiating range with proper reasoning on opportunity cost, calculates the 1,840,000 dollar total company-wide benefit using a sound method, explicitly demonstrates that the split (not the total) is what negotiation determines, and gives a governance-appropriate recommendation on corporate finance's role that is directly tied to the specific facts of the prior breakdown rather than generic advice to stay neutral. A C response correctly identifies the 42.00 and 65.00 dollar boundaries and calculates the total value created, and recommends a reasonable corporate role, but may not fully articulate why the total is independent of the specific price chosen or may address the governance question only generally. An RC response gets the direction of the analysis right but makes an error, such as incorrectly including a non-zero opportunity cost despite the stated spare capacity, incorrectly including fixed costs in the transfer price boundary, or failing to quantify the total value created at all. An NC response does not correctly identify both price boundaries, confuses the divisional split with the total company benefit, or ignores the governance and facilitation-role question entirely.

title: Precision Print Solutions Inc: Redesigning the Costing System with Activity-Based Costing
case_background:
Precision Print Solutions Inc (PPS) is a commercial printing company based in Kitchener, Ontario, serving business customers with a mix of high-volume standard print runs, such as large batches of identical marketing flyers, and low-volume custom jobs, such as short-run specialty packaging or highly customized event materials requiring frequent machine reconfiguration. You, CPA, are a management accounting consultant engaged by PPS's owner, Theo Marchetti, who has become concerned that the company's pricing decisions may be systematically wrong because of how overhead is currently allocated to jobs.

PPS currently applies manufacturing overhead to every print job using a single plant-wide predetermined overhead rate based on machine hours. Theo's sales team quotes prices to customers using this same overhead rate as the basis for estimating each job's full cost, then adds a standard markup. Over the past two years, Theo has noticed a pattern that concerns him: PPS appears to be winning almost all of the large, high-volume standard jobs it bids on, often underbidding competitors, while consistently losing bids for small, highly customized short-run jobs to smaller specialty print shops that seem able to quote much lower prices for this type of work. Theo initially assumed PPS was simply less efficient at custom work, but a former classmate who now works as a CPA suggested to him that the real problem might be in how PPS's costing system allocates overhead, systematically overcosting standard jobs and undercosting custom jobs, which would explain exactly the bidding pattern he has observed.

You reviewed PPS's overhead structure in detail with the plant controller and identified that the single machine-hour-based rate blends together three fundamentally different types of overhead activity that behave very differently depending on job characteristics. Machine setup activity, which involves reconfiguring presses, changing plates, and adjusting settings between different jobs, is driven primarily by the number of separate setups required, not by machine hours; a short custom job with many small print runs can require far more setups relative to its size than a single long run of a standard job. Quality inspection activity, which involves checking colour accuracy, print alignment, and material quality, is driven by inspection hours, which also tend to be disproportionately higher per unit for custom work given its typically tighter specifications. Machine running activity, the actual time presses spend printing, is the only one of the three that is genuinely proportional to machine hours.

To demonstrate the distortion concretely to Theo, you selected two representative recent jobs from PPS's job records: a large standard job for a national retail flyer campaign, and a small custom job for a boutique client's specialty product packaging. You gathered the actual machine hours, number of setups, and inspection hours each job required, and you plan to recost both jobs under a properly designed activity-based costing system using the three distinct cost pools and compare the ABC-derived overhead cost to what PPS's current plant-wide rate assigns to each job. Theo has asked you to quantify precisely how much the current system is overcosting the standard job and undercosting the custom job, and to explain, in terms he can use directly with his sales team, why this cost distortion is producing the exact competitive bidding pattern he has observed. He has also asked for your recommendation on whether PPS should formally switch to activity-based costing company-wide, and if so, what practical steps and data collection PPS would need to put in place to make ABC operational rather than a one-time analytical exercise.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: PPS Current Plant-Wide Overhead Rate and Annual Overhead Data
exhibits.content: PPS's total annual manufacturing overhead is 3,750,000 dollars, and total annual machine hours across all jobs are 150,000 hours, producing a single plant-wide predetermined overhead rate of 3,750,000 divided by 150,000, equal to 25.00 dollars per machine hour. This single rate is applied to every job based solely on the machine hours the job consumes, regardless of how many setups or how much inspection time the job actually required. The plant controller confirmed that the 3,750,000 dollars of total annual overhead breaks down into three underlying activity pools: setup-related overhead of 1,200,000 dollars, quality inspection overhead of 900,000 dollars, and machine running overhead (power, depreciation, and consumables tied directly to press running time) of 1,650,000 dollars, and that these three activities have historically simply been summed together and divided by total machine hours to produce the single rate currently in use.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Activity-Based Costing Driver Data
exhibits.content: Working with the plant controller, the three overhead pools were traced to more appropriate activity drivers based on total annual activity across all of PPS's jobs. Setup-related overhead of 1,200,000 dollars is driven by the number of machine setups performed, with a total of 4,000 setups performed company-wide annually, producing a setup activity rate of 1,200,000 divided by 4,000, equal to 300.00 dollars per setup. Quality inspection overhead of 900,000 dollars is driven by inspection hours, with a total of 30,000 inspection hours performed company-wide annually, producing an inspection activity rate of 900,000 divided by 30,000, equal to 30.00 dollars per inspection hour. Machine running overhead of 1,650,000 dollars is driven by machine hours, with total machine hours of 150,000 confirmed consistent with Exhibit 1, producing a machine running activity rate of 1,650,000 divided by 150,000, equal to 11.00 dollars per machine hour.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 3: Representative Job Data, Standard Flyer Job and Custom Packaging Job
exhibits.content: Job Standard, a large national retail flyer campaign print run, consumed 500 machine hours, required 2 machine setups, and required 40 inspection hours during the job. Job Custom, a small boutique client specialty product packaging run, consumed only 20 machine hours, but required 8 machine setups due to multiple small production batches with different specifications, and required 25 inspection hours given the tighter colour and material tolerances the client specified. Under PPS's current plant-wide rate of 25.00 dollars per machine hour, Job Standard is assigned overhead of 500 multiplied by 25.00 dollars, equal to 12,500 dollars, and Job Custom is assigned overhead of 20 multiplied by 25.00 dollars, equal to 500 dollars.

requirements: Recost Job Standard and Job Custom using activity-based costing with the three activity pools and rates provided, and compare the ABC-derived overhead cost to what the current plant-wide rate assigns to each job.
Explain, using your calculations, how the current costing system's distortion is producing the specific competitive bidding pattern Theo has observed, in terms he can communicate to his sales team.
Recommend whether PPS should formally adopt activity-based costing company-wide, and outline the practical steps required to make it operational.

model_response.understand_issue: Theo Marchetti, PPS's owner, has engaged me as a management accounting consultant to determine whether PPS's single plant-wide overhead rate is distorting job costs and driving PPS's pattern of winning standard job bids while losing custom job bids. The audience is Theo, for direct use with his sales team, and the ask is threefold: recost two representative jobs under activity-based costing and compare to the current system, explain how the resulting distortion explains the observed bidding pattern, and recommend whether and how PPS should adopt ABC company-wide.
model_response.analyze_case:
Under PPS's current plant-wide rate of 25.00 dollars per machine hour (3,750,000 dollars total overhead divided by 150,000 total machine hours), Job Standard is assigned overhead of 500 machine hours multiplied by 25.00 dollars, equal to 12,500 dollars, and Job Custom is assigned overhead of 20 machine hours multiplied by 25.00 dollars, equal to 500 dollars. This single rate assumes overhead consumption is driven purely by machine hours, but the underlying activity data shows this assumption is false for two of the three overhead pools.

Recosting both jobs using the three ABC rates from Exhibit 2: for Job Standard, machine running overhead is 500 machine hours multiplied by 11.00 dollars, equal to 5,500 dollars; setup overhead is 2 setups multiplied by 300.00 dollars, equal to 600 dollars; and inspection overhead is 40 inspection hours multiplied by 30.00 dollars, equal to 1,200 dollars. Summing these three components, 5,500 plus 600 plus 1,200, gives Job Standard's ABC overhead cost of 7,300 dollars.

For Job Custom, machine running overhead is 20 machine hours multiplied by 11.00 dollars, equal to 220 dollars; setup overhead is 8 setups multiplied by 300.00 dollars, equal to 2,400 dollars; and inspection overhead is 25 inspection hours multiplied by 30.00 dollars, equal to 750 dollars. Summing these three components, 220 plus 2,400 plus 750, gives Job Custom's ABC overhead cost of 3,370 dollars.

Comparing the two systems: Job Standard's overhead cost falls from 12,500 dollars under the current plant-wide rate to 7,300 dollars under ABC, a reduction of 5,200 dollars, meaning the current system overcosts Job Standard by 5,200 dollars. Job Custom's overhead cost rises from 500 dollars under the current plant-wide rate to 3,370 dollars under ABC, an increase of 2,870 dollars, meaning the current system undercosts Job Custom by 2,870 dollars. This is a direct, quantified demonstration of cost cross-subsidization: standard, high-volume jobs like Job Standard consume relatively few setups and inspection hours per machine hour, so allocating overhead purely on machine hours assigns them far more overhead than the activities they actually consume justify. Custom, low-volume jobs like Job Custom consume a disproportionate number of setups and inspection hours relative to their modest machine-hour usage, so the plant-wide rate assigns them far too little overhead.

This distortion directly explains Theo's observed bidding pattern. Because PPS's sales team quotes prices based on the current plant-wide rate, standard jobs like Job Standard are being quoted with 5,200 dollars of overhead cost they do not actually deserve, artificially inflating PPS's bid price relative to its true cost, and yet PPS is still winning these bids, meaning PPS's true underlying cost advantage on standard work is even larger than its bids currently reflect, letting it win competitively even while overpricing on paper. Conversely, custom jobs like Job Custom are being quoted with only 500 dollars of overhead when they actually consume 3,370 dollars, a 2,870 dollar understatement, meaning PPS is unknowingly bidding custom jobs below their true cost, yet still losing to specialty shops, which strongly suggests those competitors have a genuinely lower true cost structure for custom work, not simply a costing artifact, since PPS is already underbidding its own true cost and still losing.
model_response.recommendation:
I recommend PPS adopt activity-based costing on at least a management-reporting and bid-pricing basis, using the three activity pools identified: setups at 300.00 dollars per setup, inspection hours at 30.00 dollars per hour, and machine running at 11.00 dollars per machine hour. This would immediately correct the systematic overcosting of standard jobs and undercosting of custom jobs demonstrated above, giving Theo's sales team accurate cost information for every future bid.

For standard jobs, I recommend PPS's sales team recognize that current bids likely have more pricing room than the existing cost system suggests, since true costs are lower than currently quoted, potentially allowing PPS to bid more aggressively on volume-driven contracts while maintaining margin, reinforcing its already strong competitive position in this segment. For custom jobs, I recommend PPS's sales team either raise quoted prices to reflect true cost, since the current 500 dollar overhead figure is understating true job cost by 2,870 dollars, or make a deliberate strategic decision about whether PPS wants to continue competing in the custom job segment at all, since the ABC analysis suggests PPS may not have a genuine cost advantage there and the pattern of underbidding true cost and still losing bids points to a real cost disadvantage versus specialty competitors, not merely a costing distortion.

To make ABC operational rather than a one-time exercise, I recommend PPS's plant controller begin routinely capturing setup counts and inspection hours by job in the existing job-tracking system, alongside machine hours already captured, and that PPS review and refresh the three activity rates at least annually as overhead spending and activity volumes change, starting with a full recost of PPS's active job backlog to identify any other significantly mispriced jobs currently in the pipeline.
model_response.conclusion: The plant-wide overhead rate has been quietly overcosting PPS's standard jobs by thousands of dollars each and undercosting custom jobs by a similar magnitude, and this distortion, not a genuine efficiency gap, explains much of the competitive bidding pattern Theo has observed. Moving PPS's sales team onto activity-based cost data should let Theo bid more confidently and profitably on standard work while making an informed strategic choice about the custom job segment rather than continuing to compete there on distorted numbers.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly calculates the three ABC activity rates (300.00 per setup, 30.00 per inspection hour, 11.00 per machine hour)
Correctly recosts Job Standard's ABC overhead as 7,300 dollars (5,500 plus 600 plus 1,200)
Correctly recosts Job Custom's ABC overhead as 3,370 dollars (220 plus 2,400 plus 750)
Correctly quantifies the 5,200 dollar overcosting of Job Standard under the current system
Correctly quantifies the 2,870 dollar undercosting of Job Custom under the current system
Explains the cross-subsidization mechanism: machine-hour allocation overstates overhead for jobs with few setups/inspection hours relative to machine hours, and understates it for jobs with many
Connects the overcosting of standard jobs to why PPS still wins those bids despite the inflated quoted cost

Connects the undercosting of custom jobs to the insight that PPS is losing bids even while underbidding its own true cost, implying a genuine competitive disadvantage rather than only a costing artifact
Recommends a specific pricing action for standard jobs (e.g., more aggressive bidding given true cost is lower)
Recommends a specific pricing or strategic action for custom jobs (raise prices or reconsider the segment)
Recommends practical operational steps to sustain ABC data collection (setup counts, inspection hours tracked by job going forward)
Recommends periodic refresh of activity rates rather than treating the analysis as a one-time exercise

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Follows the CPA Way, using the ABC recost to directly test and confirm Theo's classmate's hypothesis about the costing system
Translates the technical ABC recost into a direct, actionable explanation of the sales team's competitive bidding pattern
Explains the ABC concept and findings in terms usable directly by Theo with his non-accounting sales team

rating_guidance: A CD response correctly calculates all three ABC rates and both jobs' recosted overhead, correctly quantifies both the overcosting and undercosting amounts, and goes beyond the arithmetic to draw the sharper strategic insight that PPS's custom job bidding loss reflects a genuine cost disadvantage (since PPS is already underbidding its own true ABC cost and still losing), not just a costing artifact, translating this into a specific segment strategy recommendation. A C response correctly performs all the ABC calculations and identifies the overcosting and undercosting amounts and gives a reasonable explanation of cross-subsidization, but may not push through to the sharper insight about the custom segment's true competitive position. An RC response attempts the ABC recost but makes a calculation error in one of the three activity pools, or correctly calculates the numbers but fails to connect them coherently to Theo's observed bidding pattern. An NC response does not correctly apply the three ABC rates, continues to rely on the plant-wide rate for its conclusions, or provides a recommendation unconnected to the quantitative findings.

enabling_focus: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
Adding Value
title: Prairie Snack Co: Evaluating a Special Order from a National Retailer
case_background:
Prairie Snack Co (PSC) is a mid-sized food manufacturer based in Winnipeg, Manitoba, producing granola bars sold under its own PSC brand to independent grocery stores and regional distributors across the Prairie provinces. You, CPA, are PSC's controller, reporting to the founder and CEO, Wendell Osei. PSC operates a single production line with annual practical capacity of 3,000,000 units, and current annual production and sales volume is 2,750,000 units, leaving 250,000 units of unused annual capacity under normal operations.

Wendell has been approached by SaveMart, a national grocery retail chain, with an offer to purchase a one-time special order of 100,000 granola bar units, to be sold under SaveMart's own private-label packaging rather than the PSC brand. SaveMart has offered a price of 1.55 dollars per unit, well below PSC's normal wholesale price of 2.20 dollars per unit charged to its regular independent grocery and distributor customers. Wendell's initial reaction was that the SaveMart price is too low to be worth pursuing, since it does not come close to covering PSC's normal per-unit costs, but he has asked you to properly evaluate the order using relevant costing before he responds to SaveMart, since he knows from past experience that special order pricing decisions are not as simple as comparing the offered price to the normal wholesale price.

You confirmed with the plant manager that fulfilling the SaveMart order would not require PSC to turn away any of its regular customers, since the order fits entirely within the 250,000 units of currently unused annual capacity, meaning none of PSC's existing regular-priced business would be displaced. You also confirmed the order has two specific incremental requirements beyond PSC's normal production process. First, SaveMart requires its own private-label packaging rather than PSC's standard branded packaging, which will cost an incremental 0.12 dollars per unit in specialized packaging material and additional labour to apply it. Second, because the private-label packaging design is entirely new, PSC's plate and die shop will need to produce a one-time custom die and printing plate set specifically for this order, at a fixed one-time cost of 8,000 dollars that would not recur on any future order.

Wendell has raised two additional concerns he wants addressed before making a final decision. First, PSC's annual fixed manufacturing overhead of 1,800,000 dollars is currently allocated across all units produced under PSC's standard costing system; he wants to understand clearly why this fixed overhead should or should not factor into the special order decision given the order fits within existing unused capacity. Second, Wendell is worried about a longer-term relationship risk: if PSC accepts a private-label order at 1.55 dollars per unit, he is concerned that either SaveMart could use this as a foothold to push for lower pricing on a larger recurring volume in the future, or that PSC's existing regular wholesale customers could learn about the discounted SaveMart pricing and demand similar concessions, potentially eroding PSC's normal 2.20 dollar price point across its regular customer base. Wendell has asked you to quantify the financial impact of the SaveMart order on a relevant costing basis, and to give him a clear recommendation that also addresses these qualitative relationship risks before he responds to SaveMart's offer.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: PSC Standard Unit Cost and Capacity Data
exhibits.content: PSC's practical annual production capacity on its single production line is 3,000,000 units. Current annual production and sales volume to PSC's regular customer base is 2,750,000 units, leaving 250,000 units of unused annual capacity available without any capital investment or additional shift scheduling. PSC's standard variable cost per unit consists of the following: ingredients (oats, honey, nuts, and other raw materials) at 0.68 dollars per unit, direct labour at 0.35 dollars per unit, and variable manufacturing overhead (packaging line power, minor consumables) at 0.22 dollars per unit, for a total standard variable cost of 1.25 dollars per unit. PSC's annual fixed manufacturing overhead, covering the production line lease, permanent salaried supervisory staff, and equipment depreciation, is 1,800,000 dollars annually, a cost level that does not change with production volume within the plant's 3,000,000 unit practical capacity range. PSC's normal wholesale selling price to its regular independent grocery and distributor customers is 2.20 dollars per unit.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: SaveMart Special Order Terms
exhibits.content: SaveMart has offered to purchase a one-time special order of 100,000 granola bar units at a price of 1.55 dollars per unit, to be packaged under SaveMart's own private-label branding rather than PSC's standard branded packaging. The order would be fulfilled entirely from PSC's existing unused capacity, requiring no displacement of any regular customer order, confirmed by the plant manager based on current production scheduling. The private-label packaging requires specialized packaging material and additional application labour not used in PSC's standard branded packaging process, at an incremental cost of 0.12 dollars per unit above PSC's standard variable cost structure. Because the private-label design is new, PSC's plate and die shop will incur a one-time fixed cost of 8,000 dollars to produce the custom die and printing plate set required specifically for this order's packaging; this cost would not recur on any future order regardless of volume, and the die and plate set would have no resale or alternative use value if the SaveMart order does not proceed.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 3: Relationship and Market Considerations
exhibits.content: Wendell has raised two relationship risk concerns regarding the SaveMart order. First, while SaveMart has described this specific offer as a one-time trial order, Wendell is aware from industry contacts that national retailers sometimes use a discounted trial order as a basis to negotiate a larger recurring private-label supply contract at similar or further-reduced pricing, which could pressure PSC's overall margin profile if SaveMart later seeks a much larger ongoing volume at close to 1.55 dollars per unit. Second, Wendell is concerned about price transparency risk: PSC's existing regular wholesale customers, who currently pay 2.20 dollars per unit, are not party to the SaveMart negotiation and are unaware of its terms, but Wendell believes there is a moderate risk that pricing information could become known in the grocery trade over time, potentially prompting some regular customers to request similar discounts, which would erode PSC's normal pricing across its larger, ongoing customer base of 2,750,000 units annually, a volume far larger than the 100,000 unit SaveMart order itself.

requirements: Using relevant costing, determine the financial impact of accepting the SaveMart special order, and explain why PSC's fixed manufacturing overhead should or should not be included in the analysis.
Recommend whether PSC should accept the SaveMart special order on financial grounds alone.
Discuss the relationship and pricing-transparency risks Wendell raised, and explain how they should factor into the final decision beyond the pure financial analysis.

model_response.understand_issue: Wendell Osei, PSC's founder and CEO, has asked me, as controller, to evaluate a one-time special order from SaveMart using relevant costing before he responds to their offer. The audience is Wendell, and the ask is threefold: quantify the financial impact of accepting the order and explain the correct treatment of fixed overhead, give a financial-grounds recommendation, and address the longer-term relationship and pricing-transparency risks that a purely financial analysis would not capture.
model_response.analyze_case:
PSC's fixed manufacturing overhead of 1,800,000 dollars annually should not be included in the relevant cost of the SaveMart order because the order fits entirely within PSC's existing 250,000 units of unused annual capacity, confirmed by the plant manager. Fixed manufacturing overhead is being incurred at the same 1,800,000 dollar level regardless of whether PSC produces 2,750,000 units for its regular customers alone or 2,850,000 units including the SaveMart order, since production remains well within the 3,000,000 unit practical capacity. Because this cost does not change based on the decision, it is irrelevant to the special order evaluation; only costs and revenues that differ between accepting and rejecting the order are relevant.

The relevant variable cost per unit for the SaveMart order is PSC's standard variable cost of 1.25 dollars (0.68 ingredients plus 0.35 direct labour plus 0.22 variable overhead) plus the incremental private-label packaging cost of 0.12 dollars per unit specific to this order, for a total relevant variable cost of 1.37 dollars per unit. Against SaveMart's offered price of 1.55 dollars per unit, this produces a contribution margin of 1.55 minus 1.37, equal to 0.18 dollars per unit. Across the full order volume of 100,000 units, this generates total contribution margin of 100,000 multiplied by 0.18 dollars, equal to 18,000 dollars.

Against this contribution margin, the order also requires a one-time fixed cost of 8,000 dollars for the custom die and printing plate set, which is entirely relevant to this decision since it would not be incurred at all if the order is rejected and has no alternative use or resale value if accepted. Netting this one-time cost against the contribution margin, 18,000 dollars minus 8,000 dollars, gives a net financial benefit of 10,000 dollars from accepting the SaveMart order.

This demonstrates why Wendell's initial instinct, comparing SaveMart's 1.55 dollar price to PSC's normal 2.20 dollar wholesale price, was the wrong basis for the decision. Under a full-cost or normal-pricing lens, the SaveMart price looks unacceptably low; PSC's normal price needs to cover its variable cost of 1.25 dollars plus a share of the 1,800,000 dollars of fixed overhead plus a normal profit margin. But because the order uses otherwise idle capacity that would not generate any revenue at all if left unused, and because none of PSC's regular 2.20 dollar business is displaced, the correct comparison is only against the 1.37 dollar relevant cost, not the 2.20 dollar normal price or any cost figure that embeds fixed overhead. On this relevant costing basis, the order is financially favourable by 10,000 dollars.
model_response.recommendation:
On financial grounds alone, I recommend PSC accept the SaveMart special order, since it generates a net benefit of 10,000 dollars using idle capacity that would otherwise sit unused and produce no return at all. This is a modest but genuine financial benefit, and it comes with no displacement of any of PSC's regular, higher-margin business.

However, I recommend Wendell weigh the two relationship risks he raised before finalizing this decision, since the 10,000 dollar benefit is small enough that either risk could outweigh it if it materializes. On the recurring-volume risk, I recommend PSC explicitly document with SaveMart, in writing, that the 1.55 dollar price applies only to this specific 100,000 unit trial order and does not establish any precedent or expectation for future order pricing, so that if SaveMart does return seeking a larger recurring contract, PSC negotiates that separately and on its own merits rather than being anchored to the trial price.

On the pricing-transparency risk to PSC's regular 2.20 dollar customer base, I recommend PSC ensure the SaveMart private-label product is packaged and marketed in a way clearly distinct from PSC's own branded product, reducing the chance that regular customers or the broader trade associate the discounted price with PSC's core branded business, and I recommend Wendell consider whether the reputational and pricing-integrity risk to PSC's much larger 2,750,000 unit regular business is worth taking for a 10,000 dollar one-time gain on only 100,000 units. If Wendell judges this risk to be more than moderate, I would recommend PSC either decline the order or negotiate a higher price with SaveMart specifically to build in a larger margin of safety against this risk, even if it means SaveMart may decline the order.
model_response.conclusion: The SaveMart order is financially favourable by a modest 10,000 dollars once analyzed on a proper relevant costing basis using PSC's idle capacity, a very different conclusion than Wendell's initial instinct based on comparing it to PSC's normal wholesale price. Given how small this financial benefit is relative to PSC's much larger regular customer base, the final decision should rest heavily on Wendell's judgment of the recurring-volume and pricing-transparency risks, protected where possible through clear contractual terms and distinct product branding.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly identifies that fixed manufacturing overhead is irrelevant because the order fits within unused capacity and does not change total fixed cost
Correctly calculates the relevant variable cost per unit of 1.37 dollars (1.25 plus 0.12 incremental packaging)
Correctly calculates the contribution margin per unit of 0.18 dollars (1.55 minus 1.37)
Correctly calculates total contribution margin of 18,000 dollars across the 100,000 unit order
Correctly nets the one-time 8,000 dollar die and plate cost against the contribution margin to arrive at a net benefit of 10,000 dollars
Correctly explains why comparing SaveMart's price to PSC's normal 2.20 dollar wholesale price is the wrong basis for the decision
Confirms no displacement of regular customer volume occurs and explains why this matters to the relevant costing conclusion
Recommends acceptance on financial grounds with the correct 10,000 dollar figure cited
Recommends a specific contractual protection against the order being treated as a pricing precedent for future recurring volume
Recommends a specific mitigation for the pricing-transparency risk to PSC's regular customer base, such as distinct branding or packaging separation
Explicitly weighs the modest size of the 10,000 dollar benefit against the scale of PSC's much larger regular customer base at risk

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Structures the analysis following CPA Way principles, distinguishing the relevant costing conclusion from the broader qualitative business judgment
Directly corrects Wendell's initial flawed comparison and explains the correct decision basis in terms he can use
Does not incorrectly treat the 8,000 dollar die and plate cost as relevant on a per-unit rather than lump-sum, one-time basis
Presents the relevant costing conclusion and the qualitative risk discussion in a clearly separated, decision-useful structure suited to a CEO audience

rating_guidance: A CD response correctly performs the full relevant costing analysis (1.37 dollar relevant unit cost, 18,000 dollar contribution margin, 10,000 dollar net benefit after the one-time die cost), correctly explains why fixed overhead and the normal 2.20 dollar price are irrelevant, and meaningfully weighs the qualitative relationship risks against the modest size of the financial benefit rather than treating the financial and qualitative analyses as disconnected sections. A C response correctly performs the relevant costing calculations and reaches the 10,000 dollar figure and a reasonable accept recommendation, but may address the qualitative risks in a more generic way without tying them back to the modest size of the financial benefit. An RC response attempts the analysis but makes an error, such as including fixed overhead in the relevant cost, comparing the SaveMart price to the 2.20 dollar normal price instead of the 1.37 dollar relevant cost, or failing to net the one-time die cost against the contribution margin. An NC response concludes the order should be rejected purely because 1.55 dollars is below the 2.20 dollar normal price or below the 1.25 dollar variable cost including an allocated share of fixed overhead, without any relevant costing analysis.

Adding Value
Communicating
title: Summit Aerospace Components Inc: Optimizing Product Mix Under a Machine Hour Constraint
case_background:
Summit Aerospace Components Inc (SACI) is a contract manufacturer based in Mississauga, Ontario, producing precision machined parts for aerospace industry clients under long-term supply agreements. SACI produces three parts on the same bank of CNC machining centres: Bracket, a structural mounting bracket; Housing, a gearbox housing component; and Valve, a hydraulic control valve body. You, CPA, are SACI's management accountant, reporting to the VP of operations, Cheryl Danto.

SACI's CNC machining centres are the company's single production bottleneck. Total available CNC machine hours are 20,000 hours per month across all machines, and this capacity cannot be meaningfully increased in the short term, since additional CNC machines require a lead time of over a year to procure and install, and SACI's clients will not accept extended delivery timelines given the safety-critical nature of aerospace components. Cheryl has asked you to review SACI's current production planning approach, since she has noticed the plant scheduling team has historically prioritized production based on which part earns the highest contribution margin per unit, without considering how much scarce machine time each part actually consumes to earn that margin.

Current monthly customer demand exceeds what SACI's 20,000 hours of machine capacity can produce across all three parts combined, meaning SACI cannot fulfill every client's full order in any given month and must choose which orders to prioritize. Cheryl confirmed the monthly demand, selling price, and variable cost for each part, along with the CNC machine hours each unit requires, and she has asked you to determine the production mix that maximizes SACI's total monthly contribution margin given the 20,000 hour constraint, compare this to what the current per-unit-contribution-margin prioritization approach would produce, and quantify the financial impact of correcting the scheduling team's approach.

Cheryl has also flagged a client relationship consideration that complicates a purely mechanical optimization. Housing is produced exclusively for SACI's oldest and largest aerospace client, a Tier 1 supplier to a major aircraft manufacturer, under a long-term supply agreement that includes minimum annual volume commitments and significant contractual penalties if SACI fails to deliver at least 70 percent of the client's monthly ordered volume in any given month. Cheryl is concerned that an unconstrained contribution-margin-per-machine-hour optimization could result in zero or near-zero Housing production in some months, which would breach this contractual minimum and expose SACI to significant penalty costs and reputational damage with its most important client, a consequence that would not show up anywhere in a simple monthly contribution margin maximization calculation.

Cheryl has asked you to first perform the theoretically optimal product mix analysis to establish the maximum possible monthly contribution margin under the machine hour constraint, and then to layer in the Housing contractual minimum as a practical constraint on top of the theoretical optimum, providing a revised, contractually compliant production plan along with the contribution margin this compliant plan would generate, so she can understand both the size of the true optimization opportunity and the cost of maintaining the contractual commitment to SACI's Tier 1 client.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Part-Level Selling Price, Variable Cost, and Machine Hour Data
exhibits.content: Bracket: selling price 340.00 dollars per unit, variable cost 180.00 dollars per unit, contribution margin 160.00 dollars per unit, CNC machine hours required per unit 4.0 hours, producing a contribution margin per machine hour of 160.00 divided by 4.0, equal to 40.00 dollars per hour. Housing: selling price 520.00 dollars per unit, variable cost 260.00 dollars per unit, contribution margin 260.00 dollars per unit, CNC machine hours required per unit 8.0 hours, producing a contribution margin per machine hour of 260.00 divided by 8.0, equal to 32.50 dollars per hour. Valve: selling price 215.00 dollars per unit, variable cost 95.00 dollars per unit, contribution margin 120.00 dollars per unit, CNC machine hours required per unit 2.0 hours, producing a contribution margin per machine hour of 120.00 divided by 2.0, equal to 60.00 dollars per hour.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Monthly Customer Demand and Total Machine Hours Required
exhibits.content: Confirmed monthly customer demand for each part, and the total CNC machine hours that would be required to fully satisfy that demand, is as follows. Bracket: monthly demand of 3,000 units, requiring 3,000 multiplied by 4.0 hours, equal to 12,000 machine hours to fully satisfy. Housing: monthly demand of 1,500 units, requiring 1,500 multiplied by 8.0 hours, equal to 12,000 machine hours to fully satisfy. Valve: monthly demand of 4,000 units, requiring 4,000 multiplied by 2.0 hours, equal to 8,000 machine hours to fully satisfy. Total machine hours required to fully satisfy all three parts' demand is 12,000 plus 12,000 plus 8,000, equal to 32,000 hours, against total available capacity of only 20,000 hours per month, confirming the CNC machining centres are a binding constraint and SACI cannot produce enough of all three parts to meet full demand in any given month.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 3: Housing Contractual Minimum Volume Commitment
exhibits.content: Housing is produced exclusively under a long-term supply agreement with SACI's largest aerospace client, a Tier 1 supplier to a major commercial aircraft manufacturer. The agreement requires SACI to deliver at least 70 percent of the client's monthly ordered volume of 1,500 units in any given month, meaning a contractual minimum of 1,500 multiplied by 70 percent, equal to 1,050 units of Housing must be produced and delivered monthly. Failure to meet this minimum triggers financial penalties under the agreement and places SACI at risk of losing preferred supplier status on future contract renewals with this client, which Cheryl has described as SACI's single most important customer relationship, representing a substantial share of SACI's total annual revenue beyond just the Housing part line. Cheryl has confirmed no such contractual minimum exists for either the Bracket or Valve product lines, both of which are sold to multiple different aerospace clients under standard purchase order arrangements without long-term minimum volume commitments.

requirements: Determine the theoretically optimal monthly production mix that maximizes SACI's total contribution margin under the 20,000 hour CNC machine capacity constraint, ranking parts by contribution margin per machine hour rather than per unit.
Quantify the financial difference between this optimal mix and what a naive per-unit contribution margin prioritization approach would produce.

Revise the production plan to incorporate the Housing contractual minimum volume commitment, and quantify the contribution margin impact of maintaining this commitment relative to the unconstrained theoretical optimum.

model_response.understand_issue: Cheryl Danto, SACI's VP of operations, has asked me, as management accountant, to correct the plant scheduling team's product mix approach, which currently prioritizes production based on contribution margin per unit rather than per unit of the true scarce resource, CNC machine hours. The audience is Cheryl, and the ask is threefold: determine the theoretically optimal production mix under the 20,000 hour constraint, quantify the value lost under the current flawed approach, and produce a contractually compliant revised plan that respects the Housing minimum volume commitment to SACI's most important client.
model_response.analyze_case:
When a single resource constrains production, the correct optimization criterion is contribution margin per unit of the scarce resource, not contribution margin per unit of output. Ranking the three parts by contribution margin per machine hour: Valve earns 60.00 dollars per hour, Bracket earns 40.00 dollars per hour, and Housing earns 32.50 dollars per hour. The theoretically optimal plan produces parts in this order until either demand is fully satisfied for a part or capacity runs out.

Producing Valve first, its full monthly demand of 4,000 units requires 4,000 multiplied by 2.0 hours, equal to 8,000 machine hours, leaving 20,000 minus 8,000, equal to 12,000 hours of remaining capacity. Producing Bracket next, its full monthly demand of 3,000 units requires 3,000 multiplied by 4.0 hours, equal to 12,000 machine hours, exactly consuming all remaining capacity and leaving zero hours for Housing. Under this theoretical optimum, Housing production would be zero units in the month.

Total contribution margin under this theoretical optimum is Valve's 4,000 units multiplied by 120.00 dollars, equal to 480,000 dollars, plus Bracket's 3,000 units multiplied by 160.00 dollars, equal to 480,000 dollars, plus Housing's zero units, for a total of 480,000 plus 480,000, equal to 960,000 dollars per month.

By contrast, the scheduling team's current naive approach, prioritizing by contribution margin per unit (Housing at 260.00 dollars per unit ranks highest, then Bracket at 160.00 dollars, then Valve at 120.00 dollars), would produce Housing first: full demand of 1,500 units requires 1,500 multiplied by 8.0 hours, equal to 12,000 machine hours, leaving 20,000 minus 12,000, equal to 8,000 hours remaining. Producing Bracket next with the remaining 8,000 hours, 8,000 divided by 4.0 hours per unit, equal to 2,000 units (below full demand of 3,000), consuming all remaining capacity and leaving zero hours for Valve. Total contribution margin under this naive approach is Housing's 1,500 units multiplied by 260.00 dollars, equal to 390,000 dollars, plus Bracket's 2,000 units multiplied by 160.00 dollars, equal to 320,000 dollars, plus Valve's zero units, for a total of 390,000 plus 320,000, equal to 710,000 dollars per month.

The financial cost of the scheduling team's naive per-unit prioritization approach is 960,000 dollars minus 710,000 dollars, equal to 250,000 dollars per month, or 3,000,000 dollars annually, in foregone contribution margin, simply from ranking products by the wrong criterion. This confirms Cheryl's concern was well founded and represents a very large, purely mechanical correction opportunity with no operational change required beyond re-sequencing the scheduling logic.

However, the theoretical optimum produces zero Housing units, which breaches the contractual minimum of 1,050 units (70 percent of the client's 1,500 unit monthly order) described in Exhibit 3. A contractually compliant plan must first reserve 1,050 units multiplied by 8.0 hours, equal to 8,400 machine hours for Housing, leaving 20,000 minus 8,400, equal to 11,600 hours for Bracket and Valve. Applying the same per-hour ranking to the remaining capacity: Valve first, full demand of 4,000 units requires 8,000 hours, leaving 11,600 minus 8,000, equal to 3,600 hours for Bracket, producing 3,600 divided by 4.0 hours per unit, equal to 900 units of Bracket (below full demand of 3,000). Total contribution margin under this compliant plan is Housing's 1,050 units multiplied by 260.00 dollars, equal to 273,000 dollars, plus Valve's 4,000 units multiplied by 120.00 dollars, equal to 480,000 dollars, plus Bracket's 900 units multiplied by 160.00 dollars, equal to 144,000 dollars, for a total of 273,000 plus 480,000 plus 144,000, equal to 897,000 dollars per month.
model_response.recommendation:
I recommend SACI's scheduling team immediately switch its production prioritization logic from contribution margin per unit to contribution margin per CNC machine hour, since correcting this alone represents approximately 250,000 dollars per month, or roughly 3,000,000 dollars annually, in currently foregone contribution margin under the unconstrained optimum, before even considering the Housing contractual constraint.

Given the Housing contractual minimum, I recommend SACI adopt the contractually compliant plan of 1,050 Housing units, 4,000 Valve units, and 900 Bracket units monthly, generating 897,000 dollars of monthly contribution margin. This is 960,000 minus 897,000, equal to 63,000 dollars per month lower than the unconstrained theoretical optimum, but I recommend Cheryl treat this 63,000 dollar monthly gap as the quantified cost of maintaining SACI's most important client relationship and its contractual compliance, since breaching the Housing minimum risks financial penalties and the loss of preferred supplier status with a client Cheryl has identified as representing a substantial share of SACI's total revenue, a risk far larger than 63,000 dollars per month if the relationship were damaged.

I further recommend SACI's sales and contracts team explore whether Bracket and Valve, both sold without long-term minimum commitments, could be renegotiated with their respective clients toward longer-term agreements with modest price premiums, given SACI now has clear visibility that these two parts, particularly Valve, generate the highest margin per unit of scarce capacity and represent SACI's most valuable use of constrained machine time.
model_response.conclusion: Correcting the scheduling team's prioritization logic from a per-unit to a per-machine-hour basis is worth approximately 250,000 dollars per month on an unconstrained basis, and even after fully respecting the Housing contractual minimum to protect SACI's most important aerospace client relationship, the compliant plan still captures 897,000 dollars of the 960,000 dollar theoretical optimum, a result far superior to the 710,000 dollars the current flawed approach would have delivered while also fully protecting the Housing relationship.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly calculates contribution margin per machine hour for all three parts (Valve 60.00, Bracket 40.00, Housing 32.50)
Correctly ranks parts by contribution margin per machine hour rather than per unit
Correctly builds the unconstrained theoretical optimal plan (4,000 Valve, 3,000 Bracket, 0 Housing) respecting the 20,000 hour limit
Correctly calculates the theoretical optimum total contribution margin of 960,000 dollars
Correctly builds the naive per-unit-prioritized plan (1,500 Housing, 2,000 Bracket, 0 Valve) and calculates its 710,000 dollar contribution margin
Correctly quantifies the 250,000 dollar monthly cost of the scheduling team's flawed prioritization approach
Correctly calculates the Housing contractual minimum of 1,050 units (70 percent of 1,500)
Correctly builds the contractually compliant plan (1,050 Housing, 4,000 Valve, 900 Bracket) reserving hours for Housing first
Correctly calculates the compliant plan's total contribution margin of 897,000 dollars
Correctly quantifies the 63,000 dollar monthly cost of maintaining the Housing contractual commitment relative to the unconstrained optimum
Frames the 63,000 dollar gap as a reasoned cost of protecting the client relationship rather than simply a loss
Recognizes the contractual and relationship risk associated with the Tier 1 aerospace client as distinct from the pure optimization exercise
Recommends renegotiating longer-term agreements for Bracket and/or Valve given their now-demonstrated high value per scarce hour

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Correctly applies theory of constraints logic in the proper sequence, from unconstrained optimum through to a practically constrained final plan
Clearly presents three distinct scenarios (naive, theoretical optimum, contractually compliant) with contribution margin figures for each so Cheryl can compare them directly

rating_guidance: A CD response correctly performs all three production mix scenarios (naive per-unit, unconstrained per-hour optimum, and contractually compliant plan), correctly calculates all three contribution margin totals (710,000, 960,000, and 897,000 dollars), and explicitly frames the 250,000 dollar prioritization-error gap and the 63,000 dollar contractual-compliance gap as two distinct, separately meaningful financial insights for Cheryl. A C response correctly builds the unconstrained optimal plan using contribution margin per machine hour and calculates a reasonable compliant plan incorporating the Housing minimum, but may not fully quantify the naive-approach comparison or the precise cost of contractual compliance. An RC response attempts a constrained optimization but makes an error, such as ranking by contribution margin per unit instead of per hour for the final recommended plan, or failing to reserve capacity for the Housing minimum before allocating the remainder. An NC response does not use machine hours as the ranking basis at all, ignores the binding constraint, or fails to address the Housing contractual minimum entirely.

title: Northern Alloys Manufacturing: Investigating Standard Costing Variances
case_background:
Northern Alloys Manufacturing (NAM) operates an aluminum ingot production facility in Trois-Rivieres, Quebec, supplying aluminum ingots to automotive and construction industry customers across Eastern Canada. You, CPA, are NAM's newly appointed cost accountant, reporting to the plant controller, Simone Belliveau. NAM uses a standard costing system for its ingot production, with standard costs for direct material and direct labour established annually by the engineering and industrial relations teams based on expected input quantities and prices.

Simone has asked you to prepare the monthly variance analysis for the aluminum ingot production line following the most recently completed month, since NAM's plant manager, Real Poitras, has raised concerns after noticing the month's total production cost came in noticeably above what the standard cost per ingot would predict, and he wants to understand specifically where the overspending occurred before deciding whether any corrective action is needed on the production floor. Simone has emphasized to you that a proper variance analysis must separate price-related causes from quantity or efficiency-related causes for both material and labour, since the appropriate corrective action, and the individual likely responsible, differs significantly depending on which type of variance is driving the overspending; a purchasing manager controls material price, while a production supervisor controls material usage efficiency and labour efficiency, and NAM's unionized labour agreement, not plant management, controls the labour rate.

During the month, NAM produced 8,000 aluminum ingots. You pulled the standard cost card for the ingot, which specifies the standard quantity of raw aluminum input and standard direct labour hours required per ingot, along with the standard price per kilogram of raw material and the standard labour rate per hour. You then pulled the actual production data for the month from NAM's shop floor and purchasing records, confirming the actual kilograms of raw aluminum consumed and the actual price paid per kilogram, as well as the actual direct labour hours worked and the actual average labour rate paid during the month.

Real has specifically asked whether the overspending is more attributable to NAM's purchasing team paying more than expected for raw aluminum, given recent volatility in global aluminum commodity prices that has been widely reported in industry news, or whether it reflects the production floor using more raw material or labour hours than the standard allows, which could point to a process or equipment issue on the line, such as excessive scrap or rework, that would need to be investigated and corrected. He has also asked about the labour side specifically, since NAM recently settled a new three-year collective agreement with its production union that included a wage increase effective partway through this fiscal year, and Real wants to know how much of any labour variance is attributable to that agreed wage increase, which management has no ability to reverse, versus how much reflects a genuine efficiency problem in how labour hours are being used on the production floor, which would be a legitimate target for supervisory corrective action.

Simone has asked you to calculate the material price variance, material quantity variance, labour rate variance, and labour efficiency variance for the month, reconcile each pair of variances to the total material and total labour variance, and provide Real with a clear explanation of what each variance means, who is generally responsible for it, and what specific investigation or corrective action, if any, you would recommend based on the pattern the numbers reveal.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Standard Cost Card, Aluminum Ingot
exhibits.content: NAM's standard cost card for one aluminum ingot specifies the following inputs. Direct material: standard quantity of 25 kilograms of raw aluminum per ingot, at a standard price of 2.40 dollars per kilogram, for a standard material cost per ingot of 25 multiplied by 2.40, equal to 60.00 dollars. Direct labour: standard hours of 1.5 hours per ingot, at a standard labour rate of 28.00 dollars per hour, for a standard labour cost per ingot of 1.5 multiplied by 28.00, equal to 42.00 dollars. These standards were set at the start of the fiscal year by NAM's engineering team, based on expected process yields and the labour rate in effect under the prior collective agreement, and are reviewed and updated annually or when a material change such as a new collective agreement occurs.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Actual Production Data for the Month
exhibits.content: During the month, NAM produced 8,000 aluminum ingots. Actual direct material consumed was 205,000 kilograms of raw aluminum, purchased and used at an actual average price of 2.55 dollars per kilogram, for total actual material cost of 205,000 multiplied by 2.55 dollars, equal to 522,750 dollars. Actual direct labour hours worked on ingot production during the month totalled 11,600 hours, paid at an actual average labour rate of 27.50 dollars per hour, for total actual labour cost of 11,600 multiplied by 27.50 dollars, equal to 319,000 dollars. The standard quantity of material allowed for actual production of 8,000 ingots is 8,000 multiplied by 25 kilograms, equal to 200,000 kilograms, at the standard price of 2.40 dollars, for a standard material cost allowed of 480,000 dollars. The standard direct labour hours allowed for actual production of 8,000 ingots is 8,000 multiplied by 1.5 hours, equal to 12,000 hours, at the standard rate of 28.00 dollars, for a standard labour cost allowed of 336,000 dollars.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 3: Notes on Aluminum Commodity Prices and the New Collective Agreement
exhibits.content: NAM's purchasing team has confirmed that global benchmark aluminum commodity prices rose significantly during the month due to widely reported supply disruptions at several major international smelters, an industry-wide market condition outside the direct control of NAM's own purchasing team, though the purchasing team is still responsible for negotiating supplier contracts and timing purchases within that broader market environment. Separately, NAM's new three-year collective agreement with its production union, ratified earlier in the fiscal year, included a wage increase for production labour effective partway through the year; however, NAM's standard cost card in Exhibit 1 was confirmed by Simone to have already been updated to reflect the new 28.00 dollar contractual rate at the time the standards were set for this fiscal year, meaning the actual labour rate paid this month, 27.50 dollars per hour, already reflects the new contract and is in fact slightly below, not above, the updated standard rate.

requirements: Calculate the material price variance and material quantity variance for the month, reconcile them to the total material variance, and identify who is generally responsible for each.
Calculate the labour rate variance and labour efficiency variance for the month, reconcile them to the total labour variance, and identify who is generally responsible for each.

Based on your variance analysis, advise Real on which specific variances warrant further investigation and corrective action, and which do not, addressing his specific questions about aluminum commodity price volatility and the new collective agreement.

model_response.understand_issue: Simone Belliveau, NAM's plant controller, has asked me, as cost accountant, to prepare the month's material and labour variance analysis for the aluminum ingot line and explain the findings to plant manager Real Poitras. The audience is Real, and the ask is threefold: calculate and reconcile the material price and quantity variances, calculate and reconcile the labour rate and efficiency variances, and advise specifically which variances warrant floor-level corrective action versus which reflect factors outside management's control, addressing Real's specific questions about commodity price volatility and the new collective agreement.
model_response.analyze_case:
For direct material, the material price variance is calculated as (actual price minus standard price) multiplied by actual quantity purchased: (2.55 dollars minus 2.40 dollars) multiplied by 205,000 kilograms, equal to 0.15 dollars multiplied by 205,000, equal to 30,750 dollars unfavourable, since NAM paid more per kilogram than standard. The material quantity variance is calculated as (actual quantity used minus standard quantity allowed) multiplied by standard price: (205,000 kilograms minus 200,000 kilograms) multiplied by 2.40 dollars, equal to 5,000 kilograms multiplied by 2.40 dollars, equal to 12,000 dollars unfavourable, since NAM used more material than the standard allows for 8,000 ingots. These two variances reconcile to the total material variance: total actual material cost of 522,750 dollars minus total standard material cost allowed of 480,000 dollars, equal to 42,750 dollars unfavourable, which matches the sum of the price variance (30,750 unfavourable) and the quantity variance (12,000 unfavourable), 30,750 plus 12,000, equal to 42,750 dollars.

For direct labour, the labour rate variance is calculated as (actual rate minus standard rate) multiplied by actual hours worked: (27.50 dollars minus 28.00 dollars) multiplied by 11,600 hours, equal to negative 0.50 dollars multiplied by 11,600, equal to 5,800 dollars favourable, since NAM paid less per hour than standard. The labour efficiency variance is calculated as (actual hours worked minus standard hours allowed) multiplied by standard rate: (11,600 hours minus 12,000 hours) multiplied by 28.00 dollars, equal to negative 400 hours multiplied by 28.00 dollars, equal to 11,200 dollars favourable, since NAM used fewer labour hours than the standard allows for 8,000 ingots. These two variances reconcile to the total labour variance: total actual labour cost of 319,000 dollars minus total standard labour cost allowed of 336,000 dollars, equal to 17,000 dollars favourable, which matches the sum of the rate variance (5,800 favourable) and the efficiency variance (11,200 favourable), 5,800 plus 11,200, equal to 17,000 dollars.

Addressing Real's specific questions: the 30,750 dollar unfavourable material price variance is consistent with the widely reported global aluminum commodity price increase noted in Exhibit 3, an industry-wide market condition largely outside NAM's own purchasing team's control, though the purchasing team remains responsible for whether NAM's specific supplier contracts and purchase timing captured the best available price within that environment.
