Nadia and Priya want to understand the tax differences between these two financing approaches from ICB's perspective, specifically around how interest costs on the loan would be treated for tax purposes compared to how dividends paid on the new preferred shares would be treated, since they have heard in general terms that interest and dividends are not treated the same way for corporate tax purposes but do not understand the mechanics or why it matters for their decision. They have also asked whether bringing in outside investors through preferred shares would create any complications with their existing 50/50 common share ownership structure or their ability to control ICB's decisions going forward, since neither Nadia nor Priya wants to give up any control of the company to these outside investors even though they are comfortable receiving their investment capital.

You need to explain the differing tax treatment of interest on the proposed loan versus dividends on the proposed preferred shares from ICB's perspective as the payer, address how each option would affect ICB's after tax cost of financing the expansion, and address Nadia and Priya's control concern regarding the preferred share investors.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Bank Term Loan Proposal
exhibits.content: ICB's commercial bank has proposed a term loan of 950,000 dollars over a seven year term at a fixed interest rate, with regular blended principal and interest payments due monthly regardless of ICB's actual cash flow or profitability in any given period. The loan would be secured against the new brewing equipment and a general security interest over ICB's other business assets. Interest on the loan would be a fixed, contractually required cost to ICB for as long as the loan remains outstanding, and failure to make scheduled payments would place ICB in default under the loan agreement.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Proposed Preferred Share Investment
exhibits.content: Three outside investors, all family friends of Nadia and Priya, have expressed willingness to collectively invest 950,000 dollars in ICB in exchange for a new class of preferred shares carrying a fixed annual dividend rate, with dividends payable only when declared by ICB's board and only to the extent ICB has sufficient available cash flow, and with no dividend obligation in any year ICB chooses not to declare one, though unpaid dividends may accumulate depending on the specific share terms negotiated. The preferred shares would also carry specific redemption terms allowing ICB to redeem the shares from the investors after a set number of years, returning their original capital. As currently proposed, the new preferred shares would carry no voting rights and no right to participate in ICB's common share value or business decisions beyond the specific fixed dividend and redemption terms attached to the shares themselves.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 3: Nadia and Priya's Notes on Control
exhibits.content: Priya and I have worked incredibly hard to build ICB exactly the way we envisioned it, and while we are grateful these family friends are willing to invest and help us grow, we do not want to give up any say over how the brewery is run, what new products we develop, or any other business decisions, even though we are happy for them to earn a fair return on their investment. We want to understand clearly whether accepting their investment through these preferred shares would give them any voice in running ICB, or any claim on the actual value and future growth of the business beyond getting their fixed return and eventually their capital back, since maintaining our 50/50 partnership and full control between the two of us is very important to both of us.

requirements: Explain the differing tax treatment of interest paid on the proposed bank loan versus dividends paid on the proposed preferred shares from ICB's perspective, and how this affects ICB's after tax cost of financing under each option.
Advise Nadia and Priya on how the proposed preferred share structure would affect control of ICB, addressing their specific concern about voice in business decisions and claims on future value.

Recommend which financing approach, or combination of approaches, ICB should pursue for the expansion, taking into account both the tax cost and the cash flow flexibility differences between the two options.

model_response.understand_issue: I am acting as CPA advisor to Nadia Okafor and Priya Sandhu, the two founding shareholders of Ironwood Craft Brewery Inc, who are evaluating debt versus preferred share equity financing for a 950,000 dollar expansion. They need a clear explanation of the differing tax treatment of interest versus dividends from ICB's perspective, an assessment of whether the proposed preferred share structure would affect their control of the company, and a recommendation on which financing approach to pursue considering both tax cost and cash flow flexibility.
model_response.analyze_case:
Tax treatment of interest versus dividends: interest paid by ICB on the proposed bank loan is generally deductible to ICB in computing its income for tax purposes, provided the borrowed funds are used for the purpose of earning income from the business, which is clearly the case here given the funds are being used to finance new brewing equipment and expanded production capacity. This means each dollar of interest ICB pays reduces its taxable income and therefore its corporate tax payable, so the true after tax cost of the loan to ICB is the stated interest rate reduced by the tax savings generated from the deduction, meaning the effective cost of the debt financing is lower than the stated interest rate alone would suggest. Dividends paid by ICB on the proposed preferred shares, by contrast, are not deductible to ICB in computing its income, since dividends are a distribution of profit to shareholders after tax rather than a business expense incurred to earn income, meaning ICB must first pay corporate tax on its income and only then can pay dividends out of what remains after tax. This means the preferred share dividends are paid entirely from after tax dollars, with no offsetting deduction reducing ICB's taxable income the way loan interest does, making the true cost of the preferred share financing, dollar for dollar of the same stated rate, more expensive to ICB on an after tax basis than the loan interest would be.

Cash flow flexibility trade off: while the loan is more tax efficient due to interest deductibility, it carries fixed, legally required monthly payments regardless of ICB's actual performance during the expansion ramp up period, and missing payments would place ICB in default, a serious consequence, particularly during a period when the new production line and taproom addition may take time to reach full profitability. The preferred share structure, by contrast, offers meaningful cash flow flexibility, since dividends are only payable when declared by the board and only to the extent ICB has sufficient cash flow, meaning ICB is not contractually obligated to make payments during a slow period the way it would be under the loan, even though unpaid dividends may accumulate depending on the specific terms negotiated. This flexibility has real value to ICB precisely because the expansion involves execution risk and a ramp up period where cash flow may be uneven, and this benefit needs to be weighed against the higher after tax cost of the preferred share financing when comparing the two options overall, not simply comparing the two on tax cost in isolation.

Control implications of the preferred shares: based on the terms described, the proposed preferred shares would carry no voting rights and no participation in ICB's common share value or growth beyond the specific fixed dividend and redemption terms attached to the shares. This means the three outside investors would have no vote on business decisions, no seat at the table on strategic direction, product development, or any other operational matter, and no claim on any of ICB's growth in value beyond receiving their fixed return and eventual return of capital upon redemption. Nadia and Priya's 50/50 common share ownership and full voting control of ICB would remain entirely intact and unaffected by this structure, since the investors are receiving a fixed income type return through the preferred shares, similar economically in some respects to a lender, without any of the ownership rights that would come with holding common shares or voting preferred shares. This structure directly addresses Nadia and Priya's stated concern, allowing them to raise capital from the outside investors while retaining complete control of ICB's business decisions and full ownership of ICB's future growth in value between themselves.
model_response.recommendation:
I recommend Nadia and Priya consider a combination of both financing sources rather than treating this strictly as an either/or decision, given the different strengths of each: using some bank debt to take advantage of the lower after tax cost from interest deductibility, sized to a level ICB can comfortably service even during a slower ramp up period for the expanded production line and taproom, combined with some preferred share capital from the outside investors to provide additional funding with cash flow flexibility during the higher risk early period of the expansion, reducing the risk of loan default if the expansion takes longer than expected to reach full profitability.

If Nadia and Priya prefer a single source rather than a blended approach, I recommend they weigh the loan's lower after tax cost against its rigid payment obligations carefully against their own conservative assessment of how quickly the expansion is likely to generate sufficient incremental cash flow, since the more tax efficient loan option is also the riskier one from a cash flow standpoint during the ramp up period.

On control, I recommend Nadia and Priya proceed with confidence that the preferred share structure as currently proposed, with no voting rights and no participation in common share value, fully preserves their 50/50 ownership and complete control of ICB, provided the final share terms are drafted precisely to reflect this and reviewed carefully by legal counsel before the investment closes, to ensure no unintended rights are inadvertently granted to the investors.
model_response.conclusion: A blended approach using both bank debt and preferred share capital allows Nadia and Priya to capture the lower after tax cost of deductible interest while managing cash flow risk during the expansion's ramp up period, and the proposed preferred share structure, properly documented, lets them bring in outside capital without sacrificing any control over Ironwood Craft Brewery's business decisions or future growth.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly explains that interest paid by ICB is generally deductible in computing taxable income, provided borrowed funds are used to earn income
Correctly explains that dividends paid on preferred shares are not deductible to ICB, being a distribution of after tax profit
Correctly concludes the after tax cost of the loan is lower than the after tax cost of preferred share financing at the same stated rate, due to interest deductibility
Identifies and appropriately weighs the cash flow flexibility of the preferred share structure (dividends only when declared) against the fixed payment obligation of the loan
Connects the cash flow flexibility trade off specifically to the expansion's execution risk and ramp up period
Correctly concludes the proposed non-voting, non-participating preferred shares would not give the investors any voice in business decisions
Correctly concludes the preferred shares would not give the investors any claim on ICB's growth in value beyond their fixed return and capital redemption
Correctly confirms Nadia and Priya's 50/50 common share control remains fully intact under the proposed structure

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Addresses all three requirements (tax treatment/after tax cost, control implications, recommendation) in an organized, complete manner
Recommends a blended debt and preferred share financing approach rather than a simplistic either/or answer, tailored to the expansion's risk profile
Explains deductibility and after tax cost concepts in plain terms accessible to non-specialist owner-managers
Notes that final share terms should be carefully drafted and reviewed by counsel to ensure no unintended rights are granted to investors
Frames the recommendation around ICB's ability to comfortably service fixed obligations given the expansion's uncertain ramp up timeline
Does not invent a specific numeric interest or dividend rate not given in the case facts

marking_guide.competency: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
marking_guide.criterion: Presents a balanced view of both financing options rather than steering Nadia and Priya toward whichever option is simpler to explain

rating_guidance: A competent response correctly explains that loan interest is deductible while preferred share dividends are not, correctly concludes the loan has a lower after tax cost as a result, and correctly confirms the proposed preferred share terms fully preserve Nadia and Priya's control given the absence of voting rights and participation rights. A response with distinction goes further to properly weigh the cash flow flexibility benefit of the preferred shares against their higher after tax cost, explicitly connecting this trade off to the expansion's execution and ramp up risk, and recommends a thoughtful blended financing approach rather than treating the decision as a simple binary choice. Responses that recommend the loan purely because it is cheaper on an after tax basis, without engaging with the cash flow risk during the ramp up period, reflect an incomplete analysis and should not exceed reaching competent. Responses that incorrectly conclude the preferred shareholders would gain some form of control or claim on business value, contrary to the terms clearly described in the case, reflect a significant technical error and should not be rated competent on that requirement.

enabling_focus: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
Communicating
Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
title: Meridian Consulting Group: Personal Services Business Risk in a Contractor Arrangement
case_background:
Simon Achterberg is a senior IT infrastructure consultant who, until eighteen months ago, worked as a full time employee of a large technology consulting firm. Simon left that role and incorporated Meridian Consulting Group Inc (MCG), through which he now provides IT infrastructure consulting services. Shortly after incorporating, Simon entered into a services agreement with his former employer, under which MCG provides Simon's consulting services back to that same company on essentially a full time basis, with Simon working from the client's offices most days, using the client's equipment and systems, attending the client's regular staff meetings, following the client's direction on which projects to work on and how to prioritize them, and having no other clients at all, since the arrangement with his former employer occupies effectively all of his available working time.

Simon has come to you, MCG's newly engaged CPA, because his previous accountant, who set up the arrangement, told him this structure would allow him to pay significantly less overall tax than he was paying as an employee, by having MCG bill the client a consulting fee, paying Simon a modest salary from MCG, and retaining and eventually distributing the remainder through dividends and other corporate planning, taking advantage of the lower small business corporate tax rate along the way. Simon has been operating this way for eighteen months and is now concerned, after a conversation with a friend in a similar situation who mentioned the term personal services business, that his arrangement might not actually work the way his previous accountant described, and he wants you to give him a straight, honest assessment.

Simon has described his working arrangement in detail. He has one client, his former employer, and has had no other clients or engagements of any kind since incorporating MCG. He works from the client's offices four to five days most weeks, using a laptop and systems provided by the client, and reports to the same manager he reported to before he left his employee role, following that manager's direction on priorities and project assignments in essentially the same way he did as an employee. MCG has no employees other than Simon himself, no other staff, no office space of its own, and no meaningful business assets beyond a small amount of cash in its bank account. Simon does not believe he could have been expected to incur a loss in this arrangement at any point, since he is paid a stable fee by the client regardless of how any specific project turns out, similar to how his salary worked when he was an employee.

You need to explain to Simon what a personal services business is, walk through the factors relevant to determining whether MCG could be considered a personal services business under his current arrangement, and explain clearly what the tax consequences would be if MCG is in fact a personal services business, including how this differs from his previous accountant's described tax planning outcome.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Simon's Working Arrangement Details
exhibits.content: MCG entered into a services agreement with Simon's former employer approximately eighteen months ago, shortly after Simon left his employee role there. Under the agreement, MCG bills the client a fixed monthly consulting fee for Simon's IT infrastructure consulting services. Simon works from the client's offices four to five days most weeks, uses a laptop, network access, and other systems provided by the client, and has continued reporting to the same manager he reported to as an employee, following that manager's direction on which projects to prioritize and how his time should be allocated on a week to week basis. Simon has had no other clients or engagements of any kind since MCG was incorporated, and the client relationship with his former employer represents effectively all of MCG's revenue and all of Simon's available working time.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: MCG Corporate Structure and Assets
exhibits.content: MCG has no employees other than Simon, who is also its sole shareholder and director. MCG has no office space or facilities of its own, no equipment beyond a personal cell phone Simon uses which was not specifically acquired for business purposes, and holds only a modest cash balance in its corporate bank account representing retained fees from the client engagement, net of Simon's salary and MCG's minimal operating costs. MCG has not pursued or obtained any other client engagements, has no marketing or business development activity, and has no website or public presence beyond what would be needed to invoice its one client.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 3: Simon's Notes on the Original Advice and His Concerns
exhibits.content: My previous accountant told me that by setting things up this way, I could have MCG pay corporate tax at a lower rate on the consulting fees, pay myself a smaller salary that would be taxed personally, and eventually take money out through dividends in a more tax efficient way over time, compared to just being taxed on my full salary as an employee like before. I liked the idea because on paper it seemed like a straightforward way to reduce my overall tax bill for doing essentially the same work I was already doing. Now that a friend mentioned the term personal services business to me, I am worried that the whole setup might not actually work the way I was told, and I want you to tell me honestly whether that concern is valid, what it would mean for MCG and for me personally if it turns out my arrangement is a problem, and what, if anything, could be done differently going forward.

requirements: Explain to Simon what a personal services business is and identify the factors from his specific arrangement that are relevant to determining whether MCG would be considered a personal services business.
Explain the tax consequences that would apply to MCG if it is determined to be a personal services business, and how this differs from the outcome Simon's previous accountant described.
Advise Simon on what, if anything, could be done differently going forward to address the risk, and what he should do about the arrangement as it has operated for the past eighteen months.

model_response.understand_issue: I am acting as newly engaged CPA to Simon Achterberg, sole owner of Meridian Consulting Group Inc, who is concerned after a friend raised the term personal services business in connection with his single-client consulting arrangement with his former employer. Simon needs an honest, direct assessment of whether MCG's arrangement could be considered a personal services business, what the tax consequences would be if so, how this differs from what his previous accountant described, and what he should do both going forward and about the past eighteen months of operation under this structure.
model_response.analyze_case:
What a personal services business is: a personal services business generally arises where an individual provides services through a corporation, but the relationship between that individual and the party receiving the services is, in substance, the same as an employment relationship, such that if the corporation did not exist, the individual would reasonably be considered an employee of the party paying for the services rather than an independent contractor running their own business. The core idea is that incorporating should not, by itself, convert what is really an employment relationship into a business relationship simply for tax advantage, so specific tax rules apply to remove the tax benefits that would otherwise be available to a genuinely independent small business corporation when the corporation is really just standing in for what would otherwise be an employment relationship.

Factors relevant to Simon's arrangement: the analysis generally looks at the overall degree of control the payer exercises over how, when, and where the work is done, whether the worker provides their own tools and equipment or uses the payer's, whether the worker bears any meaningful financial risk of loss or only receives a stable fee regardless of outcome, and whether the worker is integrated into the payer's organization performing services similar to those of existing employees, as well as whether the worker has other clients and is genuinely operating an independent business as opposed to serving a single client on effectively a full time basis. Applying these factors to Simon's described arrangement, virtually every indicator points toward an employment-like relationship rather than an independent business: the client's manager directs Simon's day to day priorities and project assignments in essentially the same manner as before he was an employee, indicating a high degree of control; Simon works from the client's premises using the client's equipment and systems rather than his own; Simon bears no meaningful risk of loss, being paid a stable fee regardless of how any project turns out, similar to a salary; MCG has no other clients, no marketing activity, and no meaningful independent business assets or operations beyond servicing this single relationship; and the arrangement began immediately upon Simon leaving his employee role at the same company, continuing what is functionally the same working relationship. Taken together, these facts present a strong case that MCG's relationship with the client is, in substance, an employment relationship, and MCG would very likely be considered to be carrying on a personal services business in respect of this arrangement.

Tax consequences if MCG is a personal services business: the rules applicable to a personal services business generally significantly restrict the deductions MCG can claim against the consulting fee income, limiting them to essentially only the salary and benefits actually paid to Simon as the incorporated employee and a small number of other specifically permitted items, disallowing the wider range of business expense deductions a genuinely independent consulting corporation could otherwise claim. In addition, income from a personal services business generally does not qualify for the lower small business corporate tax rate that Simon's previous accountant was counting on, and is instead taxed at a rate that is higher than even the general corporate rate, specifically intended to remove any tax rate advantage from routing what is really employment income through a corporation. The combined effect of restricted deductions and a higher corporate tax rate on this income means the tax planning outcome Simon's previous accountant described, involving a lower corporate rate on retained fees and efficient extraction through dividends over time, would not actually be available if MCG's arrangement is properly characterized as a personal services business, and the overall structure could in fact result in a higher combined tax cost than if Simon had simply remained an employee, the opposite of the intended benefit.
model_response.recommendation:
I recommend Simon treat this as a serious risk requiring prompt attention rather than something to leave unresolved, given how strongly the facts point toward personal services business characterization. Simon should not assume the arrangement will go unnoticed or unchallenged, and continuing to operate and file on the previous accountant's original basis without addressing the risk would not be a responsible path forward.

Going forward, I recommend Simon and I review MCG's prior filings together to properly assess and, if warranted, voluntarily correct how MCG's income and deductions have been reported for the periods MCG has operated under this arrangement, since proactively addressing a filing position believed to be incorrect is preferable to waiting for it to be identified through a review or audit. I recommend Simon discuss with the client whether the underlying working relationship could be restructured to reduce the personal services business indicators, such as Simon working with greater independence, using his own equipment, having more control over how and when work is performed, and MCG actively pursuing additional client relationships so the business is not dependent on a single client functioning as a de facto employer, understanding that some of these changes may not be practical or may not fully resolve the underlying risk if the substance of the relationship does not genuinely change.

Simon should also understand that if genuine independence from this single client cannot realistically be achieved given the nature of the engagement, continuing to operate MCG in its current form provides little to no tax advantage over simply being an employee, and he may want to reconsider whether incorporation continues to make sense for this specific arrangement at all.
model_response.conclusion: Simon's concern is well founded, the facts as described point strongly toward MCG carrying on a personal services business, which would eliminate the tax benefits his previous accountant promised and could result in a worse overall tax outcome than employment, making it important for Simon to address both the going forward structure of the relationship and the correctness of MCG's past filings without delay.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly explains the concept of a personal services business as a corporation used to provide services that would otherwise be characterized as an employment relationship
Identifies the key factors relevant to the analysis: control, ownership of tools/equipment, financial risk of loss, integration into the payer's organization, and reliance on a single client

Correctly applies each relevant factor to Simon's specific facts (manager direction, client's equipment/premises, no financial risk, single client, immediate transition from employee) rather than reciting factors generically
Reaches a well-supported conclusion that MCG's arrangement strongly indicates personal services business characterization
Correctly explains that a personal services business faces significantly restricted expense deductions, generally limited to salary and a narrow set of permitted items
Correctly explains that personal services business income does not qualify for the small business rate and is taxed at a rate higher than the general corporate rate
Correctly identifies that the previous accountant's described tax planning outcome would not be achieved and could result in a worse tax outcome than remaining an employee
Recommends reviewing and potentially correcting prior filings rather than only addressing the arrangement prospectively

Recommends practical, realistic changes to the working arrangement (own equipment, greater independence, seeking other clients) while noting these may not fully resolve the risk if substance does not change

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Addresses all three requirements (definition/factor analysis, tax consequences, going forward and past-period advice) in a structured, complete manner
Gives Simon a direct, honest, non-evasive answer to his explicit request for a straight assessment

marking_guide.competency: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
marking_guide.criterion: Does not soften or avoid the likely unfavourable conclusion to preserve client comfort, and recommends proactive correction of past filings rather than allowing a known incorrect position to continue
Does not invent specific numeric tax rates for personal services business income not confirmed as stable, describing the mechanism and relative rate comparison conceptually
Notes that incorporation may provide little or no benefit for this specific single-client arrangement even if restructured, prompting Simon to reconsider the structure's value entirely
Recognizes the limits of what can be concluded without a full technical review and appropriately frames the assessment as a strong likelihood rather than an absolute certainty

rating_guidance: A competent response correctly explains the personal services business concept, applies the relevant factors specifically to Simon's facts to reach a well-supported conclusion that the arrangement likely qualifies, and correctly explains both the restricted deduction and higher tax rate consequences that would follow. A response with distinction goes further to give Simon a direct, unhedged honest answer as he explicitly requested, recommends proactively reviewing and correcting prior period filings rather than only fixing the arrangement going forward, and appropriately notes that superficial changes may not resolve the risk if the underlying substance of the single-client relationship does not genuinely change. Responses that describe the personal services business factors only in the abstract without applying them to Simon's specific facts, or that avoid reaching a clear conclusion out of excessive hedging, fail to meet what this case specifically calls for and should not exceed reaching competent. Responses that reassure Simon the arrangement is likely fine, or that fail to address the prior eighteen months of filings at all, reflect either a technical misjudgment or an ethical avoidance of an uncomfortable but necessary conversation and should not be rated competent.

enabling_focus: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
title: Borealis Construction Ltd: Percentage of Completion on a Fixed-Price Municipal Contract
case_background:
Borealis Construction Ltd (Borealis) is a private company reporting under ASPE, owned by two engineers who founded it twelve years ago. In January of Year 1, Borealis signed a fixed-price contract with the City of Millbrook to build a recreation centre for total contract revenue of $18,000,000, with completion expected by the end of Year 3. You are the controller. It is now December 31, Year 2, and the CFO has asked you to prepare the revenue recognition entries for the year before the bank-required review engagement begins next week.

At contract inception, Borealis estimated total costs to complete the project at $15,000,000, giving an expected gross margin of $3,000,000. Borealis has historically used the completed-contract method for all of its projects because most jobs run less than twelve months and management felt percentage-of-completion was unnecessary. This recreation centre is the company's first multi-year contract, and the bank that holds Borealis's operating line has asked that the interim financial statements reflect the economics of the project as it progresses, since the loan covenant is tested annually and the bank wants visibility into work in progress.

As at December 31, Year 1, costs incurred on the project totalled $4,860,000, and the project engineer confirmed that the original total cost estimate of $15,000,000 remained reasonable at that time. No revenue was recognized in Year 1 because Borealis used the completed-contract method.

During Year 2, steel and rebar prices rose sharply due to a supply disruption affecting the whole region. By December 31, Year 2, cumulative costs incurred on the project were $10,800,000. The project engineer has revised the estimate of costs to complete the remaining work to $5,400,000, bringing total estimated project cost to $16,200,000 (up from $15,000,000). This is a $1,200,000 increase in the total cost estimate, driven almost entirely by the steel price increase. Billings to the City to date total $11,500,000, and cash collected to date totals $10,900,000. The contract has no unusual penalty clauses and the City has paid every invoice within its normal 30-day terms, so there is no indication of a collectibility problem.

The CFO wants your recommendation on whether Borealis should switch to percentage-of-completion for this contract, and if so, what revenue, cost of revenue, and gross profit should be recognized for Year 2, along with the balance sheet presentation of billings versus costs and recognized profit.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Project Cost and Billing Summary
exhibits.content:
Contract price: $18,000,000
Original estimated total cost (at inception, Year 1): $15,000,000
Costs incurred, cumulative to December 31, Year 1: $4,860,000
Costs incurred, cumulative to December 31, Year 2: $10,800,000
Revised estimated total cost to complete as at December 31, Year 2: $16,200,000 (costs incurred to date of $10,800,000 plus estimated costs to complete of $5,400,000)
Billings to the City, cumulative to December 31, Year 2: $11,500,000
Cash collected from the City, cumulative to December 31, Year 2: $10,900,000
The project engineer's sign-off letter, dated December 30, Year 2, states that the revised cost estimate is reliable and that no further material cost increases are anticipated, since the steel purchase needed for the remaining structural work has now been locked in under a fixed-price supply agreement.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Bank Covenant and Financial Statement Note
exhibits.content: Borealis's operating line of credit agreement with its bank requires a minimum current ratio of 1.25:1, tested annually based on year-end audited or reviewed financial statements. The bank's relationship manager sent a letter in November of Year 2 noting that because this is Borealis's first multi-year, fixed-price contract, the bank expects the financial statements to present work-in-progress on a basis that reflects economic performance rather than only cash billed, so that covenant testing is meaningful. The letter does not mandate a specific accounting policy but states the bank will review whichever policy Borealis adopts, provided it is applied consistently with ASPE. Borealis's other, shorter-duration contracts (each under nine months) will continue to be accounted for under the completed-contract method, since percentage-of-completion is not expected to produce materially different results for those short jobs.

requirements: Advise the CFO on whether Borealis should change its accounting policy for this contract from completed-contract to percentage-of-completion, referencing the relevant ASPE criteria.

Calculate the revenue, cost of revenue, and gross profit that should be recognized for Year 2 under your recommended method, and describe the balance sheet presentation of the project at December 31, Year 2.

model_response.understand_issue: The issue is which revenue recognition policy Borealis should apply to its first multi-year, fixed-price construction contract, and what amounts should be recognized in Year 2. ASPE 3400 permits either the percentage-of-completion method or the completed-contract method for long-term contracts, but percentage-of-completion is only appropriate when the extent of progress and total costs can be reliably estimated. The company must also determine the correct Year 2 revenue, cost, and gross profit figures and the associated balance sheet presentation for the bank.
model_response.analyze_case:
Under ASPE 3400, percentage-of-completion is the preferred method for long-term contracts when performance consists of a number of acts and reasonably dependable estimates exist of the extent of progress and of costs to complete. Here, the project engineer's sign-off letter confirms the revised cost estimate is reliable and that the remaining structural steel has been locked in under a fixed-price agreement, which removes most of the estimation risk that caused the Year 2 cost overrun in the first place. There is also no indication of collectibility problems, since the City has paid every invoice on time. These facts support a reliable estimate of both progress and remaining costs, so a switch to percentage-of-completion is appropriate and is consistent with the bank's request for statements that reflect economic performance rather than cash billed. This is properly treated as an accounting policy adoption for this contract type (a first-time policy for a new class of transaction), not a correction of an error, since completed-contract was reasonable for Borealis's historical short-duration jobs.

Percent complete at December 31, Year 2 = costs incurred to date / total estimated cost = $10,800,000 / $16,200,000 = 66.7%.

Revenue to be recognized cumulatively through Year 2 = 66.7% x $18,000,000 = $12,000,000.
Cumulative gross profit through Year 2 = 66.7% x ($18,000,000 - $16,200,000) = 66.7% x $1,800,000 = $1,200,000.
Cumulative cost of revenue through Year 2 = $12,000,000 - $1,200,000 = $10,800,000, which reconciles to costs incurred to date, as expected since no Year 1 revenue was recognized under completed-contract.

Because Borealis recognized no revenue in Year 1 under completed-contract, and the switch to percentage-of-completion is being applied starting from this contract's inception (a policy choice for this new contract type rather than a change requiring retrospective restatement of a prior policy), the full cumulative amounts are recognized in Year 2: Revenue of $12,000,000, cost of revenue of $10,800,000, and gross profit of $1,200,000, all recognized as Year 2 results.

On the balance sheet at December 31, Year 2, cumulative costs incurred plus recognized gross profit ($10,800,000 + $1,200,000 = $12,000,000) exceed cumulative billings of $11,500,000 by $500,000. This $500,000 excess is presented as an asset, typically described as costs and recognized profit in excess of billings, within current assets. Accounts receivable of $600,000 (billings of $11,500,000 less cash collected of $10,900,000) is presented separately as a normal trade receivable.
model_response.recommendation: I recommend Borealis adopt percentage-of-completion for the recreation centre contract effective for Year 2 reporting, since reasonably dependable estimates of progress and remaining cost now exist, supported by the engineer's sign-off and the fixed-price steel commitment. Borealis should recognize $12,000,000 of revenue, $10,800,000 of cost of revenue, and $1,200,000 of gross profit for Year 2, all as current-year amounts since no revenue was recognized in the prior year. The balance sheet should present a current asset of $500,000 for costs and recognized profit in excess of billings, plus accounts receivable of $600,000. Borealis should continue using completed-contract for its other short-duration contracts, since applying percentage-of-completion only to contracts long enough to make estimation reliable is permitted and appropriately tailored to risk. Management should disclose the accounting policy for long-term contracts in the notes, including the basis for the percentage complete calculation, and should disclose the nature and amount of the cost estimate revision that occurred during the year given its size relative to the original estimate. The CFO should also flag to the bank, in advance of the review engagement, that Year 2 results include the full cumulative effect of the switch, so the bank does not misread the current ratio movement as a one-time anomaly.
model_response.conclusion: Borealis should switch to percentage-of-completion for the recreation centre contract because reliable estimates of progress and cost now exist. For Year 2, Borealis should recognize revenue of $12,000,000, cost of revenue of $10,800,000, and gross profit of $1,200,000, with a $500,000 current asset for costs and recognized profit in excess of billings. This presentation better reflects economic performance for the bank's covenant review and complies with ASPE 3400.

marking_guide.criterion: Identifies that ASPE 3400 permits percentage-of-completion when reasonably dependable estimates of progress and cost exist, and applies this criterion to the specific facts (engineer sign-off, fixed-price steel lock-in)
Correctly calculates percent complete of 66.7 percent using costs incurred to date over total estimated cost
Correctly calculates Year 2 revenue of $12,000,000 and gross profit of $1,200,000
Recognizes that because no revenue was recognized in Year 1, the full cumulative amount is recognized in Year 2 with no catch-up adjustment needed
Correctly presents costs and recognized profit in excess of billings of $500,000 as a current asset, distinct from the $600,000 trade receivable
Addresses disclosure of the accounting policy and the significant cost estimate revision
Notes that continuing completed-contract for short-duration jobs while using percentage-of-completion for this contract is an appropriate, consistent application rather than a policy inconsistency

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Structures the response using the CPA Way, separately addressing the policy question and the quantitative calculation before concluding

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Ties the recommendation back to the bank's stated purpose (meaningful covenant testing) rather than treating the accounting question in isolation

rating_guidance: A response reaching NC either fails to identify percentage-of-completion as the applicable issue or makes no attempt at the calculation. RC is reached when the candidate identifies the correct method and attempts the percent-complete calculation but makes an error in the cumulative logic (for example, forgetting no Year 1 revenue was recognized, or miscalculating the excess-of-billings asset) or omits the balance sheet presentation entirely. C is reached when the candidate correctly identifies the ASPE 3400 criteria supporting the switch, correctly computes percent complete, revenue, and gross profit of $12,000,000 and $1,200,000, and presents the $500,000 excess-of-billings asset with reasonable disclosure commentary. CD requires all of the C-level elements plus a well-integrated discussion connecting the accounting recommendation to the bank's covenant-testing purpose and clear articulation of why this is a policy choice for a new contract class rather than an error correction.

enabling_focus: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
title: Frostline Outdoor Gear Inc: Inventory Valuation and Going Concern Risk
case_background:
You are the audit senior on the Frostline Outdoor Gear Inc (Frostline) engagement, a private company that sells winter apparel and equipment through 14 retail stores across Alberta and Saskatchewan. Frostline's fiscal year end is December 31, Year 2, and you are performing year-end fieldwork. The engagement partner has asked you to draft a memo addressing two matters before the file can be finalized: the valuation of aged winter inventory, and whether the going concern assumption remains appropriate.

Frostline has experienced two consecutive unusually warm winters across its operating region, and revenue has fallen from $22,000,000 in Year 0 to $19,300,000 in Year 1 and $17,500,000 in Year 2. Store traffic reports show a steady decline each quarter of Year 2. Management's response has been to reduce store hours in the shoulder season and to delay reordering, but they have not closed any stores.

Frostline's operating line of credit with its bank has a limit of $4,000,000, and $3,600,000 was drawn as at December 31, Year 2. The line matures in five months, and the bank has indicated in writing that renewal will depend on the year-end financial statements and on a return to covenant compliance. The loan agreement requires a current ratio of at least 1.5:1 and a debt-to-equity ratio no greater than 2:1, tested at each year end. Based on draft financial statements before any audit adjustments, the current ratio is 1.42:1 and debt-to-equity is 2.15:1, both in breach.

During inventory count observation, you noted that a significant portion of on-hand inventory consists of insulated parkas purchased two winters ago, now well out of current season styling. This aged parka inventory has a cost of $1,400,000. Management has begun a clearance program selling these parkas at 40 percent of original cost through a liquidation section of each store, and the clearance program has been running for six weeks with steady but modest sell-through. Total inventory on the draft balance sheet is $6,200,000 with no writedown recorded against the aged parkas.

Management's cash flow forecast, prepared for the bank renewal discussion, projects that Frostline will generate sufficient cash from the spring clearance season and a planned reduction in store count (two underperforming stores identified for closure by June of Year 3) to remain within its revised borrowing capacity, assuming the bank renews the line. Management has not yet obtained a signed commitment letter from the bank for renewal.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Aged Inventory Detail
exhibits.content:
Aged winter parka inventory, purchased two seasons ago: cost $1,400,000, 18,000 units at average cost of $77.78 per unit.
Clearance program: units are being sold at 40 percent of original cost, or approximately $31.11 per unit, in a dedicated liquidation section of each store.
Six weeks of clearance sales to date have moved 3,200 units, generating cash proceeds of $99,552, consistent with the 40 percent pricing.
Store managers estimate remaining selling costs (markdown staff time, in-store signage, point-of-sale processing) at approximately 3 percent of selling price for the remaining units.
No units of this aged parka style remain on order from suppliers, and management does not intend to purchase this style again.
All other inventory categories (boots, mid-layer apparel, accessories, and current-season outerwear) are turning at normal historical rates with no indicators of impairment noted during the count or in subsequent cutoff testing.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Bank Correspondence and Cash Flow Forecast Summary
exhibits.content:
Letter from Frostline's bank, dated December 18, Year 2: the bank confirms the operating line matures in five months and states that renewal is not automatic, and will be assessed based on the audited Year 2 financial statements, covenant compliance, and an updated business plan. The letter explicitly states the bank has not yet approved renewal and reserves the right to require additional security or to decline renewal.
Management's 12-month cash flow forecast, prepared in January Year 3, projects a return to covenant compliance by Q2 Year 3, driven by: (1) proceeds from the parka clearance program, (2) closure of two stores with combined negative contribution margin, expected to reduce annual overhead by $480,000, and (3) a planned $500,000 reduction in seasonal inventory purchases for the upcoming fall season. The forecast assumes the bank renews the operating line on substantially similar terms; no alternative financing source has been identified if the bank declines renewal.
The two stores identified for closure have leases that expire in April and June of Year 3 respectively, with no renewal options being exercised.

requirements: Assess whether the aged parka inventory is fairly valued and recommend any adjustment required, with supporting calculations.
Assess whether a material uncertainty related to going concern exists and discuss the implications for the audit report.

model_response.understand_issue: Two related issues require assessment: whether the aged parka inventory is recorded above its net realizable value and requires a writedown, and whether events and conditions, including declining sales, covenant breaches, and an uncommitted bank renewal, cast significant doubt on Frostline's ability to continue as a going concern, with implications for financial statement disclosure and the audit report.
model_response.analyze_case:
Inventory must be measured at the lower of cost and net realizable value. Net realizable value is estimated selling price less estimated costs to complete and sell. The clearance pricing of 40 percent of cost, or $31.11 per unit, is directly observable and supported by six weeks of actual sales at that price, which is strong evidence of net realizable value rather than a management estimate alone. Estimated selling costs of 3 percent of selling price are $0.93 per unit, giving net realizable value of $30.18 per unit. Cost per unit is $77.78. Since net realizable value ($30.18) is well below cost ($77.78), a writedown is required.

Writedown calculation: 18,000 units x ($77.78 - $30.18) = 18,000 x $47.60 = $856,800. This should be recorded as a reduction to inventory and an increase to cost of sales (or a separate inventory writedown expense line) in Year 2. Note that 3,200 units have already sold during the subsequent clearance period; using the actual clearance price as evidence of NRV for the remaining 14,800 units is appropriate and is stronger audit evidence than a forecast, since it reflects an actual, realized transaction price close to year end.

On going concern, CAS 570 requires evaluation of whether events or conditions, individually or in combination, cast significant doubt on the entity's ability to continue as a going concern. Relevant conditions here include two consecutive years of declining revenue, a covenant breach on both the current ratio and debt-to-equity tests before adjustment, a line of credit maturing within the next twelve months with no signed renewal commitment, and a cash flow forecast that depends entirely on that uncommitted renewal with no identified alternative financing. These are not offsetting mitigating factors in the sense CAS 570 requires, since management's plan (the clearance program and store closures) helps operating results but does not eliminate dependence on the bank's discretionary renewal decision. The inventory writedown will worsen the current ratio and debt-to-equity further, since it reduces both inventory and equity, moving the ratios further from covenant compliance rather than closer.

Current ratio impact: reducing inventory (a current asset) by $856,800 with no change to current liabilities will reduce the current ratio below the already non-compliant 1.42:1, reinforcing rather than resolving the covenant breach. This strengthens, not weakens, the case that a material uncertainty exists.
model_response.recommendation: I recommend Frostline record an $856,800 writedown of the aged parka inventory to bring it to net realizable value of $30.18 per unit, supported by the actual clearance sales prices observed during the six-week program, which constitute persuasive audit evidence. On going concern, I recommend the engagement team conclude that a material uncertainty related to going concern exists, given the covenant breaches, the maturing and uncommitted line of credit, two years of revenue decline, and management's forecast dependence on a bank decision not yet made. Frostline's financial statements should include disclosure of these conditions, management's mitigating plans (clearance program, store closures, reduced purchasing), and an explicit statement that a material uncertainty exists that may cast significant doubt on the entity's ability to continue as a going concern, prepared using the going concern basis unless liquidation or cessation of operations is planned or is the only realistic alternative, which does not appear to be the case here. The audit report should include a separate Material Uncertainty Related to Going Concern section as required by the CASs, and should not be modified to an adverse or qualified opinion provided disclosure is adequate; if management refuses adequate disclosure, a qualified or adverse opinion should be considered. I recommend the team request an updated, dated bank communication closer to report date and evaluate subsequent events through the report date for any change in the bank's renewal position.
model_response.conclusion: The aged parka inventory should be written down by $856,800 to reflect net realizable value based on observed clearance pricing. A material uncertainty related to going concern exists due to covenant breaches, an uncommitted line of credit renewal, and a forecast wholly dependent on that renewal, requiring explicit disclosure and a Material Uncertainty Related to Going Concern section in the audit report, assuming disclosure is adequate.

marking_guide.criterion: Identifies that inventory must be measured at lower of cost and net realizable value and applies this to the aged parka facts
Correctly calculates net realizable value per unit of $30.18 and total writedown of $856,800
Recognizes that actual clearance sales during the subsequent period are strong, objective evidence of NRV, stronger than a forecast
Identifies the going concern indicators (declining revenue, covenant breach, uncommitted renewal, forecast dependence) per CAS 570
Concludes a material uncertainty related to going concern exists rather than concluding no issue exists or that liquidation basis is required

Identifies the correct audit report implication (Material Uncertainty Related to Going Concern section, contingent on adequate disclosure) rather than jumping straight to a qualified or adverse opinion
Connects the inventory writedown to the covenant calculation, noting it worsens rather than improves compliance
Recommends further audit procedures such as obtaining updated bank correspondence closer to report date

marking_guide.competency: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
marking_guide.criterion: Maintains professional skepticism toward management's forecast, which depends on an event outside management's control

rating_guidance: NC responses fail to identify either issue or make no quantitative attempt. RC responses identify inventory should be written down and that going concern is a concern, but miscalculate the writedown, miss the NRV evidence from actual clearance sales, or jump directly to a qualified opinion without considering disclosure adequacy. C responses correctly calculate the $856,800 writedown using the clearance evidence and correctly identify a material uncertainty exists with the appropriate audit report treatment (a Material Uncertainty section, not automatically a modified opinion). CD responses additionally connect the two issues (the writedown worsening the covenant position), demonstrate strong professional skepticism about the uncommitted bank renewal, and recommend specific further procedures such as updated bank correspondence near report date.

enabling_focus: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
title: Harbourview Logistics Ltd: Sale-Leaseback of the Delivery Fleet
case_background:
Harbourview Logistics Ltd (Harbourview) is a private company reporting under ASPE that operates a regional trucking and delivery business out of Halifax. You are a CPA working in Harbourview's finance department. On October 1, Year 2, Harbourview sold its fleet of 20 delivery trucks to Atlantic Leasing Corp for cash proceeds of $3,000,000. The trucks had a combined carrying value of $2,200,000 on Harbourview's books immediately before the sale (original cost $3,700,000, accumulated depreciation $1,500,000), and the $3,000,000 sale price was confirmed by an independent appraisal to represent fair value, since the sale was negotiated at arm's length with a third-party leasing company that Harbourview has no other relationship with.

Simultaneously with the sale, Harbourview entered into a five-year lease to lease the same 20 trucks back from Atlantic Leasing Corp, with annual lease payments of $650,000 payable at the end of each year, beginning September 30, Year 3. The lease does not transfer ownership of the trucks to Harbourview at the end of the term, and there is no bargain purchase option. The trucks have a remaining useful life of seven years as at the sale-leaseback date. Harbourview's incremental borrowing rate, and the rate implicit in the lease, is 6 percent.

The controller, who is preparing the Year 2 financial statements, recorded the transaction as a straightforward sale with the full $800,000 gain ($3,000,000 proceeds less $2,200,000 carrying value) recognized immediately in Year 2 income, and has set up an operating lease with the $650,000 annual payments to be expensed as incurred starting in Year 3. The controller has asked you to confirm this treatment is correct before the year-end close, noting that the company's bank covenant (a minimum interest coverage ratio) is sensitive to whether the gain is recognized immediately or deferred, and to whether the lease obligation appears on the balance sheet.

Harbourview's fiscal year end is December 31.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Sale and Leaseback Terms
exhibits.content:
Sale date: October 1, Year 2
Sale price (confirmed fair value by independent appraisal): $3,000,000
Carrying value of trucks immediately before sale: $2,200,000 (original cost $3,700,000 less accumulated depreciation $1,500,000)
Leaseback term: 5 years, commencing October 1, Year 2
Annual lease payments: $650,000, payable in arrears (first payment September 30, Year 3)
Remaining useful economic life of the trucks at leaseback date: 7 years
Ownership transfer at end of lease: none; no bargain purchase option
Discount rate (Harbourview's incremental borrowing rate, equal to the rate implicit in the lease): 6 percent
Present value factor for an ordinary annuity of 5 payments at 6 percent: 4.2124

requirements: Determine whether the leaseback should be classified as a capital lease or an operating lease under ASPE 3065, showing your analysis against the relevant classification criteria.
Based on your classification, determine the correct accounting treatment for the $800,000 gain on sale and advise the controller on any correction needed to the draft Year 2 financial statements.

model_response.understand_issue: The issue is whether the leaseback of the 20 trucks should be classified as a capital lease or an operating lease under ASPE 3065, and, depending on that classification, how the $800,000 gain on the sale portion of the sale-leaseback transaction should be recognized. The controller's current treatment assumes an operating lease with immediate full gain recognition, which needs to be tested against the ASPE classification criteria before the Year 2 statements are finalized.
model_response.analyze_case:
ASPE 3065 classifies a lease as a capital lease if, at inception, it transfers substantially all the benefits and risks of ownership to the lessee. This is presumed to occur if any of the following are met: ownership transfers by the end of the lease term, the lease contains a bargain purchase option, the lease term is 75 percent or more of the asset's economic life, or the present value of the minimum lease payments is 90 percent or more of the fair value of the leased asset at inception.

Ownership transfer: none, so this criterion is not met.
Bargain purchase option: none, so this criterion is not met.
Lease term versus economic life: the lease term is 5 years and the remaining economic life of the trucks is 7 years, giving 5/7 = 71.4 percent, which is below the 75 percent threshold, so this criterion is not met.
Present value of minimum lease payments versus fair value: PV = $650,000 x 4.2124 = $2,738,060. Fair value at leaseback inception is $3,000,000 (the sale price, confirmed by independent appraisal). $2,738,060 / $3,000,000 = 91.3 percent, which is at or above the 90 percent threshold, so this criterion is met.

Because the present value criterion is met, the leaseback is a capital lease under ASPE 3065, even though the lease-term criterion alone was not met. Only one of the four criteria needs to be satisfied.

Because the leaseback is a capital lease, ASPE 3065 requires that any gain on the sale portion of a sale-leaseback transaction not be recognized immediately in income. Instead, the gain is deferred and amortized to income over the lease term in proportion to the amortization of the leased asset (or, in substance, in proportion to the related depreciation expense recognized on the leased trucks over the 5-year lease term), rather than being recognized in full at the sale date. The controller's entry recognizing the full $800,000 gain immediately in Year 2 income is incorrect and overstates Year 2 net income by the unamortized portion of the gain.

The controller also needs to recognize a capital lease obligation and a leased asset on the balance sheet at the present value of the minimum lease payments, $2,738,060, rather than treating the payments as a simple operating lease expense. The leased trucks should be depreciated by Harbourview over the lease term (since there is no transfer of ownership and no bargain purchase option, depreciation is over the shorter of the lease term and useful life, which is the 5-year lease term), and the $650,000 annual payments should be split between interest expense (at 6 percent on the outstanding obligation) and principal repayment, similar to any other capital lease.
model_response.recommendation: I recommend the controller reclassify the leaseback as a capital lease, since the present value of the minimum lease payments ($2,738,060) is 91.3 percent of the trucks' fair value, exceeding the 90 percent ASPE 3065 threshold. The controller should record a leased asset and capital lease obligation of $2,738,060 at October 1, Year 2, rather than treating future payments as operating lease expense. The $800,000 gain on sale should not be recognized in full in Year 2; instead it should be deferred and amortized to income over the 5-year lease term in proportion to the depreciation of the leased trucks, meaning only a partial amount, roughly 3 months of the first year's amortization tranche, should flow through Year 2 income, with the balance deferred on the balance sheet as a liability or contra-asset and recognized over the remaining lease term. The controller should also begin recording depreciation on the leased asset and split each $650,000 payment between interest expense and reduction of the lease obligation starting in Year 3, with an accrual for interest expense from October 1 to December 31, Year 2 already required in the Year 2 statements even though the first cash payment is not until September 30, Year 3. I recommend flagging the balance sheet and income statement impact to the CFO promptly given the bank covenant sensitivity noted, since capitalizing the lease obligation will affect leverage-based covenants and deferring the gain will reduce Year 2 net income relative to the controller's original entry.
model_response.conclusion: The sale-leaseback should be classified as a capital lease because the present value of minimum lease payments is 91.3 percent of fair value, exceeding the 90 percent threshold. The $800,000 gain on sale must be deferred and amortized over the 5-year lease term rather than recognized immediately, and a lease asset and obligation of $2,738,060 must be recorded, correcting the controller's draft treatment.

marking_guide.criterion: Identifies and applies all four ASPE 3065 capital lease classification criteria systematically
Correctly calculates present value of minimum lease payments of $2,738,060 using the given factor
Correctly calculates the 91.3 percent ratio and concludes the 90 percent threshold is met, driving capital lease classification
Recognizes that only one of the four criteria needs to be met, correctly noting the lease-term criterion (71.4 percent) alone was not sufficient
Identifies that gain on a sale-leaseback classified as a capital lease must be deferred and amortized over the lease term rather than recognized immediately
Identifies the need to record a lease asset and lease obligation at $2,738,060 rather than treating the leaseback as an operating lease expense
Addresses the need to accrue interest expense from October 1 to December 31, Year 2 despite the first payment not occurring until September 30, Year 3
Connects the correction to the bank covenant sensitivity the controller raised

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Uses a clear criterion-by-criterion structure before reaching the classification conclusion, rather than asserting the answer

rating_guidance: NC responses fail to apply the ASPE 3065 criteria or do not attempt the present value calculation. RC responses attempt the classification but make an arithmetic error in the present value test, misapply the 90 percent or 75 percent thresholds, or correctly classify the lease as capital but fail to identify that the gain must be deferred rather than recognized immediately. C responses correctly work through all four criteria, correctly compute the 91.3 percent result, correctly classify the lease as capital, and correctly identify that the $800,000 gain must be deferred and amortized rather than taken to income immediately, with a lease asset and obligation recorded at $2,738,060. CD responses additionally address the interest accrual from the sale date to year end, connect the correction's impact to the bank covenant concern the controller raised, and clearly distinguish why the lease-term criterion alone would not have triggered capital classification, showing full command of the multi-criteria test.

enabling_focus: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
title: Meridian Foods Processing: Undisclosed Related Party Purchasing Arrangement
case_background:
You are the audit manager on the Meridian Foods Processing Inc (Meridian) engagement, a private company that processes and packages frozen vegetables for grocery chains across Ontario. Meridian's fiscal year end is October 31, Year 2, and your team is in the final stages of fieldwork.

During substantive testing of the purchases cycle, the audit senior selected a sample that included several large invoices from a vendor called Coastal Packaging Supplies Ltd, which supplies the corrugated cardboard boxes Meridian uses to ship its products. Total purchases from Coastal Packaging during Year 2 were $2,400,000, representing Meridian's single largest packaging supplier relationship, used for roughly 70 percent of Meridian's box volume. While performing a corporate registry search as part of standard vendor due diligence procedures, the senior discovered that Coastal Packaging Supplies Ltd is 100 percent owned by Robert Hale, who is the brother of Meridian's CEO, David Hale. This relationship is not disclosed anywhere in Meridian's draft financial statements, and it was not disclosed to the engagement team during the related party inquiries performed at planning, when David Hale specifically stated that Meridian had no related party transactions requiring disclosure other than his own compensation, which is separately disclosed.

Your team obtained pricing quotes from two comparable, unrelated corrugated packaging suppliers in the same region for equivalent volume and specification. Both quotes came in within a narrow range, averaging approximately $1,850,000 for the same annual volume Meridian purchased from Coastal Packaging, roughly 23 percent below what Meridian actually paid. Coastal Packaging's invoices are approved by the plant operations manager, who reports to David Hale, and there is no evidence the box pricing was ever competitively tendered or benchmarked internally, despite Meridian's purchasing policy manual requiring competitive tender for any single-supplier relationship exceeding $500,000 annually.

When the engagement partner raised the discovery directly with David Hale, he acknowledged the relationship but stated he did not consider it a related party transaction requiring disclosure because he personally has no ownership interest in Coastal Packaging and does not participate in setting its prices, and that Coastal Packaging has supplied Meridian reliably for eight years, well before he became CEO three years ago.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Vendor Comparison and Purchasing Policy
exhibits.content:
Coastal Packaging Supplies Ltd, Year 2 purchases from Meridian: $2,400,000, for corrugated boxes covering approximately 70 percent of Meridian's annual box volume.
Comparable quote, Vendor A (unrelated, same region, same specification and volume): $1,870,000.
Comparable quote, Vendor B (unrelated, same region, same specification and volume): $1,830,000.
Average of comparable quotes: $1,850,000.
Difference between actual Coastal Packaging spend and the average comparable quote: $2,400,000 minus $1,850,000 equals $550,000, or approximately 30 percent above market (equivalently, the market quotes average roughly 23 percent below what Meridian actually paid).
Meridian's purchasing policy manual, section 4.2: any single-supplier relationship exceeding $500,000 in annual spend requires a documented competitive tender at least every two years. No tender documentation exists for the Coastal Packaging relationship in the purchasing files for the current year or the prior two years reviewed.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: CEO Communication and Corporate Registry Extract
exhibits.content:
Corporate registry extract for Coastal Packaging Supplies Ltd, dated within the audit period: sole shareholder and director listed as Robert Hale. Company incorporated eight years ago.
Minutes of the engagement team's planning-stage related party inquiry meeting, held with David Hale (CEO) and the CFO: David Hale stated, when asked directly whether any officer, director, or their close family members had ownership interests in any of Meridian's suppliers or customers, that there were none requiring disclosure beyond his own employment compensation arrangement, which is separately presented in the financial statement notes.
Follow-up conversation between the engagement partner and David Hale after the discovery: David Hale stated he does not believe disclosure is required because he holds no ownership interest in Coastal Packaging himself and is not involved in setting its prices, and noted the supplier relationship predates his tenure as CEO by five years.

requirements: Assess whether Coastal Packaging Supplies Ltd meets the definition of a related party under the applicable accounting framework, and identify what disclosure, if any, is required in Meridian's financial statements.
Discuss the implications of this discovery for the audit, including the fraud risk considerations and the required response given the CEO's initial inaccurate representation.

model_response.understand_issue: The issues are whether Coastal Packaging Supplies Ltd, owned by the CEO's brother, is a related party requiring disclosure under ASPE 3840, and what the pricing evidence and the CEO's inaccurate initial representation mean for audit risk, including fraud risk under CAS 240 and the reliability of management representations more broadly on this engagement.
model_response.analyze_case:
Under ASPE 3840, related parties include, among others, an entity's management and members of the immediate family of that management, and entities controlled by such individuals. Immediate family generally includes those family members who could be expected to influence, or be influenced by, that individual in dealings with the reporting enterprise, which commonly includes siblings depending on the closeness of the relationship and its potential to affect dealings. Here, Robert Hale is the CEO's brother and the sole owner of Coastal Packaging, and the operational facts, an eight-year, single-largest-supplier relationship approved by a subordinate of the CEO with no competitive tender despite a policy requirement, and pricing roughly 30 percent above two independently obtained market quotes, are strong indicators that the CEO's family relationship is influencing dealings between Meridian and the supplier, regardless of David Hale's assertion that he personally holds no ownership stake and does not set prices. Ownership by the related individual is not required for a related party relationship to exist; influence and the potential for non-arm's-length dealing between the two parties is the relevant test, and the pricing gap combined with the lack of competitive tender is objective evidence consistent with such influence. Coastal Packaging therefore meets the definition of a related party, and the Year 2 purchases of $2,400,000 constitute a related party transaction requiring disclosure of the nature of the relationship, a description of the transaction, and the dollar amount, unless it can be demonstrated the transaction occurred at an amount equal to the exchange amount that would have been agreed between arm's-length parties, which the pricing evidence here contradicts. Since the transaction did not occur at arm's-length value, it should be recorded and disclosed at the exchange amount actually transacted (the $2,400,000 actually paid, since that is the amount of the transaction), with disclosure of the related party nature, rather than being restated to the hypothetical market amount, and disclosure should clearly identify Coastal Packaging as a related party and describe the relationship.

On audit implications, CAS 550 requires the auditor to obtain sufficient appropriate audit evidence about related party relationships and transactions and to evaluate whether the entity has appropriately identified, accounted for, and disclosed them. David Hale's initial representation that no such relationships existed, made directly to the engagement team during planning inquiries, was inaccurate and is now known to be false. This is significant: it calls into question the reliability of other management representations obtained on this engagement and raises fraud risk considerations under CAS 240, since a related party transaction concealed from the auditor, priced above market, and routed around the company's own tender policy is a classic indicator of possible management override of controls or self-dealing (even if the direct beneficiary is the CEO's brother rather than the CEO personally, the CEO benefits from goodwill or reciprocal benefit with his brother, and misappropriation or improper benefit cannot be ruled out without further work).
model_response.recommendation: I recommend the engagement team conclude that Coastal Packaging Supplies Ltd is a related party of Meridian under ASPE 3840, and require management to disclose the relationship, the nature of the transactions, and the $2,400,000 Year 2 purchase amount in the financial statement notes, since the pricing evidence rebuts any assertion the transaction was at arm's-length value. I recommend the team extend related party procedures across all periods presented and reconsider whether other related undisclosed relationships exist, given that David Hale's initial representation proved unreliable; this should include a fresh, more probing related party inquiry with both David Hale and other members of management and, if appropriate, those charged with governance (an audit committee or the board, if one exists separately from management). I recommend documenting this matter as a fraud risk factor under CAS 240 given the concealment, the bypassing of Meridian's own tender policy, and the above-market pricing, and performing additional procedures such as reviewing board or management meeting minutes for any discussion of the Coastal Packaging relationship, obtaining and corroborating a written explanation from David Hale, and considering whether expanded procedures over other significant vendor relationships are warranted. I also recommend the engagement partner reassess overall engagement risk and consider whether this matter, combined with the unreliable representation, affects the nature, timing, and extent of procedures elsewhere in the audit, and communicate the matter to those charged with governance as required by CAS 260, given both the related party disclosure deficiency and the reliability concern about management's representations.
model_response.conclusion: Coastal Packaging Supplies Ltd is a related party of Meridian, and the $2,400,000 in Year 2 purchases requires disclosure as a related party transaction not at arm's-length value. The CEO's initial denial of the relationship, combined with above-market pricing and bypassed tender policy, are fraud risk indicators requiring expanded related party procedures, reassessment of management representation reliability, and communication to those charged with governance.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly applies the ASPE 3840 related party definition to conclude Coastal Packaging is a related party despite David Hale's lack of direct ownership

Identifies the specific disclosure required (nature of relationship, description of transaction, dollar amount) and that the transaction should be recorded at the actual exchange amount, not restated to market value
Identifies CAS 550 requirements regarding identification and evaluation of related party transactions
Identifies the CEO's initial inaccurate representation as undermining the reliability of management representations generally on this engagement
