Budgeted fixed overhead was $200,000, giving a standard fixed overhead rate of $50.00 per unit at the budgeted volume of 4,000 units. Tables sell for $300.00 each.

Two events occurred during the year that Denise believes explain the results. First, BFC's long-time lumber supplier closed its mill in March, forcing BFC to switch to a new supplier that charges more per kilogram and that supplies a slightly different cut of oak requiring more trimming waste on the shop floor. Second, three senior machine operators retired in the spring and were replaced with junior hires at a lower wage rate; the junior hires are still building up speed on the line.

Actual results for the year: BFC produced and sold 3,700 tables. Direct materials used totalled 78,700 kilograms at a total cost of $338,410. Direct labour totalled 8,140 hours at a total cost of $236,060. Actual variable overhead was $101,750. Actual fixed overhead was $205,000.

Denise wants you to calculate the relevant variances, explain what likely drove each one using the two events described above, and recommend whether Marc's bonus should be paid and whether any variances warrant further investigation regardless of the bonus decision.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Standard Cost Card (per table)
exhibits.content:
Direct materials: 20.0 kg of oak at $4.25/kg = $85.00
Direct labour: 2.0 hours at $30.00/hour = $60.00
Variable overhead: 2.0 hours at $12.50/hour = $25.00
Total standard variable cost per unit = $170.00
Budgeted fixed overhead = $200,000, based on budgeted production of 4,000 units (standard fixed overhead rate = $50.00/unit)
Budgeted selling price = $300.00/unit

Note: variable overhead is applied on the basis of direct labour hours. Fixed overhead is allocated on the basis of budgeted production units.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Actual Results for the Year Ended
exhibits.content:
Units produced and sold: 3,700
Direct materials purchased and used: 78,700 kg for a total of $338,410
Direct labour: 8,140 hours for a total of $236,060
Actual variable overhead: $101,750
Actual fixed overhead: $205,000
Actual average selling price: $295.00/unit

Plant manager Marc Levesque's bonus policy: 10 percent of base salary if actual total manufacturing cost does not exceed standard cost of the actual output by more than 2 percent.

requirements: Calculate the direct materials price and usage variances, the direct labour rate and efficiency variances, the variable overhead variances, and the fixed overhead budget and volume variances. Explain the likely cause of each significant variance using the information given.
Recommend whether Marc's bonus should be paid and identify which variances, if any, warrant further management investigation independent of the bonus decision.

model_response.understand_issue: BFC needs a variance analysis of the year's manufacturing results to explain why costs differed from standard and to support a bonus decision for the plant manager. The two disclosed events, a forced supplier change and a shift to junior labour, are plausible drivers of several variances and should be tied directly to the calculated numbers rather than treated as generic excuses. The bonus policy is quantitative (cost within 2 percent of standard), so the recommendation must be grounded in a clear calculation of total actual cost versus standard cost of actual output.
model_response.analyze_case:
Direct materials: standard quantity allowed (SQ) for 3,700 units = 3,700 x 20 kg = 74,000 kg. Actual price = $338,410 / 78,700 kg = $4.30/kg.
Price variance = (AP - SP) x AQ = ($4.30 - $4.25) x 78,700 = $3,935 unfavourable.
Usage variance = (AQ - SQ) x SP = (78,700 - 74,000) x $4.25 = 4,700 x $4.25 = $19,975 unfavourable.
Total DM variance = $23,910 unfavourable, which reconciles to actual cost of $338,410 less standard cost of actual output of 74,000 x $4.25 = $314,500. Both are consistent with the new supplier: a higher price per kilogram (price variance) and a different cut of oak generating more trim waste (usage variance).

Direct labour: standard hours allowed (SH) = 3,700 x 2.0 = 7,400 hours. Actual rate = $236,060 / 8,140 = $29.00/hour.
Rate variance = (AR - SR) x AH = ($29.00 - $30.00) x 8,140 = $8,140 favourable.
Efficiency variance = (AH - SH) x SR = (8,140 - 7,400) x $30.00 = 740 x $30.00 = $22,200 unfavourable.
Total DL variance = $14,060 unfavourable ($236,060 actual less $222,000 standard cost of actual output). This matches the narrative: junior operators are paid less (favourable rate) but need more hours to produce the same output (unfavourable efficiency), and the efficiency variance more than offsets the rate saving.

Variable overhead (applied on labour hours): spending variance = actual VOH - (AH x standard VOH rate) = $101,750 - (8,140 x $12.50 = $101,750) = $0. Efficiency variance = (AH - SH) x standard rate = 740 x $12.50 = $9,250 unfavourable, driven by the same extra 740 labour hours.

Fixed overhead: budget variance = actual $205,000 - budgeted $200,000 = $5,000 unfavourable. Volume variance = (budgeted units - actual units) x standard fixed rate = (4,000 - 3,700) x $50.00 = $15,000 unfavourable, reflecting under-utilized capacity from producing 300 fewer tables than planned.

Total unfavourable variance across all categories = $23,910 + $14,060 + $9,250 + $5,000 + $15,000 = $67,220 unfavourable. Standard total manufacturing cost of actual output = 3,700 x $170.00 variable + $185,000 applied fixed overhead (3,700 units x $50.00 standard fixed rate) = $629,000 + $185,000 = $814,000. Actual total manufacturing cost = $338,410 + $236,060 + $101,750 + $205,000 = $881,220. The overage is $67,220, or 8.26 percent above standard ($67,220 / $814,000), which exceeds the 2 percent bonus threshold.
model_response.recommendation:
Marc's bonus should not be paid this year. Actual manufacturing cost exceeded the standard cost of actual output by 8.26 percent, well above the 2 percent threshold in the bonus policy, even though part of the overage (the volume variance) reflects lower sales demand rather than plant floor performance and is arguably outside Marc's control.

That said, the bonus decision should not be the end of the analysis. The controllable variances most worth investigating are the material usage variance ($19,975 U) and the labour efficiency variance ($22,200 U), because both are operational, recurring, and directly tied to the two events disclosed. Management should confirm with the new lumber supplier whether a different cut specification or better trim-waste handling can close the usage gap, and should quantify how long the junior operators are expected to need before reaching standard efficiency, since 740 extra hours a year at $30.00 plus $12.50 variable overhead is a recurring $31,450 annual cost if it does not improve.

The material price variance and the fixed overhead volume variance are largely uncontrollable in the short term (a market-driven supply change and a demand shortfall, respectively) and should be excluded from Marc's personal performance evaluation even though they remain part of total cost. Denise should consider redesigning the bonus formula to isolate controllable variances only, so Marc is not penalized for a supplier closure or a sales shortfall outside his control.
model_response.conclusion: Total actual manufacturing cost exceeded standard by $67,220, or 8.26 percent, driven mainly by the new supplier's higher material price and trim waste and by the junior labour crew's lower efficiency. Because this exceeds the 2 percent bonus threshold, Marc's bonus should not be paid under the current policy, but the material usage and labour efficiency variances warrant follow-up regardless, and Denise should reconsider whether uncontrollable variances belong in the bonus formula at all. Denise should share the variance breakdown with Marc directly so the bonus decision is transparent, and should schedule a mid-year checkpoint to confirm the labour efficiency gap is closing as the newer operators gain experience.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly calculates the direct materials price variance ($3,935 U) and usage variance ($19,975 U)
Correctly calculates the direct labour rate variance ($8,140 F) and efficiency variance ($22,200 U)
Correctly calculates variable overhead spending ($0) and efficiency ($9,250 U) variances
Correctly calculates fixed overhead budget ($5,000 U) and volume ($15,000 U) variances
Reconciles total actual manufacturing cost to standard cost of actual output and correctly compares the overage to the 2 percent bonus threshold
Links each significant variance to the specific narrative cause (supplier change, junior labour) rather than describing variances in the abstract
Distinguishes controllable variances from uncontrollable ones in forming the bonus recommendation

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Reaches a clear, supported recommendation on the bonus decision rather than presenting numbers without a conclusion
Identifies a forward-looking action (supplier discussion, training timeline) rather than only explaining what already happened
Presents the variance analysis in an organized, labelled format that a non-accountant reader (the CFO) could follow

rating_guidance: A response reaching Competent must correctly compute at minimum the direct materials and direct labour variances with the right formulas and signs, reconcile total actual cost to standard cost of actual output, and reach a supported bonus recommendation. Not Competent responses miscalculate two or more major variances, confuse price/rate variances with usage/efficiency variances, or recommend a bonus decision without tying it to the 2 percent threshold calculation. Reaching Competent Developed requires going further: separating controllable from uncontrollable variances in the bonus discussion, correctly noting that the volume variance stems from a sales shortfall rather than a plant efficiency problem, and proposing a specific improvement to the bonus formula or a concrete follow-up action on the two operational variances. Candidates who only calculate variances without any interpretation of cause, or who ignore the overhead variances entirely, should be capped at Reaching Competent.

enabling_focus: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
Adding Value
title: Chinook Solar Developments Inc. - Capital Budgeting Under Rate Uncertainty
case_background:
Chinook Solar Developments Inc. (CSD) is a private Alberta company that develops and operates utility-scale solar farms. You, a CPA, are a financial analyst reporting to the VP Finance, Priya Sandhu. CSD is evaluating a proposed 40-megawatt solar farm near Medicine Hat and Priya has asked you to assess whether the project should proceed.

The project requires an initial investment of $12,000,000 for panels, inverters, and site preparation, all incurred at the start of the project (time 0). The equipment has a useful life of 10 years, at the end of which it will be fully retired with no salvage value, net of dismantling costs. For this analysis, depreciation for tax purposes should be treated as straight-line over the 10-year life ($1,200,000 per year), consistent with CSD's internal planning convention for projects of this type.

Once operational, the farm is expected to generate incremental annual revenue of $3,200,000 from long-term power purchase agreements, against incremental annual cash operating costs (maintenance, land lease, insurance) of $1,050,000. CSD's combined federal and provincial tax rate is 26 percent. No incremental working capital is required because power sales are settled through an automated monthly billing arrangement with the utility.

CSD's board has approved a 9 percent discount rate for solar projects of this risk class, based on the company's weighted average cost of capital. However, the provincial government is currently reviewing the renewable energy subsidy program that supports the power purchase agreement pricing, and Priya is concerned that if the subsidy is reduced or the program's political risk increases, the appropriate discount rate for this specific project could rise to 11 percent to reflect the added uncertainty.

Priya wants you to calculate the project's net present value at the board-approved 9 percent rate, recalculate it at the higher 11 percent rate to test the project's sensitivity to the subsidy review, and recommend whether CSD should commit to the project now or wait for more clarity on the subsidy program before proceeding. Priya has also indicated that CSD's board meets only once per quarter, so if the project is not approved at the upcoming meeting, the next opportunity to formally commit capital would not arise until several months later, which is one reason the timing question matters as much as the raw NPV figure itself.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Project Cash Flow Assumptions
exhibits.content:
Initial investment (time 0): $12,000,000
Project life: 10 years
Salvage value at end of year 10: $0 (net of dismantling costs)
Annual incremental revenue: $3,200,000
Annual incremental cash operating costs: $1,050,000
Annual tax depreciation (straight-line): $1,200,000
Combined income tax rate: 26 percent
Incremental working capital required: nil
Board-approved discount rate for this project class: 9 percent
Stress-test discount rate if subsidy program risk increases: 11 percent

requirements: Calculate the project's annual after-tax operating cash flow and the project's net present value at the board-approved 9 percent discount rate.
Recalculate the net present value at 11 percent and recommend whether CSD should proceed with the project now, and what this sensitivity implies about the timing of the investment decision.

model_response.understand_issue: CSD must decide whether a $12,000,000 solar farm investment creates value for the company, and how sensitive that decision is to a pending subsidy review that could raise the project's required return from 9 percent to 11 percent. The analysis needs an after-tax cash flow build using the revenue, cost, depreciation, and tax rate given, followed by NPV at both discount rates to give Priya a clear basis for either approving the project now or deferring until the subsidy outcome is known.
model_response.analyze_case:
Annual after-tax operating cash flow:
EBITDA = Revenue - Cash operating costs = $3,200,000 - $1,050,000 = $2,150,000
EBIT = EBITDA - Depreciation = $2,150,000 - $1,200,000 = $950,000
Tax = $950,000 x 26% = $247,000
Net income = $950,000 - $247,000 = $703,000
After-tax cash flow = Net income + Depreciation (non-cash add-back) = $703,000 + $1,200,000 = $1,903,000 per year, for years 1 through 10.

NPV at 9 percent: the 10-year annuity discount factor at 9 percent is [1 - (1.09)^-10] / 0.09. (1.09)^10 = 2.36736, so 1 / 2.36736 = 0.42241. Factor = (1 - 0.42241) / 0.09 = 0.57759 / 0.09 = 6.4177.
PV of cash flows = $1,903,000 x 6.4177 = $12,213,000 (rounded).
NPV = $12,213,000 - $12,000,000 = approximately $213,000, positive.

NPV at 11 percent: the 10-year annuity discount factor at 11 percent is [1 - (1.11)^-10] / 0.11. (1.11)^10 = 2.83942, so 1 / 2.83942 = 0.35218. Factor = (1 - 0.35218) / 0.11 = 0.64782 / 0.11 = 5.8893.
PV of cash flows = $1,903,000 x 5.8893 = $11,207,000 (rounded).
NPV = $11,207,000 - $12,000,000 = approximately negative $793,000.

The project is marginally positive at the board's approved 9 percent rate, with an NPV of roughly $213,000, but the decision flips to a loss of roughly $793,000 if the discount rate rises to 11 percent to reflect subsidy-related risk. Because the swing in NPV between the two rates (approximately $1,006,000) is far larger than the base-case NPV itself, the project's viability hinges almost entirely on the outcome of the subsidy review rather than on the operating assumptions, which appear reasonably stable. It is also worth noting that this NPV calculation assumes the revenue and operating cost figures hold as projected for the full 10-year term; CSD has not been asked to stress-test those two assumptions here, but Priya should be aware that a small adverse movement in either one, layered on top of the discount rate risk already identified, would push the base-case NPV negative even at 9 percent.
model_response.recommendation:
CSD should not commit irrevocably to the project on the strength of the current 9 percent analysis alone, because the positive NPV of approximately $213,000 is small relative to the $1,000,000-plus swing that occurs if the discount rate moves to 11 percent. A project whose accept/reject conclusion reverses on a plausible, foreseeable change in one input is not a project the board should approve without addressing that input first.

Priya should request a firm timeline from the province on the subsidy review and, if practical, seek to lock in the power purchase agreement pricing or obtain contractual protection against a subsidy reduction before signing equipment and construction contracts. If the subsidy review outcome will not be known for several months and CSD faces no competitive urgency to build now (no risk of losing the site or the interconnection queue position), deferring the final investment decision until the subsidy risk resolves is the more prudent course, since the downside case is a loss of value, not simply a lower gain.

If CSD must decide immediately due to site or interconnection deadlines, the marginal positive NPV at 9 percent provides some support for proceeding, but only if management is comfortable that 9 percent, not 11 percent, remains the appropriate rate, and only alongside sensitivity testing on the revenue and cost assumptions as well, since those were not stress-tested here.
model_response.conclusion: At the board-approved 9 percent discount rate the project has a positive NPV of approximately $213,000, but at 11 percent the NPV turns negative by approximately $793,000, so the investment decision is not robust to the pending subsidy review. CSD should seek clarity on the subsidy outcome or contractual price protection before committing capital, and should only proceed now if a binding deadline forces the decision and management remains confident the 9 percent rate holds. Priya should set a firm checkpoint date tied to the subsidy review's expected announcement, and should revisit the operating cost and revenue assumptions with fresh sensitivity ranges before any final capital commitment is made to the board.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly builds the after-tax operating cash flow of $1,903,000 per year using EBITDA, depreciation, and the 26 percent tax rate
Correctly applies the 10-year annuity discount factor at 9 percent and calculates NPV of approximately $213,000
Correctly recalculates NPV at 11 percent, obtaining a negative NPV of approximately $793,000
Correctly adds back depreciation as a non-cash item rather than treating it as a cash outflow
Recognizes that no incremental working capital adjustment is needed and does not introduce one incorrectly
Interprets the magnitude of the NPV swing between 9 percent and 11 percent relative to the base-case NPV to assess decision robustness

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Recommends a course of action (defer, seek price protection, or proceed only under conditions) rather than simply reporting two NPV figures
Identifies a practical risk-mitigation step (locking in pricing, confirming subsidy timeline) beyond the numeric analysis
Clearly labels and separates the two NPV scenarios so the VP Finance can see the sensitivity at a glance

rating_guidance: Competent responses correctly build the annual after-tax cash flow, apply the correct annuity factor at 9 percent to reach an NPV near $213,000, and make a basic accept or reject call. Not Competent responses miscompute the tax shield, fail to add back depreciation, use the wrong number of periods, or omit the second discount rate scenario entirely. Competent Developed responses correctly complete both NPV scenarios, explicitly compare the size of the rate-driven swing to the base-case NPV, and use that comparison to justify a nuanced recommendation about deferring the decision or seeking price protection rather than a flat accept or reject. Candidates who calculate only one of the two required NPVs, or who recommend proceeding without acknowledging the sensitivity to the subsidy review, should not be rated above Reaching Competent.

enabling_focus: Adding Value
Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
title: Northshore Marine Components Ltd. - Make-or-Buy Decision on the X-200 Bracket
case_background:
Northshore Marine Components Ltd. (NMC) manufactures metal parts for boat builders in British Columbia. You, a CPA, are the cost accountant reporting to the plant controller, Steven Ainsley. NMC currently produces 15,000 units per year of the Model X-200 engine mounting bracket in-house and has received a firm quote from an outside supplier, Coastal Precision Metals, to supply the same part.

The X-200's current standard cost is $21.00 per unit: direct materials $8.00, direct labour $6.00, variable overhead $3.00, and allocated fixed overhead $4.00. The $4.00 fixed overhead allocation is based on total budgeted fixed overhead of $60,000 for the X-200 line, spread over 15,000 units. Of that $60,000, only $22,000 relates to a line supervisor position that would be eliminated if production of the X-200 were discontinued; the remaining $38,000 is an allocation of general plant overhead (building depreciation, plant manager's salary) that NMC would continue to incur regardless of this decision.

Coastal Precision Metals has quoted $17.50 per unit delivered to a nearby freight terminal, and NMC would incur an additional $0.50 per unit in freight to move the parts from the terminal to its own warehouse, for a total landed cost of $18.00 per unit.

Steven has also identified that if the X-200 line is discontinued, the freed production capacity could immediately be used to manufacture an additional 15,000 units per year of NMC's Model T-90 tiller arm, a product currently selling out its available capacity, generating a contribution margin of $2.25 per unit ($33,750 in total).

Steven wants a relevant-cost analysis of whether NMC should continue making the X-200 in-house or switch to Coastal Precision Metals, including the effect of redeploying the freed capacity to the T-90 line, and a recommendation supported by the numbers.

Steven has also noted that switching suppliers carries some execution risk: NMC has worked with its own machinists on the X-200 line for many years and has never sourced this particular bracket externally, so a transition would require a qualification period with Coastal Precision Metals before full-volume shipments begin. He wants the financial analysis to stand on its own, with any qualitative transition risk addressed separately in the recommendation rather than built into the cost figures themselves.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: X-200 Bracket Cost and Sourcing Data
exhibits.content:
Current annual volume: 15,000 units
Standard cost per unit (in-house): direct materials $8.00, direct labour $6.00, variable overhead $3.00, allocated fixed overhead $4.00, total $21.00
Total budgeted fixed overhead allocated to X-200 line: $60,000, of which $22,000 is the avoidable salary of the dedicated line supervisor and $38,000 is unavoidable general plant overhead allocation
Outside supplier quote (Coastal Precision Metals): $17.50/unit delivered to freight terminal
Additional freight to NMC's warehouse: $0.50/unit
If the X-200 line is discontinued, the freed capacity can produce 15,000 additional units per year of the T-90 tiller arm, generating a contribution margin of $2.25/unit

requirements: Calculate the total relevant cost of making the X-200 in-house versus buying it from Coastal Precision Metals, including the opportunity benefit of redeploying freed capacity to the T-90 line.
Recommend whether NMC should continue making the X-200 in-house or outsource it, and identify at least one qualitative factor that could change the recommendation.

model_response.understand_issue: NMC must decide whether to keep making the X-200 bracket in-house or buy it from an outside supplier. This is a classic relevant-cost, make-or-buy decision: the analysis must isolate only the avoidable costs of making (excluding the $38,000 of unavoidable allocated overhead) and must credit the buy option with the value of the freed capacity that could be redeployed to produce an additional 15,000 units of the T-90 line, which is currently selling out its capacity. The comparison also needs to make clear to Steven which figures are truly incremental so the recommendation is not distorted by allocated costs that will continue regardless of the sourcing decision.
model_response.analyze_case:
Relevant cost to make in-house: only the variable costs and the avoidable fixed cost are relevant. The $38,000 of unavoidable general plant overhead allocation continues regardless of the decision and must be excluded.
Variable cost per unit = $8.00 + $6.00 + $3.00 = $17.00
Total variable cost for 15,000 units = $17.00 x 15,000 = $255,000
Avoidable fixed cost (supervisor) = $22,000
Total relevant cost to make = $255,000 + $22,000 = $277,000

Relevant cost to buy: landed cost per unit = $17.50 + $0.50 freight = $18.00
Total cost to buy 15,000 units = $18.00 x 15,000 = $270,000

Opportunity benefit of freed capacity: if the X-200 is outsourced, the capacity can produce 15,000 additional T-90 units at a contribution margin of $2.25 each.
Total contribution from redeployed capacity = $2.25 x 15,000 = $33,750

Net relevant cost to buy, after crediting the opportunity benefit = $270,000 - $33,750 = $236,250

Comparison: relevant cost to make ($277,000) versus net relevant cost to buy ($236,250). Buying is cheaper by $277,000 - $236,250 = $40,750 per year. This saving arises from two sources: the direct cost saving of making versus buying before considering capacity ($277,000 - $270,000 = $7,000 in favour of buying) plus the full $33,750 contribution margin captured by redeploying the freed capacity to the T-90 line, which is the larger driver of the recommendation. It is worth noting that the $7,000 direct cost saving between making and buying, before considering the redeployed capacity, is relatively thin on its own, roughly 2.5 percent of the total make cost, which means the outsourcing decision is really being driven by the T-90 opportunity rather than by a large inherent cost advantage at Coastal Precision Metals. The freed-capacity benefit is also conditional on demand for the T-90 line remaining strong for the full year; if T-90 demand were to soften, the $33,750 contribution assumed here would not fully materialize, and the decision would then rest almost entirely on the much thinner $7,000 direct cost advantage of buying over making.
model_response.recommendation:
NMC should outsource the X-200 bracket to Coastal Precision Metals and redeploy the freed capacity to expand T-90 production. The relevant-cost analysis shows an annual saving of $40,750 for NMC as a whole, and the bulk of that saving, $33,750, comes from capturing contribution margin on T-90 units that the plant currently cannot produce because it is at capacity, not merely from a small per-unit cost difference between making and buying.

Before finalizing the decision, Steven should confirm several qualitative factors. First, whether Coastal Precision Metals can reliably meet NMC's quality standards and delivery schedule, since a quality failure on an engine mounting bracket carries safety and warranty risk for the boat builders NMC supplies. Second, whether the $22,000 supervisor position can genuinely be eliminated or reassigned, and what severance or transition cost that might involve in the year of the switch, which was not included in this ongoing-cost comparison. Third, whether the freed capacity can realistically absorb the full 15,000 additional T-90 units given labour and equipment constraints on that line, since the $33,750 benefit only materializes if that additional volume is actually sold, not just produced.

If the T-90 line does not have a firm market for the extra 15,000 units, the recommendation should be revisited using only the $7,000 saving from the direct make-versus-buy comparison, which is a much thinner margin and may not justify losing in-house control over a safety-critical part.
model_response.conclusion: Outsourcing the X-200 bracket and redeploying the freed capacity to the T-90 line saves NMC approximately $40,750 per year on a relevant-cost basis, driven primarily by the contribution margin captured on additional T-90 sales. The recommendation is to switch to Coastal Precision Metals, subject to confirming supplier quality, transition costs, and that the T-90 market can actually absorb the additional volume. Steven should document the transition plan, including a firm timeline for confirming supplier quality and redeploying the freed capacity, so the projected saving is realized rather than merely assumed on paper.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly excludes the $38,000 unavoidable fixed overhead from the relevant cost to make
Correctly calculates total relevant cost to make of $277,000 (variable $255,000 plus avoidable fixed $22,000)
Correctly calculates total landed cost to buy of $270,000 including freight
Correctly incorporates the $33,750 opportunity benefit of redeployed capacity into the buy decision
Correctly concludes buying saves NMC $40,750 per year and shows the calculation supporting that figure
Distinguishes the two separate sources of the saving (direct cost difference versus opportunity benefit)

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Reaches a clear outsourcing recommendation supported by the quantitative analysis
Identifies qualitative risks (supplier quality, transition cost, market absorption of T-90 volume) that could alter the recommendation

rating_guidance: Competent responses correctly separate avoidable from unavoidable fixed overhead, calculate the relevant cost to make and the landed cost to buy, and factor the freed-capacity opportunity benefit into a net buy cost of $236,250. Not Competent responses include the unavoidable $38,000 allocation in the make cost, ignore the freight addition to the buy quote, or omit the opportunity cost of capacity entirely. Competent Developed responses go further by decomposing the $40,750 total saving into its two distinct sources and using that breakdown to flag that the recommendation is materially dependent on the T-90 line actually being able to sell the additional volume, tying the quantitative result to a genuine business risk rather than a generic caveat list.

enabling_focus: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving

title: Summit Outdoor Retail Corp. - Debt Versus Equity Financing for Store Expansion
case_background:
Summit Outdoor Retail Corp. (SORC) is a publicly traded chain of outdoor recreation stores across Western Canada. You, a CPA, are a financial analyst reporting to the CFO, Bill Tanaka. SORC's board has approved an expansion plan to open eight new stores, requiring $5,000,000 in capital, and Bill has asked you to compare two financing alternatives before the board finalizes the decision next week.

Option A is a five-year bank term loan of $5,000,000 at a fixed interest rate of 6.0 percent per year. Option B is an equity issuance of 250,000 new common shares at the current market price of $20.00 per share, raising the same $5,000,000.

SORC currently has 1,000,000 common shares outstanding and net income of $2,000,000 for the most recent year, giving current earnings per share of $2.00. SORC also currently carries $3,000,000 of existing debt at a fixed interest rate of 5.5 percent, which will remain outstanding regardless of which new financing option is chosen. SORC's combined income tax rate is 27 percent.

Management projects that the eight new stores will generate incremental earnings before interest and taxes (EBIT) of $1,200,000 per year once fully ramped up, in addition to the company's current level of earnings.

Bill is primarily concerned with two things: the effect of each financing option on earnings per share in the first full year of operation, and the effect on SORC's financial risk, since the company's bank covenants restrict the ratio of total debt to EBITDA. Bill wants you to calculate projected EPS under each option and recommend which financing approach the board should adopt, addressing both the EPS impact and the risk implications.

Bill has also flagged that SORC's institutional shareholders have expressed a preference for management to avoid unnecessary dilution where possible, given the stock has traded below its five-year average price-to-earnings multiple for the past two quarters. At the same time, the board's audit committee has asked that any new financing proposal include a clear statement of the resulting leverage position relative to SORC's existing bank covenants, since the company renewed its credit facility only eight months ago.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Current and Projected Financial Data
exhibits.content:
Current net income: $2,000,000
Current shares outstanding: 1,000,000
Current EPS: $2.00
Existing debt: $3,000,000 at 5.5 percent interest (unaffected by the financing choice)
Combined income tax rate: 27 percent
Capital required for expansion: $5,000,000
Option A: five-year bank term loan, $5,000,000 at 6.0 percent interest
Option B: issue 250,000 new common shares at $20.00 per share ($5,000,000)
Projected incremental EBIT from the eight new stores, once fully ramped up: $1,200,000 per year

requirements: Calculate SORC's projected earnings per share under Option A (debt financing) and Option B (equity financing), showing all supporting calculations including current EBIT.
Recommend which financing option the board should choose, addressing both the EPS impact and the financial risk implications of each option.

model_response.understand_issue: SORC must choose between debt and equity financing for a $5,000,000 store expansion. The decision affects both shareholders' near-term earnings per share and the company's financial risk profile, since new debt would increase leverage against a bank covenant tied to debt-to-EBITDA. The analysis requires first backing into SORC's current EBIT from the given net income and existing interest expense, then projecting EPS under each financing structure using the incremental EBIT from the new stores. The comparison also needs to make explicit how much total debt SORC would be carrying under Option A, since EPS alone does not capture the covenant and liquidity implications of the added leverage.
model_response.analyze_case:
Step 1: Determine current EBIT.
Current interest expense = $3,000,000 x 5.5% = $165,000
Current net income = $2,000,000, and net income = EBT x (1 - tax rate), so EBT = $2,000,000 / (1 - 0.27) = $2,000,000 / 0.73 = $2,739,726
Current EBIT = EBT + interest = $2,739,726 + $165,000 = $2,904,726

Step 2: Projected EBIT after expansion = $2,904,726 + $1,200,000 = $4,104,726

Option A (debt financing):
New loan interest = $5,000,000 x 6.0% = $300,000
Total interest = $165,000 existing + $300,000 new = $465,000
EBT = $4,104,726 - $465,000 = $3,639,726
Net income = $3,639,726 x (1 - 0.27) = $3,639,726 x 0.73 = $2,657,000 (rounded)
Shares outstanding remain at 1,000,000
EPS = $2,657,000 / 1,000,000 = $2.66

Option B (equity financing):
Interest remains at the existing $165,000 only, since no new debt is raised
EBT = $4,104,726 - $165,000 = $3,939,726
Net income = $3,939,726 x 0.73 = $2,876,000 (rounded)
Shares outstanding = 1,000,000 + 250,000 = 1,250,000
EPS = $2,876,000 / 1,250,000 = $2.30

Comparison: Option A produces EPS of $2.66, roughly $0.36 higher than Option B's $2.30, meaning debt financing is accretive to EPS relative to issuing new shares. This is expected given that the after-tax cost of the new debt (6.0% x (1 - 0.27) = 4.4 percent effective) is well below the return the expansion is expected to generate, so leveraging the project with debt benefits existing shareholders on a per-share basis, at the cost of increased financial risk. It is also worth noting that the difference between the two options grows larger the more the new stores outperform the $1,200,000 EBIT projection, since debt financing lets shareholders keep all of the additional upside on a fixed number of shares, while equity financing spreads that same upside across 250,000 additional shares regardless of how well the expansion performs.
model_response.recommendation:
On an EPS basis alone, Option A (the bank term loan) is superior, producing EPS of $2.66 versus $2.30 under equity financing, a difference of roughly 16 percent. This makes debt financing attractive to existing shareholders in the near term, since it avoids diluting their ownership and lets them capture more of the expansion's return per share.

However, the recommendation cannot rest on EPS alone. Adding $5,000,000 of new debt increases SORC's total debt from $3,000,000 to $8,000,000, more than 2.6 times its prior level, at a time the company is also taking on the operating risk of eight new stores that have not yet proven themselves. Bill should confirm SORC's current debt-to-EBITDA ratio against the covenant limit before committing to Option A. Projected EBITDA is close to $4,104,726 EBIT plus non-cash depreciation, which was not separately provided, so this covenant check needs to be completed before the loan is finalized, as breaching the covenant could trigger penalty pricing or acceleration.

If the covenant has ample headroom and SORC's cash flow from existing operations is stable enough to service $465,000 of total annual interest even if the new stores underperform in their first year, Option A is recommended. If covenant headroom is tight or the board wants to preserve future borrowing capacity for opportunistic acquisitions, a blended approach, part debt and part equity, or Option B outright, may be more prudent despite the EPS dilution.
model_response.conclusion: Debt financing produces higher projected EPS of $2.66 versus $2.30 under equity financing, because the after-tax cost of the new debt is well below the expected return on the expansion. The recommendation is to proceed with the bank term loan provided SORC's debt-to-EBITDA covenant has sufficient headroom to absorb $8,000,000 of total debt; otherwise a partial equity component should be considered to protect covenant compliance. Bill should present both the EPS comparison and the covenant headroom analysis together to the board, so the financing decision reflects both shareholder return and the company's capacity to absorb the added leverage safely.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly backs into current EBIT of $2,904,726 using net income, tax rate, and existing interest
Correctly calculates projected EBIT after expansion of $4,104,726
Correctly calculates Option A (debt) EPS of approximately $2.66 including both existing and new interest
Correctly calculates Option B (equity) EPS of approximately $2.30 including the new shares issued
Correctly identifies that debt financing is EPS-accretive relative to equity in this scenario
Identifies the increase in financial risk and covenant implications of adding $5,000,000 of new debt

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Balances the EPS result against financial risk rather than recommending solely on EPS
Presents the EBIT build-up and both EPS calculations in a clear, followable sequence for a non-specialist board

rating_guidance: Competent responses correctly derive current EBIT from the given net income and tax rate, project EBIT after expansion, and calculate EPS under both financing options with the correct interest and share count treatment. Not Competent responses skip the EBIT derivation and use net income directly as EBIT, forget to include existing interest expense, or use the wrong share count for Option B. Competent Developed responses correctly complete all calculations and explicitly connect the EPS result to the covenant and leverage risk, recommending that the debt-to-EBITDA covenant be checked before finalizing Option A rather than recommending debt financing on EPS grounds alone. A recommendation that ignores financial risk entirely, even with correct EPS math, should not be rated above Reaching Competent.

enabling_focus: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
Adding Value
title: Fraser Valley Bottling Inc. - Special Order Decision Under a Capacity Constraint
case_background:
Fraser Valley Bottling Inc. (FVB) produces craft sodas in British Columbia and sells primarily to grocery chains and independent retailers. You, a CPA, are the management accountant reporting to the sales director, Ottavio Ricci. FVB has received a one-time special order from a national discount retailer for 60,000 bottles at a price of $1.20 per bottle, and Ottavio needs an analysis before responding to the retailer's buyer by Friday.

FVB's plant has monthly production capacity of 500,000 bottles. In a normal month, FVB produces and sells 460,000 bottles to its regular customers at a price of $1.80 per bottle. Variable cost per bottle is $0.95, consisting of $0.55 in materials, $0.20 in labour, and $0.20 in variable overhead. FVB pays its sales team a commission of $0.05 per bottle on regular sales only; the special order, if accepted, would be handled directly by Ottavio's team with no commission paid. Monthly fixed overhead is $184,000 and does not change with volume within the plant's 500,000-bottle capacity.

The special order calls for 60,000 bottles, which would bring total volume to 520,000 bottles, 20,000 bottles beyond FVB's 500,000-bottle monthly capacity. Because the special order is a one-time commitment the retailer wants filled entirely in the coming month, FVB cannot simply spread it over future months; accepting it in full would require displacing 20,000 bottles of regular sales that month. The remaining 40,000 bottles of the order can be produced using currently idle capacity (the 40,000 bottles of headroom between the current 460,000 regular volume and the 500,000 capacity limit).

Ottavio wants to know whether FVB should accept the special order at $1.20 per bottle, and if not, what minimum price would make the order acceptable given the capacity constraint.

Ottavio has also noted that FVB's relationship with its regular retail customers has taken years to build, and losing shelf space to a competitor because of a temporary capacity shortfall could have consequences well beyond the current month. He wants the analysis to reflect the true trade-off between the special order and FVB's existing customer base, not simply a comparison of unit prices against unit variable costs.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Cost and Capacity Data
exhibits.content:
Monthly plant capacity: 500,000 bottles
Current regular monthly volume: 460,000 bottles at $1.80/bottle
Variable cost per bottle: materials $0.55, labour $0.20, variable overhead $0.20, total $0.95
Sales commission: $0.05 per bottle, paid only on regular sales, not on the special order
Monthly fixed overhead: $184,000, fixed within the capacity range
Special order: 60,000 bottles at $1.20/bottle, one-time, must be filled entirely in the coming month
Idle capacity available before the order: 500,000 - 460,000 = 40,000 bottles

requirements: Determine how many bottles of the special order can be produced using idle capacity and how many would displace regular sales, and calculate the net financial effect of accepting the order at $1.20 per bottle.
Calculate the minimum price FVB would need to charge for the special order to make it financially acceptable, and provide a recommendation.

model_response.understand_issue: FVB has only 40,000 bottles of idle monthly capacity but has received a special order for 60,000 bottles, meaning 20,000 bottles of the order would displace regular-priced sales. The decision requires comparing the contribution margin the special order generates against the contribution margin lost on the displaced regular sales, correctly accounting for the sales commission that applies only to regular sales, to determine whether $1.20 per bottle is an acceptable price. The analysis must also separate the portion of the order that fits within idle capacity from the portion that would displace existing sales, since treating the whole order as incremental would overstate its attractiveness.
model_response.analyze_case:
Contribution margin on regular sales (net of commission) = $1.80 - $0.95 - $0.05 = $0.80/bottle
Contribution margin on the special order (no commission) = $1.20 - $0.95 = $0.25/bottle

Of the 60,000 bottles ordered, 40,000 can be produced using idle capacity with no displacement of regular sales, and 20,000 must displace regular sales because total volume would otherwise exceed the 500,000-bottle capacity (460,000 + 60,000 = 520,000, which is 20,000 over capacity).

Contribution from the 40,000 bottles using idle capacity = 40,000 x $0.25 = $10,000
Contribution from the 20,000 bottles that displace regular sales = 20,000 x $0.25 = $5,000
Opportunity cost of the 20,000 displaced regular bottles = 20,000 x $0.80 = $16,000

Net effect of accepting the order at $1.20 = $10,000 + $5,000 - $16,000 = -$1,000

This can be verified directly: total revenue from the order = 60,000 x $1.20 = $72,000. Total cost to fulfil it properly reflects $0.95 variable cost on all 60,000 bottles ($57,000) plus the $16,000 opportunity cost of displaced regular contribution, giving total relevant cost of $73,000. Net effect = $72,000 - $73,000 = -$1,000, which matches.

Minimum acceptable price: the 40,000 idle-capacity bottles only need to cover their variable cost of $0.95 to be worthwhile from a pure contribution standpoint, but for the order overall to break even, the 20,000 displaced bottles need to cover their $0.95 variable cost plus the $0.80 opportunity cost, or $1.75 per bottle. Minimum total revenue required = (40,000 x $0.95) + (20,000 x $1.75) = $38,000 + $35,000 = $73,000. Minimum average price = $73,000 / 60,000 = $1.2167 per bottle, approximately $1.22. The offered price of $1.20 falls just short of this threshold, consistent with the $1,000 shortfall calculated above ($73,000 required less $72,000 offered = $1,000). It is also useful to note that if FVB had sufficient idle capacity to absorb the full 60,000-unit order without displacing any regular sales, the order would be straightforwardly profitable at $1.20 per bottle, since $1.20 comfortably exceeds the $0.95 variable cost. The capacity constraint, not the price itself, is what makes this order marginal.
model_response.recommendation:
FVB should not accept the special order at the offered price of $1.20 per bottle, since it would reduce total monthly contribution margin by approximately $1,000 once the opportunity cost of displaced regular sales is properly recognized. Although the order appears profitable on a superficial basis (price of $1.20 exceeds variable cost of $0.95), that comparison ignores that 20,000 of the 60,000 bottles must come at the expense of higher-margin regular sales.

Ottavio should go back to the retailer's buyer and propose a minimum price of approximately $1.22 per bottle, which would make the order breakeven, or ideally negotiate somewhat higher to leave a positive margin, for example $1.25 per bottle, which would generate a net gain of $2,000 (contribution margin of $0.30 per bottle at $1.25: $12,000 from the 40,000 idle-capacity units plus $6,000 from the 20,000 displaced units, less the unchanged $16,000 opportunity cost of displaced regular sales, using the same logic recalculated at the new price).

Alternatively, if the retailer's volume is strategically important for FVB's future growth into the discount channel, Ottavio could propose delivering only the 40,000 bottles that fit within idle capacity this month, which would be unambiguously profitable at $1.20 (contributing $10,000 with no displacement), and offer the remaining 20,000 bottles the following month once idle capacity is again available, avoiding the opportunity cost entirely.
model_response.conclusion: Accepting the full 60,000-bottle special order at $1.20 per bottle would reduce FVB's total contribution margin by approximately $1,000 because 20,000 bottles would displace higher-margin regular sales. FVB should decline the order at this price and either negotiate a minimum price of roughly $1.22 per bottle or propose splitting delivery across two months to stay within idle capacity. Ottavio should communicate the counter-proposal to the retailer promptly given the Friday deadline, and should confirm with production planning that the idle-capacity portion of any revised order can still be scheduled within the coming month.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly identifies that only 40,000 of the 60,000 bottles fit within idle capacity and 20,000 would displace regular sales
Correctly calculates contribution margin on regular sales net of commission ($0.80/bottle) and on the special order ($0.25/bottle)
Correctly calculates the opportunity cost of displaced regular sales ($16,000) and the net effect of accepting the order (-$1,000)
Correctly calculates the minimum acceptable price of approximately $1.22 per bottle using the blended idle-capacity and displaced-capacity logic
Does not treat fixed overhead of $184,000 as relevant to the decision

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Reaches a clear reject-or-renegotiate recommendation supported by the numeric shortfall
Proposes a practical alternative (split delivery across months, or a specific renegotiated price) rather than a flat rejection
Clearly presents the split between idle-capacity and displaced-capacity units so Ottavio can explain the counter-offer to the retailer's buyer

rating_guidance: Competent responses correctly split the order between idle and displaced capacity, calculate contribution margins net of the commission difference, and quantify the approximately $1,000 negative net effect of accepting the order at $1.20. Not Competent responses treat all 60,000 units as either fully within capacity or fully displacing regular sales, ignore the commission distinction, or include fixed overhead as a relevant cost. Competent Developed responses go further by deriving the precise minimum acceptable price of approximately $1.22 per bottle using the blended calculation and by proposing a genuinely useful alternative such as splitting the delivery across two months to avoid the opportunity cost altogether, showing insight beyond a simple accept or reject conclusion.

enabling_focus: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
Adding Value
title: Cascadia Health Diagnostics Inc. - Valuing the Proposed Acquisition of MedScan Imaging Partners
case_background:
Cascadia Health Diagnostics Inc. (CHD) operates a network of diagnostic imaging clinics in British Columbia. You, a CPA, are a corporate development analyst reporting to the VP Corporate Development, Renata Volkov. CHD is considering acquiring MedScan Imaging Partners, a privately held chain of four imaging clinics, and the seller has proposed an asking price of $17,500,000 for 100 percent of MedScan's equity. Renata wants an independent valuation before CHD responds to the offer.

MedScan's most recent audited annual EBITDA (earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization) was $2,400,000. A set of comparable publicly traded diagnostic imaging companies currently trade at an average enterprise value to EBITDA (EV/EBITDA) multiple of 6.5 times. MedScan carries $1,800,000 of outstanding debt and holds $300,000 of cash on its balance sheet.

Separately, MedScan's management has provided a cash flow projection showing expected free cash flow of $1,500,000 in the first year following acquisition, growing at a constant 3 percent per year thereafter into perpetuity, reflecting stable demand for imaging services in an aging population. Renata has directed you to discount this perpetuity at CHD's required return for this type of acquisition, 10 percent, using the Gordon growth model to estimate enterprise value under the discounted cash flow (DCF) approach.

Renata is aware that the multiples approach and the DCF approach may produce different results, given how sensitive perpetuity growth models are to the assumed growth rate and discount rate, and wants both approaches calculated so the two can be compared against the seller's $17,500,000 asking price. She also wants your view on which method is more reliable in this situation and what that means for CHD's negotiating position.

Renata has also noted that MedScan's four clinics operate under provincial diagnostic imaging licences that are not automatically transferable on a change of control, and that two of MedScan's three staff radiologists have indicated they would consider leaving if the practice changed hands without adequate retention arrangements. These factors do not change the arithmetic of either valuation method directly, but Renata wants them addressed as part of the overall recommendation to the board. Renata wants both quantitative valuations and these qualitative points reflected clearly in her written summary to the board.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: MedScan Financial and Market Data
exhibits.content:
MedScan most recent annual EBITDA: $2,400,000
Comparable public company average EV/EBITDA multiple: 6.5x
MedScan outstanding debt: $1,800,000
MedScan cash on hand: $300,000
Projected free cash flow, year 1 post-acquisition: $1,500,000
Long-term perpetuity growth rate: 3 percent per year
CHD's required return (discount rate) for this acquisition: 10 percent
Seller's asking price for 100 percent of MedScan equity: $17,500,000

requirements: Calculate MedScan's estimated equity value using both the EV/EBITDA multiple approach and the discounted cash flow (Gordon growth) approach.
Compare both estimates to the seller's $17,500,000 asking price and recommend a negotiating position for CHD, including which valuation method you consider more reliable in this situation and why.

model_response.understand_issue: CHD needs an independent estimate of MedScan's value to evaluate a $17,500,000 asking price using two standard methods, a market multiple approach and a discounted cash flow approach, since the two can diverge significantly depending on the assumptions used. The analysis needs to convert each method's enterprise value estimate to an equity value by adjusting for MedScan's actual debt and cash, since the asking price is for the equity, not the enterprise as a whole. Because the two methods rest on very different assumptions, market pricing multiples versus a long-run growth forecast, the analysis also needs to explain to Renata which figure should carry more weight in the negotiation.
model_response.analyze_case:
Multiples approach:
Enterprise value = EV/EBITDA multiple x EBITDA = 6.5 x $2,400,000 = $15,600,000
Equity value = Enterprise value - Debt + Cash = $15,600,000 - $1,800,000 + $300,000 = $14,100,000

DCF (Gordon growth) approach:
Enterprise value = FCF1 / (r - g) = $1,500,000 / (0.10 - 0.03) = $1,500,000 / 0.07 = $21,428,571
Equity value = Enterprise value - Debt + Cash = $21,428,571 - $1,800,000 + $300,000 = $19,928,571

Comparison to asking price: the multiples approach implies an equity value of $14,100,000, roughly $3,400,000 below the seller's $17,500,000 ask. The DCF approach implies an equity value of approximately $19,928,571, roughly $2,428,571 above the ask. The two methods bracket the asking price, with a total spread between them of nearly $5,828,571, which is unusually wide and signals that the DCF result is highly sensitive to the assumed 3 percent perpetual growth rate and 10 percent discount rate. For illustration, if the discount rate were 11 percent instead of 10 percent, the DCF enterprise value would fall to $1,500,000 / (0.11 - 0.03) = $18,750,000, an equity value of approximately $17,250,000, much closer to both the ask and the multiples result, demonstrating how a one-point change in the discount rate materially moves the DCF conclusion.

The multiples approach, by contrast, is anchored to observable market pricing of comparable companies and does not depend on a long-run growth assumption that is difficult to defend with confidence for a four-clinic private business. For that reason the multiples-based equity value of $14,100,000 is likely the more defensible starting point for negotiation, while the DCF result should be treated as an upper bound that depends heavily on MedScan sustaining 3 percent growth indefinitely, an assumption that carries real execution risk. It is also useful to frame the gap in relative terms: the DCF equity value of approximately $19,928,571 is roughly 41 percent higher than the multiples-based equity value of $14,100,000, a spread far wider than would normally be expected between two reasonable valuation methods applied to the same business, which reinforces that the growth assumption deserves direct scrutiny rather than being accepted at face value. This spread is large enough that Renata should treat the two figures as bounding a negotiation range rather than as two independently reliable point estimates of MedScan's true value.
model_response.recommendation:
CHD should treat the multiples-based equity value of approximately $14,100,000 as its primary anchor and should not accept the seller's $17,500,000 asking price without further negotiation, since that price sits roughly 24 percent above the multiples estimate and relies on the more optimistic DCF assumptions to be justified. Renata's opening negotiating position should reference the $14,100,000 multiples valuation and request supporting detail on the sustainability of MedScan's projected 3 percent long-term growth, since that single assumption is what drives the DCF result high enough to support the seller's ask.

CHD should also identify whether synergies specific to CHD, such as shared back-office functions, combined imaging equipment purchasing, or cross-referral volume between CHD's and MedScan's clinics, could justify paying above the pure standalone multiples value, since strategic buyers often pay a premium for synergies a purely financial buyer would not capture. If those synergies are credible and quantifiable, CHD has room to move toward the middle of the two valuation estimates, in the $15,500,000 to $16,500,000 range, without overpaying on a standalone basis.

Key-person risk should also be assessed given MedScan's ownership structure and radiologist relationships, and CHD should confirm that MedScan's imaging licences and accreditations transfer cleanly on a change of control, since a valuation gap this wide should not be closed on multiples and DCF numbers alone without addressing the underlying operational risks.
model_response.conclusion: MedScan's estimated equity value ranges from approximately $14,100,000 under the multiples approach to approximately $19,928,571 under the DCF approach, bracketing the seller's $17,500,000 asking price. The multiples result is more defensible given its reliance on observable market data rather than a long-run growth forecast, so CHD should anchor its negotiation near $14,100,000 and only move higher if credible synergies or a more conservative, well-supported growth assumption justify doing so. Renata should document both valuations and the sensitivity analysis for the board, and should request additional financial detail from MedScan's management to test the durability of the 3 percent growth assumption before the next round of negotiations.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly calculates enterprise value under the multiples approach ($15,600,000) and equity value ($14,100,000) after adjusting for debt and cash
Correctly applies the Gordon growth formula to calculate DCF enterprise value ($21,428,571) and equity value (approximately $19,928,571)
Correctly identifies that enterprise value must be adjusted for debt and cash to arrive at equity value comparable to the asking price
Compares both valuation estimates to the $17,500,000 asking price and quantifies the gap in each direction
Demonstrates the DCF result's sensitivity to the discount rate or growth rate assumption with a supporting recalculation
Provides a reasoned view on which method is more reliable in this situation rather than simply averaging the two

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Recommends a specific negotiating position anchored to the analysis rather than a vague statement
Identifies synergy potential or key-person and licensing risk as factors that could justify moving off the pure standalone valuation

rating_guidance: Competent responses correctly compute enterprise and equity value under both the multiples and DCF approaches, correctly adjust for MedScan's debt and cash, and compare both results to the asking price. Not Competent responses confuse enterprise value with equity value, omit the debt and cash adjustment, or misapply the Gordon growth formula. Competent Developed responses additionally demonstrate the DCF's sensitivity to its assumptions with a supporting recalculation, form a reasoned view on which method should anchor negotiations, and connect that view to a specific negotiating recommendation rather than simply reporting two numbers and letting the reader decide. Candidates who average the two valuations without discussing which is more reliable, or who accept the asking price without challenge, should be capped at Reaching Competent.

enabling_focus: Communicating
Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
title: Kootenay Craft Brewing Co. - Flexible Budget Variance Analysis and a Loan Covenant Breach
case_background:
Kootenay Craft Brewing Co. (KCB) is a private British Columbia craft brewery that distributes to restaurants and liquor retailers across the province. You, a CPA, are the controller reporting to the owner, Franz Huber. KCB's operating line of credit with its bank includes a covenant requiring annual operating income of at least $500,000; failing to meet this covenant gives the bank the right to demand immediate repayment or renegotiate terms on less favourable conditions. The bank is reviewing KCB's results next week as part of its annual loan renewal, and Franz has asked you to analyze the year's performance before that meeting.

KCB's master budget for the year called for sales of 80,000 cases at a budgeted price of $42.00 per case, budgeted variable cost of $24.00 per case, and budgeted fixed costs of $900,000.

Actual results for the year were: 72,000 cases sold at an actual average price of $44.00 per case, actual variable cost of $25.50 per case, and actual fixed costs of $875,000. The lower volume reflects a slower-than-expected launch of KCB's new distribution agreement in the Okanagan region, while the higher price reflects a price increase KCB implemented mid-year. The higher variable cost per case reflects a spike in aluminum can and hop prices during the year.

Franz is worried the bank will focus only on the fact that actual operating income fell short of the static budget and will not appreciate that KCB actually improved its price and cost performance on the volume it did achieve. He wants you to prepare a flexible budget variance analysis that separates the effect of the volume shortfall from the effect of price and cost performance, determine whether KCB's actual operating income meets the $500,000 covenant, and recommend how KCB should approach the bank renewal conversation.

Franz has also confirmed that KCB's bank relationship manager has handled the account for only six months, having taken over after the prior manager left the branch, and has not yet been through a full annual cycle with KCB. Franz believes a clear, well-organized explanation of the variance drivers will matter more than usual in building the new relationship manager's confidence in KCB's management team ahead of the covenant discussion.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Master Budget and Actual Results
exhibits.content:
Master budget: 80,000 cases at $42.00/case, variable cost $24.00/case, fixed costs $900,000
Actual results: 72,000 cases at $44.00/case, variable cost $25.50/case, fixed costs $875,000
Bank covenant: minimum annual operating income of $500,000
Cause of volume shortfall: slower-than-expected Okanagan distribution launch
Cause of price increase: mid-year price increase implemented by KCB
Cause of variable cost increase: higher aluminum can and hop input costs during the year

requirements: Prepare a variance analysis showing the static budget operating income, the flexible budget operating income, and actual operating income, and calculate the sales volume variance and the flexible budget variance, decomposed into price, variable cost, and fixed cost components.
Determine whether KCB's actual operating income satisfies the $500,000 bank covenant and recommend how Franz should approach the bank renewal discussion.

model_response.understand_issue: KCB's actual operating income needs to be tested against a $500,000 bank covenant, but a simple comparison of actual results to the master budget would unfairly blend a volume shortfall outside management's short-term control with genuine price and cost performance that management did control. A flexible budget variance analysis is needed to separate these effects so Franz can explain the shortfall credibly to the bank and demonstrate which parts of the business performed well despite lower volume. The analysis also needs to make clear to Franz which portion of the shortfall reflects volume outside management's short-term control, so the bank sees an accurate picture rather than an undifferentiated miss against budget.
model_response.analyze_case:
Static budget operating income = 80,000 x ($42.00 - $24.00) - $900,000 = 80,000 x $18.00 - $900,000 = $1,440,000 - $900,000 = $540,000

Flexible budget operating income (at actual volume of 72,000 cases, using budgeted price and cost) = 72,000 x ($42.00 - $24.00) - $900,000 = 72,000 x $18.00 - $900,000 = $1,296,000 - $900,000 = $396,000

Actual operating income = 72,000 x ($44.00 - $25.50) - $875,000 = 72,000 x $18.50 - $875,000 = $1,332,000 - $875,000 = $457,000

Sales volume variance = Flexible budget operating income - Static budget operating income = $396,000 - $540,000 = $144,000 unfavourable, entirely attributable to selling 8,000 fewer cases than budgeted (80,000 - 72,000 = 8,000 cases x $18.00 budgeted contribution margin = $144,000).

Flexible budget variance = Actual operating income - Flexible budget operating income = $457,000 - $396,000 = $61,000 favourable. This can be decomposed into three components:
Sales price variance = ($44.00 - $42.00) x 72,000 = $144,000 favourable
Variable cost variance = ($24.00 - $25.50) x 72,000 = -$108,000, i.e., $108,000 unfavourable
Fixed cost variance = $900,000 budgeted - $875,000 actual = $25,000 favourable
Sum of components = $144,000 - $108,000 + $25,000 = $61,000 favourable, which reconciles exactly to the flexible budget variance calculated above.

Covenant test: actual operating income of $457,000 is below the $500,000 covenant threshold by $43,000, meaning KCB is in breach of the covenant despite the favourable flexible budget variance. The volume shortfall alone ($144,000 unfavourable) is larger than the entire favourable price, cost, and fixed cost performance combined ($61,000 favourable), which is why total operating income still fell short of both the static budget and the covenant threshold. It is also worth noting that the $61,000 favourable flexible budget variance is smaller than the $144,000 unfavourable volume variance by a wide margin, which explains numerically why KCB's strong operational performance was not enough to prevent the covenant shortfall, even though the underlying business fundamentals actually improved during the year.
model_response.recommendation:
Franz should go into the bank renewal meeting acknowledging the covenant shortfall directly rather than downplaying it, since actual operating income of $457,000 is $43,000 below the $500,000 covenant and the bank will identify this regardless. At the same time, Franz has a genuinely strong story to tell about controllable performance: on the volume actually achieved, KCB improved operating income by $61,000 relative to what the flexible budget would have predicted, driven by a $144,000 favourable price variance from the mid-year price increase that more than offset a $108,000 unfavourable variable cost variance from higher input costs, plus $25,000 of favourable fixed cost management.

This distinction matters to the bank because it shows the shortfall is a volume and market development issue, tied to the pace of the Okanagan distribution rollout, rather than a sign that KCB's core unit economics or cost discipline have deteriorated. Franz should present the full distribution timeline for the Okanagan region and demonstrate that the volume gap is closing, supporting a request for a temporary covenant waiver or a revised, more realistic covenant level for the coming year rather than a renegotiation of the entire facility on unfavourable terms.

Franz should also proactively propose a remediation plan, for example accelerating the Okanagan rollout or securing additional distribution accounts, with quantified volume targets, since banks are generally more willing to grant a covenant waiver when management demonstrates a credible, specific path back to compliance rather than simply asking for forbearance.
model_response.conclusion: KCB's actual operating income of $457,000 falls $43,000 short of the $500,000 bank covenant, driven by a $144,000 unfavourable volume variance from the slower Okanagan rollout that outweighed a $61,000 favourable flexible budget variance from strong price and cost management. Franz should present this distinction clearly to the bank, request a covenant waiver or revised threshold, and support the request with a concrete volume recovery plan. Franz should prepare the variance analysis and remediation timeline well ahead of the bank meeting so the covenant conversation opens with KCB's own explanation rather than the bank's, reducing the risk of an unfavourable renegotiation.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly calculates static budget operating income ($540,000), flexible budget operating income ($396,000), and actual operating income ($457,000)
Correctly calculates the sales volume variance of $144,000 unfavourable
Correctly calculates the flexible budget variance of $61,000 favourable and decomposes it into price, variable cost, and fixed cost components that reconcile
Correctly determines actual operating income falls $43,000 short of the $500,000 covenant
Recognizes the loan covenant breach as a governance and stakeholder-communication issue requiring proactive disclosure to the bank
Recommends a specific course of action with the bank (waiver request, revised covenant, remediation plan) rather than only reporting the breach
Distinguishes clearly between volume-driven and performance-driven variance in language the bank (a non-technical audience) can follow

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Balances acknowledging the shortfall honestly with presenting the favourable operational story

rating_guidance: Competent responses correctly build the three-tier income comparison (static budget, flexible budget, actual), calculate the sales volume variance and flexible budget variance with the correct signs, and identify the covenant breach. Not Competent responses confuse the static and flexible budget figures, miscalculate the volume variance, or fail to test the result against the $500,000 covenant at all. Competent Developed responses additionally decompose the flexible budget variance into its price, variable cost, and fixed cost components with a correct reconciliation, and translate the numbers into a specific, credible recommendation for the bank conversation, such as a waiver request backed by a quantified remediation plan, rather than a generic statement that KCB should talk to its bank.

enabling_focus: Adding Value
Managing Self
title: Maritime AgriSupply Ltd. - Managing the Cash Conversion Cycle
case_background:
Maritime AgriSupply Ltd. (MAS) is a private distributor of farm inputs (seed, fertilizer, and equipment parts) operating across the Maritime provinces. You, a CPA, are a financial analyst reporting to the owner, Colette Landry. Although MAS has been consistently profitable, it is chronically short of cash and has drawn its $2,500,000 revolving line of credit down to $1,500,000 outstanding, close to the facility's limit. Colette suspects the problem is working capital management rather than profitability and has asked you to analyze MAS's cash conversion cycle and identify ways to free up cash.

MAS's annual sales are $18,250,000, which for simplicity can be treated as $50,000 per day (18,250,000 / 365). Annual cost of goods sold is $13,140,000, or $36,000 per day (13,140,000 / 365).

MAS currently collects accounts receivable in an average of 58 days (days sales outstanding), holds inventory for an average of 75 days (days inventory outstanding), and pays its suppliers in an average of 40 days (days payable outstanding). Colette has identified three potential changes: tightening credit terms with customers to reduce days sales outstanding to 45 days, improving inventory planning (moving toward a more just-in-time model with key suppliers) to reduce days inventory outstanding to 60 days, and renegotiating payment terms with MAS's own suppliers to extend days payable outstanding to 55 days.

Colette wants to know MAS's current cash conversion cycle, how much cash would be freed up if all three changes were implemented, and whether that freed cash would be enough to substantially pay down the $1,500,000 outstanding on the revolving line, which currently carries interest at prime plus 2 percent, for a total rate of 7.2 percent.

Colette has also noted that MAS's peak seasonal demand occurs each spring, when farmers place large orders ahead of planting season, and that the company's current cash constraints have already forced it to decline early-payment discounts from two key suppliers over the past year because it did not have cash on hand to take advantage of them. She wants the analysis to show clearly whether the proposed changes would give MAS enough breathing room to capture those discounts again next season.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Working Capital Data
exhibits.content:
Annual sales: $18,250,000 ($50,000/day)
Annual cost of goods sold: $13,140,000 ($36,000/day)
Current days sales outstanding (DSO): 58 days
Current days inventory outstanding (DIO): 75 days
Current days payable outstanding (DPO): 40 days
Proposed DSO after tightening credit terms: 45 days
Proposed DIO after inventory planning improvements: 60 days
Proposed DPO after supplier renegotiation: 55 days
Revolving line of credit outstanding: $1,500,000 at prime plus 2 percent (7.2 percent total)

requirements: Calculate MAS's current cash conversion cycle and the current dollar balances tied up in accounts receivable and inventory, and financed by accounts payable.
Calculate the total cash that would be freed up if all three proposed changes are implemented, and recommend how MAS should use that cash, including the resulting annual interest saving.

model_response.understand_issue: MAS is profitable but cash-constrained because of how long cash is tied up in receivables and inventory relative to how long it can delay paying suppliers. The cash conversion cycle quantifies this gap in days, and converting proposed improvements in each component to dollar terms will show Colette how much cash the three initiatives could free up and whether that is enough to meaningfully reduce reliance on the revolving line of credit. The analysis also needs to convert the proposed day-count improvements into dollar terms so Colette can see precisely how much cash each of the three initiatives contributes toward reducing reliance on the revolving line.
model_response.analyze_case:
Current cash conversion cycle (CCC) = DSO + DIO - DPO = 58 + 75 - 40 = 93 days

Current dollar balances:
Accounts receivable = DSO x daily sales = 58 x $50,000 = $2,900,000
Inventory = DIO x daily COGS = 75 x $36,000 = $2,700,000
Accounts payable = DPO x daily COGS = 40 x $36,000 = $1,440,000
Net working capital tied up in the cycle = $2,900,000 + $2,700,000 - $1,440,000 = $4,160,000

Proposed cash conversion cycle = 45 + 60 - 55 = 50 days, a reduction of 93 - 50 = 43 days

Cash freed by each component:
Reduction in accounts receivable = (58 - 45) x $50,000 = 13 x $50,000 = $650,000
Reduction in inventory = (75 - 60) x $36,000 = 15 x $36,000 = $540,000
Increase in accounts payable (a source of cash, since MAS pays later) = (55 - 40) x $36,000 = 15 x $36,000 = $540,000

Total cash freed = $650,000 + $540,000 + $540,000 = $1,730,000

This can be cross-checked using the reduction in the CCC directly: 43 days x approximately $40,233 average daily cash requirement (a blend of the sales-based and COGS-based rates across the three components) gives a comparable order of magnitude, though the component-by-component calculation above is the more precise method since DSO uses the sales rate while DIO and DPO use the COGS rate.

Applying the freed cash to the revolving line: $1,730,000 of freed cash exceeds the $1,500,000 currently drawn, so the full balance could be repaid, with approximately $230,000 remaining for other uses. Annual interest saved from fully repaying the $1,500,000 balance = $1,500,000 x 7.2% = $108,000 per year. It is also useful to note that the accounts payable change is the only one of the three components that generates cash without requiring any reduction in the underlying asset balance, since it simply reflects paying existing obligations later, which is why supplier negotiations often provide a fast, low-execution-risk source of working capital improvement relative to changes in customer or inventory behaviour.
model_response.recommendation:
Colette should proceed with all three initiatives, since together they free up approximately $1,730,000 in cash, which is more than enough to fully repay the $1,500,000 outstanding on the revolving line of credit and save MAS approximately $108,000 per year in interest expense. This also restores nearly the entire $2,500,000 facility as available headroom for future needs, such as a seasonal inventory build ahead of spring planting season, rather than leaving MAS with almost no cushion as it has today.

The three initiatives should be sequenced by risk and speed of implementation.
