Borealis must model the year-end position, renegotiate with the bank before signing, and the board should responsibly defer the undercapitalized U.S. expansion decision until the lease-driven leverage risk is resolved and a proper feasibility study is complete.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly identifies and applies all four ASPE 3065 capital lease indicator tests
Correctly calculates the present value of minimum lease payments using the annuity factor
Correctly concludes the 90% threshold is met and the lease must be capitalized
Quantifies the resulting debt-to-equity ratio and correctly identifies the covenant breach
Identifies that the lease commitment and U.S. expansion should be assessed together, not independently
Recommends deferring U.S. expansion pending a proper feasibility and risk study
Identifies specific unmanaged risks in U.S. expansion (tax, duties, liability, insurance gap)

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Recommends proactive lender communication before signing rather than after a covenant breach is discovered

marking_guide.competency: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
marking_guide.criterion: Does not let the client's preferred conclusion (operating lease) override the correct technical analysis
Presents the covenant impact clearly with supporting numbers the owners can act on

rating_guidance: A CD-level response performs the present value calculation correctly, reaches 91.4%, definitively concludes capital lease classification, quantifies the specific covenant breach with the recalculated ratio, and integrates the lease and expansion issues into one coherent governance recommendation with specific next steps for both the bank and the board. A C-level response gets the classification and covenant impact right but treats the strategy issue more generically. An RC-level response identifies that a present value test is needed but makes a calculation error, or correctly capitalizes the lease but fails to connect it to the covenant number, or discusses U.S. expansion risk without tying it to the lease-driven leverage constraint. An NC-level response accepts the operating lease classification without independently testing it, or ignores the covenant impact entirely.

enabling_focus: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
Adding Value
title: Vantage Robotics Corp. - CNC Equipment Purchase Decision
case_background:
Vantage Robotics Corp. is a Canadian-controlled private corporation in Guelph, Ontario that manufactures precision robotic arm components for the automotive supply chain. The company is evaluating whether to purchase a new computer numerical control (CNC) milling machine to replace an aging, less precise unit. The new machine would cost $600,000, installed and ready for use on January 1 of next year.

Management expects the new machine to generate pretax operating cost savings of $180,000 per year for five years, through reduced scrap rates, lower labour hours per part, and fewer warranty claims from out-of-tolerance components. At the end of five years, the machine is expected to be sold for $50,000. Vantage's after-tax required rate of return on equipment investments of this type is 8%.

The machine qualifies as Class 43 equipment for capital cost allowance purposes, with a declining-balance CCA rate of 30% per year, subject to the usual half-year rule in the year of acquisition. Vantage's combined federal and Ontario corporate tax rate on this type of active business income is 27%. The CNC asset pool is not expected to be terminated at the end of the five years; other Class 43 assets will remain in the pool, so no terminal loss or recapture calculation applies at disposal, only continued CCA on the remaining undepreciated capital cost.

The CFO, Denise Okafor, has asked you to evaluate whether the purchase makes financial sense on a full after-tax, time-value-of-money basis, including the CCA tax shield, and to flag anything about the tax treatment that management should be aware of before the board votes next week. She has also mentioned that a competing proposal exists to instead lease a similar machine for $135,000 per year over five years, and wants to know at a high level how that option would need to be evaluated differently, even though she has not yet obtained final lease terms.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: CNC Machine Purchase Assumptions
exhibits.content:
Installed cost of new machine: $600,000
Expected life of analysis: 5 years
Pretax annual operating cost savings: $180,000, occurring at each year end
Estimated salvage value at end of year 5: $50,000
CCA class: Class 43, declining-balance rate 30% per year
Half-year rule applies in year of acquisition
Asset pool continues after disposal (no terminal loss/recapture in this analysis)
Combined corporate tax rate: 27%
After-tax discount rate: 8%
All cash flows other than the initial cost are assumed to occur at year end.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Present Value Factors at 8%
exhibits.content:
Present value of $1 due in 5 years: 0.6806
Present value of an ordinary annuity of $1 per year for 5 years: 3.9927

These factors are provided for use in the analysis. All amounts should be rounded to the nearest dollar in the final answer.

requirements: Calculate the net present value of the CNC machine purchase, including the present value of the CCA tax shield, and conclude whether Vantage should proceed.
Explain what management needs to know about the tax treatment of the CCA pool, and outline how the lease alternative would need to be evaluated differently.

model_response.understand_issue: Vantage needs a full after-tax NPV analysis of the CNC machine purchase to support a board decision next week. The analysis must combine the after-tax operating savings, the present value of the CCA tax shield on a continuing asset pool, and the salvage value recovered at the end of year five, all discounted at the company's 8% after-tax required return. The CFO also needs a plain explanation of the CCA pool mechanics and a roadmap for comparing this to the lease alternative once final lease terms are available.
model_response.analyze_case:
Step 1: After-tax annual operating savings.
Pretax savings $180,000 x (1 - 0.27) = $131,400 per year.
Present value over 5 years = $131,400 x 3.9927 = $524,592.

Step 2: Present value of CCA tax shield (continuing pool, using the standard formula).
PVCCATS = [C x d x t / (d + k)] x [(1 + 0.5k) / (1 + k)] - [S x d x t / (d + k)] x [1 / (1 + k)^n]
Where C = $600,000, d = 0.30, t = 0.27, k = 0.08, S = $50,000, n = 5.

First bracket: C x d x t = 600,000 x 0.30 x 0.27 = $48,600
48,600 / (0.30 + 0.08) = 48,600 / 0.38 = $127,895
(1 + 0.04) / (1.08) = 1.04 / 1.08 = 0.9630
127,895 x 0.9630 = $123,163

Second bracket (reduction for salvage, since the pool continues): S x d x t = 50,000 x 0.30 x 0.27 = $4,050
4,050 / 0.38 = $10,658
10,658 x 0.6806 (PV factor for year 5) = $7,254

PVCCATS = 123,163 - 7,254 = $115,909

Step 3: Present value of salvage value received.
$50,000 x 0.6806 = $34,030

Step 4: Net present value.
NPV = -Initial cost + PV(after-tax savings) + PVCCATS + PV(salvage)
NPV = -600,000 + 524,592 + 115,909 + 34,030
NPV = $74,531

The investment produces a positive NPV of approximately $74,531, indicating it earns more than the 8% required return and creates value for Vantage after tax. The result is driven mainly by the operating cost savings; the CCA tax shield adds meaningful but secondary value, and the salvage recovery is a smaller positive contributor.
model_response.recommendation:
Recommend the board approve the CNC machine purchase, since the after-tax NPV of approximately $74,500 confirms the investment exceeds Vantage's 8% hurdle rate. Management should sensitivity-test the annual savings estimate, since a meaningful portion of the NPV depends on the $180,000 pretax savings assumption actually being realized; even a 10 to 15% shortfall in savings would materially shrink the NPV.

On the CCA pool, management should understand that because Vantage keeps other Class 43 assets in the same pool, the disposal at $50,000 in year five simply reduces the undepreciated capital cost of the ongoing pool rather than triggering an immediate recapture or terminal loss calculation. This is why the salvage value only reduces future CCA claims (captured in the PVCCATS formula) instead of creating a one-time taxable event. If Vantage were to wind down the entire Class 43 pool at some point, that treatment would change and a terminal loss or recapture could arise, so this assumption should be revisited if circumstances change.

For the lease alternative, Vantage cannot simply compare the $135,000 annual lease payments to the $180,000 purchase-related savings on a pretax basis. The lease payments are fully deductible for tax purposes and would need to be tax-effected at 27% and discounted at 8% the same way, but the analysis would exclude both CCA (since Vantage would not own the asset) and salvage value, and would need to reflect any end-of-lease buyout option, maintenance responsibilities, and off-balance-sheet versus on-balance-sheet accounting treatment. A full lease-versus-buy comparison should only be completed once final lease terms, including any purchase option price, are obtained from the lessor.
model_response.conclusion: The CNC machine purchase generates a positive after-tax NPV of approximately $74,531 at Vantage's 8% required return, driven primarily by after-tax operating savings of $524,592 and a CCA tax shield of $115,909, net of the cost and adjusted for salvage value recovery. The board should approve the purchase, understanding the CCA pool continues without recapture, and should defer the lease comparison until complete lease terms are available for an equivalent after-tax analysis.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly calculates present value of after-tax operating savings using the given annuity factor
Correctly applies the PVCCATS formula including the half-year rule adjustment factor
Correctly calculates the salvage value reduction to the CCA tax shield and the separate PV of salvage cash received
Arrives at a substantively correct NPV and draws a supportable accept/reject conclusion
Identifies the operating savings assumption as the key value driver warranting sensitivity analysis
Correctly explains that a continuing CCA pool avoids immediate recapture or terminal loss on disposal
Correctly identifies Class 43 declining-balance treatment and the half-year rule
Flags that pool termination in future would change the tax treatment
Correctly outlines how the lease alternative must be evaluated differently (after-tax lease payments, no CCA, no salvage)
Provides actionable, quantified guidance the board can use to vote next week rather than a generic discussion

rating_guidance: A CD-level response correctly executes the full PVCCATS formula with the half-year adjustment, ties the salvage reduction and salvage cash flow to separate lines, reaches an NPV close to $74,500, and gives management a precise, correct explanation of pool mechanics plus a concrete framework for the lease comparison. A C-level response gets the NPV substantively right with at most a minor arithmetic slip and correctly explains the pool treatment. An RC-level response applies the CCA tax shield formula incorrectly (for example, omitting the half-year adjustment or the salvage reduction) but otherwise structures the analysis reasonably. An NC-level response ignores the CCA tax shield entirely, compares pretax cash flows only, or fails to discount cash flows at all.

enabling_focus: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
title: Coastal Foods Processing Ltd. - Standard Cost Variances and Inventory Risk
case_background:
Coastal Foods Processing Ltd. is a mid-sized producer of frozen bakery products sold to grocery chains across Atlantic Canada. Your firm is completing the year-end audit for the fiscal year ended June 30. Coastal uses a standard costing system for its flagship frozen dinner roll product line, and the standard direct material cost is set at 2.5 kilograms of flour blend per case at a standard price of $4.00 per kilogram, for a standard material cost of $10.00 per case.

During the year, actual production was 40,000 cases. The production records show actual material usage of 105,000 kilograms of flour blend, purchased and used at an actual average price of $3.80 per kilogram. The plant controller, Wayne Fitch, explains that the company switched flour suppliers mid-year to a lower-cost vendor to save money given rising input costs across the industry, and he is pleased that the material price variance for the year is favourable.

As the senior on the engagement, you are reviewing the variance analysis working papers prepared by the intermediate on the file. You notice that while the price variance is favourable, actual material usage of 105,000 kilograms is well above the 100,000 kilograms that the standard would predict for 40,000 cases (2.5 kg x 40,000). You also note from discussions with the quality assurance manager that customer return rates for out-of-specification product (rolls that failed density or texture testing) rose from 1.2% to 3.1% of shipments in the second half of the year, roughly coinciding with the supplier switch, and that a portion of finished goods inventory on hand at year end may include product made with the new, lower-quality flour blend that has a higher risk of not meeting specifications.

Inventory is a material balance, and finished goods and raw material inventory together represent 22% of total assets. The engagement partner has asked you to work through the variance analysis, explain what it signals about inventory valuation risk, and outline how the audit approach to inventory should respond.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Standard Cost Card and Actual Production Data - Frozen Dinner Roll Line, Year Ended June 30
exhibits.content:
Standard direct material quantity per case: 2.5 kilograms of flour blend
Standard price per kilogram: $4.00
Standard material cost per case: $10.00

Actual production for the year: 40,000 cases
Actual material used: 105,000 kilograms
Actual average price paid: $3.80 per kilogram

Supplier change: Coastal switched from its longstanding flour supplier to a new, lower-priced vendor effective January 1, partway through the fiscal year, as a cost-saving initiative approved by the purchasing department without formal sign-off from quality assurance or production engineering.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Customer Return and Quality Data
exhibits.content:
Out-of-specification return rate, first half of fiscal year (July to December, prior supplier): 1.2% of units shipped
Out-of-specification return rate, second half of fiscal year (January to June, new supplier): 3.1% of units shipped

Quality assurance manager's comments: The new flour blend has a slightly different protein content that appears to affect dough consistency, requiring operators to adjust mixing times, which QA believes is contributing to the higher scrap and return rates. QA has raised concerns internally but a formal specification review of the new supplier has not yet been completed as of the audit date.

Finished goods inventory at June 30 includes approximately $410,000 of product manufactured after January 1 using the new flour blend, out of total finished goods inventory of $1,850,000.

requirements: Calculate and interpret the material price and quantity (usage) variances for the year and explain what they indicate about the supplier change.
Explain the inventory valuation and quality risk this creates, and recommend how the audit procedures over inventory should respond.

model_response.understand_issue: Coastal switched flour suppliers mid-year to save money, and the variance data needs to be analyzed to determine whether that decision actually saved money on a net basis or simply shifted cost from price into higher usage and quality problems. Because a portion of year-end finished goods inventory was produced with the new flour and quality issues have risen, there is a risk that inventory is overstated if some of that stock does not meet specification and requires write-down, and this needs to shape the audit approach.
model_response.analyze_case:
Material price variance = (Actual price - Standard price) x Actual quantity
= ($3.80 - $4.00) x 105,000 kg
= -$0.20 x 105,000
= -$21,000, which is a favourable variance of $21,000 (actual price paid was lower than standard).

Material quantity (usage) variance = (Actual quantity - Standard quantity allowed) x Standard price
Standard quantity allowed for 40,000 cases = 40,000 x 2.5 kg = 100,000 kg
= (105,000 - 100,000) x $4.00
= 5,000 x $4.00
= $20,000 unfavourable

Net material variance = $21,000 favourable - $20,000 unfavourable = $1,000 favourable overall.
Check: Actual cost (105,000 x $3.80 = $399,000) versus standard cost allowed (100,000 x $4.00 = $400,000) = $1,000 favourable, which agrees with the sum of the two variances.

The controller's satisfaction with the favourable price variance is misleading on its own. The company used 5,000 kilograms, or 5%, more flour than the standard allows for the units actually produced, almost entirely offsetting the price saving. This pattern, a favourable price variance largely consumed by an unfavourable usage variance appearing in the same period as a supplier change, is a classic signal of a lower-quality input: more material is being wasted or is failing in-process quality checks (extra mixing, scrap, rework) even though the net dollar variance still shows a small favourable result.

The more significant issue for the audit is not the $1,000 net variance itself, which is immaterial, but what it corroborates: independently, the return rate for out-of-specification product rose from 1.2% to 3.1% of shipments after the supplier switch, and QA has raised unresolved concerns about the new blend's performance. Combined with the usage variance, this indicates a real risk that some of the $410,000 of finished goods produced with the new flour and still on hand at June 30 may not meet specification and could require a lower of cost and net realizable value write-down, or in the worst case, may already be unsaleable. This is a heightened risk of material misstatement related to inventory valuation, not simply a management accounting curiosity, and it was not initially flagged by the intermediate, who only reconciled the variances mechanically without connecting them to the quality data.
model_response.recommendation:
The audit team should treat inventory valuation, specifically the portion of finished goods produced after the supplier switch, as an area of higher assessed risk of material misstatement and expand substantive procedures accordingly, rather than relying on the standard costing system's variances as sufficient evidence that inventory is fairly stated. Specific procedures should include: obtaining and testing post year-end sales and return data for product made with the new flour blend to assess actual net realizable value; inquiring further with QA and reviewing any completed or in-progress specification testing on the new blend; recalculating a sample of the $410,000 of affected finished goods against post year-end selling prices and known additional return rates to assess whether a lower of cost and net realizable value adjustment is required; and evaluating whether management's inventory valuation already reflects this risk or needs adjustment.

The team should also corroborate the standard cost card itself, confirming whether standards were updated to reflect the new flour blend's actual yield characteristics, since if standards were not updated, the usage variance may understate the true extent of the quality-driven waste. Given the return rate more than doubled and QA's concerns are documented but unresolved, this should be discussed with the engagement partner as a potential factor affecting the overall audit risk assessment for the inventory and cost of sales balances, and communicated to those charged with governance if a material adjustment is proposed.
model_response.conclusion: The $21,000 favourable price variance from the new flour supplier is almost entirely offset by a $20,000 unfavourable usage variance, netting to only $1,000 favourable, while out-of-specification returns doubled after the switch, together indicating the lower-priced flour is a lower-quality input creating real inventory valuation risk. The audit should expand substantive testing on the $410,000 of affected finished goods to assess whether a write-down to net realizable value is required, rather than accepting the favourable net variance at face value.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly calculates the material price variance and correctly labels it favourable
Correctly calculates the material quantity (usage) variance and correctly labels it unfavourable
Reconciles both variances to the net actual-versus-standard cost difference
Correctly interprets that the favourable price variance is largely offset by usage, undermining the controller's conclusion
Connects the variance pattern to independent quality/return rate data to identify a specific inventory valuation risk
Identifies the affected $410,000 of finished goods as requiring targeted substantive testing
Recommends specific procedures (post year-end sales/returns testing, NRV recalculation, QA inquiry)
Recommends elevating this to the engagement partner and considering communication to those charged with governance

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Does not accept the mechanical variance reconciliation prepared by the intermediate without professional skepticism

marking_guide.competency: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
marking_guide.criterion: Maintains objectivity despite management's positive framing of the cost-saving initiative

rating_guidance: A CD-level response correctly calculates and reconciles both variances, independently recognizes that the net favourable result masks a quality problem, explicitly links this to the doubled return rate and the specific dollar amount of at-risk inventory, and proposes a targeted, risk-responsive set of substantive procedures. A C-level response calculates the variances correctly and identifies a general inventory risk but is less precise connecting the two data sets or less specific about procedures. An RC-level response calculates the variances but treats them as a standalone management accounting exercise without linking to the audit risk implication, or identifies risk generally without quantifying it. An NC-level response miscalculates the variances, reverses the favourable/unfavourable labels, or fails to see any connection between the cost data and the quality data provided.

enabling_focus: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
title: Nordic Trail Bikes Inc. - Share Buyback and Board Independence
case_background:
Nordic Trail Bikes Inc. is a publicly traded manufacturer of mountain and gravel bicycles headquartered in Squamish, British Columbia. The company's share price has lagged its peer group for two years despite solid operating results, and an activist shareholder holding 6% of outstanding shares has publicly called for the company to return capital to shareholders through a share buyback rather than continuing to build cash reserves.

Nordic's current capital structure consists of $15,000,000 of long-term debt at a pretax interest rate of 6%, and equity with a market value of $35,000,000. The company's beta is 1.2, the current risk-free rate is 3%, and the market risk premium is estimated at 7.5%, giving a cost of equity of 12% under the capital asset pricing model. The corporate tax rate applicable to Nordic is 25%.

Management is proposing a $5,000,000 share buyback, to be funded entirely by new debt issued at a pretax rate of 6.5%, rather than using existing cash, which the CFO wants preserved for a planned expansion of the Squamish manufacturing facility. The board must decide whether to approve the buyback and, if so, how it should be financed.

Separately, the governance committee has flagged that one of Nordic's independent directors, Helen Marsh, is a managing partner at Cascadia Capital Partners, the investment bank that has been engaged to advise on and potentially underwrite the new debt issuance for the buyback. Helen did not disclose this relationship when the board first discussed engaging an advisor, and Cascadia Capital Partners would earn an underwriting fee of approximately $150,000 if the debt-funded buyback proceeds as proposed. Helen has continued to participate in board discussions and voted in favour of engaging Cascadia without disclosing the connection, which was only discovered when the corporate secretary reviewed Cascadia's engagement letter and recognized Helen's name among the firm's partners.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Nordic Trail Bikes Capital Structure and Cost of Capital Inputs
exhibits.content:
Current long-term debt: $15,000,000, pretax interest rate 6%
Current market value of equity: $35,000,000
Beta (levered, at current capital structure): 1.2
Risk-free rate: 3%
Market risk premium: 7.5%
Corporate tax rate: 25%

Proposed transaction: issue $5,000,000 of new debt at a pretax rate of 6.5% to fund a $5,000,000 share buyback. Following the transaction, total debt would be $20,000,000 and the market value of equity is expected to be approximately $30,000,000, reflecting the shares repurchased, with total firm value assumed unchanged at $50,000,000 for purposes of this analysis.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Governance Committee Notes on Director Independence
exhibits.content:
Cascadia Capital Partners was engaged in March to advise the board on capital allocation alternatives, including the potential buyback and its financing. The engagement letter, reviewed by the corporate secretary in June, lists Helen Marsh as a managing partner of Cascadia Capital Partners and indicates the firm would receive a placement/underwriting fee of approximately $150,000 if the proposed $5,000,000 debt issuance proceeds as recommended.

Board meeting minutes from March show Helen Marsh participated in the discussion recommending Cascadia's engagement and voted in favour, with no disclosure of her role at the firm recorded in the minutes. Nordic's director code of conduct requires directors to disclose any material financial interest or relationship with a proposed service provider and to recuse themselves from related discussions and votes.

requirements: Calculate Nordic's current WACC and the expected WACC after the proposed debt-funded buyback, and assess whether the financing decision makes sense from a cost of capital perspective.
Assess the governance issue involving Helen Marsh and recommend how the board should address it.

model_response.understand_issue: The board needs to know how the proposed $5,000,000 debt-funded buyback would change Nordic's weighted average cost of capital, since increased leverage affects both the cost of debt and the cost of equity through higher financial risk, before approving the financing structure. Independently, a serious governance failure has emerged: a director with an undisclosed financial relationship to the advisory firm recommending this same transaction participated in and voted on that engagement, which must be addressed regardless of whether the buyback itself is a sound financial decision.
model_response.analyze_case:
Current WACC:
D = $15,000,000, E = $35,000,000, D+E = $50,000,000
Weight of debt = 15/50 = 30%, weight of equity = 35/50 = 70%
After-tax cost of debt = 6% x (1 - 0.25) = 4.5%
Cost of equity (CAPM) = 3% + 1.2 x 7.5% = 3% + 9% = 12%
WACC = (0.30 x 4.5%) + (0.70 x 12%) = 1.35% + 8.40% = 9.75%

Post-transaction WACC, reflecting increased leverage:
New D = $20,000,000, new E = $30,000,000 (assuming total firm value stays at $50,000,000), new weight of debt = 40%, new weight of equity = 60%
New after-tax cost of debt = 6.5% x (1 - 0.25) = 4.875%

Increased leverage raises the risk borne by equity holders, so the cost of equity should be relevered rather than held at 12%. Using Hamada's equation to unlever, then relever, the beta:
Unlevered beta = Levered beta / [1 + (1 - t)(D/E)] = 1.2 / [1 + 0.75 x (15/35)] = 1.2 / [1 + 0.75 x 0.4286] = 1.2 / 1.3214 = 0.9081
New levered beta = Unlevered beta x [1 + (1 - t)(D/E)] = 0.9081 x [1 + 0.75 x (20/30)] = 0.9081 x [1 + 0.5] = 0.9081 x 1.5 = 1.3622
New cost of equity = 3% + 1.3622 x 7.5% = 3% + 10.22% = 13.22%

New WACC = (0.40 x 4.875%) + (0.60 x 13.22%) = 1.95% + 7.93% = 9.88%

The post-transaction WACC of approximately 9.88% is slightly higher than the current 9.75%. This reflects the standard tradeoff of increasing leverage: the after-tax cost of debt is cheaper than equity, but taking on more debt increases financial risk to shareholders, pushing up the required return on equity enough that overall WACC rises modestly rather than falls. The buyback does not create a cost of capital advantage; if anything it very slightly increases Nordic's cost of capital, so the case for it must rest on other grounds, such as signalling undervaluation to the market or returning excess capital, not on lowering WACC.
model_response.recommendation:
On the financing decision, the board should not approve the debt-funded buyback on the basis that it improves the cost of capital, since the analysis shows WACC rises marginally, from 9.75% to approximately 9.88%. If the board still wishes to proceed with a buyback to respond to the activist shareholder and signal confidence in the share price, it should weigh this modest cost of capital increase and the added financial risk against the CFO's stated need to preserve cash for the Squamius facility expansion, and consider whether a smaller buyback, a mix of cash and debt, or a phased approach better balances these competing priorities. The board should require updated covenant and interest coverage projections at the higher leverage level before any final approval.

On the governance issue, Helen Marsh's failure to disclose her role at Cascadia Capital Partners before participating in and voting on the decision to engage that firm is a clear breach of Nordic's director code of conduct and a serious conflict of interest, made more significant because Cascadia stands to earn a $150,000 fee specifically if the transaction Cascadia itself is recommending proceeds. The board should require Helen to fully disclose the relationship in writing, recuse her retroactively from any further deliberation or vote on the buyback financing, and have the governance committee assess whether the original March engagement decision needs to be revisited or ratified by disinterested directors. The board should also consider obtaining an independent, second financial advisory opinion on the buyback financing given Cascadia's compromised objectivity, and should review whether Helen's continued board service is appropriate given the non-disclosure, not just the underlying conflict.
model_response.conclusion: The proposed debt-funded buyback raises Nordic's WACC modestly from 9.75% to approximately 9.88% due to increased financial risk from higher leverage, so it should not be justified on cost of capital grounds and should be weighed against the competing need for expansion capital. Separately, Helen Marsh's undisclosed financial relationship with Cascadia Capital Partners while voting to engage that firm is a governance breach requiring immediate recusal, retroactive disclosure, an independent second opinion on the financing, and a formal governance committee review.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly calculates current WACC using the given weights and CAPM cost of equity
Correctly identifies that increased leverage requires relevering beta rather than holding cost of equity constant
Correctly applies Hamada's equation (or an equivalent supportable method) to unlever and relever beta
Correctly calculates the post-transaction WACC and draws the correct conclusion that it increases slightly
Recommends the buyback be weighed against the competing cash need for expansion rather than approved on cost-of-capital grounds alone
Identifies Helen Marsh's undisclosed relationship with Cascadia as a material conflict of interest
Recommends retroactive recusal and disclosure consistent with the code of conduct
Recommends an independent second opinion given Cascadia's compromised objectivity
Recommends the governance committee formally review the original engagement decision and Helen's continued suitability as a director

marking_guide.competency: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
marking_guide.criterion: Clearly frames the non-disclosure, not just the conflict itself, as the core ethical breach

rating_guidance: A CD-level response correctly relevers beta to reflect the new capital structure, arrives at a WACC close to 9.88%, correctly concludes leverage increases cost of capital rather than reducing it, and gives the governance issue a full remedy including retroactive recusal, an independent second opinion, and a committee-level review of both the engagement and the director's suitability. A C-level response calculates WACC correctly using a relevered or a stated constant beta with reasonable justification and identifies the core conflict of interest with an appropriate recusal recommendation. An RC-level response calculates WACC using the unchanged 12% cost of equity without addressing releverage, or identifies the conflict of interest without proposing a full remedy. An NC-level response miscalculates WACC, ignores the leverage change entirely, or fails to identify the governance conflict as a disclosure and recusal issue.

enabling_focus: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
Collaborating
title: Harbourview Bakery Co-op - Transfer Pricing Between Related Companies
case_background:
Harbourview Bakery Co-op operates two related Canadian-controlled private corporations under common ownership by the same family group: Harbourview Wholesale Ltd., which produces par-baked dough in a St. John's facility, and Harbourview Retail Ltd., which operates a chain of in-store bakery counters and buys dough exclusively from Harbourview Wholesale. The two companies file separate corporate tax returns and are related persons for purposes of the Income Tax Act.

Harbourview Wholesale has annual production capacity of 500,000 units of dough. It currently sells 350,000 units per year to unrelated third-party grocery chains at a market price of $3.50 per unit, and transfers the remaining volume needed by Harbourview Retail at an internal price of $2.10 per unit, which management set years ago based on Wholesale's variable production cost. Variable cost per unit is $2.10, and fixed manufacturing overhead, if allocated across full capacity of 500,000 units, would add $0.60 per unit.

Harbourview Retail currently purchases 120,000 units per year from Wholesale at the $2.10 internal price. The family's controller, Grace Tobin, wants advice on two related but distinct questions ahead of a family ownership meeting. First, from a pure internal decision-making standpoint, she wants to know whether $2.10 is the right transfer price to use for evaluating Retail's profitability and for capacity planning, given that Wholesale currently has 30,000 units of idle annual capacity (500,000 capacity less 350,000 external sales less 120,000 internal transfers). Second, and separately, the family's tax advisor has raised a concern that CRA could challenge the $2.10 transfer price under the related-party rules in the Income Tax Act if the companies are ever audited, since it is well below the $3.50 arm's length market price Wholesale charges outside customers, and Grace wants to understand why that concern exists even though the $2.10 price may be defensible for internal management purposes.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Harbourview Wholesale Production and Pricing Data
exhibits.content:
Annual production capacity: 500,000 units
Current external sales to unrelated grocery chains: 350,000 units at $3.50 per unit (arm's length market price)
Current internal transfers to Harbourview Retail: 120,000 units at $2.10 per unit
Idle capacity: 500,000 - 350,000 - 120,000 = 30,000 units per year

Variable production cost per unit: $2.10
Fixed manufacturing overhead: $300,000 per year total, or $0.60 per unit if allocated across full 500,000-unit capacity
Full absorption cost per unit at full capacity: $2.70

Harbourview Wholesale and Harbourview Retail are both wholly owned, directly or indirectly, by the same family trust and are related persons within the meaning of the Income Tax Act.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Tax Advisor's Memo Excerpt
exhibits.content: Because Harbourview Wholesale and Harbourview Retail are related persons, section 69 of the Income Tax Act deems transactions between them to occur at fair market value for tax purposes regardless of the price actually invoiced. If CRA audits the group and concludes the arm's length price for dough transferred between the companies is $3.50, the price charged to unrelated customers for the same product, CRA could reassess Harbourview Wholesale's income upward to reflect revenue at $3.50 per unit on the 120,000 units transferred internally, increasing Wholesale's taxable income, while Retail's cost base for those purchases would not automatically increase without a corresponding adjustment, creating a real risk of economic double taxation within the family group if the reassessment is not fully offset. Proper transfer pricing documentation supporting any internal price other than $3.50 has not been prepared.

requirements: Determine the transfer price Harbourview Wholesale should use for internal management decision-making purposes, given its current idle capacity, and explain the reasoning.
Explain the tax risk created by using that same price for related-party transactions and recommend how the family should address the gap between management and tax pricing.

model_response.understand_issue: Grace needs two distinct answers that should not be confused with each other: the transfer price that produces the best internal decision for the Harbourview group as a whole, based on Wholesale's cost structure and available capacity, and separately the price the Income Tax Act requires to be used for the two related corporations' tax filings, since related-party transactions are deemed to occur at fair market value under section 69 regardless of what price management chooses to use internally.
model_response.analyze_case:
For internal management decision-making, the general transfer pricing rule is that the minimum acceptable transfer price equals the selling division's variable cost per unit plus any opportunity cost of lost outside sales from making the transfer. Wholesale has 30,000 units of idle capacity per year (500,000 capacity - 350,000 external sales - 120,000 current internal transfers = 30,000 idle), meaning Wholesale is not currently at full capacity and is not displacing any external sale by supplying Retail with its existing 120,000-unit order.

Minimum transfer price = Variable cost + Opportunity cost per unit
= $2.10 + $0 (since no external sale is displaced while idle capacity exists)
= $2.10

This confirms that $2.10, the current internal price, is in fact the economically correct transfer price for management decision-making purposes as long as Retail's purchases stay within Wholesale's idle capacity. If Retail's demand grew and pushed total volume (external plus internal) above 500,000 units, Wholesale would then be displacing a $3.50 external sale to supply an additional internal unit, and the opportunity cost would rise to $3.50 - $2.10 = $1.40 per unit, making the correct minimum transfer price $2.10 + $1.40 = $3.50, the full market price, once capacity is exhausted. Grace should treat $2.10 as correct only up to the 30,000-unit idle capacity ceiling and should recalculate if internal volume grows toward or past that limit.

The tax picture is entirely separate. Section 69 does not ask what price maximizes internal decision-making value; it deems the transaction to occur at fair market value for both companies' tax returns because they are related persons, and the best evidence of fair market value here is the $3.50 price Wholesale charges arm's length customers for the identical product. Continuing to invoice internally at $2.10 while an established $3.50 comparable market price exists creates exactly the exposure the tax advisor describes: on audit, CRA can reassess Wholesale's income upward to $3.50 per unit on the 120,000 transferred units (potential income adjustment of ($3.50 - $2.10) x 120,000 = $168,000 per year), and without a matching downward adjustment to Retail's cost base, the same $168,000 of value could be taxed twice within the family group, once in Wholesale's reassessed income and again economically when Retail's inflated cost of sales understates its true margin.
model_response.recommendation:
For internal decision-making and capacity planning, Grace should continue to use $2.10 as the relevant transfer price only while Wholesale has idle capacity, and should build a trigger into the family's planning process to recalculate the price to $3.50 once combined internal and external volume approaches the 500,000-unit capacity ceiling, since below-capacity and at-capacity transfer pricing are genuinely different answers to the same question.

For tax purposes, the family should not rely on the $2.10 management price on the corporate tax filings. The group should engage its tax advisor to prepare a related-party pricing policy and supporting contemporaneous documentation, as expected under the Income Tax Act's related-party rules, that either invoices internal transfers at $3.50 to match the established comparable market price, or clearly documents a supportable arm's length rationale if a lower price is used, such as evidence that internal transfers are large-volume, no-selling-cost transactions that command a legitimate discount from the retail-facing $3.50 price. If moving to $3.50 for invoicing purposes would distort Retail's own internal profitability reporting, Grace can maintain a separate $2.10 shadow price for internal management reporting and capacity decisions while the actual intercompany invoices and tax filings use the arm's length-supportable price, which is a common and acceptable practice as long as the two are not conflated. The family should not wait for a CRA audit to address this, since the current $168,000 per year pricing gap is a recurring, cumulative exposure, not a one-time issue.
model_response.conclusion: For internal decision-making, $2.10 is the correct transfer price while Wholesale has idle capacity, since no external sale is displaced, but this reasoning has no bearing on the companies' tax obligations. Section 69 deems related-party transactions to occur at fair market value, likely $3.50 based on Wholesale's own arm's length sales, so continuing to invoice internally at $2.10 exposes the group to a reassessment of roughly $168,000 per year and possible double taxation, and the family should adopt a documented arm's length-supportable invoicing policy separate from its internal management price.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly applies the minimum transfer price rule (variable cost plus opportunity cost)
Correctly identifies Wholesale has idle capacity and therefore zero opportunity cost at current volumes
Correctly concludes $2.10 is the appropriate transfer price for internal decisions at current volume
Identifies the capacity threshold (30,000 idle units) at which the correct transfer price would change to $3.50
Correctly identifies section 69 deems related-party transactions to occur at fair market value
Correctly identifies $3.50 as the best evidence of fair market value given the existing arm's length comparable
Quantifies the potential reassessment exposure on the price gap
Identifies the double taxation risk if a matching adjustment to Retail's cost base is not made
Recommends documented arm's length pricing policy separate from the internal management price

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Clearly distinguishes the management accounting answer from the tax answer rather than conflating the two

rating_guidance: A CD-level response correctly derives $2.10 as the management transfer price using the opportunity cost framework, explicitly identifies the idle capacity ceiling at which the correct price would change, correctly applies section 69 to identify the fair market value exposure, quantifies the dollar reassessment risk, and recommends a specific dual-price solution addressing both needs. A C-level response gets both the management price and the tax exposure right without full precision on the capacity threshold or the exact dollar quantification. An RC-level response identifies one side of the issue well (either the transfer pricing mechanics or the tax exposure) but does not integrate the two, or conflates the correct management price with what should be used for tax filing. An NC-level response recommends $3.50 or full absorption cost as the internal transfer price without recognizing idle capacity, or fails to identify any related-party tax risk.

enabling_focus: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
title: Summit Mining Exploration Ltd. - Impairment of Mine Development Assets
case_background:
Summit Mining Exploration Ltd. is a publicly traded, IFRS reporting junior mining company that owns a single producing gold mine, the Blackfin site, in northern Manitoba. The mine has struggled with lower than expected ore grades over the past 18 months, and the company's share price has fallen 60% over the same period. Your firm is the external auditor and is completing the audit for the fiscal year ended December 31.

The Blackfin mine assets represent a single cash generating unit (CGU) under IAS 36 and are carried on the balance sheet at $18,500,000. Management has prepared a five-year cash flow forecast supporting its conclusion that no impairment is required, based on gold price assumptions and production estimates from the mine's operating team. Separately, an unsolicited offer was received in November from a competing mining company to purchase the Blackfin assets outright for $11,000,000, net of estimated disposal costs, which management has characterized internally as opportunistic and below what they believe the assets are worth, and has not seriously entertained.

As the audit senior, you have been asked to test management's impairment analysis. Management's five-year cash flow forecast projects the following pretax operating cash flows: Year 1, $3,200,000; Year 2, $3,400,000; Year 3, $3,600,000; Year 4, $3,100,000; Year 5, $2,800,000, declining in the later years as the ore body is expected to deplete. Management has used a discount rate of 10%, which you have separately corroborated as reasonable given Summit's risk profile and the risk-free rate, project-specific risk premium, and comparable mining company data reviewed by your valuation specialist.

Given the declining production forecast, the recent unsolicited sale offer well below carrying value, and the sustained share price decline, the engagement partner has also asked you to consider whether these facts raise a going concern question that needs to be evaluated separately from the impairment calculation itself.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Blackfin Mine Cash Flow Forecast and Carrying Value
exhibits.content:
Carrying amount of Blackfin mine CGU at December 31: $18,500,000

Management's forecast pretax operating cash flows (value in use basis):
Year 1: $3,200,000
Year 2: $3,400,000
Year 3: $3,600,000
Year 4: $3,100,000
Year 5: $2,800,000

Discount rate used by management, corroborated by the audit valuation specialist as reasonable: 10%

Unsolicited third-party offer received in November to purchase the Blackfin assets: $11,000,000, net of estimated costs of disposal. Management has not accepted or seriously pursued this offer, believing it undervalues the asset.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Additional Financial and Market Information
exhibits.content:
Summit's share price has declined from $2.40 to $0.96 over the trailing 18 months, a 60% decline, against a broader gold mining sector index decline of 18% over the same period.

Summit has a $4,000,000 operating line of credit that matures in 8 months and is subject to renewal at the lender's discretion based on the borrowing base, which is tied in part to the carrying value of mine assets. Management's cash flow forecast assumes the line is renewed on similar terms; no written renewal commitment has yet been obtained from the lender.

Ore grade results from the two most recent quarterly drill programs came in 15% and 22% below the geological model used to build the current five-year forecast, a trend management attributes to normal short-term variability rather than a change to the long-term model.

requirements: Calculate the recoverable amount of the Blackfin CGU under IAS 36 and determine whether an impairment loss must be recognized, and if so, in what amount.
Assess whether the facts in the case raise a going concern question separate from the impairment issue, and describe how the audit should respond to both matters.

model_response.understand_issue: Under IAS 36, Summit must test the Blackfin CGU for impairment and record a loss if its carrying amount exceeds its recoverable amount, which is the higher of fair value less costs of disposal and value in use, and the value in use forecast needs to be discounted and compared against both the carrying value and the unsolicited sale offer. Separately, the combination of a below-carrying-value sale offer, a severe share price decline exceeding the sector, worsening drill results, and an unconfirmed credit line renewal raises questions about whether Summit can continue as a going concern that must be evaluated independently of the impairment calculation, even though the two issues share underlying facts.
model_response.analyze_case:
Value in use calculation, discounting management's forecast pretax cash flows at 10%:
Year 1: $3,200,000 / 1.10 = $2,909,091
Year 2: $3,400,000 / 1.21 = $2,809,917
Year 3: $3,600,000 / 1.331 = $2,704,733
Year 4: $3,100,000 / 1.4641 = $2,117,206
Year 5: $2,800,000 / 1.61051 = $1,738,616
Total value in use = $2,909,091 + $2,809,917 + $2,704,733 + $2,117,206 + $1,738,616 = $12,279,563

Fair value less costs of disposal, based on the unsolicited offer: $11,000,000 (already stated net of disposal costs).

Recoverable amount = higher of value in use ($12,279,563) and fair value less costs of disposal ($11,000,000) = $12,279,563.

Carrying amount ($18,500,000) exceeds recoverable amount ($12,279,563), so an impairment loss must be recognized under IAS 36.
Impairment loss = $18,500,000 - $12,279,563 = $6,220,437

This is a material impairment representing approximately 34% of the CGU's carrying value and should be recorded as an expense in the current year's financial statements, with the CGU written down to $12,279,563.

On going concern, the facts extend well beyond the impairment issue itself. The value in use forecast, even before impairment, already reflects declining cash flows toward the end of the five-year period, consistent with a depleting mine. The two most recent drill programs came in materially below the geological model underlying that same forecast (15% and 22% below), which raises doubt about whether management's cash flow assumptions, and therefore the value in use just calculated, are themselves overstated; if drill results continue to trend below model, next year's impairment could be larger still. The 60% share price decline, well in excess of the 18% sector decline, signals the market may already be pricing in risks beyond what management's forecast reflects. Most significantly, the $4,000,000 operating line matures in 8 months, is discretionary for the lender, is tied to a borrowing base linked to mine asset carrying value that is about to fall by over $6,200,000, and has no written renewal commitment, creating real uncertainty about Summit's ability to refinance a facility it may depend on for working capital within the next twelve months.
model_response.recommendation:
The audit team should require management to record the $6,220,437 impairment loss, since the carrying amount is not supported by either the value in use calculation or the unsolicited offer, both of which the team has corroborated (the discount rate through the valuation specialist, and the offer through inspection of the third-party correspondence). If management resists recording the loss, this should be escalated to the engagement partner as a potential disagreement requiring resolution before the audit opinion can be finalized.

Separately, the team should require management to perform and document a formal going concern assessment covering at least twelve months from the date the financial statements are authorized for issue, specifically addressing the credit line renewal risk, the deteriorating drill results, and the impact of the impairment on the borrowing base. The team should request written correspondence or a term sheet from the lender regarding renewal intentions rather than relying on management's assumption, and should evaluate whether the combination of factors constitutes a material uncertainty requiring disclosure under IAS 1, even if management ultimately concludes the going concern basis remains appropriate. If a material uncertainty exists and is properly disclosed, the audit report should include an emphasis of matter or equivalent paragraph in accordance with the applicable auditing standard; if management's going concern assumption itself is not supportable, this would have far more pervasive implications for the financial statements and the audit opinion.
model_response.conclusion: The Blackfin CGU's recoverable amount of $12,279,563, based on value in use exceeding the fair value less costs of disposal offer of $11,000,000, is below the $18,500,000 carrying value, requiring an impairment loss of $6,220,437. The deteriorating drill results, disproportionate share price decline, and uncertain credit line renewal tied to the now-lower asset base together raise a genuine going concern question that must be separately assessed, documented by management, and, if a material uncertainty exists, disclosed and reflected in the audit report.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly discounts the five-year forecast cash flows to calculate value in use
Correctly identifies recoverable amount as the higher of value in use and fair value less costs of disposal
Correctly calculates the impairment loss as the excess of carrying amount over recoverable amount
Correctly concludes the offer of $11,000,000 does not represent recoverable amount since value in use is higher
Identifies going concern as a distinct issue from impairment requiring separate assessment
Links the credit line renewal risk, borrowing base, and impairment impact into the going concern analysis
Identifies the deteriorating drill results as a risk to the reliability of management's own forecast
Recommends obtaining external corroboration of the lender's renewal intentions rather than accepting management's assumption
Discusses appropriate reporting implications if a material uncertainty related to going concern exists

marking_guide.competency: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
marking_guide.criterion: Maintains professional skepticism toward management's dismissal of the unsolicited offer and forecast assumptions

rating_guidance: A CD-level response correctly calculates value in use, correctly identifies it (not the lower offer) as recoverable amount, arrives at the $6,220,437 impairment figure, and separately builds a well-supported going concern analysis tying together the credit line, borrowing base, and drill result trends with a specific recommendation for external corroboration and appropriate reporting. A C-level response gets the impairment calculation right and identifies a going concern concern generally without fully integrating the specific facts. An RC-level response makes a calculation error in the value in use figure or confuses fair value less costs of disposal with recoverable amount, or raises going concern only superficially. An NC-level response fails to identify any impairment despite the clear indicators, or fails to recognize a going concern question at all.

enabling_focus: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
title: Pinecrest Senior Living Inc. - Acquisition Financing and a Related-Party Vote
case_background:
Pinecrest Senior Living Inc. is a publicly traded operator of retirement residences in Alberta and Saskatchewan. The board is considering the acquisition of Willowbrook Retirement Communities, a privately held operator of three residences in Regina, for a purchase price of $12,000,000. Pinecrest's current net income is $2,400,000 on 1,000,000 shares outstanding, an EPS of $2.40, and management projects the acquisition will add $1,500,000 of pretax income annually once integrated.

Two financing options are on the table. Under the debt option, Pinecrest would borrow the full $12,000,000 at a pretax interest rate of 7%. Under the equity option, Pinecrest would issue new shares sufficient to raise $12,000,000, which management estimates would dilute existing shareholders by 15%. Pinecrest's combined corporate tax rate is 26%. The board has asked for an EPS impact analysis of both options to help inform its decision, alongside the usual considerations of leverage, financial flexibility, and risk.

Separately, one of Pinecrest's independent directors, Robert Kessler, is the brother-in-law of Willowbrook's majority owner, Diane Marchetti, a relationship that has never been disclosed to the board or reflected in Pinecrest's related-party disclosures. Robert has participated in every board discussion of the Willowbrook acquisition, including reviewing and commenting on the valuation range and due diligence findings, and is expected to vote when the matter comes to a formal board resolution next month. The corporate secretary became aware of the family relationship only through a casual conversation at a company social event and has raised it informally with the CFO, who is uncertain whether it needs to be reported anywhere or whether it is simply a personal matter unrelated to Robert's professional judgment.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Pinecrest Financial Data and Financing Options
exhibits.content:
Current net income: $2,400,000
Current shares outstanding: 1,000,000
Current EPS: $2.40

Willowbrook acquisition price: $12,000,000
Expected additional pretax income from Willowbrook once integrated: $1,500,000 annually
Combined corporate tax rate: 26%

Debt financing option: borrow $12,000,000 at 7% pretax interest, no change in shares outstanding.
Equity financing option: issue new shares to raise $12,000,000, estimated to dilute existing shareholders by 15%, meaning new total shares outstanding equal current shares divided by (1 - 15%).

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Corporate Secretary's Notes on Director Relationship
exhibits.content:
Robert Kessler has served as an independent director of Pinecrest for four years and sits on the acquisitions committee. Diane Marchetti, majority owner and CEO of Willowbrook Retirement Communities, is married to Robert's brother. This relationship has not been disclosed in Pinecrest's management information circular or director questionnaires, which ask directors to identify any family relationships with parties to a proposed material transaction.

Robert has attended and actively participated in four board and committee discussions of the Willowbrook acquisition since due diligence began, including a session where the acquisitions committee debated whether the $12,000,000 purchase price was appropriately supported by Willowbrook's financial projections, projections that Diane Marchetti's team prepared and presented.

requirements: Calculate the pro forma EPS under both the debt and equity financing options and recommend which financing structure the board should pursue, considering both EPS impact and risk.
Assess the governance issue involving Robert Kessler and recommend how the board should address it before the acquisition vote.

model_response.understand_issue: The board needs a clear comparison of how debt versus equity financing for the Willowbrook acquisition would each affect Pinecrest's earnings per share, since this is a key input to the financing decision alongside leverage and risk considerations. Separately, and more urgently, a director with an undisclosed family relationship to the seller has been actively participating in the acquisition's due diligence and valuation discussions, which is a related-party governance issue that must be resolved before the board can validly vote on the transaction, regardless of which financing option is chosen.
model_response.analyze_case:
Debt financing option:
Additional pretax interest expense = $12,000,000 x 7% = $840,000
After-tax interest cost = $840,000 x (1 - 0.26) = $621,600
After-tax contribution from Willowbrook = $1,500,000 x (1 - 0.26) = $1,110,000
Net addition to income = $1,110,000 - $621,600 = $488,400
Pro forma net income = $2,400,000 + $488,400 = $2,888,400
Shares outstanding remain at 1,000,000
Pro forma EPS (debt) = $2,888,400 / 1,000,000 = $2.89

Equity financing option:
No new interest expense.
After-tax contribution from Willowbrook = $1,500,000 x (1 - 0.26) = $1,110,000
Pro forma net income = $2,400,000 + $1,110,000 = $3,510,000
New shares outstanding, reflecting 15% dilution = 1,000,000 / (1 - 0.15) = 1,000,000 / 0.85 = 1,176,471 shares
Pro forma EPS (equity) = $3,510,000 / 1,176,471 = $2.98

On a pure EPS basis, the equity option produces a slightly higher pro forma EPS ($2.98) than the debt option ($2.89), because the after-tax cost of the new debt ($621,600) exceeds the earnings dilution effect of issuing new shares at this particular scale. This is a less common result than the usual assumption that debt is more EPS-accretive, and it occurs here because the acquisition's return does not comfortably exceed the after-tax cost of debt once combined with Pinecrest's existing leverage profile.

However, EPS accretion is not the only consideration. The debt option increases Pinecrest's leverage and fixed financing obligations, raising financial risk and potentially constraining future borrowing capacity or covenant headroom, while the equity option preserves balance sheet flexibility at the cost of permanent ownership dilution to existing shareholders. The board needs to weigh the modest EPS advantage of the equity option against Pinecrest's current leverage level, its plans for further growth or acquisitions, and shareholders' likely reaction to dilution, none of which can be resolved by the EPS calculation alone.
model_response.recommendation:
Based on the EPS analysis, the equity financing option is marginally more accretive and avoids increasing Pinecrest's fixed debt obligations, and should be the board's preferred option unless there are offsetting considerations, such as an already depressed share price that would make issuing equity unusually costly to existing shareholders, or a strategic preference to preserve equity capacity for future opportunities. The board should also stress test the debt option's interest coverage impact given Pinecrest's existing debt load, since the $488,400 net income improvement under the debt scenario would shrink or disappear if actual Willowbrook earnings fall short of the $1,500,000 projection.

On the governance issue, the board cannot proceed to a valid vote on the Willowbrook acquisition while Robert Kessler's undisclosed family relationship to Diane Marchetti remains unaddressed. Robert should be required to formally disclose the relationship immediately, in writing, consistent with Pinecrest's director questionnaire obligations, and should be recused from all further acquisitions committee and board deliberation and voting on the Willowbrook transaction. Given that Robert has already participated in due diligence discussions, including debating the adequacy of the purchase price supported by projections prepared by Diane's own team, the board should have those specific discussions and the resulting valuation conclusions independently reviewed by disinterested directors or an external advisor to confirm Robert's participation did not improperly influence the outcome. The board should also update its related-party disclosure and director questionnaire processes going forward and consider whether this omission needs to be addressed in Pinecrest's public disclosure obligations as a publicly traded company.
model_response.conclusion: The equity financing option produces a modestly higher pro forma EPS of $2.98 versus $2.89 under debt financing, but the board's final choice should also weigh leverage capacity and dilution, not EPS alone. Robert Kessler's undisclosed brother-in-law relationship with Willowbrook's owner is a serious related-party governance breach requiring immediate disclosure, retroactive recusal, and independent review of the valuation work he already influenced before the board can validly vote on the acquisition.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly calculates pro forma net income and EPS under the debt financing option
Correctly calculates the diluted share count and pro forma EPS under the equity financing option
Correctly concludes equity is marginally more EPS-accretive than debt in this scenario
Discusses risk and leverage considerations beyond EPS to inform the final financing recommendation
Identifies Robert Kessler's undisclosed family relationship as a material related-party conflict of interest
Recommends immediate written disclosure and retroactive recusal from all further deliberation and voting
Recommends independent review of valuation discussions Robert already influenced
Identifies the public disclosure and questionnaire process implications for a publicly traded company

marking_guide.competency: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
marking_guide.criterion: Recognizes the board cannot validly proceed to a vote until the conflict is resolved
Presents both the EPS comparison and the governance remedy in a clear, board-ready format

rating_guidance: A CD-level response correctly computes both EPS scenarios including the precise diluted share count, correctly identifies equity as marginally more accretive while still weighing leverage risk, and gives the governance issue a complete remedy including disclosure, recusal, and independent re-review of the tainted valuation discussions. A C-level response gets both EPS figures right and correctly identifies the core conflict of interest with a basic recusal recommendation. An RC-level response makes an error in the diluted share calculation or the after-tax interest calculation, or identifies the conflict without addressing the need to review Robert's prior influence on the valuation work. An NC-level response fails to tax-effect the interest expense or Willowbrook's income, produces materially wrong EPS figures, or does not recognize the related-party relationship as requiring any board action.

enabling_focus: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
Adding Value
title: Silverline Consulting Group - Incorporation Decision and a New Service Line
case_background:
Silverline Consulting Group is currently operated by Maya Alberts as an unincorporated sole proprietorship providing organizational change management consulting to mid-sized companies across Alberta. In the most recently completed fiscal year, the business generated net income before tax of $150,000, all of which was reported on Maya's personal tax return as business income and taxed at her marginal personal rates, which reach approximately 47% combined federal and provincial tax on income in this range.

Maya's accountant has suggested she consider incorporating Silverline as a Canadian-controlled private corporation to take advantage of the small business tax rate. If incorporated, the first $500,000 of active business income earned by the corporation each year would be taxed at a combined federal and provincial small business rate of 12.2%, well below her personal marginal rate, with additional personal tax only arising when funds are withdrawn from the corporation as salary or dividends. Maya does not currently need to withdraw all of the business's profit for personal living expenses and would like to leave a substantial portion inside the company to reinvest in growing the practice.

Separately, Maya is evaluating whether to launch a new fixed-fee advisory retainer service line, offering ongoing monthly strategic advisory packages to clients rather than one-off project engagements. She estimates the new service line would require additional fixed costs of $84,000 per year, covering a part-time associate consultant and additional software licensing, and each retainer client would generate revenue of $2,500 per year with variable delivery costs (associate time, travel, materials) of $750 per client, for a contribution margin of $1,750 per client. Maya wants to know how many retainer clients she would need to sign to break even on the new service line, and how many she would need to generate a target contribution of $126,000 toward covering Silverline's existing overhead and profit goals.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Incorporation Comparison Data
exhibits.content:
Current year net business income (unincorporated, before personal tax): $150,000
Maya's approximate combined federal and provincial personal marginal tax rate at this income level: 47%

If incorporated:
Combined federal and provincial small business tax rate on active business income up to $500,000: 12.2%
Corporation would retain after-tax earnings for reinvestment rather than distributing the full amount to Maya immediately.
Additional personal tax would apply only when funds are withdrawn as salary (taxed as employment income, deductible to the corporation) or dividends (taxed as dividend income with the dividend tax credit, not deductible to the corporation).

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Proposed Retainer Service Line Cost Data
exhibits.content:
Additional fixed costs of the new retainer service line: $84,000 per year (part-time associate consultant salary and software licensing)
Annual retainer fee revenue per client: $2,500
Variable delivery cost per client per year (associate time, travel, materials): $750
Contribution margin per client per year: $2,500 - $750 = $1,750

Maya's target: cover the $84,000 of new fixed costs and contribute an additional $126,000 toward Silverline's existing overhead and personal profit goals for the year, for a total required contribution of $210,000.

requirements: Explain and quantify the tax deferral advantage available if Maya incorporates Silverline and retains earnings in the corporation, and identify what she should be aware of before proceeding.
Calculate the breakeven number of retainer clients and the number needed to achieve Maya's target contribution, and advise on the retainer service line decision.

model_response.understand_issue: Maya needs to understand the tax deferral advantage of incorporating Silverline and retaining earnings inside the corporation rather than continuing to be taxed personally on all business income at her marginal rate, along with what she needs to know before proceeding, such as the eventual personal tax on withdrawals. Separately, she needs a cost-volume-profit analysis of the proposed retainer service line to determine how many clients are needed to break even and to reach her target contribution, to decide whether the new service line is a realistic addition to the practice.
model_response.analyze_case:
Tax deferral advantage of incorporation:
As a sole proprietor, the full $150,000 of net business income is taxed personally in the year earned at approximately 47%, regardless of whether Maya withdraws the cash for personal use or leaves it in the business bank account, because there is no legal separation between Maya and the business.
Personal tax as sole proprietor = $150,000 x 47% = $70,500
After-tax funds available (whether spent or reinvested) = $150,000 - $70,500 = $79,500

If incorporated, and assuming Maya retains the full $150,000 of active business income inside the corporation rather than withdrawing it (since she stated she does not currently need it all for personal living expenses):
Corporate tax at the small business rate = $150,000 x 12.2% = $18,300
After-tax funds retained in the corporation for reinvestment = $150,000 - $18,300 = $131,700

The difference, $131,700 versus $79,500, is $52,200 of additional after-tax capital available inside the corporation this year to reinvest in growing the practice, such as funding the new retainer service line's fixed costs. This is a tax deferral, not a permanent tax saving: when Maya eventually withdraws these retained funds as salary or dividends, personal tax will apply at that time (on salary, deductible to the corporation but taxed personally as employment income; on dividends, not deductible to the corporation but eligible for the dividend tax credit reflecting the corporate tax already paid, under the integration system). The advantage is timing, having significantly more after-tax cash available now to reinvest and grow the business, compounding returns on that larger base, rather than a permanent reduction in total lifetime tax.

Retainer service line cost-volume-profit analysis:
Breakeven clients = Fixed costs / Contribution margin per client = $84,000 / $1,750 = 48 clients

Clients needed for target contribution of $126,000 above fixed costs (total required contribution of $84,000 + $126,000 = $210,000):
$210,000 / $1,750 = 120 clients

Maya would need to sign 48 retainer clients just to cover the new fixed costs of the service line, and 120 clients to hit her stated target contribution toward existing overhead and profit goals. She needs to realistically assess whether her practice's current client base and sales capacity can support signing 120 ongoing retainer relationships, which is a substantially different commitment level than one-off project work.
model_response.recommendation:
Maya should proceed with incorporating Silverline, since retaining earnings inside the corporation at the 12.2% small business rate frees up approximately $52,200 of additional after-tax capital this year alone compared to remaining unincorporated, capital that can help fund the new retainer service line's fixed costs without drawing down personal savings or debt financing. She should discuss with her accountant an appropriate salary versus dividend mix for whatever amount she does need to withdraw personally each year, to optimize the integration between corporate and personal tax over time, and should confirm Silverline's consulting activities qualify as active business income eligible for the small business deduction rather than being characterized as a personal services business, which would eliminate the small business rate advantage and deny normal business expense deductions.

On the retainer service line, Maya should treat 48 clients as the minimum viable scale before the new offering breaks even, and 120 clients as the scale needed to make a meaningful contribution to her broader profit goals. Given that 120 recurring retainer relationships likely represents a significant expansion from her current project-based client volume, she should build a realistic client acquisition timeline, potentially starting with a lower initial target such as 48 to 60 clients to validate demand and delivery capacity with the part-time associate before scaling further, and should reassess the fixed cost structure if she needs to add a second associate to service a client base approaching 120.
model_response.conclusion: Incorporating Silverline and retaining the $150,000 of income inside the corporation at the 12.2% small business rate, rather than being taxed personally at 47%, frees up approximately $52,200 of additional after-tax capital this year as a tax deferral, not a permanent saving, since personal tax will apply on eventual withdrawals. The retainer service line requires 48 clients to break even and 120 clients to reach Maya's target contribution, and she should validate demand at a smaller scale before committing to the full 120-client target.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly calculates personal tax and after-tax cash as a sole proprietor at 47%
Correctly calculates corporate tax and after-tax retained earnings at the 12.2% small business rate
Correctly quantifies the incremental after-tax capital advantage of incorporating ($52,200)
Correctly characterizes the benefit as a tax deferral, not a permanent saving, and explains eventual personal tax on withdrawal
Flags the personal services business risk that could deny the small business deduction
Correctly calculates the contribution margin per client
Correctly calculates breakeven clients at 48
Correctly calculates clients needed for the $126,000 target contribution at 120
Provides practical advice on realistic scaling given the gap between current volume and the 120-client target
Connects the incorporation tax savings to funding the new service line, integrating the two parts of the case

rating_guidance: A CD-level response correctly quantifies both the sole proprietor and corporate after-tax positions, explicitly frames the benefit as a deferral with a clear explanation of eventual personal tax on withdrawal, flags the personal services business risk, and correctly calculates both the breakeven and target-contribution client counts with practical scaling advice. A C-level response gets both quantitative pieces right (the $52,200 tax deferral figure and the 48/120 client figures) without full discussion of the deferral nuance or PSB risk. An RC-level response calculates one side correctly but makes an error on the other, such as treating the tax saving as permanent rather than a deferral, or miscalculating the required contribution margin target. An NC-level response fails to compute the after-tax comparison correctly or fails to use contribution margin correctly in the breakeven calculation.

enabling_focus: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
title: Cascade Retail Group - Bill-and-Hold Revenue Recognition
case_background:
Cascade Retail Group is a private, IFRS reporting furniture wholesaler based in Winnipeg that supplies hotel chains and commercial property developers. Your firm is completing the year-end audit for the fiscal year ended October 31.
