During final testing of revenue, you identify a single large transaction with Meridian Hospitality Group, a hotel chain customer, involving $2,400,000 of custom lobby and guest room furniture.

Meridian placed the order in August, and Cascade manufactured and completed the furniture by early October. However, Meridian's new hotel property, where the furniture will ultimately be installed, is still under construction and will not be ready to receive delivery until approximately January of next year. At Meridian's request, Cascade has stored the completed furniture in its own Winnipeg warehouse, in the same general storage area used for other customers' in-process and finished goods inventory, without any special segregation, labelling, or physical separation identifying it as sold to Meridian.

Cascade's controller, invoiced Meridian for the full $2,400,000 in October and recorded the full amount as revenue in the current fiscal year, on the basis that Meridian has paid a substantial deposit, has legal title to the specific furniture under the sales contract, and has confirmed in writing that it wants and needs Cascade to hold the goods until its property is ready. The controller has told you this is a straightforward bill-and-hold sale and that revenue recognition is appropriate because Cascade has fulfilled its manufacturing obligation and Meridian has committed to the purchase.

As the audit senior, you have been asked to evaluate whether revenue recognition for this transaction is appropriate under IFRS 15 and to assess the audit risk and procedures required, given that revenue recognition around period end is inherently an area of higher risk and this transaction represents a significant portion of the fourth quarter's furniture division revenue.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Meridian Hospitality Transaction Details
exhibits.content:
Order date: August 14
Furniture completed and ready for delivery: October 3
Contract price: $2,400,000, invoiced in full on October 15 and recorded as revenue in the fiscal year ended October 31
Deposit received from Meridian: $600,000 (25% of contract price), balance invoiced as due on delivery per original contract terms, though Cascade recorded the full amount as a receivable and as revenue

Reason for delay: Meridian's new hotel property is still under construction; general contractor delays mean the property will not be ready to receive furniture delivery until approximately January of next year, roughly three months after the audit's October 31 year end.

Storage arrangement: The completed furniture is stored in Cascade's main Winnipeg warehouse, in the same general area as other customers' finished goods inventory awaiting shipment. It has not been physically segregated into a separate area, has not been specially labelled or tagged as sold to Meridian, and remains generally accessible and moveable by warehouse staff in the ordinary course of operations.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Relevant IFRS 15 Bill-and-Hold Criteria
exhibits.content:
Under IFRS 15, for a bill-and-hold arrangement to support revenue recognition before physical delivery, an entity must be able to demonstrate that control of the product has transferred to the customer even though the customer does not yet have physical possession. This generally requires all of the following to be met: the reason for the bill-and-hold arrangement must be substantive, such as the customer requesting the arrangement for its own legitimate reasons; the product must be identified separately as belonging to the customer; the product must currently be ready for physical transfer to the customer; and the entity cannot have the ability to use the product or to direct it to another customer.

Cascade's revenue recognition policy note in the draft financial statements states that revenue on custom furniture orders is recognized when manufacturing is complete and the customer has been invoiced, which does not explicitly reference the bill-and-hold criteria above.

requirements: Assess whether the Meridian transaction meets the criteria for revenue recognition under IFRS 15's bill-and-hold guidance, and determine the appropriate accounting treatment.
Identify the audit risk this transaction represents and describe the audit procedures that should be performed in response.

model_response.understand_issue: The Meridian transaction needs to be tested against IFRS 15's specific bill-and-hold criteria to determine whether Cascade was entitled to recognize the full $2,400,000 of revenue in the current year despite the furniture still sitting, undelivered and unsegregated, in Cascade's own warehouse at year end. This also needs to be evaluated as an audit risk area, since revenue recognition near period end is inherently susceptible to management bias or error, and this single transaction is large enough to be material to the current year's results if recognized prematurely.
model_response.analyze_case:
IFRS 15 requires all of the following criteria to be met before control is considered transferred in a bill-and-hold arrangement: a substantive reason for the arrangement, separate identification of the product as belonging to the customer, current readiness for physical transfer, and the entity's inability to use the product or direct it to another customer. Each criterion needs to be tested against the facts.

Substantive reason: Meridian's hotel property is genuinely still under construction and unable to receive delivery, a real and legitimate customer-driven reason for the delay, not one manufactured to accelerate Cascade's revenue recognition. This criterion is met.

Ready for physical transfer: The furniture was completed by October 3 and is manufactured to Meridian's specifications, ready to ship as soon as the property is available. This criterion is met.

Separate identification: This is where the arrangement fails. The furniture is stored in the same general warehouse area as other customers' inventory, with no special segregation, labelling, or tagging identifying it as sold to Meridian specifically. Without separate identification, there is no clear evidence that this specific furniture, as opposed to functionally identical units, has been set aside exclusively for Meridian, and it remains accessible to and moveable by warehouse staff in the ordinary course of business.

Inability to use or redirect: Related to the identification failure, because the goods are not segregated or specially tagged, Cascade retains the practical ability to use this specific furniture for another customer or purpose, even if it does not currently intend to. The absence of physical segregation is itself evidence that Cascade has not relinquished this ability.

Because the separate identification and non-redirection criteria are not met, the bill-and-hold arrangement does not support revenue recognition under IFRS 15, regardless of Meridian's deposit, legal title provisions in the contract, or written confirmation of its intent to purchase. Control has not transferred. Revenue of $2,400,000 has been recognized prematurely and should not be recognized in the fiscal year ended October 31; the $600,000 deposit received should be recorded as a contract liability (deferred revenue), and the furniture should remain in Cascade's inventory at cost until control actually transfers, most likely upon delivery in January.
model_response.recommendation:
The engagement team should propose an adjustment to reverse the $2,400,000 of revenue and related cost of sales recognized on the Meridian transaction, reclassifying the $600,000 deposit received as a contract liability and reinstating the completed furniture in inventory at cost. Given the transaction's size relative to fourth-quarter furniture division revenue, this adjustment is likely material and should be communicated to those charged with governance regardless of whether management agrees to book it, and if management declines to adjust, the team needs to assess the impact on the audit opinion.

Because this transaction reveals that Cascade's revenue recognition policy for custom orders does not reference the bill-and-hold criteria at all, and was applied by the controller in a way that does not meet those criteria, the team should treat revenue recognition as a significant risk under the applicable auditing standards addressing fraud risk in revenue recognition, and should expand testing beyond this single transaction. Specific procedures should include reviewing all other bill-and-hold or delayed-delivery arrangements recorded as revenue during the year to determine whether the same segregation and control-transfer deficiencies exist, inspecting shipping and delivery documentation for a sample of year-end revenue transactions generally, discussing with management whether the accounting policy note should be corrected to properly reflect the bill-and-hold criteria, and considering whether this represents a control deficiency in the revenue recognition process that should be communicated in a management letter.
model_response.conclusion: The Meridian transaction fails the separate identification and non-redirection criteria of IFRS 15's bill-and-hold guidance because the furniture is not segregated or specially tagged in Cascade's warehouse, meaning control has not transferred and the $2,400,000 of revenue was recognized prematurely. The audit team should propose reversing the revenue, reclassifying the deposit as a contract liability, treat revenue recognition as a significant risk, and expand testing to other bill-and-hold transactions and the underlying accounting policy.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly identifies and applies all four IFRS 15 bill-and-hold criteria to the fact pattern
Correctly concludes the separate identification and non-redirection criteria are not met
Correctly concludes revenue should not be recognized and control has not transferred
Correctly identifies the appropriate accounting treatment (contract liability for deposit, inventory reinstated at cost)
Identifies revenue recognition as a significant audit risk given the size and nature of the transaction
Recommends expanding testing to other bill-and-hold or delayed delivery transactions
Recommends communication to those charged with governance given materiality
Identifies the accounting policy note deficiency and control implications for a management letter

marking_guide.competency: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
marking_guide.criterion: Does not accept the controller's characterization of the transaction without independently testing it against the specific criteria

marking_guide.competency: Acting Ethically and Demonstrating Professional Values
marking_guide.criterion: Maintains professional skepticism given the pattern of a large transaction recognized just before year end

rating_guidance: A CD-level response systematically works through all four IFRS 15 bill-and-hold criteria against the specific facts, correctly identifies which two fail and why, definitively concludes revenue should be reversed, quantifies the correct adjustment, and builds a complete audit response including expanded testing and governance communication. A C-level response reaches the correct no-revenue-recognition conclusion with reasonable application of the criteria but is less thorough on one or two criteria. An RC-level response identifies bill-and-hold as the relevant guidance but reaches an unsupported or partially reasoned conclusion, or identifies the accounting issue without developing an adequate audit response. An NC-level response accepts the controller's revenue recognition without testing it against the bill-and-hold criteria at all.

enabling_focus: Applying a Professional Approach to Decision-Making and Problem-Solving
Adding Value
title: Elmwood Enterprises Ltd. - Business Sale Structure and Valuation Check
case_background:
Elmwood Enterprises Ltd. is a private, profitable landscaping and property maintenance company owned entirely by its founder, Teresa Ibarra, through a Canadian-controlled private corporation. Teresa is 61 and has received an arm's length offer of $3,000,000 from a national property services company to acquire the business. Teresa's adjusted cost base in her Elmwood shares is $200,000, and her tax advisor has confirmed the shares qualify as qualified small business corporation (QSBC) shares, making Teresa eligible to shelter capital gains using her lifetime capital gains exemption, currently set at $1,016,836.

The purchaser has indicated flexibility on deal structure and has asked Teresa's team to evaluate two options: a share sale, where the purchaser buys Teresa's shares directly for $3,000,000, or an asset sale, where Elmwood Enterprises Ltd. itself sells its business assets for $3,000,000 and the after-tax proceeds are then distributed to Teresa as a dividend. Teresa's combined marginal personal tax rate on capital gains is 53.53%, and her combined marginal rate on eligible dividends is approximately 39%. Teresa's advisor has estimated that under the asset sale structure, corporate-level tax on the recapture and capital gain arising from the asset sale would total approximately $650,000, leaving $2,350,000 in the corporation available to distribute as a dividend.

Separately, Teresa has asked whether $3,000,000 is even a fair price, since she has never had the business formally valued and is relying entirely on the purchaser's opening offer. Elmwood's most recent free cash flow was $350,000, expected to grow at a stable 2% per year going forward, and Teresa's advisor believes a discount rate of 12% is appropriate for a business of Elmwood's size and risk profile.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Share Sale vs. Asset Sale Tax Comparison
exhibits.content:
Offer price: $3,000,000
Teresa's adjusted cost base in Elmwood shares: $200,000
Lifetime capital gains exemption available (shares confirmed QSBC-qualified): $1,016,836
Teresa's combined marginal tax rate on capital gains (taxable portion): 53.53%
Teresa's combined marginal tax rate on eligible dividends: 39%

Share sale: Teresa sells her shares directly to the purchaser for $3,000,000; capital gain taxed personally, sheltered in part by the lifetime capital gains exemption.

Asset sale: Elmwood Enterprises Ltd. sells its business assets for $3,000,000; advisor has calculated corporate-level tax (combining recapture of capital cost allowance and capital gains tax) of approximately $650,000, leaving $2,350,000 available in the corporation, which would then be paid to Teresa as an eligible dividend and taxed again at the personal level.

This is an arm's length transaction; section 84.1 anti-surplus-stripping rules, which apply to non-arm's length share sales, are not relevant here.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Business Valuation Inputs
exhibits.content:
Elmwood's most recent annual free cash flow: $350,000
Expected stable long-term growth rate: 2% per year
Appropriate discount rate for a business of this size and risk profile, per Teresa's advisor: 12%

Teresa's advisor recommends using the Gordon growth (constant growth perpetuity) model to estimate a discounted cash flow value of the business as a cross-check against the purchaser's $3,000,000 offer, given the business is expected to generate stable, growing free cash flow indefinitely rather than facing a defined end date or wind-down.

requirements: Calculate and compare Teresa's after-tax net proceeds under the share sale and the asset sale structures, and recommend which structure she should pursue.
Calculate an estimated value of Elmwood using the discounted cash flow approach and assess whether the $3,000,000 offer appears fair.

model_response.understand_issue: Teresa needs a clear after-tax comparison of the share sale versus asset sale structures to understand how much cash she would actually keep under each option, since the two structures are taxed very differently and the purchaser has left the choice open. She separately needs an independent estimate of what Elmwood is worth using a discounted cash flow approach, since she has only ever seen the purchaser's opening offer and has no basis of her own for knowing whether $3,000,000 is a fair starting point for negotiation.
model_response.analyze_case:
Share sale after-tax proceeds:
Capital gain = $3,000,000 - $200,000 adjusted cost base = $2,800,000
Taxable capital gain (50% inclusion rate) = $2,800,000 x 50% = $1,400,000
Lifetime capital gains exemption shelters $1,016,836 of the actual capital gain, which shelters $1,016,836 x 50% = $508,418 of the taxable capital gain
Remaining taxable capital gain subject to tax = $1,400,000 - $508,418 = $891,582
Tax payable = $891,582 x 53.53% = $477,264
After-tax proceeds to Teresa = $3,000,000 - $477,264 = $2,522,736

Asset sale after-tax proceeds:
Corporate-level tax on the sale (recapture and capital gains combined, as estimated by the advisor) = $650,000
After-tax proceeds remaining in the corporation = $3,000,000 - $650,000 = $2,350,000
This amount is distributed to Teresa as an eligible dividend:
Personal tax on the dividend = $2,350,000 x 39% = $916,500
After-tax proceeds to Teresa = $2,350,000 - $916,500 = $1,433,500

Comparing the two: the share sale nets Teresa $2,522,736, while the asset sale nets only $1,433,500, a difference of $1,089,236 in Teresa's favour under the share sale structure. This large gap arises because the share sale is taxed only once, at the personal level, and benefits from the lifetime capital gains exemption, while the asset sale is taxed twice, once inside the corporation on the sale itself and again personally when the proceeds are extracted as a dividend, with no equivalent exemption available at either level for an asset sale.

DCF valuation cross-check, using the Gordon growth model:
Value = Next year's free cash flow / (Discount rate - Growth rate)
Next year's free cash flow = $350,000 x 1.02 = $357,000
Value = $357,000 / (0.12 - 0.02) = $357,000 / 0.10 = $3,570,000

The DCF estimate of $3,570,000 is meaningfully higher than the purchaser's $3,000,000 offer, by $570,000, or about 19%. This suggests the opening offer may understate the business's fair value based on its current cash flow trajectory, though Teresa's advisor should stress test the growth and discount rate assumptions, since a small change in either has a large effect on a perpetuity valuation, and should consider whether the purchaser's offer already reflects deal-specific factors (synergies, control premium, or lack thereof) not captured in a standalone DCF.
model_response.recommendation:
Teresa should pursue the share sale structure, since it is projected to net her approximately $1,089,236 more after tax than the asset sale, driven primarily by the availability of the lifetime capital gains exemption and the avoidance of double taxation. She should confirm with her advisor that all QSBC qualification requirements, including the holding period and the 90% active business asset test at the time of sale, remain satisfied through closing, since any purification issues could jeopardize the exemption. If the purchaser has a genuine preference for an asset deal, typically because it allows a stepped-up cost base for future capital cost allowance claims, Teresa should be prepared to negotiate a higher purchase price under an asset structure to compensate for her materially worse after-tax outcome, rather than accepting the same $3,000,000 headline price under either structure.

On valuation, Teresa should not treat the purchaser's $3,000,000 offer as the final word. The DCF estimate of $3,570,000 provides reasonable support for negotiating a higher price, though she should have her advisor sensitize the valuation to a range of growth and discount rate assumptions rather than relying on a single point estimate, and should consider commissioning a more complete business valuation, including a market or comparable transaction approach, before finalizing her negotiating position. Given the meaningful gap between the offer and the DCF estimate, Teresa is in a reasonable position to counter above $3,000,000.
model_response.conclusion: The share sale nets Teresa approximately $2,522,736 after tax, compared to only $1,433,500 under an asset sale, a difference of roughly $1,089,236 favouring the share structure due to the lifetime capital gains exemption and single layer of taxation, so Teresa should pursue the share sale or negotiate a higher price if an asset deal is required. The DCF estimate of $3,570,000 exceeds the purchaser's $3,000,000 offer by about 19%, suggesting Teresa has room to negotiate a higher price and should not accept the opening offer as fair value without further support.

marking_guide.criterion: Correctly calculates the capital gain and taxable capital gain on the share sale
Correctly applies the lifetime capital gains exemption to the taxable capital gain (50% of the exemption amount)
Correctly calculates after-tax proceeds under the share sale structure
Correctly calculates after-tax proceeds under the asset sale structure, including both corporate and personal tax layers
Correctly identifies double taxation as the reason for the asset sale's inferior outcome and recommends the share sale structure
Notes the need to confirm continued QSBC qualification (purification/holding period) through closing
Correctly applies the Gordon growth model with next year's cash flow (not current year's)
Correctly calculates the DCF value at $3,570,000
Correctly compares the DCF estimate to the offer and draws a supportable negotiating conclusion
Notes the sensitivity of a perpetuity valuation to growth and discount rate assumptions

rating_guidance: A CD-level response correctly calculates both after-tax proceeds figures with the LCGE properly applied at the taxable (50%) level, correctly identifies double taxation as the driver of the gap, correctly applies the Gordon growth model using next year's cash flow, and integrates both analyses into a coherent negotiating recommendation that also flags QSBC qualification risk and valuation sensitivity. A C-level response gets both tax structures and the DCF calculation substantively correct without full discussion of qualification risk or sensitivity. An RC-level response makes an error such as applying the full LCGE dollar amount directly against the taxable capital gain instead of at 50%, or using current year's cash flow instead of next year's in the Gordon growth model. An NC-level response fails to apply the capital gains exemption correctly, ignores the double taxation effect of the asset sale, or omits the DCF calculation entirely.
